project.goal: Stream and render live payment events with fraud scoring for on-call engineers

security.auth: JWT token from internal auth service
security.masking: PAN masking regex applied before all terminal writes
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for Kafka and enrichment API

data.analytics: ClickHouse for historical decline rate queries

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for build, test, release
devops.hosting: Go binary via GitHub Releases and Homebrew tap

constraints.pci: No raw PANs in terminal, logs, or exports

reasoner_reviews.product: The tool addresses a real pain point for on-call engineers: waiting for a dashboard to load during a payments incident. Streaming directly from Kafka ensures zero lag between event occurrence and terminal display. The merchant and error-code filter flags are critical for triaging multi-merchant incidents quickly.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Cobra is the right choice — single binary distribution eliminates dependency hell on engineer laptops. The enrichment call to the fraud scoring service must be non-blocking: buffer events and fetch scores asynchronously to avoid stalling the Kafka consumer. ClickHouse connectivity should be optional and lazy-loaded only when the engineer invokes historical query commands.
reasoner_reviews.security: PAN masking must be applied as a pipeline transform before any byte reaches stdout, stderr, or the CSV writer. The JWT auth token should be loaded from an environment variable or a credential helper, never hardcoded. Audit logging of tool invocations and export actions satisfies PCI-DSS Requirement 10 for data access tracking.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The Kafka consumer must use a dedicated consumer group that does not share offset commits with production consumers. The tool must handle partition rebalance gracefully without dropping visible events. CSV exports are session-scoped to avoid accumulating PCI-sensitive data on engineer workstations.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with Cobra root command and subcommands: stream (live Kafka consumer with TUI), query (ClickHouse historical breakdown), export (snapshot filtered events to CSV)
backend.api_patterns: Internal HTTP client for fraud scoring enrichment API; ClickHouse Go driver for analytical queries; confluent-kafka-go for Kafka consumer
backend.business_modules: kafka_consumer (partition assignment, offset management), event_enricher (async fraud score fetch with timeout), pan_masker (regex pipeline for card data), tui_renderer (bubbletea-based live table), csv_exporter (filtered snapshot writer)
backend.failure_handling: Enrichment API timeouts fall back to rendering score as 'pending'; Kafka consumer retries on transient broker errors with exponential backoff; ClickHouse connection failure returns cached result or descriptive error

security.auth_design: JWT token loaded from PAYMENT_OPS_TOKEN env var or ~/.payment-ops/credentials file; token validated on startup before Kafka connection
security.authorization_model: Token claims restrict which merchant IDs and payment rails the engineer can subscribe to
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Datadog custom event on tool start, filter changes, and CSV export actions including operator identity and timestamp
security.security_risks: Risk of PAN leakage through enrichment API response caching — mitigation: never cache enriched events containing card metadata to disk

data.entities: transaction_events (kafka schema: id, merchant_id, amount, currency, card_bin_masked, payment_method, fraud_score, decision, decline_reason, rail, ts), fraud_signals (enrichment response: score, model_version, reason_codes[]), settlement_batches (ClickHouse: batch_id, merchant_id, amount, settled_at, lag_seconds), decline_reasons (ClickHouse: reason_code, count, merchant_id, window_start)
data.storage_design: No local persistence of raw events; ClickHouse queries run against existing analytics cluster read replica
data.analytics_events: Per-session metrics: events_consumed, enrichment_latency_p99, filters_applied, exports_performed

devops.deployment_topology: Go binary cross-compiled for linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64; distributed via GitHub Releases with SHA256 checksums and Homebrew tap formula
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (golangci-lint), unit tests, integration tests against ClickHouse testcontainer, goreleaser for multi-arch release
devops.observability_stack: Datadog DogStatsD sidecar optional for engineers who have it installed; metrics emitted via UDP with graceful fallback
devops.rollback_strategy: Engineers pin binary version via Homebrew; rollback is brew switch to previous formula

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a PCI-DSS constrained CLI tool for payment ops engineers streaming live Kafka events. Key decisions: Cobra for subcommand structure (stream/query/export), non-blocking async enrichment to avoid stalling the consumer, PAN masking as a mandatory pipeline stage before any output, and ClickHouse as a lazy-loaded historical query backend. No frontend specialist needed — this is CLI architecture only.
target_output.title: Payment Event Stream CLI — Architecture Plan
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI tool using Cobra that streams live transaction events from Kafka, asynchronously enriches each event with real-time fraud scores from an internal scoring service, and renders a live terminal table showing fraud score distributions, settlement lag, and decline rate breakdowns. PAN masking is applied as a mandatory pipeline transform before all terminal and export output. Engineers can filter by merchant ID, payment method, or decline code and export filtered snapshots to session-scoped CSV files. Distributed as a cross-platform binary via GitHub Releases and a Homebrew tap.
target_output.architecture_overview: CLI-native architecture with three Cobra subcommands: 'stream' opens a Kafka consumer on the transaction_events topic and renders a bubbletea TUI table refreshed per partition batch; 'query' connects to a ClickHouse read replica for historical decline and settlement lag breakdowns by time window; 'export' writes a filtered event snapshot to a local CSV. A pan_masker pipeline stage intercepts all event data before any write to stdout or file, replacing card BINs and PANs with masked equivalents (e.g., 411111XXXXXX1111). Fraud score enrichment runs asynchronously via a bounded goroutine pool calling the internal scoring API over TLS 1.3, with scores joined to the TUI table once available. Authentication uses a JWT loaded from PAYMENT_OPS_TOKEN env var, validated against the internal JWKS endpoint on startup.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Go 1.22. CLI framework: Cobra v1.8. TUI rendering: Bubbletea v0.25 with Lipgloss for table styling. Kafka client: confluent-kafka-go v2 (librdkafka bindings). ClickHouse client: clickhouse-go/v2. HTTP enrichment client: net/http with a 200ms timeout. PAN masking: compiled regexp patterns for Luhn-format strings. Build: goreleaser for multi-arch cross-compilation. CI: GitHub Actions. Observability: DogStatsD UDP client (optional). Distribution: GitHub Releases + Homebrew tap.
target_output.functional_feature_map: stream command: subscribes to Kafka transaction_events topic, renders live TUI with columns for merchant_id, payment_method, fraud_score, decision, decline_reason, rail, and latency. Filters: --merchant, --method, --error-code flags applied server-side via consumer filter or client-side on decoded events. query command: connects to ClickHouse, runs pre-built queries for decline rate by reason_code (last N hours), settlement_lag percentiles by merchant, and fraud score distribution histograms. export command: writes current TUI filter view to a timestamped CSV in the current directory with PAN masking enforced.
target_output.system_components: Cobra CLI root with stream/query/export subcommands. Kafka consumer module (confluent-kafka-go, dedicated consumer group payment-ops-cli-cg). Async enrichment pool (10 goroutines, 200ms timeout, score cache per event ID for TUI display). PAN masker (regex pipeline applied to all EventRecord fields before output). Bubbletea TUI renderer (live table, auto-resize on terminal width change). ClickHouse query executor (lazy connection, parameterized queries for decline and lag reports). CSV exporter (session-scoped file, masked fields only). DogStatsD metrics emitter (optional, UDP, fire-and-forget).
target_output.workflows: Live Stream Workflow: Engineer runs 'payops stream --merchant=M123 --method=card'; tool loads JWT, connects to Kafka transaction_events topic on consumer group payment-ops-cli-cg, begins consuming partition batch; each event is passed through pan_masker then dispatched to enrichment pool; TUI table renders within 100ms of event arrival showing fraud_score, decision, and decline_reason; screen refreshes every 500ms. Historical Query Workflow: Engineer runs 'payops query decline-rates --window=4h'; tool establishes ClickHouse connection to read replica, executes parameterized decline_reasons query grouped by reason_code and merchant_id over the specified window, and renders a ranked table in the terminal sorted by count descending. Export Workflow: Engineer runs 'payops export --output=incident-2026-03-15.csv' during active stream; current filter view is flushed to CSV with masked fields; audit event emitted to Datadog with operator JWT subject and export filename.
target_output.data_model: transaction_events (Kafka schema): id STRING, merchant_id STRING, amount INT64 (cents), currency STRING ISO4217, card_bin_masked STRING (first 6 digits only), payment_method ENUM card/ach/wallet, fraud_score FLOAT32, decision ENUM approved/declined/review, decline_reason STRING, payment_rail ENUM visa/mc/amex/ach/sepa, event_ts TIMESTAMP. settlement_batches (ClickHouse): batch_id UUID, merchant_id STRING, gross_amount INT64, net_amount INT64, settled_at TIMESTAMP, lag_seconds INT32. decline_reasons (ClickHouse): reason_code STRING, merchant_id STRING, count INT64, window_start TIMESTAMP, window_end TIMESTAMP. fraud_signals (enrichment API response): event_id STRING, score FLOAT32, model_version STRING, reason_codes STRING[].
target_output.api_design: Internal HTTP enrichment client calls POST /v1/score/batch with body {event_ids: [...], features: {...}}; returns {scores: [{event_id, score, model_version, reason_codes}]}. ClickHouse queries: SELECT reason_code, merchant_id, count(*) FROM decline_reasons WHERE window_start >= now() - interval N HOUR GROUP BY reason_code, merchant_id ORDER BY count DESC. No external API surface — CLI is a consumer-only tool. Kafka consumer group: payment-ops-cli-cg, topic: transaction_events, auto.offset.reset: latest.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS Level 1 controls: PAN masker applied as mandatory pipeline stage — no raw PANs reach stdout, file writes, or enrichment API payloads. JWT loaded from env var PAYMENT_OPS_TOKEN; JWKS endpoint validated on startup with 5-minute refresh. TLS 1.3 enforced for all Kafka broker and enrichment API connections. Audit events logged via Datadog custom events on tool start, filter change, and export, capturing operator JWT subject, command flags, and timestamp. CSV exports restricted to session scope — no background persistence. Consumer group isolation prevents offset pollution of production consumers.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distribution: goreleaser builds linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64 binaries on each GitHub tag. Artifacts published to GitHub Releases with SHA256 checksums and signed with cosign. Homebrew tap formula auto-updated via goreleaser GitHub Action. CI pipeline: golangci-lint for static analysis, go test ./... with Kafka and ClickHouse testcontainers for integration coverage, goreleaser snapshot for PR validation. Version management: engineers pin via 'brew pin payment-ops-cli' and upgrade explicitly. Rollback: brew switch to previous formula. No server-side deployment — entirely client-side binary.
target_output.observability: Datadog DogStatsD UDP (optional, graceful no-op if unavailable): payops.events_consumed counter (tags: merchant_id, method), payops.enrichment_latency histogram (p50/p99), payops.export_count counter. Tool logs structured JSON to stderr for debug mode (--debug flag). ClickHouse query durations logged to stderr in verbose mode. On-call engineers monitor Kafka consumer lag for payment-ops-cli-cg in Datadog to ensure the tool is not falling behind the stream.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Near-zero infrastructure cost — binary runs on engineer laptops. ClickHouse queries hit an existing read replica with no additional compute. Enrichment API calls are bounded by the 10-goroutine pool and 200ms timeout. Kafka consumer group uses at-most 1 partition per running instance to limit broker overhead. GitHub Releases storage is negligible.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (3 weeks): Cobra CLI skeleton, Kafka consumer with confluent-kafka-go, PAN masker pipeline, bubbletea TUI table rendering merchant/fraud_score/decision columns, JWT auth, GitHub Actions CI with testcontainers, GitHub Releases distribution. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Async enrichment goroutine pool with fraud_score join to TUI, --merchant/--method/--error-code filter flags, CSV export command with audit logging. Phase 3 (1 week): ClickHouse query subcommands for decline rates and settlement lag, Homebrew tap setup, Datadog DogStatsD metrics, debug/verbose mode flags.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with golangci-lint enforced in CI. PAN masker must have 100% unit test coverage with Luhn-format test vectors including edge cases (partial PANs, tokenized values). All Cobra command handlers tested with captured stdout. Integration tests use testcontainers for Kafka and ClickHouse. No third-party HTTP client libraries — use net/http with explicit timeouts. No global state — all dependencies injected via command context.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: enrichment API latency spikes cause TUI to render stale fraud scores during incidents — exactly when scores are most needed. Mitigation: render 'pending' scores immediately and update cells when enrichment resolves; add --no-enrich flag to bypass scoring entirely. Risk: ClickHouse read replica lag causes stale decline rate data. Mitigation: display data freshness timestamp in query output. Tradeoff: no local event persistence simplifies PCI compliance but means engineers cannot replay a session — acceptable given Kafka retention covers post-incident analysis. Risk: binary distribution via Homebrew introduces a supply-chain trust dependency — mitigate with cosign signing and published checksums.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose confluent-kafka-go over sarama for librdkafka performance and consumer group rebalance handling. Selected bubbletea over tview for composable TUI component model. Confirmed session-scoped CSV exports satisfy PCI requirement without requiring server-side DLP. Decided against persistent local DB for event history — Kafka retention serves this purpose.

project.goal: Cross-border remittance app with transparent FX and sub-minute settlement
project.target_users: Migrant workers, 10M MAU target

frontend.approach: Expo managed workflow for OTA updates

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3.2 with Spring WebFlux for reactive corridors

security.screening: OFAC SDN screening via internal sanctions service

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 with read replicas per region
data.cache: Redis 7 for FX quote TTL (30-second lock), session cache

constraints.screening: OFAC must clear before fund movement

reasoner_reviews.product: At round 4, the product scope is well-locked. The weakness in previous rounds was insufficient detail on corridor-specific routing logic and the FX quote lock mechanism. The 30-second quote TTL stored in Redis is the right approach but the plan must specify what happens when a quote expires mid-checkout flow — the app must re-fetch and surface the updated rate to the user before allowing confirmation.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Previous plan underspecified the payment rail selection logic. The corridor router must evaluate SWIFT, local ACH equivalents, and mobile money networks (e.g., M-Pesa for East Africa corridors) based on beneficiary country and pick-up method. Spring WebFlux is appropriate for the reactive corridor orchestration layer given the need to fan out to multiple rail providers concurrently. The GKE multi-region topology needs explicit coverage for active-active vs active-passive per corridor.
reasoner_reviews.security: OFAC screening gap from round 3 remains: the plan mentioned screening at transfer initiation but did not specify re-screening on fund movement if the transfer is queued for more than 24 hours. FinCEN requires re-screening if a significant time delay occurs between acceptance and execution. This must be addressed.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10M MAU at sub-200ms API response requires horizontal pod autoscaling on the transfer and quote services with PodDisruptionBudgets to prevent scaling events from interrupting in-flight transactions. PostgreSQL connection pooling via PgBouncer is mandatory at this scale — raw connection limits will be hit well before 10M MAU.

frontend.app_structure: React Native Expo with feature-based folder structure: /corridors, /transfer, /kyc, /beneficiaries, /history, /notifications
frontend.pages_and_flows: Onboarding (phone verification, KYC upload), Home (balance + recent transfers), Send Money (corridor select → FX quote → beneficiary → funding source → confirm → tracking), History, Profile + Settings
frontend.state_management: Redux Toolkit for transfer state machine and KYC status; RTK Query for API calls with automatic re-fetch on focus; Expo SecureStore for tokens
frontend.performance_notes: React Native Hermes engine; virtualized transfer history lists; image compression for KYC document uploads before upload; offline-capable corridor list cached locally

backend.service_design: Microservices: transfer-service (orchestrates corridor routing and lifecycle), quote-service (FX rate fetch and TTL lock), kyc-service (document verification webhook handler), notification-service (FCM push), sanctions-service (OFAC screening)
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.0; idempotency keys on all transfer creation and payment endpoints; webhook receiver for KYC provider callbacks
backend.background_jobs: Scheduled re-screening job for queued transfers > 24 hours; nightly settlement reconciliation against correspondent bank reports; FX rate refresh from provider APIs every 30 seconds
backend.failure_handling: Transfer state machine with explicit states (initiated/screening/funded/routing/in_transit/settled/failed); compensating transactions for partial failures in corridor routing

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth for phone-based identity; custom JWT minted on backend with transfer permission claims after biometric step-up; certificate pinning in React Native via react-native-ssl-pinning
security.authorization_model: User can only access own remittance_orders and beneficiaries; admin roles for compliance team access to KYC queue
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Immutable audit log for every OFAC screening result, KYC status change, and fund movement event; retained 7 years per BSA

data.entities: remittance_orders (id, sender_id, beneficiary_id, corridor_id, amount_sent, currency_sent, amount_received, currency_received, fx_quote_id, rail ENUM swift/ach/mobile_money, status, created_at), fx_quotes (id, corridor_id, rate, fee, expires_at, locked_by_order_id), beneficiaries (id, user_id, name, country, pick_up_method ENUM bank/cash/mobile_money, account_details_encrypted), kyc_verifications (id, user_id, provider_ref, status ENUM pending/approved/rejected, document_type, verified_at), wallets (id, user_id, balance INT64 cents, currency, last_updated)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with regional read replicas; Redis for FX quote TTL locks; GCS for KYC document storage with CMEK

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot clusters in us-central1 (primary), europe-west1 (EU corridors), asia-southeast1 (APAC corridors); Cloud Load Balancing for geo-routing; Cloud Armor WAF
devops.ci_cd_design: CircleCI pipelines: unit tests, integration tests, React Native Expo build for iOS and Android, Docker image build and push to Artifact Registry, Helm chart deploy to staging then production with manual approval gate
devops.observability_stack: Grafana + Prometheus; custom corridor SLA dashboard tracking p50/p95 settlement time per corridor; alerting on settlement SLA breach and OFAC screening timeout

ISS-001.title: OFAC re-screening gap for queued transfers
ISS-001.detail: Plan specifies OFAC screening at initiation but does not address re-screening for transfers queued more than 24 hours. FinCEN requires re-screening on execution if significant time has elapsed.

ISS-002.title: FX quote expiry handling during checkout not specified
ISS-002.detail: The plan does not describe what the app does when a 30-second FX quote expires mid-checkout. The user experience and re-quote flow must be explicitly designed.

ISS-003.title: PostgreSQL connection pool limits at 10M MAU not addressed
ISS-003.detail: No mention of PgBouncer or connection pooling strategy. At 10M MAU the transfer service will exhaust PostgreSQL max_connections without pooling.

focus_issues.title: OFAC re-screening gap for queued transfers
focus_issues.detail: FinCEN requires re-screening on execution for transfers queued >24h. Not addressed in previous plan.

focus_issues.title: FX quote expiry handling during checkout not specified
focus_issues.detail: 30-second TTL expiry mid-checkout flow not handled in previous plan.

previous_plan.title: CrossBorder Remittance Mobile Platform — Round 3
previous_plan.executive_summary: React Native app with Java/Spring Boot backend on GKE for cross-border remittance targeting 10M MAU. Multi-rail corridor routing with OFAC screening at initiation.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth with biometric step-up; OFAC screening at transfer initiation; KYC document retention 5 years.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 revision targeting two blocking issues: OFAC re-screening gap (critical, FinCEN compliance) and FX quote expiry handling (high, UX). Also addressing PgBouncer for 10M MAU PostgreSQL scaling. The re-screening job must be a scheduled sanctions-service task that queries for transfers in 'funded' or 'routing' state older than 24 hours and re-screens before execution. The FX quote expiry flow requires a modal in the checkout screen and a re-quote API call with the new rate presented before confirmation is re-enabled.

fix_report.action_taken: Added scheduled OFAC re-screening job in sanctions-service that queries remittance_orders WHERE status IN (funded, routing) AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL 24 HOURS, re-screens each transfer against the current OFAC SDN list, and halts fund movement for any newly matched transfers pending compliance review. Job runs every 6 hours.
fix_report.expected_outcome: FinCEN compliance gap closed; all queued transfers re-screened before execution regardless of queue duration.

fix_report.action_taken: Added FX quote expiry handling to checkout workflow: app polls quote TTL remaining via GET /v1/quotes/:id/status; when TTL drops below 5 seconds, a non-blocking warning is shown; on expiry, checkout is paused and a re-quote modal is displayed showing the updated rate and fee; user must confirm before proceeding. Added re-quote endpoint POST /v1/quotes/refresh.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Users are never silently charged a different rate than shown; FX quote expiry is handled gracefully without losing checkout progress.

target_output.title: CrossBorder Remittance Mobile Platform — Revision 4
target_output.executive_summary: React Native cross-border remittance app backed by Java 21 Spring Boot microservices on GKE Autopilot handling 10 million MAU. This revision resolves the OFAC re-screening compliance gap by adding a 6-hour scheduled re-screening job for queued transfers, specifies FX quote expiry handling with a checkout re-quote modal, and adds PgBouncer connection pooling for PostgreSQL scalability. Multi-rail corridor routing (SWIFT, ACH equivalents, mobile money) with sub-60-second settlement confirmation for top corridors.
target_output.architecture_overview: Multi-region GKE Autopilot deployment across us-central1, europe-west1, and asia-southeast1. Core microservices: transfer-service (corridor orchestration, Spring WebFlux reactive), quote-service (FX rate cache with 30-second Redis TTL), kyc-service (document verification webhook handler), sanctions-service (real-time OFAC screening + 6-hour re-screening scheduled job), notification-service (FCM push for settlement updates). React Native Expo app with Redux Toolkit transfer state machine. PostgreSQL 16 with PgBouncer connection pooling per service deployment and regional read replicas for history queries. Certificate pinning on mobile client for all API traffic.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native with TypeScript, Expo managed workflow, Redux Toolkit + RTK Query, react-native-ssl-pinning. Backend: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.2, Spring WebFlux. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with PgBouncer 1.22 (pool_mode=transaction), regional read replicas. Cache: Redis 7 for FX quote TTL and session cache. Auth: Firebase Auth + custom JWT. Cloud: GCP GKE Autopilot, Cloud Load Balancing, Cloud Armor WAF. CI/CD: CircleCI. Observability: Grafana + Prometheus. KYC documents: GCS with CMEK encryption.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Send Money flow: corridor selection (30 active corridors), FX quote lock with 30-second TTL and expiry re-quote modal, beneficiary selection or creation, funding source debit (card or bank), OFAC screening gate, rail routing decision (SWIFT/ACH/mobile money), real-time settlement push notification. KYC flow: document upload with OCR pre-check, identity verification webhook from provider, status polling in app. Pick-up Agent Locator: geolocation-based agent search for cash pick-up corridors. Transaction History: paginated with real-time status updates for in-transit transfers.
target_output.system_components: transfer-service (Spring WebFlux, manages remittance_order state machine), quote-service (FX rate provider integration, Redis TTL lock, re-quote endpoint), kyc-service (document upload to GCS, webhook handler for verification callbacks), sanctions-service (real-time OFAC screening + 6-hour scheduled batch re-screening job), notification-service (FCM integration for push), PgBouncer (transaction-mode pooling, 50-connection max per service pod), PostgreSQL 16 primary + read replicas per region, Redis 7 cluster, GKE Autopilot multi-region, Cloud Armor WAF.
target_output.workflows: Transfer Initiation Workflow: User selects corridor and amount; app fetches FX quote (POST /v1/quotes) with 30s TTL stored in Redis; user selects beneficiary and funding source; on confirm, transfer-service creates remittance_order in 'initiated' state; sanctions-service screens against OFAC SDN list; if clear, status advances to 'funded'; corridor router selects optimal rail (SWIFT for EU bank corridors, local ACH equivalent for domestic clearing, M-Pesa API for East Africa cash pick-up); settlement confirmation webhook from rail provider triggers status to 'settled' and FCM push to sender. FX Quote Expiry Workflow: App polls GET /v1/quotes/:id/status every 5 seconds; when TTL <= 5 seconds, warning toast displayed; on expiry, checkout paused, re-quote modal shows new rate and fee delta; user must tap 'Accept New Rate' to proceed; POST /v1/quotes/refresh creates new quote and resumes checkout. OFAC Re-Screening Workflow: Every 6 hours, sanctions-service scheduled job queries remittance_orders WHERE status IN ('funded','routing') AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours'; each transfer re-screened against current OFAC SDN list; matched transfers moved to 'compliance_hold' status, compliance team alerted via PagerDuty; unmatched transfers proceed to routing.
target_output.data_model: remittance_orders (id UUID PK, sender_id UUID FK, beneficiary_id UUID FK, corridor_id UUID FK, fx_quote_id UUID FK, amount_sent INT64 cents, currency_sent CHAR3, amount_received INT64, currency_received CHAR3, rail ENUM swift/ach/mobile_money, status ENUM initiated/screening/funded/routing/in_transit/settled/failed/compliance_hold, screening_result JSONB, created_at, updated_at). fx_quotes (id UUID PK, corridor_id UUID FK, rate DECIMAL, fee INT64 cents, expires_at TIMESTAMP, locked_by_order_id UUID). beneficiaries (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, name TEXT, country CHAR2, pick_up_method ENUM bank/cash/mobile_money, account_details_encrypted BYTEA). kyc_verifications (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, provider_ref TEXT, status ENUM pending/approved/rejected, document_type ENUM passport/national_id/drivers_license, verified_at TIMESTAMP). wallets (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, balance INT64 cents, currency CHAR3). corridors (id UUID PK, source_country CHAR2, dest_country CHAR2, available_rails TEXT[], active BOOL).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/transfers (initiate remittance, returns order_id and status). GET /v1/transfers/:id (poll status). POST /v1/quotes (create FX quote, returns quote_id, rate, fee, expires_at). GET /v1/quotes/:id/status (TTL remaining, current rate). POST /v1/quotes/refresh (re-quote for expired TTL, returns new quote). POST /v1/beneficiaries (create beneficiary). GET /v1/beneficiaries (list sender's beneficiaries). POST /v1/kyc/initiate (begin KYC flow). GET /v1/agents?country=KE&lat=&lng= (agent locator). All endpoints require Firebase JWT + custom claims. Idempotency-Key header required on POST /v1/transfers.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth with phone verification; biometric step-up for transfers >$200 (Android BiometricPrompt, iOS LocalAuthentication). Certificate pinning via react-native-ssl-pinning for all API traffic. TLS 1.3 enforced. Real-time OFAC SDN screening via sanctions-service before any fund movement. Scheduled 6-hour re-screening for transfers queued >24 hours — addresses FinCEN requirement for re-screening on execution delay. KYC document stored in GCS with CMEK; 5-year BSA retention. Immutable audit log table for all screening results, KYC status changes, and fund movements retained 7 years. GDPR: right-to-erasure for non-transaction personal data; EU corridors processed in europe-west1 region. Cloud Armor WAF with OWASP rule set on API gateway.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot multi-region: us-central1 (primary, all corridors), europe-west1 (EU corridors, GDPR data residency), asia-southeast1 (APAC corridors). Cloud Load Balancing with geo-routing. Each microservice deployed as a GKE Deployment with HorizontalPodAutoscaler (CPU 60% target, min 3 replicas, max 30) and PodDisruptionBudget (maxUnavailable 1). PgBouncer deployed as sidecar in transfer-service pods (transaction pool mode, 50-connection pool per pod). CircleCI pipeline: unit tests, integration tests with Testcontainers PostgreSQL and Redis, React Native Expo build (iOS/Android), Docker image push to Artifact Registry, Helm deploy to staging with automated smoke tests, manual promotion gate to production. Rollback: Helm rollback to previous release. KYC document lifecycle: GCS with object versioning, 5-year retention lifecycle rule.
target_output.observability: Grafana + Prometheus. Key dashboards: corridor settlement SLA (p50/p95 settlement time per corridor and rail), OFAC screening latency (p99 must be <2s), FX quote conversion rate (quotes created vs transfers initiated), KYC approval funnel by document type. Alerts: settlement SLA breach (>60s for top 5 corridors), OFAC screening timeout, sanctions-service scheduled job failure, PostgreSQL connection pool saturation. Prometheus metrics: transfer_initiation_total (labels: corridor, rail), settlement_duration_seconds histogram, ofac_screening_duration_seconds, quote_expiry_rate (quotes expired before use by corridor).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot scales to 10M MAU with HPA on transfer and quote services. PgBouncer eliminates PostgreSQL connection exhaustion at scale. Redis cluster handles FX quote TTL cache at high QPS. Estimated GCP cost at 10M MAU: ~$45,000/month (GKE compute, PostgreSQL replicas, GCS for KYC documents, Cloud Load Balancing). Cost controls: use Committed Use Discounts for baseline GKE node capacity; archive KYC documents to Coldline after 1 year active use; Redis eviction policy for expired quote cache entries.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (16 weeks): Core transfer flow for 5 corridors (US→Mexico, US→Philippines, UK→India, US→Nigeria, US→El Salvador), Firebase Auth, KYC integration with Onfido, OFAC real-time screening, FCM push notifications, React Native MVP, GKE us-central1 deployment, CI/CD pipeline. Phase 2 (8 weeks): 25 additional corridors, mobile money rails (M-Pesa, GCash), FX quote expiry re-quote modal, PgBouncer connection pooling, europe-west1 region for EU GDPR compliance, Grafana corridor SLA dashboards. Phase 3 (6 weeks): OFAC 6-hour re-screening scheduled job, agent locator map, bill payment vertical, HPA tuning for 10M MAU load, FinCEN MSB compliance reporting automation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.2; Spotless formatter and Checkstyle in CI. Transfer state machine transitions must have unit tests for every valid and invalid transition. React Native: ESLint + Prettier; Detox E2E tests for send money flow, KYC upload, and push notification receipt. API contracts versioned with OpenAPI 3.0 and contract tests via Spring Cloud Contract. No direct PostgreSQL connections from application code — all traffic through PgBouncer. Feature flags via GCP Feature Flags for gradual corridor rollouts.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Rail provider outage on a high-volume corridor (e.g., Mexico) cascades to sender experience. Mitigation: corridor router maintains fallback rail priority list and automatically routes to backup on provider timeout. Risk: OFAC SDN list update latency — sanctions-service refreshes list every 15 minutes from OFAC website; a brief window exists where a newly sanctioned entity could pass screening. Mitigation: 6-hour re-screening job provides second gate. Tradeoff: FX quote 30-second TTL balances rate accuracy against user checkout time — shorter TTLs increase quote abandonment, longer TTLs expose the platform to FX rate risk. Tradeoff: Biometric step-up only above $200 trades security for UX on small transfers common in the target demographic.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved: OFAC re-screening implemented as 6-hour scheduled job in sanctions-service. Resolved: FX quote expiry handled with re-quote modal in checkout flow. Resolved: PgBouncer transaction-mode pooling added to all service deployments. Selected Spring WebFlux for reactive corridor orchestration over blocking Spring MVC to support concurrent rail fan-out.

metadata.notes: Round 4 revision resolving critical OFAC re-screening gap and FX quote expiry handling for 10M MAU remittance app.

project_goal.value: Train and serve gradient-boosted and transformer-based fraud scoring models for card-not-present merchants, returning authorization scores in under 50ms with continuous retraining on chargeback data
project_goal.rationale: Core business need: real-time ML-based fraud prevention adapting to evolving attack patterns

target_users.value: Card-not-present merchants and their card processors via API integration
target_users.rationale: Merchants consume scores through processor integrations; internal data scientists manage models

project_class.rationale: Primary value is ML model training and inference pipeline

capabilities.value: backend, data, ai_llm, integrations, analytics, batch_jobs, public_api
capabilities.rationale: Inference API, batch retraining, analytics dashboard, and external integrations required

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-50ms scoring SLA, champion/challenger model evaluation, continuous retraining pipeline

risk_level.rationale: Handles card transaction data; incorrect scores cause financial losses or false declines

data_sensitivity.rationale: Authorization requests contain card metadata subject to PCI-DSS

external_exposure.rationale: API access restricted to authenticated merchant processors via mTLS and API keys

access_model.value: API key + mTLS for processor integrations; Cognito JWT for internal dashboard and model management
access_model.rationale: Dual access model separates processor traffic from internal data science workflows

feature_scope.value: Real-time fraud scoring API, feature engineering pipeline, model training and evaluation, champion/challenger promotion, chargeback ingestion, merchant risk profiling, explainability reports
feature_scope.rationale: Full ML lifecycle from data ingestion through inference and model governance

mvp_scope.value: XGBoost scoring API with velocity-based features, nightly retraining on chargeback data, Cognito-protected model management dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Establishes core inference path and retraining loop before adding transformer models

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, mTLS for processor endpoints, PCI-DSS Level 1 card data masking, Cognito MFA for internal users, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS Level 1 and financial data sensitivity mandate these controls

frontend_stack.value: None — internal model management via API and CLI tooling only
frontend_stack.rationale: ai_system class without frontend; data scientists use Jupyter and CLI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; inference server with ONNX Runtime for model serving
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for low-latency async API; ONNX Runtime for vendor-neutral model execution

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for feature store and analytics; S3 for model artifacts and raw chargeback files; Redis 7 for velocity counter cache
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse excels at high-throughput analytical queries for feature computation; Redis for sub-millisecond counter lookups

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate for inference and API; ECS scheduled tasks for batch retraining; S3 for model artifact registry
hosting_target.rationale: Fargate eliminates cluster management while supporting independent scaling of inference and retraining workloads

privacy_retention_policy.value: Authorization request logs retained 7 years for PCI audit; raw card data masked before persistence; chargeback records retained 5 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: PCI-DSS audit trail and financial record retention requirements

future_scope.value: Transformer-based sequence model for behavioral anomaly detection; real-time A/B scoring for merchant-level model personalization
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap after XGBoost baseline is validated in production

constraints.value: 50ms p99 scoring SLA; PCI-DSS Level 1 compliance; no storage of full PANs; model explainability required for issuer dispute documentation
constraints.rationale: Processor SLA contractual obligation and regulatory requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with model inference traces; custom metrics for fraud score distribution, false positive rate, and model drift; PagerDuty for SLA breaches
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; model drift detection critical for fraud system health

execution_preference.value: Iterative ML sprints with two-week cycles; model promotion gated on offline evaluation thresholds
execution_preference.rationale: ML development cadence with quality gates prevents degraded models from reaching production

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS Level 1: card data tokenization, no full PAN storage, quarterly penetration tests, annual QSA audit; model explainability documentation for issuer dispute resolution
compliance_context.rationale: Card-not-present transaction data and merchant processor contracts mandate PCI-DSS Level 1

llm_integration.value: Transformer-based sequence encoder (BERT-style) fine-tuned on transaction event sequences for behavioral anomaly embedding; ONNX-exported and served via ONNX Runtime sidecar
llm_integration.rationale: Sequence model captures temporal card usage patterns XGBoost cannot encode in tabular features

project.goal: Sub-50ms fraud scoring for card-not-present authorization requests with continuous model retraining on chargeback feedback
project.target_users: Card processors and CNP merchants via API; internal data scientists via CLI and notebooks
project.timeline: MVP in 16 weeks; transformer model in 6 months

backend.framework: FastAPI with async endpoints; ONNX Runtime for model inference

security.auth: mTLS for processor endpoints; Cognito JWT for internal API; API key header as secondary credential
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 in transit; AES-256 at rest in ClickHouse; S3 SSE-KMS for model artifacts

data.db: ClickHouse for feature store and analytics

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with model evaluation gates
devops.hosting: ECS Fargate for inference and batch retraining

constraints.pci: No full PAN storage; card data masked before persistence

confirmed_decisions.feature_store: ClickHouse over Feast for operational simplicity

reasoner_reviews.product: The champion/challenger promotion gate with precision-recall thresholds is the right approach to prevent model degradation. Ensure chargeback-to-authorization join latency does not bottleneck the nightly retraining job — ClickHouse materialized views should pre-aggregate this.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ONNX Runtime sidecar pattern decouples model format from serving infrastructure. The velocity counter design in Redis must handle atomic increments with TTL correctly to avoid race conditions during high-throughput bursts above 10K TPS. Consider read-replica ClickHouse nodes for analytics queries to protect the feature store path.
reasoner_reviews.security: All card data must be truncated to BIN + last4 before insertion into ClickHouse. mTLS certificates for processor endpoints must be rotated on a 90-day schedule via ACM. PCI scope reduction requires a network segmentation audit confirming the inference cluster cannot reach systems outside PCI scope.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 50ms p99 SLA is achievable only if Redis velocity lookups complete in under 5ms. Benchmark the ONNX model warm-path latency under load before committing to the SLA in processor contracts. Retraining job must not saturate ClickHouse I/O during business hours — schedule after midnight UTC.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Previous plan rounds lacked explicit model versioning strategy in the artifact registry. The S3 model artifact path must include a content-addressable hash to prevent silent overwrites during concurrent retraining runs. Fix report must address version collision risk.

backend.service_design: Two Fargate services: InferenceService (FastAPI + ONNX Runtime sidecar, 4 tasks min, auto-scale to 20 on queue depth) and RetrainingService (scheduled ECS task triggered by EventBridge nightly at 01:00 UTC). Feature extraction as a shared Python library imported by both.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/score accepts authorization_request JSON, returns fraud_score (0.0-1.0), decision (approve/review/decline), reason_codes[], model_version_id, latency_ms. POST /v1/models/promote for champion promotion. GET /v1/models/active for current champion metadata.
backend.llm_integration_design: BERT-style transaction sequence encoder pre-trained on 18 months of authorization sequences, fine-tuned on fraud labels. ONNX-exported to S3 with SHA-256 content hash. ONNX Runtime sidecar on InferenceService loads model on startup; reload triggered by POST /internal/model/reload with new S3 path. Embedding concatenated with tabular features before XGBoost scoring layer.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly chargeback ingestion: pull dispute files from S3 drop zone, join to authorization_requests in ClickHouse, compute updated fraud labels. Retraining: XGBoost training on labeled feature vectors with stratified split, SHAP explainability export, challenger evaluation against champion on holdout set, promotion if F1 improvement > 0.005.
backend.failure_handling: Redis unavailability falls back to degraded scoring using only static merchant-level features with explicit score_confidence=low flag in response. ONNX Runtime load failure triggers circuit breaker returning 503 to processor; Datadog alert fires within 30s.

security.auth_design: mTLS with ACM-issued certificates for all processor-facing /v1/score endpoints. API key header (X-Merchant-API-Key) validated against Secrets Manager for secondary auth layer. Internal management endpoints require Cognito JWT with data_scientist or admin role.
security.secrets_management: Model S3 paths and DB credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation. PCI-scoped secrets isolated in a separate secret prefix. Lambda rotation function for Redis auth token.
security.privacy_controls: Full PANs never stored; authorization_requests persisted with BIN (first 6) + last4 + card_hash (SHA-256 of full PAN + merchant salt). Card hash used for velocity lookups without storing raw PAN. All ClickHouse columns containing card data encrypted at column level with KMS.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every /v1/score call logged to audit_log ClickHouse table with: request_id, merchant_id, card_hash, model_version_id, score, decision, latency_ms, timestamp. CloudTrail for AWS API calls. Logs retained 7 years in S3 Glacier after 90 days in ClickHouse.

data.entities: authorization_requests (id UUID, merchant_id, card_bin, card_last4, card_hash, amount_cents, currency, device_fingerprint, ip_hash, mcc, timestamp). fraud_scores (id UUID, request_id FK, model_version_id FK, score FLOAT, decision ENUM approve/review/decline, reason_codes TEXT[], latency_ms INT, scored_at). chargebacks (id UUID, request_id FK, reason_code, dispute_amount_cents, received_at, label BOOL). merchant_profiles (id UUID, merchant_id UNIQUE, risk_tier ENUM low/medium/high, monthly_volume_cents, chargeback_rate_30d, updated_at). model_versions (id UUID, model_type ENUM xgboost/transformer, s3_path, sha256_hash, training_run_id, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, promoted_at, status ENUM challenger/champion/retired).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree tables for authorization_requests and fraud_scores (partitioned by toYYYYMM(timestamp), sorted by merchant_id, timestamp). Redis sorted sets for velocity counters keyed by card_hash:window with TTL. S3 versioned bucket for model artifacts with lifecycle rule archiving to Glacier after 1 year.
data.analytics_events: Score distribution histogram per merchant per hour, false positive rate computed from chargebacks joined to fraud_scores where decision=approve, model drift metric (KL divergence of score distribution vs baseline), processing latency p50/p95/p99.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster: InferenceService (us-east-1a and 1c, ALB with mTLS termination), RetrainingService (single-AZ scheduled task). S3 artifact bucket in us-east-1. ClickHouse cluster on EC2 r6i.2xlarge (3-node with replication). Redis ElastiCache cluster mode disabled (single primary for atomic operations).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, unit tests (pytest), integration tests against staging ClickHouse replica, model evaluation gate (challenger must exceed champion F1 by 0.005 before deploy), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling update. Model promotion workflow requires manual approval in GitHub Environments.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with distributed traces across InferenceService and Redis. Custom Datadog metrics: fraud_score_distribution (histogram), model_drift_kl_divergence (gauge), velocity_cache_hit_rate (gauge). PagerDuty: p99 latency > 50ms, Redis hit rate < 90%, model drift > 0.15 KL.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS deploys maintain 2 previous task definition revisions. Model rollback: POST /internal/model/reload with previous champion S3 path. Automated rollback triggered if p99 latency exceeds 75ms for 3 consecutive minutes post-deploy.

ISS-001.title: Model artifact S3 path has no content-addressable versioning
ISS-001.detail: Previous plan stored model artifacts at s3://models/champion/model.onnx, allowing silent overwrites during concurrent retraining runs. No SHA-256 hash in path prevents auditability and safe rollback.

history.note: Identified in round 2 audit — path scheme not changed
Partial fix attempted but hash not incorporated into path, only stored as metadata tag

ISS-002.title: Redis velocity counter increment has race condition under burst load
ISS-002.detail: Plan uses GET + SET pattern for velocity counter updates instead of atomic INCR with EXPIRE, creating a race window that can undercount events during 10K+ TPS bursts, causing fraud signals to be missed.

history.note: Raised in round 3; fix_report claimed INCR but implementation section still described GET/SET pattern

ISS-003.title: Chargeback-to-authorization join latency not addressed in retraining pipeline
ISS-003.detail: Nightly retraining job performs a full table join of chargebacks against authorization_requests at runtime. At 200M daily authorizations and 0.1% chargeback rate, this join takes 40+ minutes, delaying retraining completion past the maintenance window.

history.note: Newly identified in round 3 audit

Replace GET/SET velocity counter pattern with atomic Redis INCR + EXPIRE commands

previous_plan.title: Transaction Anomaly Detection Platform — Round 3 Plan
previous_plan.executive_summary: Real-time fraud scoring system for CNP merchants using XGBoost and ONNX Runtime on ECS Fargate with ClickHouse feature store and Redis velocity counters. Nightly retraining on chargeback data with champion/challenger promotion.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI InferenceService on ECS Fargate behind ALB with mTLS; ONNX Runtime sidecar loads model from S3; Redis for velocity counters; ClickHouse for feature store and analytics; EventBridge schedules nightly RetrainingService.
previous_plan.data_model: authorization_requests, fraud_scores, chargebacks, merchant_profiles, model_versions tables in ClickHouse. Model artifacts at s3://models/champion/model.onnx (unfixed path).
previous_plan.workflows: Score request featurizes card_hash velocity from Redis GET/SET counters (unfixed), runs ONNX inference, returns score and reason codes. Nightly retraining joins chargebacks to authorizations at runtime (slow join, unfixed).

best_plan.title: Transaction Anomaly Detection Platform — Round 2 Plan
best_plan.executive_summary: Solid foundation but artifact versioning and velocity counter issues first identified here remain open.
best_plan.architecture_overview: ECS Fargate inference cluster, ClickHouse feature store, Redis velocity counters, S3 model registry, nightly retraining pipeline.
best_plan.data_model: Core entities present; model_versions table lacks sha256_hash column in this version.
best_plan.security_and_compliance: PCI controls defined; mTLS specified but certificate rotation schedule absent in this version.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 focuses on closing ISS-001 and ISS-002 which have persisted across three rounds. For ISS-001, the model artifact S3 path scheme must incorporate the SHA-256 hash as part of the object key, not just metadata, and the model_versions table must record this hash for rollback targeting. For ISS-002, every velocity counter interaction must use Redis atomic INCR with EXPIRE rather than GET/SET; the architecture section, data flow description, and backend code guidance must all be internally consistent. ISS-003 (retraining join performance) is addressed via a ClickHouse materialized view that pre-joins chargebacks to authorization features incrementally on chargeback insert.

fix_report.action_taken: S3 artifact path scheme changed to s3://fraud-models/{model_type}/{sha256_hash}.onnx. model_versions table updated to include sha256_hash VARCHAR(64) as a required column populated at training completion. Model reload endpoint updated to accept s3_path containing the hash. Rollback procedure targets specific hash path.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Concurrent retraining runs write to distinct S3 paths; rollback is deterministic by hash reference; no silent overwrites possible

fix_report.action_taken: All velocity counter update logic replaced with atomic Redis INCR {card_hash}:{window} followed by EXPIRE {card_hash}:{window} {ttl_seconds} using a Lua script to ensure atomicity. GET/SET pattern removed from all plan sections. Feature extraction library updated to use pipeline([INCR, EXPIRE]) for each of the three velocity windows (15m, 1h, 24h).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Velocity counter accuracy maintained at peak TPS with no race window; undercounting eliminated

target_output.title: Transaction Anomaly Detection Platform — Fraud Scoring ML System
target_output.executive_summary: A PCI-DSS Level 1 compliant real-time fraud scoring platform for card-not-present merchants, built on FastAPI and ONNX Runtime on AWS ECS Fargate with a ClickHouse feature store and Redis velocity counters. Transformer and XGBoost models are served via an ONNX sidecar, scoring authorization requests in under 50ms p99. A nightly ECS retraining pipeline ingests chargeback dispute data, evaluates a challenger model against the champion on a holdout set, and promotes on F1 improvement threshold, with model artifacts stored at content-addressable S3 paths to prevent overwrites.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-tier ECS Fargate architecture: InferenceService (FastAPI + ONNX Runtime sidecar) sits behind an ALB with mTLS termination for processor traffic and handles real-time scoring requests. RetrainingService runs as a scheduled ECS task via EventBridge at 01:00 UTC, ingesting chargeback S3 drop files, updating a ClickHouse materialized view (labeled_authorization_features), training the challenger model, and uploading artifacts to s3://fraud-models/{model_type}/{sha256_hash}.onnx. Redis 7 ElastiCache stores velocity counters for 15-minute, 1-hour, and 24-hour windows using atomic INCR+EXPIRE Lua scripts. Cognito secures internal management endpoints; mTLS with ACM-rotated certificates secures processor endpoints. Datadog APM traces every scoring path end-to-end.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI (async), ONNX Runtime 1.17 sidecar, XGBoost 2.0, scikit-learn for preprocessing, SHAP for explainability. Feature Store: ClickHouse 24.x on EC2 r6i.2xlarge (3-node replication). Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache (cluster mode disabled for atomic Lua scripts). Model Registry: S3 versioned bucket with SSE-KMS. Auth: AWS Cognito for internal users, mTLS via ACM for processor endpoints. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB, EventBridge, ECR, CloudTrail. Observability: Datadog APM, custom metrics, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Real-Time Scoring: POST /v1/score receives authorization_request, extracts card_hash velocity counters from Redis atomically, fetches merchant_profile from ClickHouse (cached 5 minutes), runs ONNX inference, returns fraud_score, decision (approve/review/decline), reason_codes, model_version_id, latency_ms. Model Management: POST /v1/models/promote triggers champion promotion with s3_path and sha256_hash validation; GET /v1/models/active returns current champion metadata. Retraining Pipeline: nightly chargeback ingestion, labeled_authorization_features materialized view refresh, XGBoost challenger training, SHAP explainability export, holdout evaluation, conditional promotion. Merchant Risk Profiling: asynchronous updates to merchant_profiles based on 30-day rolling chargeback_rate. Explainability Reports: SHAP feature importance exported to S3 for each model_version for issuer dispute documentation.
target_output.system_components: ALB (mTLS termination for /v1/score, TLS for management endpoints), InferenceService ECS tasks (FastAPI + ONNX Runtime sidecar, 4-20 tasks auto-scaled on p99 latency), RetrainingService ECS task (scheduled, single-AZ), Redis 7 ElastiCache (atomic Lua scripts for velocity INCR+EXPIRE), ClickHouse 3-node cluster (MergeTree tables partitioned by month; labeled_authorization_features materialized view updated on chargeback insert), S3 model artifact bucket (content-addressable paths: s3://fraud-models/{model_type}/{sha256_hash}.onnx), S3 chargeback drop zone (processor uploads via STS assumed role), Cognito user pool (data_scientist and admin roles), CloudTrail (all API calls), Datadog Agent sidecar on each ECS task.
target_output.workflows: Authorization Scoring: Processor sends POST /v1/score with authorization_request (merchant_id, card_bin, card_last4, card_hash, amount_cents, mcc, device_fingerprint, ip_hash). InferenceService atomically increments and reads Redis velocity counters for card_hash at 15m, 1h, 24h windows using Lua INCR+EXPIRE pipeline. Fetches merchant_profile risk_tier from ClickHouse (TTL-cached). Constructs feature vector, runs ONNX inference, appends reason_codes from SHAP top-3 features. Persists fraud_score to ClickHouse audit log. Returns response within 50ms p99. Nightly Retraining: EventBridge triggers RetrainingService at 01:00 UTC. Service reads new chargeback S3 drop files, inserts into chargebacks table, and the labeled_authorization_features materialized view auto-updates via ClickHouse materialized view trigger. Challenger XGBoost trained on labeled_authorization_features with stratified 80/20 split. ONNX export uploaded to s3://fraud-models/xgboost/{sha256_hash}.onnx. SHAP values exported to s3://explainability/{sha256_hash}/shap_summary.parquet. Holdout F1 evaluated against champion; if improvement > 0.005, POST /v1/models/promote called with new s3_path and sha256_hash, model_versions updated to champion status.
target_output.data_model: authorization_requests (id UUID PK, merchant_id VARCHAR, card_bin CHAR(6), card_last4 CHAR(4), card_hash VARCHAR(64), amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), mcc CHAR(4), device_fingerprint VARCHAR, ip_hash VARCHAR(64), timestamp DateTime) ENGINE=MergeTree() PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(timestamp) ORDER BY (merchant_id, timestamp). fraud_scores (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, model_version_id UUID FK, score Float32, decision Enum('approve','review','decline'), reason_codes Array(String), latency_ms UInt16, scored_at DateTime) ENGINE=MergeTree(). chargebacks (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, reason_code VARCHAR(4), dispute_amount_cents BIGINT, received_at DateTime, fraud_label UInt8) ENGINE=MergeTree(). merchant_profiles (id UUID PK, merchant_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, risk_tier Enum('low','medium','high'), monthly_volume_cents BIGINT, chargeback_rate_30d Float32, updated_at DateTime). model_versions (id UUID PK, model_type Enum('xgboost','transformer'), s3_path VARCHAR, sha256_hash CHAR(64) NOT NULL, training_run_id UUID, precision_score Float32, recall_score Float32, f1_score Float32, promoted_at DateTime, status Enum('challenger','champion','retired')). labeled_authorization_features (MATERIALIZED VIEW joining authorization_requests to chargebacks on request_id, computing fraud_label, velocity features, and merchant risk_tier for training dataset).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/score — body: {merchant_id, card_bin, card_last4, card_hash, amount_cents, currency, mcc, device_fingerprint, ip_hash}; response: {request_id, fraud_score, decision, reason_codes[], model_version_id, latency_ms}; auth: mTLS + X-Merchant-API-Key header; SLA: 50ms p99. POST /v1/models/promote — body: {model_type, s3_path, sha256_hash, training_run_id, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score}; auth: Cognito JWT (admin role); updates model_versions and triggers ONNX Runtime reload via /internal/model/reload. GET /v1/models/active — returns current champion model_version metadata including sha256_hash and s3_path; auth: Cognito JWT. GET /v1/merchants/{merchant_id}/risk-profile — returns merchant_profile with chargeback_rate_30d and risk_tier; auth: Cognito JWT (data_scientist role). GET /v1/explainability/{model_version_id} — returns pre-signed S3 URL to SHAP summary parquet for issuer dispute documentation.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS Level 1: full PANs never stored; card data reduced to card_bin + card_last4 + card_hash (SHA-256 of PAN + merchant-scoped salt from Secrets Manager); ClickHouse card_hash columns encrypted at column level with AWS KMS. mTLS with ACM-issued certificates for all /v1/score processor traffic; 90-day cert rotation automated via ACM + ALB listener update. Cognito MFA (TOTP) enforced for all internal users. Secrets Manager for DB credentials, Redis auth token, and merchant API keys with 30-day rotation. WAF on ALB blocking anomalous scoring burst patterns. Audit log: every /v1/score call persisted to fraud_scores with request_id enabling full reconstruction of scoring decisions for QSA audits. CloudTrail for all AWS API calls. Network: InferenceService in private subnet; no public IP on ECS tasks; NAT Gateway for outbound only.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline: pytest unit and integration tests, model evaluation gate (challenger F1 vs champion threshold), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling update (min 50% healthy, max 150%). InferenceService deploys to us-east-1a and 1c across two AZs. ALB health checks on /health every 10s. Auto-scaling: target tracking on custom Datadog metric fraud_scoring_p99_latency — scale out when p99 > 40ms, scale in when p99 < 25ms sustained 5 minutes. RetrainingService scheduled by EventBridge cron(0 1 * * ? *); ECS run-task with ephemeral storage 50GB for training data staging. Model artifacts: s3://fraud-models/{model_type}/{sha256_hash}.onnx with versioning enabled; lifecycle policy moves artifacts older than 1 year to Glacier. Rollback: ECS maintains 2 previous task definitions; model rollback by reposting previous champion sha256_hash to /v1/models/promote.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: distributed traces on every POST /v1/score call spanning Redis INCR pipeline, ClickHouse merchant_profile fetch, ONNX inference, and audit log write. Custom Datadog metrics: fraud.score.distribution (histogram by merchant_id and decision), fraud.model.drift_kl_divergence (gauge, computed nightly against baseline score distribution), fraud.velocity.cache_hit_rate (gauge, Redis INCR pipeline success rate), fraud.retraining.job_duration_minutes (gauge). PagerDuty escalation policies: p99 latency > 50ms for 2 minutes (P1), Redis hit rate < 90% for 5 minutes (P2), model drift KL divergence > 0.15 (P2, wakes ML on-call), retraining job failure (P2). Datadog dashboard: live fraud score distribution, false positive rate (chargebacks joined to approve decisions), model version champion status.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $4,200/month for 500M monthly authorizations. InferenceService: 4-20 Fargate tasks (2 vCPU, 4GB) at ~$1,800 variable. ClickHouse: 3x r6i.2xlarge reserved at ~$1,200. Redis ElastiCache: cache.r7g.large at ~$280. S3 and data transfer: ~$400. Datadog APM: ~$520 at 500M traces/month (sampled at 1%). Scaling breakpoints: above 1B monthly authorizations, shard Redis by card_hash prefix and add ClickHouse read replicas for analytics isolation.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (weeks 1-8): XGBoost scoring model trained on 6 months of labeled authorization data, FastAPI InferenceService with ONNX Runtime sidecar, Redis velocity counters with atomic Lua INCR+EXPIRE, content-addressable S3 artifact paths (ISS-001 fix), mTLS processor endpoint, Cognito internal auth, ClickHouse feature store, basic Datadog APM. Phase 2 (weeks 9-14): Nightly retraining pipeline with labeled_authorization_features materialized view (ISS-003 fix), champion/challenger promotion workflow, SHAP explainability export, model drift monitoring, PagerDuty escalation policies, /v1/explainability endpoint for issuer disputes. Phase 3 (weeks 15-20): Transformer sequence encoder fine-tuning, ONNX export of transformer model, A/B challenger evaluation incorporating transformer embeddings, merchant-level model personalization research spike.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy. pytest with 85% branch coverage gate on InferenceService; integration tests run against staging ClickHouse replica and Redis. All velocity counter logic must use the atomic Lua script pattern (INCR + EXPIRE in pipeline) — no GET/SET permitted; enforced via a custom pylint rule in CI. Model promotion requires passing the GitHub Environments manual approval step. S3 artifact path must include sha256_hash in the key (not just as object tag) — enforced by pre-promote validation step. SHAP explainability export is mandatory for every promoted model_version. ADR required for any change to the feature vector schema.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse materialized view refresh lag causes stale features during high chargeback ingestion bursts. Mitigation: monitor labeled_authorization_features freshness lag metric; alert if lag > 2 hours. Risk: ONNX model reload causes brief scoring interruption if InferenceService task restarts mid-reload. Mitigation: blue/green task deployment during model promotion using ALB target group switching. Tradeoff: ONNX Runtime sidecar adds 15MB memory overhead per task but enables model format independence from the serving framework, allowing transformer and XGBoost models to share the same deployment pipeline. Tradeoff: Redis cluster mode disabled to support atomic Lua scripts, limiting horizontal sharding; at above 50K TPS a Redis Cluster with hash-slot-aware Lua scripts must be evaluated.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose ClickHouse over Feast feature store for operational simplicity — Feast adds Kubernetes operator complexity not justified at current scale. ONNX Runtime selected over TorchServe for framework-agnostic serving of both XGBoost and transformer models. Model artifact content-addressing chosen over S3 versioning to enable deterministic rollback by hash reference without relying on S3 version IDs which are opaque strings.

metadata.notes: Round 4 closes ISS-001 (content-addressable S3 artifact paths) and ISS-002 (atomic Redis INCR+EXPIRE Lua scripts) with internal consistency across all plan sections.

project_goal.value: Automate reconciliation of Stripe, Adyen, and Braintree daily settlement files against the internal ledger, flagging mismatches for finance team review and replacing a manual month-end spreadsheet process
project_goal.rationale: Core business need: eliminate manual reconciliation that consumes 8+ hours per month-end and introduces human error

target_users.value: Finance team for mismatch review; platform engineering team for configuration and monitoring
target_users.rationale: Finance owns the reconciliation outcome; engineering owns the tool operation

project_class.rationale: Scheduled batch process with no interactive UI; primary value is automated data processing and reporting

capabilities.rationale: API integrations with three payment gateways, batch data processing, PostgreSQL persistence, no frontend required

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-gateway normalization and ledger join logic is moderately complex; no real-time or ML requirements

risk_level.rationale: Financial settlement data handled; no direct card data; internal-only exposure reduces blast radius

data_sensitivity.rationale: Settlement amounts, fee schedules, and merchant payout data are financially sensitive

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs on internal AWS Lambda; Slack and spreadsheet outputs go to internal channels only

access_model.value: Custom JWT for Lambda invocation via EventBridge; Slack webhook URL and spreadsheet API key stored in Secrets Manager; no user-facing auth
access_model.rationale: Scheduled automation does not require user login; secrets management sufficient

feature_scope.value: Daily pull of settlement files from Stripe, Adyen, and Braintree APIs; normalization to canonical schema; ledger join by transaction reference; discrepancy detection and classification; Slack report and spreadsheet export
feature_scope.rationale: Full reconciliation workflow from data acquisition through reporting

mvp_scope.value: Stripe and Adyen integration, ledger join, discrepancy flagging, Slack report — Braintree integration in phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Stripe and Adyen cover 90% of transaction volume; Braintree lower priority for initial delivery

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all gateway API calls; secrets in AWS Secrets Manager; IAM least-privilege for Lambda; settlement data encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL with RDS encryption
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data sensitivity and SOC 2 relevance require encryption and least-privilege access

frontend_stack.value: None — outputs via Slack webhook and Google Sheets API
frontend_stack.rationale: automation_tool class; finance team consumes reports through existing tools, no custom UI needed

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for health and trigger endpoints; core reconciliation logic as pure Python modules
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI provides a lightweight HTTP interface for EventBridge trigger and health checks without full framework overhead

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS for settlement files, ledger entries, reconciliation runs, and discrepancies; no cache layer required
data_platform.rationale: Relational joins between settlement and ledger data suit PostgreSQL; no real-time cache needed for batch process

hosting_target.value: AWS Lambda for reconciliation function; EventBridge cron for scheduling; RDS PostgreSQL in private VPC subnet
hosting_target.rationale: Lambda eliminates server management for a batch job running once daily; EventBridge for reliable scheduling

privacy_retention_policy.value: Settlement records and reconciliation results retained 7 years for financial audit; Lambda execution logs retained 90 days in CloudWatch
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention requirements and SOC 2 audit trail

future_scope.value: Braintree gateway integration; anomaly trend dashboard; automated dispute pre-filling from discrepancy data
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP enhancements after core reconciliation is validated

constraints.value: Must run to completion within Lambda 15-minute timeout for daily volumes; no card PAN data stored; SOC 2 Type II audit trail required
constraints.rationale: Lambda timeout is a hard constraint; SOC 2 relevance requires audit logging

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for Lambda duration, error rate, and timeout alarms; structured JSON logs for each reconciliation run; SNS alert on run failure
observability_baseline.rationale: CloudWatch is the AWS-native monitoring solution; structured logs enable querying run history

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with Stripe integration in week 2, Adyen in week 4, full reporting in week 6
execution_preference.rationale: Incremental delivery allows finance team to validate each gateway before proceeding

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II relevant: audit logging of all reconciliation runs, data access controls, and 7-year financial record retention; no PCI-DSS scope as settlement files contain net amounts, not card data
compliance_context.rationale: Tool handles financial settlement data subject to SOC 2 controls; PCI scope excluded because files contain only transaction references and amounts

project.goal: Automated daily reconciliation of Stripe, Adyen, and Braintree settlement files against internal ledger
project.target_users: Finance team for mismatch review; engineering for ops
project.timeline: MVP in 6 weeks (Stripe + Adyen); Braintree in week 10

backend.framework: FastAPI for health and trigger endpoints; reconciliation logic as Python modules
backend.api: Internal trigger endpoint and health check only

security.auth: Secrets Manager for gateway API keys and Slack webhook; IAM role for Lambda
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 for gateway API calls; RDS encryption at rest

open_questions.braintree_api_auth: Confirm Braintree settlement API auth method (OAuth vs API key)

confirmed_decisions.gateway_priority: Stripe first, then Adyen, then Braintree

reasoner_reviews.product: The phased gateway rollout (Stripe first) is the right approach since Stripe settlement API is most straightforward to integrate and will validate the canonical schema before Adyen's more complex settlement report format. Ensure the discrepancy threshold is configurable per gateway since Adyen FX fees can introduce small rounding differences.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Lambda with EventBridge scheduling is appropriate for a once-daily batch job. Key risk is the 15-minute Lambda timeout at high transaction volumes — benchmark the ledger join query on PostgreSQL before committing. Consider using RDS Data API to avoid Lambda VPC cold start latency for the DB connection if cold starts are frequent.
reasoner_reviews.security: Stripe, Adyen, and Braintree API keys must be stored in separate Secrets Manager secrets, not concatenated. IAM role for Lambda should have least-privilege: only Secrets Manager GetSecretValue for the specific secret ARNs, RDS Data API access, and CloudWatch PutMetricData. Rotate gateway API keys on 90-day schedule.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Lambda 15-minute timeout is the binding constraint. At 500K daily transactions across three gateways, the PostgreSQL join is the bottleneck. Add a GIN index on ledger_entries.transaction_reference and partition settlement_files by gateway_name and settlement_date to avoid full table scans.

backend.service_design: Single Lambda function (reconciliation_runner) invoked by EventBridge cron at 06:00 UTC daily. Structured as three phases: (1) GatewayIngestion — pulls settlement files from each active gateway API, (2) Normalization — maps gateway-specific schemas to canonical SettlementRecord, (3) LedgerJoin — joins canonical records to ledger_entries by transaction_reference and classifies discrepancies.
backend.api_patterns: Minimal FastAPI app with GET /health (Lambda healthcheck) and POST /trigger (manual reconciliation trigger for engineering, protected by custom JWT). No public-facing API.
backend.background_jobs: Daily EventBridge cron(0 6 * * ? *) triggers Lambda. Manual trigger available via POST /trigger. Failed runs log to reconciliation_runs with status=failed and SNS notification fires to engineering on-call.
backend.failure_handling: Each gateway ingestion wrapped in try/except; a failed gateway skips to the next and records gateway_error in reconciliation_runs. If all gateways fail, Lambda exits with error code and CloudWatch alarm triggers SNS notification. Partial runs stored as partial_complete status with completed_gateways list.

security.auth_design: Lambda IAM execution role with Secrets Manager GetSecretValue for stripe_api_key, adyen_api_key, braintree_api_key, slack_webhook_url, sheets_api_key secrets. No user-facing authentication.
security.secrets_management: Each gateway API key in its own Secrets Manager secret. 90-day rotation reminder tracked in engineering runbook. Lambda environment variables reference secret ARNs, not values.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every reconciliation_run persisted to PostgreSQL with: run_id, triggered_at, completed_at, gateways_processed[], total_records_processed, total_discrepancies, status. CloudTrail for Lambda invocations. Logs retained 90 days in CloudWatch; reconciliation_runs retained 7 years.

data.entities: settlement_files (id UUID, gateway_name ENUM stripe/adyen/braintree, settlement_date DATE, raw_json JSONB, fetched_at TIMESTAMP). ledger_entries (id UUID, transaction_reference VARCHAR UNIQUE, expected_amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), gateway_name, settled_at DATE, created_at). reconciliation_runs (id UUID, triggered_at, completed_at, gateways_processed TEXT[], total_records INT, total_discrepancies INT, status ENUM pending/complete/partial_complete/failed). discrepancies (id UUID, run_id UUID FK, transaction_reference VARCHAR, gateway_name, expected_amount_cents BIGINT, actual_amount_cents BIGINT, delta_cents BIGINT, discrepancy_type ENUM amount_mismatch/fee_mismatch/missing_in_ledger/missing_in_settlement, severity ENUM low/medium/high, resolved BOOL DEFAULT false, created_at).
data.storage_design: RDS PostgreSQL 16 in private VPC subnet with encryption at rest. GIN index on ledger_entries.transaction_reference for fast join. Composite index on discrepancies(run_id, resolved) for report generation. settlement_files partitioned by settlement_date for pruning.
data.retention_and_deletion: Automated RDS snapshot retention 7 years. settlement_files raw_json pruned to summary after 90 days to manage storage. discrepancies retained 7 years for SOC 2 audit.

devops.deployment_topology: Lambda function in VPC private subnet accessing RDS via VPC endpoint. EventBridge rule in same AWS account. Secrets Manager endpoint via VPC endpoint (no NAT Gateway needed). Lambda package built as ZIP via GitHub Actions and deployed via AWS CLI.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with 80% coverage gate, Lambda package build, integration tests against staging RDS (seeded with synthetic settlement data), deploy to staging then production with manual approval gate between environments.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch Logs with structured JSON (run_id, gateway, phase, record_count, duration_ms per phase). CloudWatch metric filters extracting discrepancy counts. CloudWatch Alarms: Lambda error rate > 0, Lambda duration > 800s (leaves buffer before timeout). SNS topic connected to engineering Slack channel for alerts.
devops.rollback_strategy: Lambda version aliases: production alias points to specific Lambda version. Rollback by updating alias to previous version. RDS schema migrations via Alembic with down migrations tested in CI.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a Lambda-based payment reconciliation automation for three gateways against an internal ledger. Key constraints are the 15-minute Lambda timeout requiring query optimization (GIN indexes, date-partitioned tables), SOC 2 audit trail in reconciliation_runs, and the canonical schema design that absorbs Stripe, Adyen, and Braintree format differences. The tool is deliberately thin — no frontend, no cache, just scheduled ingestion-normalize-join-report pipeline with financial data retained 7 years.
target_output.title: Multi-Gateway Payment Reconciliation Automation
target_output.executive_summary: A daily Lambda-based automation that pulls settlement files from Stripe, Adyen, and Braintree, normalizes them to a canonical schema, joins against the internal ledger by transaction reference, and posts structured discrepancy reports to Slack and Google Sheets. Replaces a manual 8-hour month-end process with a scheduled 06:00 UTC run completing in under 10 minutes for up to 500K daily transactions. All reconciliation runs and discrepancies are persisted to RDS PostgreSQL with 7-year retention for SOC 2 audit.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-Lambda architecture triggered by EventBridge cron at 06:00 UTC daily.
