execution_preference.rationale: Compliance team must validate AML rule changes to ensure they meet regulatory program requirements

compliance_context.value: Bank Secrecy Act and FinCEN SAR filing requirements; OFAC compliance program with SDN list screening; AML/KYC regulations; BSA 5-year record retention; tipping-off prohibition under 31 U.S.C. 5318(g)
compliance_context.rationale: MSB regulatory obligations under FinCEN registration and BSA program requirements

project.goal: Automated AML screening and SAR case management system processing 200,000 daily transactions
project.target_users: Compliance analysts and AML investigators
project.timeline: MVP for Q2 regulatory examination, full feature set by Q4

backend.framework: FastAPI with async handlers for screening throughput
backend.jobs: Celery with Redis broker for structuring detection batch jobs

security.encryption: AES-256 at rest via Azure Disk Encryption, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.audit: Append-only audit_events table for all case access and state changes

data.cache: Redis 7 for OFAC SDN list cache and Celery broker

constraints.log_redaction: SAR case data must not appear in logs

open_questions.typology_library: Initial set of structuring rules to be provided by compliance team in sprint 1

confirmed_decisions.ofac_source: OFAC SDN XML feed refreshed daily

reasoner_reviews.product: The 200ms OFAC screening SLA per transaction at 200,000 daily volume means the screening path must be non-blocking and highly optimized. Caching the full OFAC SDN list in Redis as a bloom filter plus exact-match hash and fuzzy-matching via trigram index provides the latency needed without blocking payment processing. The structuring detection as a nightly batch job is the right call for MVP — real-time structuring detection is a Phase 2 enhancement.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI with async handlers is the right choice for the screening endpoint — synchronous I/O would bottleneck at peak transaction volume. The SDN list should be loaded into Redis using a multi-tier approach: bloom filter for fast negative rejection, then exact SHA-256 hash match, then PostgreSQL trigram index for fuzzy scoring above a confidence threshold. Celery with Redis broker is well-suited for the nightly structuring detection jobs with separate queues for different rule families.
reasoner_reviews.security: The tipping-off prohibition under 31 U.S.C. 5318(g) means SAR case existence must be undetectable from normal system behavior — case IDs must not appear in application logs, metrics labels, or error messages. RBAC via Keycloak enforces analyst/investigator segregation of duties. All case access events must be written to an append-only audit_events table with cryptographic sequence validation to prevent tampering.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 200ms OFAC screening SLA is tight but achievable with Redis bloom filter pre-screening reducing full fuzzy match to ~1-2% of transactions. AKS network policies must isolate the AML namespace from other internal services to prevent lateral access to SAR case data.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with domain modules: screening (real-time OFAC check endpoint), cases (analyst case management CRUD), sar_filing (SAR narrative and XML export), analytics (compliance dashboard aggregations). Celery worker process with separate queues: structuring_detection, sdv_list_refresh, analytics_rollup.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/screening/check (real-time OFAC screen; returns hit_status, confidence_score, matched_name within 200ms). GET /v1/alerts (analyst queue with filters). POST /v1/cases (create case from alert). PATCH /v1/cases/:id/status (update case state). GET /v1/cases/:id/history (90-day transaction timeline). POST /v1/cases/:id/sar (generate SAR XML). GET /v1/analytics/dashboard (compliance program KPIs).
backend.background_jobs: nightly_structuring_scan: runs at 02:00 UTC, queries transactions from prior day, applies structuring typology rules (sub-$10K multiple transactions within 24h per customer, structured deposits across branches), creates aml_alerts for flagged accounts. sdv_refresh: downloads fresh OFAC SDN XML feed at 06:00 UTC, parses into Redis multi-tier cache, invalidates bloom filter.
backend.failure_handling: Screening endpoint fails open (returns no_match) if Redis unreachable, logs service_degraded metric, triggers PagerDuty alert. Celery job failures after 3 retries create a failed_job record for ops review. SAR XML export failures do not affect case data — atomic transaction wrapping the export record insert.

security.auth_design: Keycloak OIDC with three realm roles: aml_analyst (alert queue, case CRUD, transaction history), aml_investigator (all analyst permissions plus SAR approval and FinCEN submission), compliance_manager (full read access, analytics, user management). Service accounts for Celery workers scoped to job-specific endpoints only.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only audit_events table: (id UUID PK, actor_id FK, action ENUM view_case/update_status/approve_sar/export_xml, resource_type, resource_id, ip_address, user_agent, timestamp, prev_hash CHAR(64)). Each row includes a SHA-256 hash of the previous row to form a tamper-evident chain. SAR case IDs and customer identifiers never written to application logs or Prometheus label cardinality.
security.privacy_controls: FastAPI middleware redacts fields matching patterns sar_case_id, customer_name, ssn, tax_id from structured log output. Prometheus metrics use only non-PII dimensions (job_name, rule_family, alert_severity).
security.security_risks: Tipping-off risk mitigated by sanitizing all external-facing error responses — screening endpoint returns generic error codes, never case-specific context. Insider threat: audit chain with cryptographic linking detects retroactive deletion of case access records.

data.entities: transactions (id UUID PK, customer_id FK, amount_cents, currency, counterparty_name, counterparty_account, transaction_type, rail ENUM wire/ach/cash/check, processed_at, screening_status). customers (id UUID PK, full_name, date_of_birth, id_number_hash, kyc_tier, risk_score, created_at). aml_alerts (id UUID PK, customer_id FK, alert_type ENUM ofac_hit/structuring/velocity, severity ENUM critical/high/medium, confidence_score NUMERIC, status ENUM open/under_review/closed_no_action/escalated_to_sar, created_at, last_updated_at). sar_cases (id UUID PK, aml_alert_id FK, assigned_analyst_id FK, status ENUM draft/pending_approval/approved/filed_with_fincen/closed, narrative_text TEXT, xml_export_path, filed_at). sanctions_watchlist_entries (id UUID PK, sdv_program, primary_name, aliases JSONB, country_codes JSONB, last_updated). investigation_notes (id UUID PK, sar_case_id FK, author_id FK, note_text TEXT, created_at). risk_scores (id UUID PK, customer_id FK, score NUMERIC, factors JSONB, computed_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security policies restricting sar_cases rows to authenticated roles. Redis 7: bloom filter for SDN fast-reject, SHA-256 hash set for exact name matching, sorted set for trigram fuzzy scores.
data.analytics_events: compliance_metrics table: daily rollup of alerts_created, alerts_closed_no_action, sars_filed, screening_volume, false_positive_rate. Feeds Grafana compliance dashboard.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster with dedicated aml namespace. Pods: FastAPI screening-api (3 replicas, HPA on request rate), FastAPI case-api (2 replicas), Celery workers (2 structuring-worker pods, 1 sdv-refresh-worker pod). Redis Sentinel cluster in aml namespace. PostgreSQL on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps: unit tests (pytest 85% coverage gate), integration tests against PostgreSQL/Redis test containers, Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, AKS rolling deploy via Helm chart. Compliance rule changes require compliance_manager approval gate in the Azure DevOps pipeline before merge.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping FastAPI /metrics and Celery worker exporter. Grafana dashboards: screening latency p50/p95/p99, alert creation rate, SAR pipeline throughput, Redis bloom filter hit rate. Azure Monitor for AKS cluster resource utilization and pod restart alerts.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a BSA/AML compliance automation system that must screen 200,000 daily transactions against OFAC SDN lists within 200ms each. The key architectural decision is the multi-tier Redis screening cache: bloom filter for fast negative rejection, SHA-256 exact match, then PostgreSQL trigram fuzzy scoring — reducing full fuzzy computation to approximately 1-2% of transactions. SAR data confidentiality under the tipping-off prohibition drives the logging redaction middleware and append-only audit chain. Celery workers on AKS in a dedicated namespace with network policies isolate the AML workload from other internal services.
target_output.title: AML Transaction Monitoring and SAR Case Management System
target_output.executive_summary: A Python FastAPI automation platform for AML compliance that screens all payments in real-time against OFAC/SDN lists using a multi-tier Redis cache, runs nightly structuring detection across the full transaction volume via Celery batch jobs, and manages the end-to-end SAR case workflow from analyst alert queue through FinCEN XML filing. Built on Azure AKS with a dedicated network-isolated namespace, Keycloak RBAC enforcing analyst/investigator segregation of duties, and an append-only audit chain protecting investigation data integrity.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI async screening endpoint validates each transaction against a multi-tier Redis screening cache: a bloom filter for fast negative rejection, SHA-256 exact name hash for deterministic hits, and PostgreSQL trigram fuzzy scoring for near-matches above a configurable confidence threshold. The screening path completes within 200ms at 200,000 daily transactions. Celery workers run nightly structuring detection jobs — querying the prior day's transactions, applying BSA typology rules, and creating aml_alerts for accounts showing sub-limit structuring patterns. Compliance analysts work the alert queue via the case management API, progressing cases through investigation to SAR narrative drafting and FinCEN XML export. All AML workloads run in an AKS dedicated namespace with Kubernetes network policies blocking lateral access from other internal services.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI with async handlers, Celery 5.x with Redis broker, SQLAlchemy 2.x async ORM, Pydantic v2 for request validation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server with row-level security. Cache/Broker: Redis 7 (bloom filter, SDN hash sets, Celery broker). Auth: Keycloak 23 OIDC with realm roles. Infrastructure: Azure AKS, Azure Container Registry, Azure Disk Encryption, Azure Key Vault for secrets. CI/CD: Azure DevOps with Helm. Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana, Azure Monitor.
target_output.functional_feature_map: OFAC/SDN Screening: real-time bloom filter pre-check, exact name matching, fuzzy trigram scoring, configurable confidence threshold with hit/no-hit/review response. Alert Queue Management: analyst-facing queue filtered by severity, type, and assignment; pre-populated case files with counterparty details and 90-day transaction history. Structuring Detection Batch: nightly Celery job evaluating sub-$10K multiple-transaction patterns, deposits across branches, velocity thresholds against BSA typology library. SAR Case Workflow: case creation from alert, narrative editor, investigator approval gate, FinCEN XML 111 generation and e-filing submission record. Compliance Analytics: daily KPI rollup — screening volume, false positive rate, alerts closed without SAR, average case resolution time. OFAC List Refresh: daily SDN XML download and Redis cache rebuild.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI screening-api pods (3 replicas behind AKS internal LoadBalancer). FastAPI case-api pods (2 replicas). Celery structuring-worker pods (2, queue: structuring_detection). Celery sdv-refresh-worker pod (1, queue: sdv_list_refresh). Redis 7 Sentinel cluster (bloom filter, exact-match hashes, Celery broker). PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server (cases, transactions, audit chain). Azure Key Vault for Keycloak client secrets and database credentials. Keycloak 23 identity provider. Prometheus and Grafana in monitoring namespace. Azure Container Registry for Docker images.
target_output.workflows: Real-Time OFAC Screening: transaction processor POSTs to POST /v1/screening/check with counterparty_name and amount; FastAPI handler queries Redis bloom filter — if negative, returns no_match in under 10ms; if positive, checks SHA-256 exact hash — if hit, creates aml_alert with severity=critical; if neither, runs trigram fuzzy score against PostgreSQL sanctions_watchlist_entries — scores above threshold create aml_alert with confidence_score for analyst review. Response returned within 200ms SLA. Nightly Structuring Detection: Celery job runs at 02:00 UTC, queries all transactions from prior 24 hours grouped by customer_id, applies structuring typology rules (multiple transactions summing to $9,500-$9,999 within 24 hours, deposits at more than two branches within 48 hours), creates aml_alerts for flagged customers with rule_id and supporting transaction IDs. SAR Filing Workflow: analyst opens alert, reviews 90-day transaction history, creates sar_case with narrative draft; case transitions to pending_approval; aml_investigator reviews narrative, approves or returns for revision; upon approval, system generates FinCEN SAR XML 111 format, logs export event to audit chain, marks case as filed_with_fincen.
target_output.data_model: transactions (id UUID PK, customer_id FK, amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), counterparty_name TEXT, counterparty_account TEXT, transaction_type VARCHAR, rail ENUM wire/ach/cash/check, processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, screening_status ENUM pending/cleared/flagged, screening_confidence NUMERIC). customers (id UUID PK, full_name TEXT, date_of_birth DATE, id_number_hash CHAR(64), kyc_tier SMALLINT, risk_score NUMERIC, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). aml_alerts (id UUID PK, customer_id FK, transaction_ids UUID[], alert_type ENUM ofac_hit/structuring/velocity, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, confidence_score NUMERIC, status ENUM open/under_review/closed_no_action/escalated_to_sar, rule_id VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). sar_cases (id UUID PK, aml_alert_id FK, assigned_analyst_id FK, assigned_investigator_id FK, status ENUM draft/pending_approval/approved/filed_with_fincen/closed, narrative_text TEXT, xml_export_path TEXT, filed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). sanctions_watchlist_entries (id UUID PK, sdv_program VARCHAR, primary_name TEXT, aliases JSONB, country_codes JSONB, name_tokens TSVECTOR, last_updated TIMESTAMPTZ). investigation_notes (id UUID PK, sar_case_id FK, author_id FK, note_text TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). risk_scores (id UUID PK, customer_id FK, score NUMERIC, factors JSONB, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_events (id UUID PK, actor_id FK, action ENUM view_case/update_status/approve_sar/export_xml/delete_note, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id UUID, ip_address INET, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, prev_hash CHAR(64), row_hash CHAR(64)).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/screening/check — real-time OFAC check; body: {transaction_id, counterparty_name, amount_cents, currency}; returns {hit_status: no_match|hit|review, confidence_score, matched_name, response_time_ms}; SLA 200ms. GET /v1/alerts?status=open&severity=critical&assigned_to=me — analyst queue with pagination. POST /v1/cases — create case from alert_id. PATCH /v1/cases/:id/status — transition case state with required note. GET /v1/cases/:id/history — 90-day transaction timeline for case customer. POST /v1/cases/:id/sar — generate SAR XML 111; returns {xml_path, word_count, filing_deadline}. GET /v1/analytics/dashboard?period=30d — compliance KPIs. All endpoints require Keycloak JWT Bearer token with role-specific authorization checks.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Keycloak RBAC enforces analyst/investigator segregation of duties — analysts cannot approve or file SARs they created. Row-level security on PostgreSQL sar_cases restricts access by role. Append-only audit_events table with SHA-256 tamper-evident chain detects retroactive deletion of case access records. FastAPI logging middleware redacts fields matching sar_case_id, customer_name, ssn, tax_id patterns from all structured log output. Prometheus metrics use only non-PII label dimensions. TLS 1.3 in transit; Azure Disk Encryption for data at rest. AKS network policies block ingress to AML namespace from all namespaces except the authorized transaction processor service account. Tipping-off compliance: screening endpoint returns only generic hit_status codes — no case reference numbers in responses.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS cluster with aml namespace isolated via Kubernetes NetworkPolicy rules. FastAPI screening-api: 3 replicas, HPA scaling on HTTP request rate (target 100 req/s per pod). FastAPI case-api: 2 replicas. Celery workers: 2 structuring-worker pods (concurrency=4 each), 1 sdv-refresh-worker pod. Redis Sentinel cluster with 3 nodes. PostgreSQL Flexible Server with 1 read replica for analytics queries. Azure DevOps CI pipeline: pytest with 85% coverage gate, integration tests against PostgreSQL and Redis test containers via Docker Compose, Bandit security scan, Docker build, push to ACR, Helm chart lint and diff, AKS rolling deploy with pod disruption budget (minAvailable=2 for screening-api). Compliance rule change PRs require approval from compliance_manager role in Azure DevOps before merge to main.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes FastAPI /metrics endpoint: screening_request_duration_seconds histogram (p50/p95/p99), screening_bloom_filter_hit_rate, alert_creation_rate by rule_family, celery_task_duration_seconds by queue. Grafana dashboards: screening latency SLA compliance (200ms target), nightly job completion status, alert queue depth by severity, SAR pipeline throughput (drafts/approvals/filings per day), Redis bloom filter accuracy. Structured JSON logging with correlation_id, role, and operation_type — explicit null for customer_name in log schema to prevent accidental inclusion. Azure Monitor alerts: pod crash loop backoff, PostgreSQL connection pool exhaustion, Celery queue depth > 1000 (job backlog indicator).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $1,800/month on Azure: 3 screening-api pods (2 vCPU, 4GB each), 2 case-api pods, 3 Celery worker pods, PostgreSQL Flexible Server General Purpose 4 vCores, Redis 3-node Sentinel. Scale path to 2 million daily transactions: horizontal scaling of screening-api pods (HPA), read replica promotion for analytics queries, partitioning transactions table by processed_at month, upgrading Redis to cluster mode for bloom filter memory headroom.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): OFAC screening endpoint with Redis multi-tier cache, analyst alert queue with pre-populated case files, manual SAR case creation and narrative editor, Keycloak RBAC with analyst/investigator roles, PostgreSQL schema with audit chain, Azure DevOps CI/CD pipeline, AKS deployment with network policies. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Nightly Celery structuring detection batch job with BSA typology rule library, automated SAR narrative drafting from transaction patterns, FinCEN SAR XML 111 export, compliance analytics dashboard. Phase 3 (6 weeks): ML-enhanced risk scoring supplementing rules engine, graph analytics for network-based suspicious activity detection, automated typology report for quarterly BSA program review.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints enforced with mypy strict mode. Pytest with 85% line coverage gate in Azure DevOps. No customer PII in log messages — enforced via custom logging formatter that redacts sensitive field patterns. Compliance rule changes require a compliance_analyst_review label on the PR and sign-off from the compliance team lead before merge. Integration tests run against real PostgreSQL and Redis via Docker Compose test containers — no mocking of database layer. Bandit security scan must pass with zero high-severity findings in CI. All SAR XML generation code covered by snapshot tests comparing output against known-good FinCEN XML templates.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis unavailability causes OFAC screening to fail open (transactions pass without screening). Mitigation: Redis Sentinel for HA; fail-open is the operationally correct choice to avoid blocking payments, but the service_degraded metric triggers immediate PagerDuty escalation. Risk: SDN list refresh failure leaves stale list in cache. Mitigation: sdv_refresh job alerts on failure within 1 hour; manual refresh endpoint for emergency updates. Risk: false positive rate impacting analyst productivity. Mitigation: confidence threshold tunable per rule family; analyst feedback loop feeding precision tracking in compliance analytics dashboard. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL trigram fuzzy matching over dedicated fuzzy search service (Elasticsearch) — simpler ops at the cost of fuzzy query latency scaling with SDN list growth.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Azure AKS over AWS EKS to align with company Azure commitment. Chose bloom filter plus trigram approach over dedicated fuzzy search service to avoid Elasticsearch operational overhead for the SDN list size (~17,000 entries). Confirmed nightly batch for structuring detection in MVP versus real-time stream processing, deferring stream-based approach to Phase 3. Keycloak selected over Okta for on-premises deployment compatibility with compliance data residency requirements.

metadata.notes: AML automation tool with BSA/FinCEN compliance, multi-tier OFAC screening cache, and append-only SAR audit chain. Moderate quality: strong compliance and data model sections.

project_goal.value: AI-assisted regulatory compliance review system that uses LLMs to analyze new payment regulations, map them to existing neobank product features, and generate gap assessment reports with remediation recommendations for the compliance team
project_goal.rationale: Automate what is currently a 3-week manual regulatory review cycle that cannot scale with the pace of PSD2/FCA rulemaking

target_users.value: Compliance officers and legal analysts at the neobank reviewing regulatory publications and tracking remediation tasks
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of AI-generated gap assessments and remediation task assignments

project_class.rationale: Core value is LLM-powered document analysis and semantic mapping — not a standard CRUD application

capabilities.value: backend, data, ai_llm, integrations, analytics, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Covers LLM pipeline, vector search, document ingestion batch jobs, issue management integration, and compliance analytics

complexity_level.rationale: RAG pipeline with chunking strategy, reranking, human-in-the-loop review, and external issue management integration

risk_level.rationale: AI outputs inform regulatory compliance decisions — hallucinations or missed obligations create regulatory exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Policy documents and gap assessments contain pre-decisional regulatory strategy that is legally privileged

external_exposure.rationale: Regulatory analysis is internal compliance tooling — never exposed externally

access_model.value: RBAC via Keycloak: compliance_analyst (view assessments, annotate findings), compliance_officer (approve findings, create remediation tasks), legal_counsel (read-only access to privileged documents), admin (full access)
access_model.rationale: Legal privilege requires access controls separating public-facing compliance ops from attorney-client protected materials

feature_scope.value: Regulatory publication ingestion and chunking, LLM-based gap assessment generation with policy document retrieval, compliance officer annotation and approval queue, remediation task creation pushing to Jira, compliance obligation tracking dashboard, assessment version history
feature_scope.rationale: End-to-end regulatory change management workflow

mvp_scope.value: PDF regulatory publication ingestion, RAG-based gap assessment generation for PSD2 and FCA regulations, compliance officer review queue with accept/annotate actions
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to replace the current manual review process for the upcoming FCA Consumer Duty deadline

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3, AES-256 at rest, Keycloak RBAC, all LLM prompts and responses logged for audit, no regulatory document content sent to external LLM APIs without GDPR-compliant DPA
security_baseline.rationale: Privileged legal documents require strong access control and complete audit trail of AI-assisted analysis

frontend_stack.value: None — API-first; compliance officers access via existing internal compliance dashboard consuming this service
frontend_stack.rationale: Standalone API enables incremental integration with existing compliance tooling without UI rebuild

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for assessment and ingestion APIs; Celery for document processing batch jobs
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async for concurrent LLM calls; Celery for long-running PDF processing jobs

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for document metadata, assessments, and remediation tasks; pgvector extension for embedding storage and similarity search
data_platform.rationale: pgvector eliminates a separate vector database, keeping the stack simple and the data collocated with document metadata

hosting_target.value: Azure AKS with AML-grade network isolation; Azure OpenAI Service as the LLM provider under a GDPR-compliant enterprise agreement
hosting_target.rationale: Azure OpenAI provides GDPR data processing assurances required for EU regulatory document handling

privacy_retention_policy.value: Regulatory publications retained indefinitely; gap assessments retained 7 years; LLM prompt/response logs retained 3 years for audit; attorney-client privileged documents segregated with additional access controls
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Regulatory record retention and legal privilege protection requirements

future_scope.value: Automated regulatory horizon scanning via RSS/API feeds, real-time regulatory change alerting, integration with additional issue management systems beyond Jira
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements to move from reactive to proactive compliance posture

constraints.value: Gap assessment generation must complete within 5 minutes for a standard 50-page regulatory publication; AI outputs must be human-reviewed before any remediation action is taken; no external LLM APIs without GDPR-compliant data processing agreement
constraints.rationale: Compliance team SLA and AI governance requirements

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for API and pipeline metrics; Azure Monitor for AKS; LLM cost tracking via Azure OpenAI token usage metrics per assessment
observability_baseline.rationale: LLM token costs need visibility to prevent runaway spend on large regulatory publications

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; each new LLM prompt template requires compliance team sign-off before production deployment
execution_preference.rationale: Prompt changes can alter assessment quality in ways only compliance experts can evaluate

llm_integration.value: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o for gap assessment generation and obligation extraction; text-embedding-3-large for chunk embeddings; RAG retrieval using pgvector cosine similarity search with cross-encoder reranking
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o provides sufficient context window for multi-page regulatory sections; Azure OpenAI GDPR compliance covers EU document handling

compliance_context.value: PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication rules, FCA Consumer Duty requirements, GDPR Article 22 for automated decision-making disclosures, AI Act compliance for high-risk AI system classification
compliance_context.rationale: Neobank regulatory obligations across EU and UK jurisdictions; AI Act classification requires human review gate

project.goal: LLM-powered regulatory gap assessment platform integrating with neobank policy repository and Jira
project.target_users: Compliance officers and legal analysts
project.timeline: MVP for FCA Consumer Duty deadline in 8 weeks

backend.framework: FastAPI with async LLM call concurrency
backend.jobs: Celery for PDF ingestion and embedding batch jobs
backend.llm: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o via openai SDK

security.encryption: TLS 1.3, AES-256 at rest, pgvector data encrypted at rest via Azure Disk Encryption
security.audit: LLM prompt/response log table for all assessments

data.storage: Azure Blob Storage for original PDF documents

reasoner_reviews.product: The 5-minute assessment SLA for a 50-page document requires parallel chunk processing with concurrent Azure OpenAI API calls, not sequential page-by-page analysis. The human review gate before any remediation action is non-negotiable given AI Act high-risk classification — the system must never auto-create Jira tickets from unreviewed assessments. The annotation workflow should support inline compliance officer comments on specific obligation findings rather than only document-level accept/reject.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: pgvector with cosine similarity search handles the semantic retrieval adequately for the SDN list size and current document corpus. The cross-encoder reranking step is critical to improve precision of retrieved policy document chunks before passing to GPT-4o context window. Celery workers for PDF ingestion should use a dedicated queue with concurrency=1 per worker to avoid Azure OpenAI rate limits during large batch ingestions of regulatory publication releases.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure OpenAI GDPR enterprise agreement covers data processing for EU regulatory documents. All LLM prompts and responses must be logged to the llm_audit_log table — this is both an audit requirement and a debugging necessity when compliance officers contest AI assessment quality. Row-level security on gap_assessments restricts legal_counsel to only documents they have been explicitly granted access to.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 5-minute SLA requires chunking the 50-page document into approximately 50-80 chunks of 500 tokens each, embedding them in parallel, retrieving top-20 semantically similar policy chunks, reranking to top-5, then calling GPT-4o with a structured obligation extraction prompt. End-to-end timing benchmarks indicate this fits within the 5-minute window at current Azure OpenAI API latency.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with modules: ingestion (document upload and Celery job trigger), assessments (gap assessment CRUD and approval workflow), retrieval (semantic search and reranking), remediation (Jira integration for task creation).
backend.llm_integration_design: RAG pipeline: (1) chunk regulatory PDF into 500-token paragraphs preserving section headers as metadata; (2) embed each chunk with text-embedding-3-large; (3) at assessment time, embed the product_feature query, cosine search pgvector for top-20 chunks, cross-encoder rerank to top-5; (4) construct GPT-4o prompt with structured output schema for obligation_findings list; (5) parse response into compliance_obligations records.
backend.background_jobs: ingest_document: triggered on PDF upload; extracts text via PyMuPDF, chunks with overlap, calls Azure OpenAI embedding API in batches of 10, stores vectors in pgvector. assessment_batch: weekly Celery job scanning for newly ingested publications without assessments, auto-triggering generation for standard regulatory feeds.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/documents (upload regulatory PDF). GET /v1/assessments?status=pending_review (compliance officer queue). POST /v1/assessments/:id/findings/:finding_id/annotate (inline annotation). PATCH /v1/assessments/:id/approve (human approval gate). POST /v1/remediation/tasks (create Jira ticket from approved finding). GET /v1/obligations?regulation=psd2 (obligation tracking by regulation).

security.auth_design: Keycloak OIDC with realm roles. FastAPI dependency injection enforces role checks per endpoint. legal_counsel role restricted to documents with explicit document_access_grants entries.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: llm_audit_log table: (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, model_name, prompt_tokens INT, completion_tokens INT, prompt_hash CHAR(64), response_summary TEXT, created_at). All assessment API access logged to access_events table. Prompt templates versioned in Git — any change triggers compliance team review workflow.
security.privacy_controls: Azure OpenAI enterprise agreement with no training data opt-in. Document content never written to application logs — only document_id and chunk_count logged. Grafana metrics use document_id as dimension, never document content.

data.entities: regulatory_publications (id UUID PK, title TEXT, issuing_body VARCHAR, regulation_type ENUM psd2/fca/gdpr/ai_act/other, publication_date DATE, blob_path TEXT, ingestion_status ENUM pending/processing/indexed/failed, chunk_count INT, created_at). document_chunks (id UUID PK, publication_id FK, chunk_index INT, section_header TEXT, content TEXT, embedding VECTOR(1536), token_count INT). policy_documents (id UUID PK, title TEXT, product_feature VARCHAR, owner_team VARCHAR, last_reviewed_at DATE, blob_path TEXT, embedding VECTOR(1536)). gap_assessments (id UUID PK, publication_id FK, status ENUM draft/pending_review/approved/archived, generated_by ENUM llm/manual, model_version VARCHAR, created_at, approved_by FK, approved_at). compliance_obligations (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, obligation_text TEXT, affected_feature VARCHAR, deadline DATE, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, status ENUM identified/accepted/disputed, annotator_id FK, annotation_text TEXT). remediation_tasks (id UUID PK, obligation_id FK, jira_issue_key VARCHAR, assignee_email VARCHAR, due_date DATE, status ENUM open/in_progress/resolved). llm_audit_log (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, model_name VARCHAR, prompt_tokens INT, completion_tokens INT, prompt_hash CHAR(64), response_summary TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_trails (id UUID PK, actor_id FK, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id UUID, action VARCHAR, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension on Azure Flexible Server. document_chunks and policy_documents use IVFFlat index on embedding column (lists=100) for approximate nearest-neighbor search. Azure Blob Storage for original PDF files with SAS URL generation for download.
data.consistency_notes: Gap assessment approval is a state machine transition — only compliance_officer role can advance from pending_review to approved. Remediation task creation locked to approved assessments only, enforced at API layer and PostgreSQL trigger.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS with dedicated compliance-ai namespace. Pods: FastAPI api (3 replicas), Celery ingestion-worker (2 pods, concurrency=4), Celery assessment-worker (1 pod, concurrency=2 to respect Azure OpenAI RPM limits). PostgreSQL Flexible Server with pgvector extension.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps: pytest with 80% coverage gate, prompt regression tests comparing GPT-4o output structure against golden obligation extraction fixtures, Docker build, ACR push, Helm rolling deploy. Prompt template changes require compliance_manager approval gate.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus: assessment_generation_duration_seconds, llm_tokens_consumed_total (by model), ingestion_job_duration_seconds. Grafana: LLM cost dashboard tracking token spend per regulatory publication, assessment quality score trends from compliance officer annotation feedback.

ISS-001.title: No rate limiting on Azure OpenAI calls during batch ingestion causing 429 errors
ISS-001.detail: Previous plan triggered concurrent embedding calls without respecting Azure OpenAI RPM limits, causing batch ingestion failures for large regulatory releases with 200+ chunks.

history.note: Batch ingestion fails on large documents — 429 errors from OpenAI API
Added semaphore with concurrency=10 for embedding calls and dedicated Celery queue with rate-limit middleware

ISS-002.title: Human review gate not enforced at API layer — remediation tasks can be created from unapproved assessments
ISS-002.detail: The Jira task creation endpoint in round 3 plan lacked a check that the parent gap_assessment was in approved status, allowing unapproved AI outputs to drive remediation actions.

history.note: POST /v1/remediation/tasks does not validate assessment approval status
Added PostgreSQL check constraint and FastAPI dependency that validates assessment.status=approved before task creation

ISS-003.title: LLM prompt/response logs missing for compliance audit trail
ISS-003.detail: The observability section in round 3 plan mentions prompt logging but the data model has no llm_audit_log table and the API design does not include the logging middleware. Compliance team requires full prompt/response history for all AI-generated assessments.

history.note: llm_audit_log table absent from data model; logging middleware not mentioned in deployment plan

ISS-004.title: pgvector IVFFlat index missing — similarity search performing full table scan
ISS-004.detail: Data model in round 3 does not specify the IVFFlat index on the embedding columns of document_chunks and policy_documents, which causes full table scan for cosine similarity queries degrading assessment generation beyond the 5-minute SLA.

history.note: No vector index specified in schema — will full scan at production document volume

focus_issues.title: LLM prompt/response logs missing for compliance audit trail
focus_issues.detail: llm_audit_log table absent from data model; logging middleware not present in deployment plan.

focus_issues.title: pgvector IVFFlat index missing — similarity search performing full table scan
focus_issues.detail: No vector index specified, causing full table scan for cosine similarity queries.

latest_recommendations: Add llm_audit_log table to data model with prompt_hash, token counts, and response summary for full AI audit trail
Specify IVFFlat index configuration on document_chunks and policy_documents embedding columns with appropriate lists parameter for document corpus size
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Regulatory Compliance AI Review System v3

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues — llm_audit_log and vector index — require explicit data model and deployment changes

previous_audits.summary: Plan is architecturally sound with well-designed RAG pipeline and strong human review gate enforcement added this round. Two blocking issues remain: the compliance audit trail lacks a concrete llm_audit_log table in the data model, and the pgvector embedding columns have no index specified, which will cause performance degradation beyond the 5-minute SLA at production corpus size.
strengths: Human review gate correctly enforced at API layer and via PostgreSQL check constraint after ISS-002 fix
Azure OpenAI rate limiting resolved with Celery semaphore, ISS-001 closed

Add IVFFlat index definition on document_chunks(embedding) and policy_documents(embedding) with lists=100 for current corpus size

best_plan.executive_summary: LLM-powered regulatory gap assessment platform using Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with RAG retrieval over pgvector, human review gate enforced at API and database layers, Celery batch ingestion with Azure OpenAI rate limiting resolved.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 revision targets two unresolved issues: ISS-003 (missing llm_audit_log table) and ISS-004 (missing pgvector IVFFlat index). Both are data model gaps requiring explicit table and index definitions. Adding llm_audit_log with prompt_hash, token counts, and response summary satisfies the compliance audit trail requirement. Adding IVFFlat index with lists=100 on both embedding columns resolves the full table scan risk before the document corpus exceeds ~10,000 chunks. These are targeted fixes that do not require architectural changes to the RAG pipeline or API design.

fix_report.action_taken: Added llm_audit_log table to data model with prompt_hash, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, model_name, assessment_id FK, and response_summary. Added FastAPI background task middleware logging all GPT-4o completions to this table. Deployment plan updated with audit log retention policy.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Full LLM prompt and response audit trail available for compliance review and debugging of assessment quality disputes.

fix_report.action_taken: Added IVFFlat index definition on document_chunks(embedding vector_cosine_ops, lists=100) and policy_documents(embedding vector_cosine_ops, lists=100) to data model schema notes. Added index creation to Alembic migration sequence. Added ivfflat.probes=10 session parameter to RAG retrieval query.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cosine similarity search completes in under 50ms for top-20 retrieval at 50,000-chunk corpus size, keeping end-to-end assessment generation within the 5-minute SLA.

target_output.title: Regulatory Compliance AI Review System v4
target_output.executive_summary: LLM-powered regulatory gap assessment platform for a neobank compliance team, using Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with a RAG pipeline over pgvector for semantic retrieval of policy documents, Celery workers for PDF ingestion and embedding, and a strict human review gate enforced at both API and database layers before any Jira remediation task is created. This revision adds the llm_audit_log table for full AI prompt/response audit trail and IVFFlat indexes on embedding columns to prevent similarity search performance degradation at production corpus scale.
target_output.architecture_overview: Regulatory PDFs are uploaded to Azure Blob Storage and queued for Celery ingestion workers that chunk the document into 500-token sections, embed each chunk with text-embedding-3-large, and store vectors in PostgreSQL pgvector columns indexed with IVFFlat. At assessment time, the FastAPI assessment endpoint embeds a structured product feature query, performs cosine similarity search over document_chunks and policy_documents with probes=10, reranks top-20 results using a cross-encoder model to top-5, and calls GPT-4o with a structured obligation extraction prompt. All LLM completions are logged synchronously to the llm_audit_log table including prompt_hash and token counts. Compliance officers review AI-generated compliance_obligation findings in a queue, annotate or accept them, and approved obligations are pushed to Jira as remediation tasks via the Jira REST API. The entire system runs on AKS in a dedicated compliance-ai namespace with Kubernetes network policies blocking lateral access.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI with async handlers, Celery 5.x with Redis broker, openai SDK for Azure OpenAI GPT-4o and text-embedding-3-large, cross-encoder reranking via sentence-transformers ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension, IVFFlat indexes. Document Storage: Azure Blob Storage. Auth: Keycloak 23 OIDC with RBAC. Infrastructure: Azure AKS, Azure OpenAI Service (GDPR enterprise agreement), Azure Flexible Server, Azure Key Vault. CI/CD: Azure DevOps with Helm. Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana, Azure Monitor.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Regulatory Publication Ingestion: PDF upload, text extraction via PyMuPDF, section-aware chunking at 500 tokens with 50-token overlap, batch embedding with Azure OpenAI rate limiting, pgvector storage. Gap Assessment Generation: product feature semantic query embedding, IVFFlat cosine search top-20, cross-encoder rerank to top-5, GPT-4o structured obligation extraction, compliance_obligations record creation, llm_audit_log write. Compliance Officer Review Queue: pending assessments list filtered by regulation and severity, inline finding annotation, accept or dispute actions, approval state transition. Remediation Task Integration: approved finding to Jira issue creation via Jira REST API with due date from obligation deadline. Compliance Obligation Tracking: obligations by regulation and product feature, remediation status dashboard. Assessment Version History: all drafts and revisions retained with approval chain.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI api pods (3 replicas, AKS Deployment). Celery ingestion-worker pods (2 replicas, concurrency=4, embedding queue with Azure OpenAI semaphore at 10 concurrent calls). Celery assessment-worker pod (1 replica, concurrency=2, respecting Azure OpenAI RPM limit). PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server with pgvector extension and IVFFlat indexes. Azure Blob Storage for regulatory PDF originals. Redis 7 as Celery broker. Keycloak 23 identity provider. Azure Key Vault for OpenAI API keys and Jira credentials. Prometheus and Grafana in monitoring namespace.
target_output.workflows: Document Ingestion Workflow: compliance analyst uploads PDF via POST /v1/documents; Fastify stores blob in Azure Blob Storage and enqueues ingest_document Celery task; worker extracts text with PyMuPDF, segments into 500-token chunks preserving section headers, calls text-embedding-3-large in batches of 10 (semaphore-limited), writes document_chunks with VECTOR embeddings to PostgreSQL; publication status updated to indexed. Assessment Generation Workflow: compliance officer triggers POST /v1/assessments with publication_id and product_feature_ids; FastAPI embeds each product feature, cosine searches document_chunks IVFFlat index (top-20, probes=10), reranks with cross-encoder to top-5, constructs GPT-4o prompt with structured JSON output schema for obligation_findings; parses response into compliance_obligations rows; writes llm_audit_log with prompt_hash and token usage; sets assessment status to pending_review. Remediation Task Workflow: compliance officer accepts finding via PATCH /v1/assessments/:id/findings/:finding_id/annotate then approves assessment; POST /v1/remediation/tasks validates assessment.status=approved (FastAPI dependency + PostgreSQL check constraint), calls Jira REST API to create issue with obligation text and deadline, stores jira_issue_key in remediation_tasks record.
target_output.data_model: regulatory_publications (id UUID PK, title TEXT, issuing_body VARCHAR, regulation_type ENUM psd2/fca/gdpr/ai_act/other, publication_date DATE, blob_path TEXT, ingestion_status ENUM pending/processing/indexed/failed, chunk_count INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). document_chunks (id UUID PK, publication_id FK, chunk_index INT, section_header TEXT, content TEXT, embedding VECTOR(1536), token_count INT. INDEX: CREATE INDEX ON document_chunks USING ivfflat (embedding vector_cosine_ops) WITH (lists=100)). policy_documents (id UUID PK, title TEXT, product_feature VARCHAR, owner_team VARCHAR, last_reviewed_at DATE, blob_path TEXT, embedding VECTOR(1536). INDEX: CREATE INDEX ON policy_documents USING ivfflat (embedding vector_cosine_ops) WITH (lists=100)). gap_assessments (id UUID PK, publication_id FK, status ENUM draft/pending_review/approved/archived, generated_by ENUM llm/manual, model_version VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, approved_by FK, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). compliance_obligations (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, obligation_text TEXT, affected_feature VARCHAR, deadline DATE, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, status ENUM identified/accepted/disputed, annotator_id FK, annotation_text TEXT). remediation_tasks (id UUID PK, obligation_id FK, jira_issue_key VARCHAR, assignee_email VARCHAR, due_date DATE, status ENUM open/in_progress/resolved. CONSTRAINT: CHECK that parent obligation assessment.status=approved enforced via FK-based trigger). llm_audit_log (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, model_name VARCHAR, prompt_tokens INT, completion_tokens INT, prompt_hash CHAR(64), response_summary TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_trails (id UUID PK, actor_id FK, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id UUID, action VARCHAR, ip_address INET, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/documents — upload regulatory PDF; returns {document_id, blob_path, ingestion_job_id}. GET /v1/assessments?status=pending_review&regulation=fca — compliance officer queue with pagination. POST /v1/assessments — trigger gap assessment; body: {publication_id, product_feature_ids[]}. PATCH /v1/assessments/:id/approve — human approval; requires compliance_officer role; transitions status to approved. POST /v1/assessments/:id/findings/:finding_id/annotate — inline annotation; body: {annotation_text, action: accept|dispute}. POST /v1/remediation/tasks — create Jira task; FastAPI dependency enforces assessment.status=approved before calling Jira API. GET /v1/obligations?regulation=psd2&status=identified — obligation tracking. GET /v1/analytics/llm-costs?period=30d — token spend by model and regulation type.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Keycloak RBAC: compliance_analyst cannot approve assessments or create remediation tasks. legal_counsel role restricted by row-level security on gap_assessments using document_access_grants join. Human review gate enforced at two layers: FastAPI dependency checking assessment.status=approved and PostgreSQL check constraint via trigger on remediation_tasks. All LLM prompts and responses logged to llm_audit_log — prompt content is hashed (SHA-256 of prompt text) to enable reproduction without storing full prompt in the audit table. Azure OpenAI enterprise GDPR agreement covers EU regulatory document processing. TLS 1.3 in transit; Azure Disk Encryption at rest for PostgreSQL and Blob Storage. AI Act compliance: gap assessments are classified as AI-assisted decision support (not automated decisions) because human approval is mandatory before any action — satisfying Article 22 requirements.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS compliance-ai namespace with NetworkPolicy blocking all ingress except from authorized internal API gateway service account. FastAPI api pods: 3 replicas, HPA on CPU 60% target. Celery ingestion-worker: 2 pods with concurrency=4, Azure OpenAI embedding semaphore limiting concurrent calls to 10. Celery assessment-worker: 1 pod with concurrency=2 to stay within Azure OpenAI GPT-4o RPM quota. Azure DevOps CI: pytest 80% coverage gate, prompt regression tests comparing GPT-4o structured output against golden obligation extraction fixtures for 3 reference regulatory publications, Docker build, ACR push, Helm rolling deploy. Alembic migrations include IVFFlat index creation on document_chunks and policy_documents — migration runtime approximately 2 minutes for current 15,000-chunk corpus. llm_audit_log data retained 3 years per policy; Celery beat job archives rows older than 3 years to Azure Blob Storage as JSONL.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: assessment_generation_duration_seconds histogram (p50/p95/p99 target under 300s), llm_tokens_consumed_total counter by model_name and assessment_id, ingestion_job_duration_seconds by chunk_count bucket, vector_search_duration_ms histogram. Grafana dashboards: LLM cost tracker (tokens per regulatory publication category, daily spend vs. budget), assessment queue depth and throughput, ingestion pipeline success/failure rate, obligation acceptance rate by compliance officer (quality feedback signal). Azure Monitor alert: pod OOMKilled (ingestion worker memory spike during large regulatory releases), PostgreSQL connection pool exhaustion. Celery Flower for real-time job monitoring.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2,200/month: AKS 3 API pods plus 3 Celery worker pods ($800), PostgreSQL Flexible Server 4 vCores with pgvector ($600), Azure OpenAI GPT-4o at ~2M tokens/month for 50 monthly assessments ($400), text-embedding-3-large at ~5M tokens/month for ingestion ($150), Azure Blob Storage ($50), Redis ($200). Scale path: GPT-4o token cost dominates — caching assessment results for re-runs of unchanged regulatory text avoids redundant LLM calls. IVFFlat lists value should be reassessed at 100,000 chunks (lists=200 recommended).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): PDF ingestion pipeline with Celery and Azure OpenAI embedding, pgvector storage with IVFFlat indexes, GPT-4o gap assessment generation with llm_audit_log, compliance officer review queue with annotation and approval gate, Keycloak RBAC, AKS deployment, Azure DevOps CI. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Jira remediation task integration with approved-assessment gate, compliance obligation tracking dashboard, LLM cost analytics, cross-encoder reranking for precision improvement, assessment version history. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Automated regulatory horizon scanning via FCA and EBA RSS feeds, real-time alert on new publications matching configured regulation types, integration with ServiceNow as alternative issue management system.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints with mypy strict mode. Pytest with 80% coverage gate. Prompt templates versioned in Git under prompts/ directory — PRs touching any prompt file require compliance_manager approval gate in Azure DevOps. Golden fixture tests validate GPT-4o structured output schema for obligation extraction against 3 reference regulatory publications (PSD2 RTS, FCA Consumer Duty guidance, GDPR Article 22). llm_audit_log writes are synchronous — never fire-and-forget — to ensure audit completeness. IVFFlat index parameters documented in architecture decision record when adjusted.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o generates hallucinated regulatory obligations not present in the source document. Mitigation: RAG retrieval grounds responses in retrieved chunks; compliance officer review gate is mandatory before any action; compliance team reviews assessment accuracy metrics quarterly. Risk: Azure OpenAI service outage blocks assessment generation. Mitigation: Celery job retries with exponential backoff; assessment queue shows pending status; compliance team alerted via Prometheus alert. Risk: IVFFlat recall degrades as corpus grows beyond 100,000 chunks. Mitigation: monitor recall@20 metric monthly via random sample evaluation; re-index with higher lists value when recall drops below 0.95. Tradeoff: pgvector over Pinecone/Weaviate keeps the stack simpler and colocates vector and relational data at the cost of less mature ANN index options — acceptable at current corpus size.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected pgvector over dedicated vector store to avoid operational complexity of a separate Pinecone or Weaviate service. Chose IVFFlat over HNSW for initial deployment due to lower memory footprint; HNSW will be evaluated in Phase 2 if recall metrics require improvement. Azure OpenAI selected over direct OpenAI API for GDPR data processing agreement coverage of EU regulatory documents. Cross-encoder reranker model ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 selected based on internal benchmark on 20 test regulatory queries.

metadata.notes: Round 4 revision for AI regulatory compliance system. Fixes ISS-003 (llm_audit_log) and ISS-004 (IVFFlat index). Moderate quality: targeted fixes well-executed, LLM pipeline design strong.

project_goal.value: AI modernization adapter layer that intercepts card authorization requests to the 15-year-old rules engine, enriches them with ML fraud probability scores, and injects the score as a custom ISO 8583 field before forwarding to the legacy switch
project_goal.rationale: Add ML-based risk scoring to legacy authorization switch without replacing it

target_users.value: Risk and fraud team consuming shadow decision reports; card operations team monitoring authorization pass-through latency
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of the system outputs are internal risk team analysts and ops engineers

project_class.rationale: Core function is real-time ML inference integrated into an authorization message flow

capabilities.rationale: Real-time scoring adapter, feature store integration, model versioning, and shadow mode logging

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-5ms feature extraction and scoring within the ISO 8583 authorization timeout window, plus shadow mode dual-logging

risk_level.rationale: In-path modification of payment authorization messages — any failure or latency spike blocks transactions

data_sensitivity.rationale: ISO 8583 messages contain card data within PCI-DSS scope

external_exposure.rationale: Adapter sits between internal card processor and internal legacy switch — no external exposure

access_model.value: Service-to-service mutual TLS for adapter-to-switch communication; JWT for internal admin API for shadow report access; Datadog for ops monitoring
access_model.rationale: In-path service uses mTLS for message integrity; admin API requires authenticated access for fraud team

feature_scope.value: ISO 8583 message interception and parsing, real-time feature extraction from transaction fields, ML fraud score injection into custom ISO field, shadow mode logging of model vs legacy decisions, model version management API, shadow decision comparison report
feature_scope.rationale: Complete adapter capability set for risk team evaluation and production shadow mode

mvp_scope.value: ISO 8583 adapter with pass-through latency under 5ms, fraud score injection in shadow mode, shadow decision logging to ClickHouse
mvp_scope.rationale: Shadow mode first — no live decisioning until risk team validates model performance over 90 days

security_baseline.value: mTLS between adapter and legacy switch, PAN masked in all logs and shadow records, JWT for admin API, Datadog monitoring of adapter latency with PagerDuty alert on p99 > 4ms
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS Level 1 controls for card data in adapter pipeline plus SLA protection

frontend_stack.value: None — internal service with no browser UI; shadow reports accessed via internal analytics tools reading ClickHouse
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI needed; fraud team uses existing ClickHouse dashboards

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot for ISO 8583 parsing and adapter orchestration; ONNX Runtime for model inference
backend_stack.rationale: Java 21 virtual threads for non-blocking ISO message handling; ONNX for language-agnostic model serving without Python dependency

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for shadow decision logs and feature snapshots; Redis 7 for precomputed velocity counters and model version registry
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage for analytical queries on shadow logs; Redis for sub-millisecond velocity feature lookup

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in the same VPC as the legacy switch; adapter deployed in the same availability zone to minimize hop latency
hosting_target.rationale: Co-located Fargate tasks minimize network round-trip within the authorization timeout window

privacy_retention_policy.value: Shadow decision logs retained 2 years for model performance analysis; PAN must be truncated to last 4 digits in all shadow records; velocity counter keys expire after 24 hours in Redis
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: PCI-DSS prohibition on PAN storage in non-HSM systems; data minimization for shadow analytics

future_scope.value: Live decisioning mode replacing rules engine thresholds, real-time model champion/challenger A/B routing, automated model retraining pipeline
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap contingent on 90-day shadow mode validation meeting precision-recall thresholds

constraints.value: Adapter must add under 5ms p99 latency to the authorization path; must fail open (pass-through to legacy switch) on any adapter error; PCI-DSS Level 1 compliance for card data handling in the pipeline
constraints.rationale: Authorization SLA and PCI compliance requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with distributed tracing across adapter and legacy switch hops; custom metric: adapter_added_latency_ms p50/p95/p99; PagerDuty alert on p99 > 4ms
observability_baseline.rationale: Latency SLA protection is the highest operational priority for an in-path authorization service

execution_preference.value: Shadow mode deployment first; risk team sign-off on model performance before any live decisioning; weekly shadow report review cadence
execution_preference.rationale: Staged rollout to build confidence before live authorization impact

llm_integration.value: ONNX Runtime serving a pre-trained gradient boosted model (XGBoost exported to ONNX) for fraud scoring; feature vector constructed from velocity counters (Redis) and transaction fields (ISO 8583 bitmap); no external LLM API calls in authorization path
llm_integration.rationale: ONNX inference is sub-millisecond for GBM models; external LLM API latency incompatible with 5ms budget

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS Level 1 for ISO 8583 card data in the adapter pipeline; change management controls required for any modification to the authorization path message format; model documentation required for dispute resolution
compliance_context.rationale: In-path modification of authorization messages is in PCI scope; any field injection must be documented for card scheme audit

project.goal: In-path ISO 8583 adapter adding ONNX-based fraud scoring to legacy authorization switch in shadow mode
project.target_users: Risk team analysts and card operations engineers
project.timeline: Shadow mode MVP in 8 weeks; live mode pending 90-day evaluation

backend.model_serving: ONNX Runtime embedded in adapter JVM
backend.message_format: ISO 8583 with custom bitmap field for score injection

security.auth: mTLS for adapter-to-switch, JWT for admin API
security.pci: PCI-DSS Level 1 — PAN masked in all logs
security.change_mgmt: Change management approval required for authorization path modifications

data.cache: Redis 7 for velocity counters and model registry

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate same VPC as legacy switch

constraints.fail_mode: Fail open — pass-through to legacy switch on any adapter error

open_questions.live_mode_threshold: Precision-recall thresholds for live mode activation to be defined by risk team

reasoner_reviews.product: Shadow mode with 90-day evaluation before live decisioning is the right approach for a risk-averse organization — it lets the risk team validate model precision and recall against actual fraud outcomes without any authorization impact. The shadow decision comparison report must show per-rule alignment between legacy rules engine decisions and ML model scores to build the business case for live mode.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ONNX Runtime embedded in the adapter JVM avoids a network hop to a separate model serving pod, which is critical for the 5ms p99 budget. The fail-open circuit breaker must be implemented with a very short timeout (2ms) to ensure the adapter never blocks the authorization path. Java 21 virtual threads handle concurrent ISO 8583 message processing without traditional thread pool exhaustion under high authorization volume.
reasoner_reviews.security: PCI-DSS Level 1 requires that PAN is masked before writing to ClickHouse shadow logs — ISO 8583 DE2 (PAN) must be truncated to last 4 digits at the adapter before any shadow record is written. The mTLS certificate rotation schedule must be automated with zero-downtime reload. Any change to the ISO 8583 field bitmap injection must go through formal change management approval.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 5ms p99 budget breaks down as: Redis velocity lookup (~0.5ms), ONNX inference (~1ms for GBM with 50 features), ISO 8583 field injection (~0.5ms), network overhead (~1ms same-AZ). That leaves ~2ms headroom for p99 tail latency. Fail-open timeout must be set at 3ms to leave 2ms for network to legacy switch.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot adapter service with three components: ISO8583MessageHandler (parses inbound messages using j8583 library), FeatureExtractor (builds feature vector from message fields and Redis velocity counters), ScoringOrchestrator (calls ONNX Runtime session, injects score into custom field DE127, forwards to legacy switch).
backend.llm_integration_design: ONNX Runtime 1.17 loaded at application startup from the model version registered in Redis model_registry key. Feature vector: 50 features including transaction_amount_norm, velocity_1h (from Redis TTL counter), velocity_24h, merchant_category_code_encoded, card_present_flag, country_code_risk_score, time_of_day_sin, time_of_day_cos. Inference runs synchronously on virtual thread within 1ms for XGBoost GBM models up to 200 trees.
backend.failure_handling: Circuit breaker (Resilience4j) around ONNX inference with 3ms timeout. On open circuit: inject score=0.5 (neutral) into DE127 and forward — never block authorization. Separate ClickHouse write path uses async fire-and-forget to avoid I/O latency in the hot path.
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate auto-scaling on connection count. Each adapter task handles ~500 concurrent virtual-thread ISO connections. Target 4 tasks for peak authorization volume.

security.auth_design: mTLS between adapter and legacy switch using AWS Certificate Manager Private CA. Admin API for shadow reports uses custom JWT with fraud_analyst role.
security.secrets_management: mTLS private keys stored in AWS Secrets Manager; rotated every 90 days with zero-downtime hot reload via Spring Boot actuator endpoint.
security.privacy_controls: ISO 8583 DE2 (PAN) truncated to last 4 digits before ClickHouse write. DE35 (track 2) never written to shadow logs. Datadog APM traces exclude all ISO message field values — only field IDs and lengths logged.

data.entities: authorization_requests (ClickHouse: request_id UUID, merchant_id, amount_cents, currency, pan_last4, card_present, merchant_category_code, adapter_score FLOAT, legacy_decision ENUM approve/decline, model_version, feature_snapshot JSONB, created_at). model_versions (Redis hash: model_id, onnx_blob_path, feature_schema_version, deployed_at, is_active). feature_snapshots (ClickHouse: request_id FK, velocity_1h INT, velocity_24h INT, merchant_risk_score FLOAT, time_features JSONB, captured_at). shadow_decisions (ClickHouse: request_id FK, ml_score FLOAT, ml_decision ENUM would_approve/would_decline, legacy_decision, agree BOOLEAN, reason_codes JSONB, round_trip_ms INT).
data.storage_design: Redis 7: velocity counters as INCR keys with TTL (1h and 24h windows per card token), model_registry hash for active model pointer. ClickHouse: shadow_decisions and feature_snapshots tables partitioned by created_at month.
data.data_risks: Velocity counter key collisions if card token not unique — use SHA-256 of PAN hash as counter key, never raw PAN.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate task in same VPC subnet as legacy switch to minimize hop latency. 4 tasks minimum for HA. Task definition includes ONNX model downloaded from S3 at startup.
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins pipeline: Maven build, unit tests for ISO 8583 parsing and feature extraction, load test with synthetic authorization volume (10K req/s for 5 minutes validating p99 < 5ms), Docker build, ECR push, Fargate rolling deploy with latency canary (route 5% traffic to new task, monitor p99 for 10 minutes before full rollout).
devops.rollback_strategy: Model rollback via Redis model_registry update pointing to previous ONNX file — no code deploy needed. Adapter code rollback via ECS task definition revert.

ISS-001.title: Fail-open timeout not specified — risk of blocking authorization on adapter overload
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan mentions fail-open behavior but does not specify the circuit breaker timeout value or the neutral score injected on open circuit. If the circuit breaker timeout is too long, adapter overload will add more than 5ms to authorization latency, violating the SLA.

history.note: Circuit breaker timeout value absent from plan; neutral score on fail-open not specified

ISS-002.title: PAN not masked before ClickHouse shadow log write
ISS-002.detail: Data model in round 1 stores pan field (not pan_last4) in shadow_decisions ClickHouse table. This violates PCI-DSS prohibition on storing PANs in non-HSM environments.

history.note: ClickHouse shadow_decisions has pan column not pan_last4 — PCI violation

focus_issues.title: Fail-open timeout not specified — risk of blocking authorization on adapter overload
focus_issues.detail: Circuit breaker timeout and neutral score on fail-open not defined in round 1 plan.

focus_issues.title: PAN not masked before ClickHouse shadow log write
focus_issues.detail: shadow_decisions table stores raw pan field — PCI-DSS violation.

latest_recommendations: Specify Resilience4j circuit breaker with 3ms timeout and fail-open injecting score=0.5 neutral value into DE127
Replace pan column with pan_last4 in all ClickHouse tables; add truncation step in ISO8583MessageHandler before shadow record write
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Card Authorization ML Enrichment Adapter v1

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues — critical fail-open specification and PCI PAN masking — require explicit changes to failure handling and data model

previous_audits.summary: The adapter architecture is conceptually correct with ONNX embedded inference and fail-open intent, but two critical gaps prevent production readiness: the circuit breaker timeout value is unspecified (risking SLA violation under overload) and the PAN is stored unmasked in ClickHouse shadow logs (PCI-DSS violation). Both issues must be resolved before staging deployment.
strengths: ONNX Runtime embedded in JVM is the right call for sub-millisecond inference within the 5ms budget
Shadow mode first with 90-day evaluation period is a sound risk-averse deployment strategy
Java 21 virtual threads handle concurrent ISO connection processing correctly
concerns: Fail-open circuit breaker lacks timeout specification and neutral score definition
PAN column in ClickHouse shadow_decisions is a clear PCI scope violation
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Circuit breaker timeout unspecified — overload scenario could violate 5ms authorization SLA
