target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses two blocking issues from round 1: ISS-001 (unspecified fail-open timeout) and ISS-002 (PAN stored unmasked in ClickHouse). Fixing ISS-001 means explicitly adding a Resilience4j circuit breaker with a 3ms timeout and documenting the neutral score=0.5 injection into DE127 on open circuit. Fixing ISS-002 means renaming the pan column to pan_last4 across all ClickHouse tables and adding the DE2 truncation step in ISO8583MessageHandler before any shadow record write. These are targeted fixes — the core ONNX adapter architecture from round 1 is sound.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Resilience4j CircuitBreaker around ONNX inference and Redis velocity lookup with explicit 3ms timeout. On open circuit or timeout: inject score=0.5 (neutral) into DE127 and pass message to legacy switch immediately. Documented in failure_handling and security_and_compliance sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Adapter will never add more than 3ms on the fail-open path, preserving the 5ms p99 SLA even under model serving overload.

fix_report.action_taken: Renamed pan to pan_last4 in all ClickHouse table definitions. Added explicit PAN truncation step in ISO8583MessageHandler: before writing any shadow record, extract DE2 value, truncate to last 4 digits, store only pan_last4. DE35 (track 2 data) never written. Documented truncation in data_model and security_and_compliance.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No raw PAN stored in ClickHouse shadow tables — PCI-DSS PAN storage prohibition satisfied. Velocity counter keys use SHA-256 hash of full PAN token, never stored.

target_output.title: Card Authorization ML Enrichment Adapter v2
target_output.executive_summary: In-path Java 21 Spring Boot adapter that intercepts ISO 8583 authorization messages, extracts a 50-feature vector using velocity counters from Redis and transaction fields, scores with an embedded ONNX XGBoost GBM model in under 1ms, and injects the fraud probability score into custom ISO field DE127 before forwarding to the legacy switch. Shadow mode logs model decisions alongside legacy rules engine outcomes to ClickHouse for 90-day performance evaluation. This revision adds explicit Resilience4j circuit breaker with 3ms timeout and fail-open neutral score, and corrects PAN masking to pan_last4 in all shadow log tables.
target_output.architecture_overview: ISO 8583 authorization messages arrive at the adapter via an internal TCP connection from the card processor. The Spring Boot ISO8583MessageHandler parses the message bitmap using j8583, immediately truncates DE2 (PAN) to last 4 digits for logging purposes, and extracts transaction features. FeatureExtractor queries Redis for velocity counters (1h and 24h per card token key using SHA-256 of tokenized PAN) and builds a 50-element feature vector. ScoringOrchestrator calls ONNX Runtime session for GBM inference (target under 1ms), injects the fraud probability score into DE127 using a custom private field definition, and forwards the enriched message to the legacy authorization switch via the existing TCP connection. A Resilience4j circuit breaker wraps the entire feature extraction and scoring path with a 3ms timeout — on open or timeout, score=0.5 is injected and the message forwarded immediately. Shadow decision records are written asynchronously to ClickHouse via a fire-and-forget virtual thread to avoid I/O on the hot path.
target_output.technology_stack: Java 21 with virtual threads, Spring Boot 3.2, j8583 library for ISO 8583 message parsing, ONNX Runtime 1.17 embedded in JVM for XGBoost GBM inference, Resilience4j circuit breaker with 3ms timeout. Data: ClickHouse 24.x for shadow decisions and feature snapshots, Redis 7 for velocity counters and model registry. Infrastructure: AWS ECS Fargate, AWS Certificate Manager Private CA for mTLS, AWS Secrets Manager. CI/CD: Jenkins. Monitoring: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ISO 8583 Message Interception: TCP connection management, j8583 bitmap parsing, DE2 truncation to pan_last4 before any logging. Feature Extraction: velocity counter lookup from Redis (1h/24h per card token), merchant category code risk encoding, time-of-day cyclical encoding, card-present flag. ONNX Scoring: GBM model inference under 1ms, fraud probability mapped to DE127 custom field. Fail-Open Circuit Breaker: Resilience4j with 3ms timeout, neutral score=0.5 injection on open circuit. Shadow Mode Logging: async ClickHouse write of shadow_decisions with pan_last4, adapter score, legacy decision, agree flag, and round-trip latency. Model Version Management: Redis model_registry hash pointing to active ONNX file path in S3. Admin API: shadow report retrieval, model version switch endpoint.
target_output.system_components: Spring Boot adapter service (ECS Fargate, 4 tasks, same VPC subnet as legacy switch). Java 21 virtual thread executor for concurrent ISO connection handling. ONNX Runtime 1.17 inference session loaded at startup from S3. Resilience4j CircuitBreaker (timeout=3ms, fail-open with score=0.5). j8583 ISO 8583 parser. Redis 7 (velocity counters with TTL, model_registry hash). ClickHouse 24.x (shadow_decisions, feature_snapshots, partitioned by month). AWS Certificate Manager Private CA for mTLS certificates. AWS Secrets Manager for mTLS private key and Redis auth. Datadog APM sidecar.
target_output.workflows: Authorization Enrichment Flow: ISO 8583 message arrives on TCP socket; ISO8583MessageHandler parses bitmap, extracts DE2 PAN and immediately computes SHA-256 card token (PAN not stored); FeatureExtractor reads Redis velocity counters for card token 1h and 24h windows; Resilience4j-wrapped ScoringOrchestrator calls ONNX Runtime session with 50-feature vector — if inference completes within 3ms, injects fraud_score into DE127; if timeout or circuit open, injects score=0.5 (neutral) into DE127 and continues; enriched ISO message forwarded to legacy switch; async virtual thread writes shadow_decisions record to ClickHouse with pan_last4, adapter_score, legacy_decision (captured from switch response). Shadow Report Generation: fraud analyst queries ClickHouse for shadow_decisions where agree=false grouped by merchant_category_code and hour_of_day; report highlights transaction segments where model would decide differently from legacy rules engine with supporting precision estimates.
target_output.data_model: authorization_requests (ClickHouse: request_id UUID, merchant_id VARCHAR, amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), pan_last4 CHAR(4), card_present BOOLEAN, merchant_category_code CHAR(4), adapter_score FLOAT32, model_version VARCHAR, created_at DateTime64). shadow_decisions (ClickHouse: request_id UUID, ml_score FLOAT32, ml_decision Enum8('would_approve'=1,'would_decline'=2), legacy_decision Enum8('approved'=1,'declined'=2), agree BOOLEAN, reason_codes String, round_trip_ms UINT16, created_at DateTime64). feature_snapshots (ClickHouse: request_id UUID, velocity_1h UINT32, velocity_24h UINT32, merchant_risk_score FLOAT32, time_sin FLOAT32, time_cos FLOAT32, card_present BOOLEAN, captured_at DateTime64). model_versions (Redis hash: model_id, onnx_s3_path, feature_schema_version, deployed_at, is_active). velocity_counters (Redis: INCR keys format card_token:SHA256:{token}:1h and :24h with matching TTL). Note: pan_last4 only — raw PAN never written to ClickHouse. DE2 truncated in ISO8583MessageHandler before any shadow record assembly.
target_output.api_design: Admin REST API (JWT-authenticated, fraud_analyst role): GET /admin/shadow/summary?date=&merchant_id= (shadow decision agreement rate and model vs legacy divergence breakdown). GET /admin/shadow/decisions?agree=false&limit=1000 (paginated list of divergent decisions for analyst review). POST /admin/models/activate — body: {model_id}; updates Redis model_registry is_active pointer and triggers ONNX session reload on all adapter tasks via AWS ECS exec broadcast. GET /admin/models — list registered model versions with deploy dates. GET /admin/health — circuit breaker state, Redis connectivity, ClickHouse write queue depth, adapter_added_latency_ms p99.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS Level 1 controls: DE2 PAN truncated to pan_last4 in ISO8583MessageHandler at the point of message parsing — before any shadow record is assembled or any log is written. DE35 (track 2 data) never extracted or stored. Velocity counter Redis keys use SHA-256 hash of PAN token — raw PAN never in Redis key space. mTLS between adapter and legacy switch using AWS CM PCA certificates rotated every 90 days with hot reload. Fail-open circuit breaker: Resilience4j 3ms timeout injects score=0.5 and passes through — adapter never blocks authorization. Datadog APM traces exclude all ISO message field values — only field IDs and response times logged. Change management approval required for any ISO 8583 bitmap field injection changes (PCI audit trail).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate 4 tasks in same VPC subnet as legacy switch (us-east-1a). Task definition mounts ONNX model from S3 at container startup via entrypoint script. Jenkins CI pipeline: Maven build, unit tests for ISO 8583 DE2 truncation (verifying pan_last4 output), ONNX inference latency benchmark (target p99 < 1ms on m6i.large), load test at 10,000 req/s for 5 minutes validating adapter_added_latency p99 < 5ms, Docker build, ECR push, Fargate canary deploy (5% traffic to new task, monitor Datadog p99 for 10 minutes, then full rollout). mTLS certificate renewal automated via Jenkins scheduled job and Secrets Manager rotation. Staging environment: single Fargate task with synthetic ISO 8583 load generator for integration testing.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM distributed trace spans: iso_parse, redis_velocity_lookup, onnx_inference, iso_field_inject, legacy_forward. Custom metric: adapter_added_latency_ms (p50 target <1ms, p95 <3ms, p99 <5ms). Circuit breaker state metric: circuit_breaker_state gauge (0=closed, 1=open, 2=half-open). Shadow agreement rate: Grafana query against ClickHouse shadow_decisions agree column. PagerDuty alert triggers on: adapter_added_latency p99 > 4ms, circuit_breaker_state=open for > 60 seconds, ClickHouse write queue depth > 10,000.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $1,200/month: 4 Fargate tasks (2 vCPU, 4GB each for ONNX model in memory), Redis r6g.large ($180), ClickHouse on c6i.xlarge self-managed ($300), Datadog APM ($200), S3 for ONNX model storage negligible. Scale path: at 5x authorization volume, increase Fargate task count to 12 via ECS auto-scaling on connection count metric; Redis cluster mode for velocity counter sharding; ClickHouse distributed table for shadow log query parallelism.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Shadow Mode (8 weeks): ISO 8583 adapter with ONNX GBM inference, Resilience4j fail-open circuit breaker (3ms timeout), shadow_decisions ClickHouse logging with pan_last4, Redis velocity counters, mTLS, Jenkins CI with load test, ECS Fargate deploy. Phase 2 Shadow Evaluation (12 weeks): 90-day shadow report review cadence, precision-recall analysis by merchant segment, model retraining pipeline triggered by chargeback data feed, risk team sign-off criteria definition. Phase 3 Live Mode (6 weeks): live decisioning mode toggled per merchant segment via feature flag, champion/challenger A/B routing, automated threshold calibration.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 strict null safety with Spotbugs. JUnit 5 with parameterized tests for ISO 8583 DE2 truncation covering all card number lengths. Load test must run in Jenkins pipeline — p99 < 5ms is a merge gate not an advisory. ONNX model files versioned by SHA-256 hash of file content. Model activation via admin API only — no manual Redis key edits in production. Circuit breaker timeout value (3ms) documented in architecture decision record with calculation rationale. Any change to the ISO 8583 field injection schema requires PCI change management ticket before code review.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ONNX model file corruption in S3 causes startup failure. Mitigation: startup script validates SHA-256 of downloaded ONNX file against model_registry hash before loading session; falls back to previous model version on validation failure. Risk: Redis velocity counter loss on cluster failover causes zero-feature vectors for 30 seconds. Mitigation: feature extraction treats zero velocity as valid input — model trained to handle missing velocity features gracefully with a sentinel value. Tradeoff: ONNX embedded in JVM vs separate model serving pod — embedded eliminates network hop at cost of JVM memory footprint (model ~200MB); acceptable for 4GB task definition. Tradeoff: ClickHouse self-managed vs AWS managed service — ClickHouse Cloud not available in us-east-1 at time of decision; revisit in Phase 3.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Circuit breaker timeout set to 3ms based on timing budget analysis leaving 2ms for p99 network overhead to legacy switch. Neutral score set to 0.5 (maximum uncertainty) rather than 0.0 to avoid triggering legacy rules engine logic that checks for zero scores. Model activation via admin API Redis pointer swap confirmed as zero-downtime approach — ONNX Runtime supports hot session reload without JVM restart.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision fixing ISS-001 (circuit breaker timeout) and ISS-002 (PAN masking). Weak quality: fixes are correct but several sections (observability, cost) remain thin and phased_implementation lacks depth.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for payment engineers that subscribes to a webhook delivery log stream and renders a real-time TUI showing delivery success rates, retry attempts, latency percentiles, and endpoint health per merchant
project_goal.rationale: Enable rapid diagnosis of webhook delivery degradation during incidents without querying the database directly

target_users.value: Payment engineers and on-call SREs diagnosing webhook delivery incidents
target_users.rationale: Primary users are engineers needing real-time visibility during webhook delivery degradation

project_class.rationale: Terminal-based tool distributed as a static binary with no server component

capabilities.rationale: Streaming data consumption, real-time TUI rendering, internal API integration, ClickHouse export

complexity_level.rationale: Single-binary CLI with streaming input and TUI rendering — no server infrastructure

risk_level.rationale: Internal engineering tool with no card data exposure; risk is operational — wrong diagnosis could delay incident response

data_sensitivity.rationale: Webhook delivery logs contain endpoint URLs and response codes but no cardholder data — payload bodies are masked at source

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs on engineer workstations and CI jump boxes — no external network exposure

access_model.value: JWT token issued by internal auth service; token stored in ~/.wh-monitor/config.toml on developer workstation; token scoped to read-only webhook delivery log stream
access_model.rationale: Read-only token prevents any write operations to webhook delivery system via the tool

feature_scope.value: Real-time TUI with per-merchant delivery success rate, p50/p99 latency, retry attempt count, consecutive failure counter, endpoint URL health status; drill-down into individual merchant webhook stream; JSON export of filtered log window for postmortem
feature_scope.rationale: Full set of signals needed for webhook delivery incident diagnosis

mvp_scope.value: TUI summary table updating every 2 seconds with delivery success rate and consecutive failure count per merchant; drill-down to per-merchant stream; JWT authentication
mvp_scope.rationale: Core diagnostic capability for on-call engineers

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for internal API connection; JWT stored in file with 0600 permissions; no webhook payload bodies displayed — only delivery metadata (status codes, latency, retry counts)
security_baseline.rationale: Prevent accidental exposure of webhook payload content which may contain PII in event payloads

frontend_stack.value: None — terminal TUI built with Bubbletea (Go) running in the engineer's terminal emulator
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool distributes as a single Go binary with embedded TUI; no browser component

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Cobra for command structure and Bubbletea for TUI rendering
backend_stack.rationale: Go produces a small static binary; Bubbletea provides the Elm Architecture TUI framework; Cobra is the standard Go CLI framework

data_platform.value: ClickHouse via internal webhook delivery log API (HTTP streaming); local in-memory ring buffer for TUI state; JSON file export for postmortem
data_platform.rationale: No local database — tool reads streaming data and maintains in-memory window; ClickHouse is the authoritative store accessed via the API

hosting_target.value: Developer workstations and CI jump boxes — binary distributed via internal Go module proxy
hosting_target.rationale: CLI tool; no server hosting — distributed as a compiled binary

privacy_retention_policy.value: No persistent local storage except exported JSON files; exported files contain delivery metadata only (no payload bodies); engineers responsible for deleting export files after postmortem
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Minimizing persistence of internal delivery log data on engineer workstations

future_scope.value: Alert threshold configuration per merchant (e.g., page on-call when consecutive failures > N), integration with PagerDuty for automated incident creation from CLI observation
future_scope.rationale: Reduce manual intervention needed during webhook delivery incidents

constraints.value: Binary must be under 20MB; TUI must update every 2 seconds maximum; no persistent local state except exported files; must work on macOS and Linux (amd64 and arm64)
constraints.rationale: Lightweight tool for fast installation on any engineer workstation or jump box

observability_baseline.value: No server-side observability — tool is client-side only; --debug flag writes verbose logs to ~/.wh-monitor/debug.log for troubleshooting API connection issues
observability_baseline.rationale: CLI tool on developer workstations does not need centralized observability infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Single engineer implementation; weekly releases via internal module proxy; semantic versioning
execution_preference.rationale: Small-scope internal tool; no need for sprint ceremonies

project.goal: Real-time webhook delivery TUI for payment engineers to diagnose delivery degradation during incidents
project.target_users: Payment engineers and on-call SREs
project.timeline: MVP in 3 weeks; full feature in 6 weeks

backend.framework: Cobra for CLI commands, Bubbletea for TUI
backend.streaming: HTTP SSE or WebSocket stream from internal webhook log API

security.data_safety: Payload bodies never displayed — delivery metadata only

data.storage: In-memory ring buffer only; JSON file export for postmortem
data.source: Internal webhook delivery log API over ClickHouse

devops.distribution: Binary via internal Go module proxy

open_questions.stream_protocol: SSE vs WebSocket — pending API team confirmation

reasoner_reviews.product: The 2-second TUI update cycle is the right balance between real-time feel and API polling overhead. Per-merchant consecutive failure count is the most actionable signal for on-call engineers — a merchant at 10 consecutive failures needs immediate attention regardless of overall success rate. The drill-down to individual merchant stream should show the last 20 delivery attempts with response code, latency, and retry number so engineers can spot patterns without writing ClickHouse queries.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Bubbletea with the Elm Architecture is well-suited for this use case — the Model/Update/View loop makes the 2-second tick refresh and drill-down navigation easy to implement without race conditions. The in-memory ring buffer should be bounded at 1,000 delivery events per merchant to prevent memory growth during extended monitoring sessions. The --export flag should capture the current window to JSON, not stream all historical data, to keep export files small and targeted for postmortem use.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT token file permissions must be enforced at startup — the tool should check 0600 on ~/.wh-monitor/config.toml and refuse to start if permissions are too open. Webhook payload bodies must never be rendered in the TUI or written to export files — this is critical to prevent accidental exposure of event payload content that may include merchant PII. The --debug flag log file must also exclude response body content.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 20MB binary size constraint is achievable with Go — a typical Cobra + Bubbletea + charmbracelet/lipgloss binary is 8-12MB. Cross-compilation for macOS and Linux amd64/arm64 is straightforward with Go's native cross-compile support using GOOS/GOARCH flags in the release build.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with three Cobra commands: monitor (default, launches TUI), export (writes JSON snapshot without TUI), auth (stores JWT token to config file). Internal package structure: api (webhook log API client), model (delivery event types and ring buffer), tui (Bubbletea components), config (config file loader).
backend.api_patterns: Internal webhook delivery log API: GET /v1/webhook-logs/stream?merchant_id=&since= returns SSE stream of delivery_event records. GET /v1/webhook-logs/merchants returns list of active merchant IDs and endpoint URLs. GET /v1/webhook-logs?merchant_id=&limit=20 returns last N delivery attempts for drill-down. All requests include Authorization: Bearer JWT header.
backend.scaling_notes: In-memory ring buffer bounded at 1,000 events per merchant. Monitor command limits concurrent merchant streams to 50 to avoid exhausting API server connections. Export command uses one-shot API call, not streaming.

security.auth_design: JWT stored in ~/.wh-monitor/config.toml. At startup, config loader checks file permissions using os.Stat() — exits with error message if permissions are not 0600. Token refresh not implemented in MVP; engineer runs 'wh-monitor auth' to re-authenticate before token expiry.
security.privacy_controls: delivery_events struct explicitly omits payload_body field. API client response parser uses json.Unmarshal into delivery_events struct — any payload_body fields in API response are silently dropped by Go struct tag omitempty. Export JSON uses the same struct, ensuring payload bodies are never in exported files.
security.security_risks: Risk of token exposure in shell history if token is passed as a CLI flag. Mitigation: token is only ever read from config file — no --token flag exposed.

data.entities: delivery_events (in-memory struct: merchant_id string, endpoint_url string, attempt_number int, status_code int, latency_ms int, error_code string, delivered_at time.Time, is_retry bool). merchant_summary (in-memory: merchant_id string, success_rate_pct float64, p50_latency_ms int, p99_latency_ms int, consecutive_failures int, total_attempts_window int, last_seen time.Time). ring_buffer (per merchant, capacity 1000, implemented as circular slice with head/tail pointers for lock-free read in Bubbletea model tick).
data.analytics_events: Export JSON schema: {exported_at: ISO8601, merchant_filter: string|null, window_seconds: int, events: [{merchant_id, endpoint_url, attempt_number, status_code, latency_ms, error_code, delivered_at, is_retry}]}.

devops.deployment_topology: No server deployment. Binary distribution via internal Go module proxy at go.internal.company.com. Release artifacts: wh-monitor-darwin-amd64, wh-monitor-darwin-arm64, wh-monitor-linux-amd64, wh-monitor-linux-arm64 uploaded to internal artifact store.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... with -race flag, go vet, staticcheck, cross-compile matrix (GOOS x GOARCH), binary size check (fail if > 20MB), goreleaser for release tagging and artifact upload to internal store.
devops.observability_stack: No server-side observability. --debug flag writes structured log lines to ~/.wh-monitor/debug.log using zerolog. Log lines include API request URL, response status, and latency — never response body content.

ISS-001.title: Ring buffer implementation missing — TUI model accumulates events unboundedly
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan describes a ring buffer but the data model shows a simple slice append for delivery_events per merchant with no capacity limit. During a prolonged monitoring session, this will grow unboundedly and eventually OOM the engineer's workstation.

history.note: delivery_events stored as growing slice — no ring buffer implementation described

focus_issues.title: Ring buffer implementation missing — TUI model accumulates events unboundedly
focus_issues.detail: delivery_events stored as growing slice with no capacity limit — OOM risk on prolonged monitoring sessions.

latest_recommendations: Replace simple slice append with a bounded ring buffer (capacity 1000 events per merchant) implemented as a circular slice with head/tail pointers, ensuring the Bubbletea model tick can read without locking

convergence_state.reason: ISS-001 ring buffer implementation requires explicit data structure change in data model and TUI component descriptions

previous_audits.summary: Strong overall plan for a focused internal CLI tool. The Bubbletea TUI design and command structure are well-specified, and the security controls around JWT token file permissions and payload body exclusion are correctly implemented. One blocking issue: the event accumulation model is a growing slice with no cap, which will OOM on a workstation during extended monitoring sessions. The ring buffer must be explicitly implemented.
strengths: Bubbletea Elm Architecture is well-suited for the 2-second tick refresh pattern
JWT config file permission check at startup is correctly specified
payload_body exclusion from delivery_events struct is a clean Go-idiomatic solution
Cross-compilation matrix covers all four required platforms
concerns: delivery_events per merchant stored as unbounded slice — OOM risk
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Unbounded event accumulation will OOM workstation on prolonged monitoring sessions
recommendations: Implement ring buffer as circular slice with capacity=1000 per merchant and head/tail index pointers; read operations in Bubbletea view function iterate from tail to head without locking since Bubbletea's Update is single-goroutine

issue_updates.title: Ring buffer missing — unbounded slice growth
issue_updates.detail: Simple slice append in TUI model with no capacity enforcement

previous_plan.title: Webhook Delivery Monitor CLI v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go 1.22 Bubbletea TUI CLI for real-time webhook delivery monitoring. Three Cobra commands: monitor, export, auth. JWT authentication with file permission enforcement.
previous_plan.data_model: delivery_events stored as []DeliveryEvent slice per merchant_id map key — unbounded growth, no ring buffer. merchant_summary computed from the full slice on each tick.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: JWT file permission check at startup, payload_body excluded from DeliveryEvent struct, TLS 1.3 for API connection.
previous_plan.workflows: monitor command opens SSE stream, appends events to per-merchant slice, Bubbletea tick recomputes merchant_summary and re-renders table every 2 seconds.

best_plan.title: Webhook Delivery Monitor CLI v1
best_plan.executive_summary: Go 1.22 Bubbletea TUI with JWT auth, payload body exclusion, 2-second refresh. Unbounded slice accumulation identified as blocking issue.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses the single blocking issue ISS-001: replacing the unbounded delivery_events slice with a bounded ring buffer per merchant. The ring buffer uses a circular slice with capacity 1000 and explicit head/tail index counters. Since Bubbletea's Update function runs on a single goroutine, view renders reading the ring buffer do not need locking — the stream goroutine writes to the ring buffer and sends a Msg to the Bubbletea program, which processes the update serially. This is a targeted data structure change that resolves the OOM risk without requiring architectural changes to the CLI command structure or API client.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced unbounded []DeliveryEvent slice per merchant with a RingBuffer struct (capacity=1000, fields: buf [1000]DeliveryEvent, head int, tail int, count int). Write method increments tail modulo 1000, overwriting oldest entry when full. Read method for view rendering iterates from head to tail in insertion order. Stream goroutine sends a DeliveryEventMsg to the Bubbletea program after each ring buffer write, triggering a View re-render via the Update function. merchant_summary computed from ring buffer contents during Update phase, stored in Model, read by View — no per-render recomputation.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Memory usage bounded at 1000 events * ~200 bytes per event = ~200KB per monitored merchant regardless of monitoring session duration. No OOM risk on prolonged incidents.

target_output.title: Webhook Delivery Monitor CLI v2
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI tool for payment engineers providing a real-time Bubbletea TUI that streams webhook delivery events from the internal delivery log API and renders per-merchant success rates, p50/p99 latency, consecutive failure counts, and endpoint health — updating every 2 seconds. Engineers can drill down into a specific merchant's delivery stream, inspect individual attempt response codes and retry timelines, and export a filtered window to JSON for incident postmortems. This revision replaces the unbounded event slice with a bounded ring buffer (capacity 1000 per merchant) to prevent OOM during prolonged monitoring sessions.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Go binary with Cobra command routing and Bubbletea TUI framework. The monitor command opens an HTTP SSE stream to the internal webhook delivery log API (JWT-authenticated, TLS 1.3), parses delivery_event records in a background goroutine, writes each event to the merchant's bounded ring buffer (circular slice, capacity 1000), and sends a DeliveryEventMsg to the Bubbletea program. The Bubbletea Update function receives the message, recomputes merchant_summary metrics from the ring buffer contents, and stores them in the Model. The View function renders the summary table or drill-down detail screen from the stored Model state. The 2-second tick triggers a full re-render even without new events to keep the display current. The export command uses a one-shot API request (not streaming) to capture the current log window and write it to a JSON file.
target_output.technology_stack: Go 1.22. CLI framework: Cobra. TUI: Bubbletea (Elm Architecture), Lipgloss for table styling, Bubbles for list and viewport components. HTTP streaming: net/http with context cancellation for SSE parsing. Config: config.toml read via Viper with 0600 permission enforcement. JSON: encoding/json with struct tags to exclude payload_body. Build: goreleaser for cross-compilation (darwin/linux, amd64/arm64) and artifact upload. CI: GitHub Actions. Distribution: internal Go module proxy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Monitor Command: SSE stream subscription, ring buffer write per event, per-merchant merchant_summary recomputation on each DeliveryEventMsg, 2-second tick re-render. Summary TUI View: sortable table of active merchants with columns for endpoint_url (truncated), success_rate_pct, p50_latency_ms, p99_latency_ms, consecutive_failures, last_seen timestamp. Drill-Down View: per-merchant stream of last 20 delivery attempts from ring buffer with columns for attempt_number, status_code, latency_ms, error_code, is_retry, delivered_at. Export Command: one-shot API fetch of current window, serialize to JSON using delivery_events struct (no payload_body), write to file path specified by --output flag. Auth Command: prompt for JWT token, write to ~/.wh-monitor/config.toml with os.Chmod 0600.
target_output.system_components: wh-monitor binary: Cobra router (monitor/export/auth commands), api.Client (HTTP SSE streaming, JWT Bearer header, TLS 1.3), model.RingBuffer (circular slice capacity=1000, head/tail counters), tui.Model (Bubbletea model holding merchant_summaries map and drill-down state), tui.SummaryView (Lipgloss table), tui.DrillDownView (Lipgloss scrollable list), config.Loader (config.toml parser with 0600 check). External dependency: internal webhook delivery log API at https://webhook-logs.internal.company.com.
target_output.workflows: Monitor Workflow: engineer runs 'wh-monitor monitor --merchant-filter acme-corp'; Cobra parses flags; config.Loader reads JWT from ~/.wh-monitor/config.toml, enforces 0600 permissions; api.Client opens SSE stream GET /v1/webhook-logs/stream with Authorization Bearer header; background goroutine parses SSE data lines into DeliveryEvent structs (payload_body field absent from struct — silently dropped by json.Unmarshal); each parsed event written to merchant's RingBuffer via buf.Write(), which overwrites oldest entry when count reaches 1000; goroutine sends DeliveryEventMsg to Bubbletea program; Update function receives message, iterates ring buffer to recompute merchant_summary metrics (success rate, p50/p99 from sorted latency slice of ring contents, consecutive_failures counter), stores in model.MerchantSummaries map; View renders Lipgloss table; 2-second TickMsg from time.Ticker triggers re-render. Drill-Down Workflow: engineer presses Enter on a merchant row in summary table; TUI transitions to DrillDownView; api.Client fetches GET /v1/webhook-logs?merchant_id=&limit=20; renders list of last 20 delivery attempts with response code, latency, retry count; engineer presses Esc to return to summary. Export Workflow: engineer runs 'wh-monitor export --merchant-id acme-corp --output ./acme-webhook-incident.json'; api.Client performs GET /v1/webhook-logs?merchant_id=acme-corp&limit=1000; marshals response into []DeliveryEvent (payload_body excluded); writes JSON to output file.
target_output.data_model: RingBuffer struct (Go): {buf [1000]DeliveryEvent, head int, tail int, count int, mu sync.Mutex}. Write(e DeliveryEvent): mu.Lock(); buf[tail%1000]=e; tail++; if count<1000 {count++} else {head++}; mu.Unlock(). ReadAll() []DeliveryEvent: returns snapshot copy of count elements in insertion order from head index. DeliveryEvent struct (Go, all fields from API): {MerchantID string, EndpointURL string, AttemptNumber int, StatusCode int, LatencyMs int, ErrorCode string, DeliveredAt time.Time, IsRetry bool}. Note: PayloadBody is intentionally absent from the struct — json.Unmarshal silently drops unknown fields. MerchantSummary struct (computed from ring buffer): {MerchantID string, SuccessRatePct float64, P50LatencyMs int, P99LatencyMs int, ConsecutiveFailures int, TotalAttemptsWindow int, LastSeen time.Time}. ExportRecord struct (JSON output): {ExportedAt time.Time, MerchantFilter string, WindowSeconds int, Events []DeliveryEvent}.
target_output.api_design: Consumed internal API endpoints (read-only, JWT Bearer auth): GET /v1/webhook-logs/stream?merchant_id=&since= — SSE stream returning delivery_event JSON objects; each SSE data line is one JSON delivery_event. GET /v1/webhook-logs?merchant_id=&limit=N — returns last N delivery attempts for drill-down view. GET /v1/webhook-logs/merchants — returns list of active merchant IDs and endpoint URLs for auto-complete. CLI command flags: wh-monitor monitor [--merchant-filter MERCHANT_ID] [--sort-by consecutive_failures|success_rate|p99_latency]. wh-monitor export --merchant-id MERCHANT_ID --output FILE [--since TIMESTAMP]. wh-monitor auth (interactive JWT token enrollment).
target_output.security_and_compliance: JWT token read from ~/.wh-monitor/config.toml. At startup, config.Loader calls os.Stat() on the config file and checks mode bits — if permissions are more permissive than 0600, the tool prints 'Config file has insecure permissions. Run: chmod 600 ~/.wh-monitor/config.toml' and exits with code 1. TLS 1.3 enforced by net/http with MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS13. PayloadBody field absent from DeliveryEvent struct — API response payload bodies silently dropped by encoding/json. Export JSON files contain only delivery metadata. --debug flag writes to ~/.wh-monitor/debug.log using zerolog at Debug level — response body content is never logged even in debug mode (api.Client reads and discards body after parsing headers for SSE).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No server deployment. Binary distribution via internal Go module proxy at go.internal.company.com. GitHub Actions release workflow: go test ./... with -race detector, go vet, staticcheck linter, goreleaser cross-compilation matrix building 4 binaries (darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, linux/amd64, linux/arm64), binary size check asserting each artifact is under 20MB, upload to internal artifact store and Go module proxy. Engineers install via 'go install go.internal.company.com/wh-monitor@latest'. Semantic versioning; CHANGELOG maintained in repository. No deployment infrastructure required.
target_output.observability: No centralized observability for a client-side CLI. --debug flag activates zerolog DEBUG level output to ~/.wh-monitor/debug.log, logging API connection establishment, SSE reconnect events, ring buffer write count per merchant, and Bubbletea tick timing — never response body content. Engineers share debug.log snippets in Slack during incident postmortems. Binary version embedded at build time via Go ldflags: -X main.Version=v1.2.3, displayed in wh-monitor --version output for support triage.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — client-side binary. Build pipeline runs on existing GitHub Actions runners. Binary size target under 20MB eliminates storage concerns for artifact hosting. At 50 monitored merchants with ring buffer capacity 1000 each, peak in-memory footprint is approximately 10MB — well within workstation memory constraints. No scaling concerns for a single-developer tool.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): monitor command with SSE streaming and Bubbletea TUI summary table, ring buffer (capacity 1000) for bounded event storage, JWT auth command with 0600 permission enforcement, payload_body exclusion, TLS 1.3, GitHub Actions CI with race detector and binary size check, goreleaser cross-compile for 4 platforms. Phase 2 (3 weeks): drill-down view for per-merchant delivery stream, export command with JSON output, --merchant-filter flag for filtering summary table, --sort-by flag for table column sorting, CHANGELOG and semantic versioning automation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with modules. go test ./... -race must pass in CI — race detector catches ring buffer concurrent access bugs. Bubbletea Update function is the single goroutine handling model mutations — stream goroutine communicates only via tea.Cmd messages, never direct model field writes. RingBuffer.Write uses a mutex for safe concurrent access from the stream goroutine; ReadAll returns a snapshot copy to avoid holding the lock during view rendering. Linting: staticcheck and go vet in CI. Integration test: mock SSE server sending 2000 delivery events to a single merchant, assert ring buffer count stays at 1000 and head pointer advanced correctly. Binary size checked in CI via 'ls -la' on goreleaser artifacts with shell assertion.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SSE stream disconnects during an incident, leaving engineer with stale data. Mitigation: api.Client reconnects automatically with exponential backoff capped at 5 seconds; TUI displays last_connected timestamp so engineers know if data is stale. Risk: Internal webhook delivery log API adds a new field including payload content in the future. Mitigation: strict struct deserialization ensures unknown fields are silently dropped — future API fields not in DeliveryEvent struct will never appear in TUI or exports. Tradeoff: in-memory ring buffer over local SQLite — SQLite would enable historical queries but adds a dependency and persistent state; ring buffer is simpler and sufficient for the real-time incident diagnosis use case. Tradeoff: SSE over WebSocket — SSE is simpler (standard net/http client, no upgrade handshake) and sufficient for unidirectional delivery event streaming.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected SSE over WebSocket for stream protocol — SSE is unidirectional and simpler to implement with Go net/http; WebSocket not needed since the CLI only reads from the stream. Ring buffer capacity set at 1000 events per merchant based on analysis: at peak delivery volume (50 events/minute per busy merchant), 1000 events covers a 20-minute incident window sufficient for postmortem context. Lipgloss selected over tview for table rendering due to better composability with Bubbletea's View function pattern.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision fixing ISS-001 ring buffer implementation. Strong quality: all sections domain-specific with concrete Go implementation details, ring buffer data structure fully specified, TUI workflow clearly traced.

project_goal.value: Desktop treasury management app for mid-market CFOs and analysts to consolidate cash positions across bank accounts and payment processors, project liquidity, and execute same-day wires and ACH transfers
project_goal.rationale: Core need replacing fragmented spreadsheet and bank portal workflows

target_users.value: CFOs and treasury analysts at mid-market companies managing multi-bank cash positions
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in discovery interviews

project_class.rationale: Electron delivers native desktop feel with secure local credential storage and banking API integrations

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, auth, payments, data, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Full capability set for treasury operations including payment initiation

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-bank connectivity, dual-approval payment workflows, and liquidity forecasting logic

risk_level.rationale: Direct payment initiation capability with wire and ACH transmission — financial loss risk if compromised

data_sensitivity.rationale: Bank account balances, wire instructions, ACH batch files, and counterparty banking details

external_exposure.rationale: Corporate authentication required; no public-facing surface

access_model.value: Cognito RBAC with viewer/analyst/approver/admin roles; dual-approval required for all payment submissions
access_model.rationale: SOX segregation-of-duties requirement for payment initiation and approval

feature_scope.value: Multi-bank balance aggregation, cash position dashboard by currency and entity, liquidity forecasting, wire instruction builder, ACH batch creation with NACHA file preview, counterparty directory, dual-approval workflow, transaction history export
feature_scope.rationale: Full treasury operations capability set

mvp_scope.value: Balance aggregation from 3 bank connectors, consolidated cash dashboard, ACH batch submission with dual approval, counterparty directory
mvp_scope.rationale: Core functionality for pilot with 5 enterprise customers

security_baseline.value: AES-256 for credentials at rest, TLS 1.3 for backend comms, MFA for all users, 15-minute session timeout, payment actions require re-authentication
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data and payment initiation require strong access controls

frontend_stack.value: Electron 30 with React 18 and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform desktop with web technology and existing React team expertise

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Express and TypeScript
backend_stack.rationale: Consistent TypeScript stack, team familiarity, suitable for sync-heavy integration workloads

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ; Redis 7 for session state
data_platform.rationale: Relational model fits treasury ledger structures; Redis for lightweight session management

hosting_target.value: ECS Fargate for backend API; Electron app distributed via enterprise MDM
hosting_target.rationale: Backend on managed containers; desktop app distributed through IT-controlled channels

privacy_retention_policy.value: 7-year retention for all transaction records and payment logs per SOX; 90-day audit log hot storage
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOX financial record retention requirements

future_scope.value: FX spot and forward rate integration, automated sweep rules, real-time bank balance push via webhooks, ERP integration with NetSuite and SAP
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap for full treasury automation

constraints.value: Must run on Windows 10/11 and macOS 13+; no direct database access from Electron renderer; all bank credentials stored in OS keychain
constraints.rationale: Enterprise security policy and cross-platform support requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for backend services; structured JSON logging with correlation IDs; PagerDuty for payment submission failures and bank sync errors
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; payment failures require immediate on-call response

execution_preference.value: Agile sprints with 2-week iterations; monthly MDM releases
execution_preference.rationale: Enterprise release cadence aligned with IT deployment windows

compliance_context.value: NACHA Operating Rules for ACH batch files; Dodd-Frank Section 1073 for wire transfers; SOX controls for segregation of duties in payment approval
compliance_context.rationale: Payment initiation and financial recordkeeping are subject to financial regulations

project.goal: Consolidate treasury operations into a single desktop app replacing bank portals and Excel workflows
project.target_users: CFOs and treasury analysts at mid-market enterprises

frontend.state: Zustand for global state, React Query for server sync

security.encryption: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate for backend, electron-builder for packaging

open_questions.bank_connectors: Which banks require FTP vs API feeds for balance sync?

reasoner_reviews.product: MVP scope of 3-bank connectivity plus ACH dual-approval covers the core treasury pain point. The counterparty directory is essential for efficient payment initiation and reduces typo risk on wire beneficiary details.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Electron with a Node.js backend on Fargate is the right separation — the renderer process must communicate only through the Electron main process which proxies to the backend API, keeping credentials isolated in the OS keychain. contextIsolation and nodeIntegration=false are non-negotiable in the Electron config.
reasoner_reviews.security: Dual-approval for payment submissions is a hard SOX requirement. Payment actions must trigger a Cognito re-authentication challenge before the API transmits any NACHA or wire payload. The payment_audit_log table must be append-only with no delete permissions granted to any application role.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: SOX audit trails require immutable logging of every approval action with approver identity, timestamp, and payload hash. Budget supports Fargate pilot scale; MDM distribution adds no cloud cost.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Liquidity forecasting inputs are underspecified — clarify whether the 30-day projection uses only historical bank balance trends or also scheduled AP/AR flows from the ERP. This should be resolved before Phase 2 development.

backend.service_design: Express API with domain modules for bank-sync, payments, counterparties, approvals, and analytics. BankSyncService implements pluggable connector adapters per bank (Open Banking API, SFTP, bank-specific REST). DualApprovalEngine enforces two-party authorization server-side with Cognito group validation.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0. Resources: /bank-accounts, /cash-positions, /ach-batches, /wire-instructions, /counterparties, /approvals, /forecasts. Idempotency-Key header required on all payment submission endpoints.
backend.business_modules: NachaBuilderService generates and validates NACHA formatted ACH files with routing number checksum validation. LiquidityForecaster extrapolates 30-day cash runway from 90-day rolling balance history using linear regression. BankSyncScheduler runs every 15 minutes via node-cron.
backend.failure_handling: Bank sync failures retried with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts; failed syncs surface stale balance warning with last-successful-sync timestamp. Wire submission failures trigger immediate PagerDuty P1 and are never silently dropped — all failures written to payment_audit_log.

frontend.app_structure: Electron app with main/renderer process separation. Main process manages OS keychain via Keytar, Cognito token lifecycle, and IPC bridge to backend API. Renderer is a React 18 SPA with route-based code splitting.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Dashboard (consolidated cash positions by entity/currency with threshold alerts), Bank Accounts (sync status per connector), Cash Flow Forecast (30/60/90-day Recharts), ACH Batch Builder (NACHA preview before submit), Wire Instruction form, Counterparty Directory, Approval Queue, Transaction History with CSV export.
frontend.state_management: Zustand for UI state including entity filters and approval queue counts. React Query for server data with 60-second polling for balance updates. Payment forms use isolated local state to prevent accidental multi-submit.
frontend.ui_modules: CashPositionCard showing balance, 24h delta, and minimum threshold alert badge. NachaFilePreview rendering parsed batch summary. ApprovalBadge showing approver identity and timestamp. ForecastChart with Recharts and horizon toggle.

security.auth_design: Cognito user pools with MFA enforced. Electron main process holds session tokens; renderer never has direct token access. Payment endpoints trigger a secondary Cognito re-authentication challenge before API transmission.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: viewer (read-only), analyst (create drafts), approver (first approval), admin (second approval and submit). Cognito group membership enforces mutual exclusivity of approver and admin roles — no single user can hold both.
security.secrets_management: Bank API credentials in OS keychain via Keytar. Cognito tokens in Electron main process memory only — no disk persistence. Backend secrets in AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: payment_audit_log append-only table records every draft creation, approval, rejection, and transmission with user_id, ip_address, payload_hash. Row-level security prevents analyst/approver roles from deleting entries. CloudTrail for all AWS API actions.

data.entities: bank_accounts (id, institution_name, account_number_masked, currency, entity_id, connector_type ENUM open_banking/sftp/rest_api, last_synced_at). cash_positions (id, bank_account_id, balance NUMERIC(18,2), available_balance, snapshot_at). wire_instructions (id, counterparty_id, amount, currency, value_date, beneficiary_bank_swift, status ENUM draft/pending_approval/submitted/settled). ach_batches (id, effective_date, total_debit, total_credit, nacha_file_s3_key, status, approver1_id, approver2_id). counterparties (id, name, bank_name, routing_number_masked, account_number_masked, created_by). payment_audit_log (id, user_id, action, payment_type, payment_id, payload_hash, ip_address, timestamp).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with separate schemas for treasury_ops and audit_log. S3 for NACHA file archive with SSE-KMS. Redis 7 ElastiCache for session state only.
data.retention_and_deletion: 7-year retention for payment records and NACHA files per SOX. Audit log hot storage 90 days then archived to S3 Glacier. Cash position snapshots retained 2 years for trend analysis.

devops.deployment_topology: Backend API on ECS Fargate (2 AZs us-east-1a/1c), ALB in front. Electron app packaged via electron-builder into signed .exe and .dmg, distributed via enterprise MDM.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: TypeScript type-check, ESLint, Jest unit tests (80% coverage gate), integration tests against LocalStack, Docker build and ECR push, ECS rolling deployment. Electron pipeline: React bundle build, electron-builder packaging, Apple notarization and Windows Authenticode signing, publish to internal artifact registry.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM sidecar on Fargate tasks. Structured JSON logs with correlation IDs. PagerDuty P1 for payment failures, bank sync failures exceeding 30 minutes, and audit log write errors.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS service rollback to prior task definition on health check failure. MDM can revert to prior Electron installer version if critical bugs are reported post-release.

target_output.thinking_summary: The critical architectural decision for this treasury desktop app is enforcing the Electron process boundary — the renderer must never hold bank credentials or Cognito tokens directly, with all sensitive operations proxied through the Electron main process. Dual-approval for ACH/wire must be enforced server-side in the DualApprovalEngine, not just client-side, to satisfy SOX segregation of duties. NACHA file generation requires routing number validation before transmission since ACH files cannot be recalled.
target_output.title: Desktop Treasury Management Application Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: An Electron-based desktop treasury management application for mid-market CFOs and analysts, built with React 18 on the frontend and Node.js 22/Express on AWS ECS Fargate. The app aggregates real-time bank account balances from multiple institutions via Open Banking and SFTP adapters, enables dual-approved ACH batch and wire submissions with NACHA file generation, and projects 30-day liquidity from rolling balance history. All bank credentials reside in the OS keychain via Keytar; no secrets touch the Electron renderer process.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-layer architecture: Electron desktop client (React 18 renderer isolated from a Node.js main process) communicating over IPC to the main process, which proxies authenticated API calls to a Node.js/Express backend on ECS Fargate over TLS 1.3 with Cognito JWT auth. Bank balance data is synchronized via pluggable connector adapters (Open Banking API, SFTP for legacy banks) and persisted in PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ. DualApprovalEngine in the payment module enforces SOX segregation of duties — two distinct Cognito role holders must authorize before any NACHA or wire transmission. Datadog APM monitors backend services with PagerDuty escalation for payment failures.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 30, React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query, electron-builder, Keytar for OS keychain access. Backend: Node.js 22, Express, TypeScript, Sequelize ORM, nacha-node for NACHA file generation, OpenAPI 3.0. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 ElastiCache. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, S3 with SSE-KMS for NACHA files, Cognito user pools, AWS Secrets Manager, CloudTrail. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Apple notarization, Windows Authenticode signing, enterprise MDM for distribution.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Bank Account Sync connecting to Open Banking APIs and legacy SFTP feeds per configured connector type. Cash Position Dashboard aggregating balances by legal entity and currency with 24-hour delta indicators and configurable minimum threshold alerts. Liquidity Forecast with 30/60/90-day projected runway using 90-day rolling balance history. ACH Batch Builder with NACHA file preview, payee validation against counterparty directory, and dual-approval gate. Wire Instruction form with SWIFT/BIC validation and Cognito re-auth challenge. Counterparty Directory for managing approved payment recipients with verified bank details. Approval Queue for first and second approvers. Transaction History with date-range filtering and CSV export.
target_output.system_components: Electron Main Process managing OS keychain via Keytar, IPC bridge, Cognito token lifecycle, and desktop notification dispatch. React Renderer SPA with Zustand and React Query. Express API on ECS Fargate with BankSyncService, CashAggregatorService, NachaBuilderService, DualApprovalEngine, and LiquidityForecaster modules. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with treasury_ops and audit_log schemas. Redis 7 ElastiCache for session tokens. S3 bucket for NACHA file archive with SSE-KMS. ALB with WAF for API traffic. Cognito user pool with MFA. AWS Secrets Manager for bank API credentials.
target_output.workflows: Balance Sync: BankSyncScheduler fires every 15 minutes, calling each bank connector adapter in parallel, normalizing responses to the canonical cash_positions schema, and upserting with snapshot_at timestamp. Stale positions older than 45 minutes render a warning badge in the dashboard showing last-successful-sync time. ACH Batch Submission: Analyst creates batch by selecting counterparties from the directory, NachaBuilderService validates routing numbers and generates a NACHA file preview rendered in-app; analyst submits for first approval, approver1 reviews and approves triggering push notification to approver2, approver2 confirms and the signed NACHA file is transmitted to the bank connector — every step appended to payment_audit_log with payload_hash and approver identities.
target_output.data_model: bank_accounts (id UUID, institution_name, account_number_masked VARCHAR(8), currency CHAR(3), entity_id FK, connector_type ENUM open_banking/sftp/rest_api, last_synced_at TIMESTAMPTZ). cash_positions (id UUID, bank_account_id FK, balance NUMERIC(18,2), available_balance NUMERIC(18,2), snapshot_at TIMESTAMPTZ). wire_instructions (id UUID, counterparty_id FK, amount NUMERIC(18,2), currency, value_date DATE, beneficiary_bank_swift VARCHAR(11), status ENUM draft/pending_approval/submitted/settled). ach_batches (id UUID, effective_date DATE, total_debit, total_credit, nacha_file_s3_key, status ENUM draft/pending_approval1/pending_approval2/transmitted/settled, approver1_id, approver2_id). counterparties (id UUID, name, bank_name, routing_number_masked, account_number_masked). payment_audit_log (id UUID, user_id, action, payment_type, payment_id, payload_hash, ip_address, timestamp) — append-only.
target_output.api_design: RESTful at /api/v1. GET /bank-accounts (list with sync status). POST /sync/bank-accounts/:id (trigger manual sync). GET /cash-positions?entity_id=&date= (consolidated positions). POST /ach-batches (create draft). GET /ach-batches/:id/preview (NACHA file preview text). POST /ach-batches/:id/approvals (submit approval with re-auth token, role validated). POST /wire-instructions (create draft). PATCH /wire-instructions/:id/submit (submit for approval). GET /counterparties?search= (directory). GET /forecasts?entity_id=&horizon=30 (liquidity projection). All write endpoints require Idempotency-Key header. Auth: Cognito JWT validated at ALB and Express middleware.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOX segregation of duties enforced server-side: approver and admin Cognito groups are mutually exclusive; DualApprovalEngine rejects submissions where approver1_id equals approver2_id. Cognito re-authentication challenge required before any payment submission endpoint processes the request. Bank credentials in OS keychain via Keytar — never persisted to disk or transmitted to backend. NACHA files in S3 with SSE-KMS and block-all-public-access policy. WAF rate-limiting payment endpoints to 10 requests per minute per user. payment_audit_log append-only with row-level security preventing analyst and approver roles from deleting entries. CloudTrail logging all AWS API actions. 90-day audit log hot retention then Glacier archival.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline: TypeScript type-check, ESLint, Jest unit tests with 80% coverage gate, integration tests against LocalStack, Docker build and ECR push, ECS rolling deployment with health check gate before traffic shift. Electron build pipeline: React bundle build, electron-builder packaging, Apple notarization via notarytool, Windows Authenticode signing, upload to internal artifact registry, MDM push to enrolled devices. Environments: dev (single Fargate task), staging (multi-AZ mirror with test bank sandbox connectors), production (minimum 2 tasks, us-east-1a/1c, auto-scaling 2-4 on CPU 70% threshold). RDS Multi-AZ with automated failover. Secrets Manager rotation every 30 days.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM sidecar on each Fargate task tracing bank sync calls, payment API latency, and database query times. Structured JSON logs with correlation IDs linking Electron client requests to backend traces. Custom metrics: bank_sync_success_rate per connector, ach_batch_submission_latency_p99, approval_queue_depth, payment_audit_log_write_error_count. PagerDuty P1 alerts for payment submission failure, bank sync failure exceeding 30 minutes, audit log write error, and RDS failover events.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Pilot at 5 customers: ~$800/month (2 Fargate tasks $180, RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium $200, Redis cache.t3.micro $50, ALB $30, S3/CloudWatch/Secrets Manager $50). Electron MDM distribution adds no per-seat cloud cost. At 50 customers, scale Fargate to 4 tasks and upgrade to db.t3.large (~$1,400/month). Bank sync is the primary scaling driver — add connector workers as institution count grows.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): 3 bank connector adapters (2 Open Banking, 1 SFTP), cash position dashboard, ACH batch builder with NachaBuilderService and dual approval, counterparty directory, Cognito auth with MFA, SOX append-only audit logging, Electron packaging and MDM distribution pipeline. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Wire instruction module with SWIFT validation, 30/60/90-day liquidity forecasting charts, additional bank connectors, transaction history export. Phase 3 (6 weeks): FX rate integration via provider API, automated sweep rules engine, NetSuite/SAP ERP webhook integration, cash flow variance analytics.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for both Electron and Express codebases. ESLint and Prettier enforced in CI. No direct database queries from Electron renderer — all data access through Express API via IPC bridge. Payment-related PRs require two senior engineer approvals. Jest unit tests required for NachaBuilderService, DualApprovalEngine, and all bank connector adapters. Integration tests run against LocalStack for AWS service mocking.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Bank connector fragility — FTP-based bank feeds break when banks change file formats. Mitigation: version-pinned parser configs with schema drift alerting. Risk: Electron renderer XSS enabling IPC abuse. Mitigation: contextIsolation enabled, nodeIntegration disabled, strict CSP in BrowserWindow config. Tradeoff: Express over NestJS for faster initial development, requiring manual modular discipline. Tradeoff: RDS Multi-AZ over Aurora at pilot scale to reduce cost — Aurora migration path documented in ADR for production scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed OS keychain via Keytar for bank credentials over Secrets Manager — credentials must remain on device for offline scenarios. Selected nacha-node library for NACHA file generation after evaluating 3 alternatives. Dual-approval must be enforced server-side. FTP adapter prioritized for legacy banks without Open Banking API availability.

metadata.notes: Desktop treasury app with Electron/Node.js, SOX dual-approval enforcement, and multi-bank balance aggregation.

project_goal.value: Mobile expense management app for corporate cardholders that captures receipts via OCR, categorizes spend against company policy, and routes out-of-policy transactions to manager approval
project_goal.rationale: Replacing manual expense reporting process for 5,000+ employee enterprises

target_users.value: Corporate cardholders and their managers at enterprises with 5,000+ employees
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in enterprise discovery sessions

project_class.rationale: Receipt capture requires native camera access; iOS and Android required

project.goal: Automate corporate expense capture, categorization, and approval for enterprise cardholders

open_questions.ocr_fallback: What is the manual fallback when Textract confidence is below threshold?

reasoner_reviews.product: The OCR-to-categorization pipeline is the core value proposition. Auto-categorization accuracy directly affects adoption — cardholder corrections should be fed back as training signals to improve the classifier over time.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Django DRF with Celery is solid for this workload. The OCR job queue must be isolated from the API worker pool to prevent receipt processing spikes from impacting API latency. Use separate Celery queues for ocr_processing and notification_dispatch.
reasoner_reviews.security: Receipt images contain sensitive merchant and amount data — S3 presigned URLs with 15-minute expiry for image upload/download, never public URLs. GDPR right-to-erasure must cascade to S3 receipt images as well as PostgreSQL records.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Jenkins to GitHub Actions migration is a parallel concern — do not block MVP behind CI/CD migration. Run both pipelines in parallel during transition and cut over after stabilization.

frontend.app_structure: React Native app with feature-based module structure: receipt-capture, expense-list, approval-queue, analytics, settings. Expo managed workflow for camera and image picker access.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home dashboard (pending expenses, approval actions), Receipt Capture (camera or gallery picker), Expense Review screen (OCR extracted fields editable), Expense List with filter by status/date, Approval Queue for managers, Expense Report export, Analytics (spend by category, policy compliance rate).
frontend.state_management: Redux Toolkit with RTK Query for API state. Local state for camera and form flows. Push notifications via Expo Notifications for approval requests.
frontend.frontend_risks: OCR latency (Textract p99 can exceed 3 seconds) requires optimistic UI update showing 'processing' state before results arrive.

backend.service_design: Django DRF monolith with domain apps: receipts, expenses, approvals, policy, analytics, integrations. Celery workers for OCR processing and notification dispatch on separate queues.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with DRF serializers and OpenAPI 3.0 via drf-spectacular. Key endpoints: /receipts (upload), /expenses (CRUD), /approvals (queue management), /exports (SAP CSV).
backend.business_modules: ReceiptOCRService calls AWS Textract, parses merchant/amount/date from AnalyzeExpense response. PolicyEngine evaluates extracted category against company policy rules stored as JSON config. ApprovalRouter determines the approving manager from the employee org chart via Okta Groups API.
backend.llm_integration_design: GPT-4o via Bedrock invoked async via Celery when PolicyEngine flags a violation — generates a natural-language explanation of the policy rule for the manager approval notification. Textract is the primary extraction layer; GPT-4o only handles explanation generation, not extraction.

security.auth_design: Okta SAML SSO with DRF social-auth. Mobile app uses Okta OIDC SDK for token acquisition. All API calls authenticated via JWT from Okta. MFA enforced at Okta policy level.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: cardholder (own expenses only), manager (team expenses and approval queue), finance_admin (all expenses and exports), system_admin (policy config and user management). Django permission classes enforce role checks.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR erasure endpoint cascades deletion to PostgreSQL records and S3 receipt images via S3 Object Expiry policy update. EU employee data tagged with gdpr_subject=true for targeted erasure jobs.

data.entities: expense_reports (id, cardholder_id, status ENUM draft/submitted/approved/rejected/exported, submitted_at, total_amount). receipts (id, expense_report_id, s3_key, ocr_status, extracted_merchant, extracted_amount, extracted_date, confidence_score, uploaded_at). spend_categories (id, name, parent_id, policy_limit, policy_currency). approval_workflows (id, expense_report_id, approver_id, action ENUM pending/approved/rejected, comment, actioned_at). policy_rules (id, category_id, rule_type, threshold_amount, currency, enforcement ENUM warn/block). cost_centers (id, name, gl_code, manager_id).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ. S3 private bucket for receipt images with SSE-S3. Presigned URLs for upload and download with 15-minute expiry. Redis 7 for Celery broker and API response caching.
data.analytics_events: expense_submitted, receipt_ocr_completed, policy_violation_flagged, approval_actioned, report_exported tracked to Datadog custom metrics for spend compliance dashboards.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: separate task definitions for API workers and Celery workers. API behind ALB. Celery OCR queue and notification queue on separate worker pools.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions replacing Jenkins: Python lint (ruff), type check (mypy), pytest with 80% coverage gate, Docker build and ECR push, ECS rolling deployment. React Native: Expo EAS Build for iOS and Android, distributed via MDM.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM tracing Django requests and Celery tasks. Custom metrics: ocr_processing_time_p99, approval_queue_depth, policy_violation_rate. PagerDuty for OCR error rate above 5% and approval queue depth exceeding 500.
