reasoner_reviews.product: The static generation approach is well-suited for status page traffic patterns — millions of reads, rare writes. The 90-day uptime chart requirement is achievable by baking historical data into the build artifact. Subscriber notifications must be handled by the upstream incident system, not this site.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro with island architecture keeps JavaScript payload minimal — only the uptime chart component needs client-side hydration. CloudFront's cache invalidation on targeted paths avoids full distribution invalidation costs. The webhook-to-rebuild pipeline must be idempotent to handle duplicate triggers from the incident system.
reasoner_reviews.security: No user data is collected or stored, which eliminates the largest attack surface class. S3 bucket must remain private with OAC; direct S3 URLs must be blocked. CloudFront response headers policy should enforce CSP with no inline scripts to prevent any injected content from executing.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Sub-2-minute publish SLA is achievable: GitHub Actions cold start ~20s, Astro build for a status page ~30s, S3 upload ~10s, CloudFront path invalidation ~60s = ~2min total. Caching invalidation costs are negligible at the expected update frequency of 2-5 incidents per month.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Previous plan rounds addressed cache invalidation strategy but lacked specificity on Astro content collection schema for the rail status data model. The build-time data contract with the upstream incident API must be explicitly versioned to prevent silent breakage.

backend.service_design: No backend service — all content generated at Astro build time. GitHub Actions acts as the orchestration layer: fetch upstream incident API, write JSON data files to Astro content collections, trigger build.
backend.api_patterns: Upstream incident management API consumed at build time via authenticated fetch in GitHub Actions step. API response schema versioned and validated against a JSON Schema before build proceeds.
backend.background_jobs: GitHub Actions scheduled workflow runs every 15 minutes as a fallback to catch missed webhooks. Idempotent build: if data unchanged, skip S3 upload and CloudFront invalidation.
backend.backend_risks: Upstream API unavailability blocks rebuild. Mitigation: cache last successful API response in S3 data-cache bucket; use cached data if API call fails, with a staleness warning injected into the build.

security.auth_design: No user authentication. Webhook receiver endpoint (AWS Lambda URL) validates HMAC-SHA256 signature from incident system before triggering GitHub Actions workflow dispatch.
security.secrets_management: GitHub Actions secrets store: INCIDENT_API_KEY, GITHUB_DISPATCH_TOKEN, WEBHOOK_SECRET. S3 deployment credentials via OIDC role — no long-lived keys.
security.abuse_prevention: CloudFront WAF with rate limiting on all paths (1000 req/min per IP). Webhook Lambda validates origin signature; invalid requests return 401 with no information disclosure.
security.security_risks: Risk: stale status displayed if rebuild pipeline fails silently. Mitigation: CloudWatch alarm on GitHub Actions failure + PagerDuty integration for ops team.

data.entities: payment_rails (id, name ENUM card/ach/sepa/swift, current_status ENUM operational/degraded/partial_outage/major_outage, last_updated), incidents (id, rail_ids[], title, status ENUM investigating/identified/monitoring/resolved, started_at, resolved_at, updates[]), maintenance_windows (id, rail_ids[], title, scheduled_start, scheduled_end, description), uptime_records (rail_id, date, uptime_pct, incident_count)
data.storage_design: All data stored as JSON files in Astro content collections, generated at build time. No database. S3 data-cache bucket holds last-known-good API response for fallback builds.
data.retention_and_deletion: Build artifacts retained in S3 for 30 days. CloudFront access logs retained 30 days. No user data to retain or delete.
data.data_risks: Risk: upstream API schema change breaks build. Mitigation: JSON Schema validation step in CI with explicit error reporting; schema version header checked before parsing.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions pipeline: webhook receipt via Lambda → GitHub repository_dispatch event → Actions workflow → Astro build → S3 sync (us-east-1 + eu-west-1 buckets) → CloudFront path invalidation on /index.html and /api/status.json.
devops.environments: staging: S3 staging bucket + CloudFront staging distribution (accessible via staging.status.paynet.internal). production: S3 prod bucket + CloudFront prod distribution (status.paynet.com).
devops.ci_cd_design: Two workflows: (1) webhook-triggered rebuild for incident updates, (2) scheduled every-15-min heartbeat rebuild. PR merges to main trigger full staging deploy + Lighthouse CI check. Production promotion is manual approval gate via GitHub Environments.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch dashboard: CloudFront request rate, cache hit ratio, 4xx/5xx rates. GitHub Actions: workflow success/failure notifications to Slack #status-page-alerts. Synthetic CloudWatch canary hits the page every 5 minutes and alerts on non-200.
devops.rollback_strategy: S3 versioning enabled on prod bucket. Rollback: reactivate previous object version and invalidate CloudFront cache. Executed via GitHub Actions workflow with manual input for target version ID.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: CloudFront propagation delay exceeds 2-min SLA during high-invalidation periods. Mitigation: invalidate only changed paths (/index.html, /incidents/[id].html) rather than wildcard invalidation.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 static site with client-side polling islands for real-time rail status; incident timeline and historical uptime fetched from public JSON API
frontend.pages_and_flows: Current Status Overview (rail indicators), Incident Timeline, Historical Uptime Graphs, Subscribe to Alerts
frontend.performance_notes: Status indicators poll public API every 30 s via SWR-style fetch; static shell loads instantly; progressive enhancement for no-JS clients
frontend.accessibility_notes: Status changes announced via aria-live regions; status colors always paired with text labels for color-blind users

ISS-001.title: Astro content collection schema not versioned against upstream incident API
ISS-001.detail: Build-time data fetch from incident management API has no schema version contract. An upstream API change could silently produce malformed status data that passes the build but displays incorrect rail statuses.

history.note: Identified in round 2 audit — no schema validation in CI pipeline.
Round 3 plan added JSON Schema mention but no enforcement mechanism specified.

ISS-002.title: Fallback build uses stale data without visible staleness indicator
ISS-002.detail: When upstream API is unavailable, the build falls back to cached S3 data but the published page shows no indication of data staleness, misleading customers about rail status accuracy.

history.note: Introduced in round 3 after fallback caching was added without staleness UX.

focus_issues.title: Astro content collection schema not versioned against upstream incident API
focus_issues.detail: No JSON Schema enforcement in CI; upstream API schema change could silently produce incorrect rail status display.

focus_issues.title: Fallback build uses stale data without visible staleness indicator
focus_issues.detail: Fallback to cached S3 data proceeds without injecting staleness warning into published page.

latest_recommendations: Add explicit JSON Schema validation step in GitHub Actions before Astro build; fail the workflow on schema mismatch and alert ops.
Inject a data-freshness timestamp and staleness banner into the Astro build when using fallback cached data.
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Payment Network Status Page — Static Delivery Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Two issues unresolved across 3 rounds; schema validation gap is a blocking concern.

previous_audits.summary: Strong static architecture with well-designed CDN topology. Two recurring issues around schema validation and fallback staleness UX remain unaddressed.
strengths: CloudFront dual-region setup is correct for the traffic profile
Webhook HMAC validation is properly specified
S3 fallback caching idea is sound in principle
concerns: JSON Schema validation described but not wired into CI workflow steps
Fallback build path produces a page indistinguishable from a fresh build
blocking_issues: ISS-001: No enforcement mechanism for schema version contract with upstream API
recommendations: Add ajv-based JSON Schema validation as a discrete GitHub Actions step before astro build; exit 1 on failure and trigger PagerDuty.
Inject a <DataStalenessAlert> Astro component when BUILD_DATA_SOURCE=cache environment variable is set during fallback builds.

issue_updates.title: Schema not versioned
issue_updates.detail: Round 3 plan described schema validation in prose but no CI workflow step was specified.

issue_updates.title: Stale data not surfaced
issue_updates.detail: Fallback path added in round 3 with no staleness UX specification.

previous_plan.title: Payment Network Status Page — Static Delivery Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Astro 4 static site on CloudFront + S3 serving real-time payment rail status for card, ACH, SEPA, and SWIFT with webhook-triggered rebuilds achieving sub-2-minute publish latency.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Webhook from incident system triggers GitHub Actions, which fetches upstream API data, runs Astro build, syncs to dual-region S3 buckets, and invalidates CloudFront targeted paths.
previous_plan.system_components: Astro 4 build runtime, GitHub Actions webhook receiver, S3 buckets (us-east-1 + eu-west-1), CloudFront distribution with OAC, Lambda webhook validator.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: S3 OAC private bucket, CloudFront HTTPS + CSP headers, HMAC-SHA256 webhook validation, WAF rate limiting.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 revision targeting two persistent issues: (1) add ajv-based JSON Schema enforcement as a hard CI gate before the Astro build step, and (2) inject a DataStalenessAlert component into the Astro build output when using fallback cached data. Both issues are straightforward to resolve in the CI pipeline and Astro component layer without changing the overall static architecture. The schema validation step should fail the workflow and trigger ops alerting on schema mismatch; the staleness banner should display data-freshness timestamp and a yellow advisory when BUILD_DATA_SOURCE=cache.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit ajv@8 JSON Schema validation as GitHub Actions step 'validate-api-schema' that runs before 'astro-build'. Schema file committed at src/data/schemas/incident-api-v2.schema.json. Workflow exits with code 1 and fires PagerDuty event on validation failure, blocking the build.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Any upstream API schema change will fail the workflow before producing a build, preventing silent malformed status display.

fix_report.action_taken: Added DataStalenessAlert Astro component rendered at top of layout when BUILD_DATA_SOURCE env var equals 'cache'. Component displays data-freshness timestamp from cached file metadata and a yellow advisory: 'Status data may be up to X minutes old. We are investigating data feed connectivity.' Fallback build sets BUILD_DATA_SOURCE=cache via GitHub Actions env step.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Customers viewing a fallback-built page see clear staleness indication with timestamp, preventing misleading operational status display.

target_output.title: Payment Network Status Page — Static Delivery Architecture v4
target_output.executive_summary: Astro 4 static site served via dual-region CloudFront + S3 delivering real-time card, ACH, SEPA, and SWIFT rail status to millions of monthly page views with zero backend infrastructure. Webhook-triggered GitHub Actions pipeline achieves sub-2-minute publish latency. Round 4 resolves schema validation enforcement (ISS-001) with a hard ajv CI gate and staleness transparency (ISS-002) with a DataStalenessAlert component injected during fallback builds.
target_output.architecture_overview: Incident management system fires a webhook to an AWS Lambda URL that validates the HMAC-SHA256 signature and dispatches a repository_dispatch event to GitHub Actions. The CI workflow fetches the upstream incident API, runs ajv schema validation against incident-api-v2.schema.json (failing hard on mismatch), writes validated data to Astro content collections, executes the Astro static build, syncs output to S3 buckets in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 via aws s3 sync --delete, and invalidates targeted CloudFront paths. When the upstream API is unreachable, the workflow falls back to the S3 data-cache bucket, sets BUILD_DATA_SOURCE=cache, and the Astro layout renders the DataStalenessAlert component. Total pipeline duration from webhook receipt to live page: under 2 minutes.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4.x with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Chart.js (island-hydrated for uptime sparklines). Build validation: ajv@8 with JSON Schema Draft-07 for incident API response validation. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with OIDC S3 deployment role. Hosting: CloudFront (price class PriceClass_100) with dual-origin S3 buckets (us-east-1 primary, eu-west-1 replica). Security: CloudFront response headers policy (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options), WAF WebACL with rate-based rule 1000 req/5min per IP. Monitoring: AWS CloudWatch, GitHub Actions Slack notifications.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Rail Status Grid: four cards (Card, ACH, SEPA, SWIFT) each displaying current operational status badge (operational/degraded/partial_outage/major_outage) and last-updated timestamp, color-coded green/yellow/orange/red. Incident Timeline: chronological list of active and recent incidents per rail with status progression (investigating → identified → monitoring → resolved) and timestamped update entries. Historical Uptime Charts: 90-day sparkline per rail showing daily uptime percentage, built with Chart.js as a hydrated island. Maintenance Calendar: upcoming scheduled maintenance windows with affected rails, scheduled times, and expected duration. DataStalenessAlert Banner: yellow advisory component displayed when build used fallback cached data, showing data-freshness timestamp and ops contact link.
target_output.system_components: Lambda webhook receiver (Node.js 22, validates HMAC-SHA256, triggers GitHub repository_dispatch). GitHub Actions workflows: webhook-rebuild.yml (event-driven) and heartbeat-rebuild.yml (cron every 15 min). ajv schema validator step consuming src/data/schemas/incident-api-v2.schema.json. Astro 4 build runtime producing static HTML/CSS/JS to dist/. S3 data-cache bucket (us-east-1) storing last-good-api-response.json with object metadata timestamp. S3 deployment buckets (us-east-1 primary, eu-west-1 replica). CloudFront distribution with two origins and OAC. CloudWatch canary synthetic monitor hitting status.paynet.com every 5 minutes.
target_output.workflows: Incident Rebuild Workflow: Incident system POSTs webhook to Lambda URL → Lambda validates HMAC-SHA256 signature (invalid: 401 + log) → Lambda calls GitHub API POST /repos/paynet/statuspage/dispatches with event type 'incident-update' → Actions workflow checkout → fetch incident API (timeout 10s) → ajv validate response (fail: exit 1 + PagerDuty P2 alert) → write validated JSON to src/data/rails.json and src/data/incidents.json → astro build → aws s3 sync dist/ to prod buckets → CloudFront invalidation on /index.html + /incidents/*.html → done in ~110s. Fallback Rebuild Workflow: Upstream API fetch fails → workflow downloads last-good-api-response.json from S3 data-cache bucket → sets env BUILD_DATA_SOURCE=cache and DATA_FRESHNESS_TS from object LastModified metadata → proceeds with Astro build → DataStalenessAlert component rendered in layout → deploy proceeds normally. Heartbeat Rebuild: Scheduled cron every 15 min runs same workflow; skips S3 upload and CloudFront invalidation if content hash unchanged (md5 of dist/index.html compared to S3 ETag).
target_output.data_model: payment_rails (id ENUM card/ach/sepa/swift, display_name, current_status ENUM operational/degraded/partial_outage/major_outage, last_updated ISO8601). incidents (id, affected_rail_ids string[], title, impact ENUM minor/major/critical, status ENUM investigating/identified/monitoring/resolved, started_at, resolved_at, update_entries: [{timestamp, message, status}]). maintenance_windows (id, affected_rail_ids string[], title, scheduled_start, scheduled_end, description, reminder_sent bool). uptime_records (rail_id, date, uptime_pct float, incident_minutes int). All fields populated from upstream incident API JSON at build time; no database. Schema validated against incident-api-v2.schema.json before consumption.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Static JSON artifacts served via CloudFront: GET /api/status.json (current rail statuses and active incidents, cache-control max-age=60), GET /api/uptime/[rail].json (90-day uptime history per rail, cache-control max-age=3600). These files are generated by Astro's static build and invalidated on each rebuild. External webhook endpoint: POST https://hooks.paynet.com/statuspage (Lambda URL, HMAC-SHA256 validated) — not a public API, internal ops use only.
target_output.security_and_compliance: S3 buckets private with CloudFront Origin Access Control; direct S3 URLs blocked via bucket policy. CloudFront response headers policy: Content-Security-Policy (default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; no inline scripts), Strict-Transport-Security max-age=31536000 includeSubDomains, X-Frame-Options DENY, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff. WAF WebACL on CloudFront distribution with rate-based rule (1000 requests per 5 minutes per IP). Webhook Lambda: HMAC-SHA256 signature validation using WEBHOOK_SECRET from Secrets Manager; invalid requests return 401 with empty body. GitHub Actions: OIDC role for S3 deployment — no long-lived AWS credentials in secrets. All secrets (INCIDENT_API_KEY, WEBHOOK_SECRET) in GitHub Actions encrypted secrets.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions OIDC authentication to AWS role arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/statuspage-deploy with s3:PutObject, s3:DeleteObject, cloudfront:CreateInvalidation permissions. Staging deploy: PR merge to main → Astro build → sync to s3://paynet-statuspage-staging → invalidate staging CloudFront → Lighthouse CI check (performance score >90, accessibility score 100). Production deploy: manual approval via GitHub Environments 'production' gate → sync to s3://paynet-statuspage-prod (us-east-1) + s3://paynet-statuspage-prod-eu (eu-west-1) → CloudFront invalidation on targeted paths. Rollback: GitHub Actions workflow 'rollback.yml' accepts S3 version ID input, restores object version, invalidates CloudFront. S3 versioning enabled on both prod buckets.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch dashboard 'StatusPage-Prod': CloudFront TotalRequests, BytesDownloaded, 4xxErrorRate, 5xxErrorRate, CacheHitRate (target >95%). CloudWatch canary 'statuspage-availability' runs every 5 min, asserts HTTP 200 and presence of rail status JSON in page source. GitHub Actions: webhook-rebuild.yml Slack notification to #status-page-alerts on success and failure. PagerDuty integration: CloudWatch alarm on 5xxErrorRate >0.1% → P2 alert; ajv schema validation failure → P2 alert; canary failure 2 consecutive checks → P1 alert.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: CloudFront scales to unlimited page views by design; no compute scaling required. Estimated cost at 5M monthly page views: CloudFront data transfer ~$85, S3 storage ~$5, Lambda webhook receiver ~$0 (well within free tier at 2-5 invocations/month), CloudWatch canary ~$0.30. Total ~$90/month. CloudFront invalidation budget: 1000 free invalidations/month; at 2-5 incidents/month with targeted path invalidation (~3 paths per incident), actual invalidation cost is $0. S3 Intelligent-Tiering applied to historical build artifacts.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Core Status Page (4 weeks): Astro project scaffold with rail status grid, incident timeline, and uptime charts. GitHub Actions webhook-triggered rebuild pipeline with HMAC validation. S3 + CloudFront infrastructure via Terraform. ajv schema validation CI step (resolves ISS-001). DataStalenessAlert component for fallback builds (resolves ISS-002). Phase 2 — Subscriber Notifications (3 weeks): Email subscription form (handled by third-party Mailchimp integration; no backend needed). Webhook subscriber registration forwarded to upstream incident system API. RSS/Atom feed generation in Astro build. Phase 3 — Embeddable Badge and Analytics (2 weeks): SVG status badge endpoint (static file updated per build). Cloudfront access log analysis via Athena for monthly traffic reporting. Embeddable iframe widget for partner documentation sites.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro TypeScript strict mode. ESLint with astro/recommended and @typescript-eslint/recommended. Tailwind CSS purge enabled. Chart.js loaded only as Astro client:idle island to avoid blocking LCP. JSON Schema for incident API stored in src/data/schemas/ and version-controlled; any upstream API contract change requires schema file update and PR review before deploy. Lighthouse CI enforced in PR checks: performance ≥90, accessibility 100, best-practices ≥90. No third-party scripts added without security review (CSP would need updating).
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GitHub Actions is a dependency in the publish pipeline; Actions outage could delay incident status updates. Mitigation: heartbeat cron as fallback, and incident system can push updates to a separate plain-text S3 file as last-resort override. Risk: Astro major version upgrade could break content collection API. Mitigation: pin to Astro 4.x minor, test upgrades in staging branch. Tradeoff: static generation vs. real-time API means status page is eventually consistent (up to 2 min lag); this is acceptable per the SLA and avoids backend infrastructure complexity. Tradeoff: dual-region S3 replication adds $5/month but provides availability if us-east-1 S3 has a regional issue.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed no server-side runtime; all subscriber notification logic delegated to upstream incident management system. Confirmed CloudFront price class PriceClass_100 (US, Canada, Europe) covers the primary customer geography. Confirmed S3 versioning over CodePipeline artifact store for simpler rollback model. Schema validation hard-fail chosen over soft-warning to prevent silent data corruption on the status page.

metadata.notes: Round 4 static website status page; resolves ISS-001 (schema validation CI gate) and ISS-002 (staleness banner) for payment network rail status display.

profile.domain: Fintech Data Engineering — Interchange Fee Pipeline

project_goal.value: Replace costly third-party ETL vendor with an in-house pipeline that ingests raw transaction logs from on-premise Oracle, applies interchange fee classification rules using card BIN lookup tables, and loads enriched records into Snowflake for finance reporting.
project_goal.rationale: Vendor ETL costs $180K/year; in-house solution on spot instances projected at $22K/year.

target_users.value: Finance analysts consuming Tableau dashboards, data engineering team operating the pipeline, internal compliance auditors
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified in stakeholder discovery.

project_class.rationale: Batch ETL pipeline with no frontend; pure data engineering.

capabilities.rationale: Data transformation, Oracle integration, and nightly batch processing.

complexity_level.rationale: BIN lookup classification logic adds domain complexity over a simple load; Airflow DAG orchestration is well-understood.

risk_level.rationale: Financial transaction data with SOC 2 obligations; internal-only exposure reduces external attack surface.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Processes raw transaction records including merchant amounts and card BINs.

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs entirely within the corporate network; no public endpoints.

access_model.value: RBAC via Snowflake role-based access: ANALYST role (read-only reporting views), PIPELINE role (write to raw and enriched schemas), ADMIN role (DDL and user management)
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege access matching SOC 2 access control requirements.

feature_scope.value: Nightly Oracle JDBC extract, Python BIN classification engine, Snowflake idempotent upsert, dbt models for fee summaries and variance reporting, data quality checks with Great Expectations, load manifests for reconciliation
feature_scope.rationale: Full replacement feature set for vendor ETL.

mvp_scope.value: Oracle extract, BIN classification, Snowflake load, basic dbt fee summary models, data quality checks on row counts and null rates
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for finance team validation before vendor contract termination.

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all data in transit, Snowflake column-level encryption for card BIN and transaction amounts, MWAA secrets in AWS Secrets Manager, Oracle connection via VPN tunnel
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data handling baseline per InfoSec policy.

frontend_stack.value: None — no frontend; pipeline outputs to Snowflake consumed by existing Tableau Server
frontend_stack.rationale: Data pipeline project class; no UI component.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12, Apache Airflow 2.9 on MWAA, dbt Core 1.8, Great Expectations 0.18
backend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; dbt for SQL transformation layer, Airflow for orchestration, GE for data quality.

data_platform.value: Snowflake (Enterprise tier, us-east-1), Oracle 19c on-premise as source, S3 staging bucket for raw extract files
data_platform.rationale: Snowflake chosen for elastic compute separation and Tableau native connector.

hosting_target.value: AWS MWAA for Airflow orchestration, EC2 Spot Instances (r6i.2xlarge) for Python classification workers, S3 for staging
hosting_target.rationale: Spot instances reduce compute cost by 70% for batch workloads tolerant of interruption.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Transaction records retained 7 years in Snowflake per financial data retention policy; raw Oracle extract files in S3 deleted after successful load validation; PAN data never stored
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial regulatory retention obligations; PAN exclusion per PCI scope reduction.

future_scope.value: Real-time interchange fee alerting for anomalous BIN categories, merchant-level fee attribution, integration with GL system for automated journal entries
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 analytics features post-vendor cutover.

constraints.value: Must complete nightly run within 4-hour batch window (00:00-04:00 UTC); must handle 5M transactions per day; EC2 Spot interruptions must not cause data loss or double-loads; total AWS cost under $2K/month
constraints.rationale: Finance SLA for morning dashboard availability and budget cap.

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for pipeline metrics, Airflow built-in task logging to CloudWatch Logs, Great Expectations data quality results to Snowflake meta schema, PagerDuty for SLA breach alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing corporate monitoring stack.

execution_preference.value: Weekly sprint cadence with finance team sign-off on dbt model outputs before production promotion
execution_preference.rationale: Finance team validation gate required before cutover.

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II scope: pipeline data access controls, change management for transformation logic, audit logging of all Snowflake writes. Financial data retention: 7-year policy. PCI scope reduction: no PAN data stored.
compliance_context.rationale: SOC 2 obligation and financial data regulation trigger compliance context requirement.

project.goal: In-house interchange fee ETL replacing $180K/year vendor tool
project.target_users: Finance analysts, data engineering, compliance auditors

security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for Oracle credentials and Snowflake keypair

data.source: Oracle 19c on-premise via JDBC over VPN
data.staging: S3 staging bucket for extract files

reasoner_reviews.product: The cost reduction case is compelling — replacing $180K annual vendor spend with an in-house solution projected at $22K is a strong ROI. The MVP scope (Oracle extract → classify → Snowflake load → dbt fee summaries) is appropriately narrow for finance team validation. The 4-hour batch window is achievable for 5M daily transactions with spot instance parallelism.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The architecture should separate concerns cleanly: Airflow for orchestration only (no data processing in DAG tasks), Python classification workers on EC2 Spot as separate processing units with S3 as the handoff, and dbt handling all SQL transformation logic in Snowflake. The BIN classification engine is the most complex piece — it must be unit-tested independently of the Airflow layer. Idempotent Snowflake upserts using MERGE with load manifest IDs prevent double-loads on spot interruptions.
reasoner_reviews.security: SOC 2 Type II requires audit logging for all Snowflake writes, change management process for dbt model changes, and access control documentation. The Oracle connection over VPN is the highest-risk integration point — credentials must be rotated on a schedule and stored in Secrets Manager, not in Airflow variables. Snowflake column-level encryption for BIN values limits exposure if read permissions are misconfigured.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: EC2 Spot interruption handling is critical for the 4-hour SLA. The classification worker must checkpoint progress to S3 every 100K records. At Spot interruption, Airflow detects task failure and reschedules the task to resume from the last S3 checkpoint, avoiding reprocessing the full day's extract. At 5M records on r6i.2xlarge (8 vCPUs), parallel processing across 4 workers completes classification in ~45 minutes.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The data quality check strategy needs specificity. 'Row count and null rate checks' is insufficient for financial data — need cardinality checks on BIN codes, referential integrity between transaction_records and interchange_categories, and threshold alerts for fee calculation variance vs. prior 7-day average. Great Expectations expectations suites should be version-controlled alongside dbt models.

backend.service_design: Three-stage pipeline: (1) Extract DAG task: Python JDBC connection to Oracle, extracts prior-day transactions to Parquet files on S3 staging. (2) Classification EC2 Spot workers: reads Parquet from S3, applies BIN lookup to assign interchange_category_id and compute fee_amount, writes enriched Parquet to S3 enriched prefix. (3) Load DAG task: Snowflake COPY INTO from S3 enriched prefix, then MERGE into transaction_records table using load_manifest_id for idempotency.
backend.api_patterns: No external API. Internal interfaces: Oracle JDBC extract via oracledb Python driver, S3 via boto3 with server-side encryption, Snowflake via snowflake-connector-python with keypair auth.
backend.background_jobs: Airflow DAGs: (1) nightly_interchange_etl (schedule 00:05 UTC): extract → classify → load → dbt_run → ge_validate → notify. (2) bin_table_refresh (schedule Sunday 02:00 UTC): downloads updated BIN lookup CSV from card network SFTP, validates schema, loads to Snowflake bin_lookup table. (3) manifest_cleanup (schedule weekly): purges S3 staging files older than 7 days after successful load confirmation.
backend.failure_handling: Spot interruption: classification worker writes checkpoint file to S3 every 100K records; on restart, reads checkpoint and resumes from last committed offset. Double-load prevention: Snowflake MERGE uses (load_manifest_id, source_transaction_id) as natural key. Oracle connection failure: DAG retries 3 times with 5-minute intervals before alerting PagerDuty. dbt model failure: Airflow marks downstream tasks as skipped, sends Slack alert to #data-engineering.
backend.backend_risks: Risk: Oracle JDBC driver compatibility with Python 3.12. Mitigation: test with oracledb 2.x thin mode which requires no Oracle client installation. Risk: BIN lookup table staleness causing misclassification. Mitigation: weekly refresh DAG with schema validation and row-count delta alert (>5% change triggers manual review).

security.auth_design: Snowflake keypair authentication for PIPELINE service account; key stored in AWS Secrets Manager. Oracle credentials (username/password) in Secrets Manager, rotated every 90 days. MWAA workers retrieve secrets at task runtime via boto3 Secrets Manager client.
security.authorization_model: Snowflake roles: RAW_WRITER (COPY INTO raw schema only), ENRICHED_WRITER (MERGE into enriched schema), DBT_RUNNER (SELECT on raw, CREATE/REPLACE in mart schema), ANALYST (SELECT on mart and reporting views). No cross-role privilege escalation paths.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Snowflake account usage views (QUERY_HISTORY, ACCESS_HISTORY) exported nightly to Snowflake SOC2_AUDIT schema. Airflow task logs shipped to CloudWatch Logs group /mwaa/interchange-etl. Load manifests table records every batch: start_time, end_time, records_extracted, records_loaded, ge_validation_result.
security.security_risks: Risk: S3 staging bucket contains unencrypted enriched transaction data between classification and Snowflake load. Mitigation: S3 SSE-KMS on staging bucket; lifecycle policy deletes objects 24 hours after successful load_manifest confirmation.

data.entities: transaction_records (load_manifest_id, source_transaction_id, transaction_date, merchant_id, merchant_category_code, card_bin VARCHAR(8), transaction_amount_cents BIGINT, currency_code, interchange_category_id FK, fee_amount_cents BIGINT, fee_rate DECIMAL(6,4), classified_at). interchange_categories (id, network ENUM visa/mc/amex/discover, card_type ENUM credit/debit/commercial, mcc_range_low INT, mcc_range_high INT, rate_pct DECIMAL(6,4), effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE). merchant_bins (bin VARCHAR(8) PK, issuing_bank, card_network, card_type, country_code, updated_at). fee_calculations (id, settlement_batch_id FK, total_transactions INT, total_fee_cents BIGINT, avg_fee_rate DECIMAL(6,4), period_date). load_manifests (id UUID, batch_date DATE, status ENUM running/success/failed, records_extracted INT, records_loaded INT, ge_result ENUM pass/warn/fail, created_at, completed_at). data_quality_checks (id, manifest_id FK, check_name, expected_value, actual_value, passed BOOL, checked_at).
data.storage_design: Snowflake: RAW schema (transaction_records_raw staging table), ENRICHED schema (transaction_records with clustering on transaction_date), MART schema (dbt-managed fee_summary and variance_report views). S3: staging/ prefix (raw Oracle Parquet extract, lifecycle delete after 24h post-load), enriched/ prefix (classified Parquet, lifecycle delete after 24h post-load), checkpoints/ prefix (classification worker checkpoint files).
data.migration_notes: Parallel run period (weeks 10-12): both vendor ETL and new pipeline load to separate Snowflake databases; finance team validates daily fee totals match within 0.01%. Cutover: single DAG parameter change redirects Tableau Snowflake connection string to new database.
data.data_risks: Risk: Oracle schema change breaks JDBC extract. Mitigation: column-level extraction (explicit SELECT column list, not SELECT *) with schema drift detection comparing column metadata on each run. Risk: BIN table has missing entries for new card products. Mitigation: unclassified BIN fallback to 'other_interchange' category with alert when unclassified rate exceeds 0.5% of daily volume.

devops.deployment_topology: MWAA environment (mw1.small, 2 schedulers) orchestrates Airflow DAGs. EC2 Spot Fleet: target capacity 4 x r6i.2xlarge, on-demand fallback 1 x r6i.2xlarge. Workers pull classification tasks from SQS queue. S3 buckets: interchange-etl-staging (SSE-KMS), interchange-etl-checkpoints, interchange-etl-dbt-artifacts. Snowflake in AWS PrivateLink from MWAA VPC.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: PR → pytest (classification engine unit tests, dbt model tests) → dbt compile --profiles-dir ci → merge to main → deploy DAG files to MWAA S3 DAGs bucket → dbt docs generate + publish to S3 static site. Production DAG promotion: manual approval required via GitHub Environments.
devops.observability_stack: Grafana + Prometheus: custom metrics from Airflow StatsD exporter (task duration, failure rate, SLA miss). CloudWatch Logs Insights: query task logs for Oracle extract row counts. Snowflake query performance monitor: long-running COPY INTO queries alert if >30 min. PagerDuty: DAG SLA miss (batch not complete by 04:30 UTC), ge_validation fail on critical expectation suite.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: MWAA version upgrade breaks DAG compatibility. Mitigation: pin Airflow constraints to mwaa-2.9.x requirements file, test upgrades in dev MWAA environment first. Risk: Spot Fleet capacity unavailable during peak AWS demand. Mitigation: on-demand fallback instance in Spot Fleet config; alert if fallback engaged.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a cost-reduction ETL pipeline to replace a $180K/year vendor tool. The core complexity is the BIN classification engine — it must be unit-tested independently, checkpoint-aware for Spot resilience, and validated against the 7-year retention financial data. Idempotent Snowflake MERGE using load_manifest_id is the keystone of the double-load prevention strategy. The SOC 2 audit logging requirement drives the load_manifests table design and Snowflake ACCESS_HISTORY export pattern. dbt handles all SQL transformation to keep Snowflake compute costs low and transformations version-controlled.
target_output.title: Interchange Fee ETL Pipeline — In-House Replacement Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 / Airflow / dbt pipeline on AWS MWAA and EC2 Spot that extracts 5M daily transaction records from on-premise Oracle via JDBC, classifies each record against card BIN lookup tables to assign interchange categories and compute fees, loads enriched records into Snowflake via idempotent MERGE, and drives finance team Tableau dashboards through dbt-managed fee summary models. Replaces a $180K/year vendor ETL with an in-house solution projected at $22K/year while maintaining SOC 2 Type II compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-stage pipeline orchestrated by Airflow on MWAA: (1) Extract stage: Python JDBC worker connects to Oracle 19c over VPN tunnel, exports prior-day transactions to Parquet files on S3 staging bucket with SSE-KMS encryption. (2) Classification stage: EC2 Spot Fleet workers (4 x r6i.2xlarge) read staging Parquet, apply BIN lookup classification logic to assign interchange_category_id and compute fee_amount_cents, checkpoint progress every 100K records to S3 for Spot interruption resilience, write enriched Parquet to S3 enriched prefix. (3) Load stage: Airflow task executes Snowflake COPY INTO from enriched S3 prefix, followed by MERGE into transaction_records using (load_manifest_id, source_transaction_id) as the idempotency key. Downstream: dbt Core 1.8 models run in Snowflake to compute fee summaries and variance metrics; Great Expectations validates data quality before Tableau reports are considered fresh.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.9 on AWS MWAA (mw1.small). Extract: Python 3.12 with oracledb 2.x (thin mode, no Oracle client), boto3 for S3. Classification: Python 3.12 with pandas, pyarrow for Parquet I/O; custom BIN classifier module. Load: snowflake-connector-python 3.x with keypair auth, COPY INTO + MERGE pattern. Transformation: dbt Core 1.8 with Snowflake adapter. Quality: Great Expectations 0.18 with Snowflake datasource. Hosting: AWS MWAA, EC2 Spot Fleet (r6i.2xlarge), S3 (SSE-KMS), Snowflake Enterprise via PrivateLink. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Grafana + Prometheus (Airflow StatsD), CloudWatch Logs.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Oracle Extract Module: daily JDBC extract using explicit column list to detect schema drift; outputs date-partitioned Parquet to s3://interchange-etl-staging/raw/YYYY-MM-DD/. BIN Classification Engine: loads merchant_bins and interchange_categories from Snowflake at worker startup; applies MCC-range and card-type rules to each transaction record; assigns interchange_category_id and computes fee_amount_cents; falls back to 'other_interchange' for unrecognized BINs with alert on unclassified rate >0.5%. Idempotent Snowflake Loader: COPY INTO raw staging table, then MERGE into transaction_records using natural key; records result in load_manifests table. dbt Fee Summary Models: daily fee_summary (total fees by interchange category, network, merchant MCC), cost_per_transaction metrics, 7-day variance vs. budget rates. Great Expectations Validation: row count delta check (±10% vs. prior 7 days), null rate checks on interchange_category_id and fee_amount_cents, BIN unclassified rate threshold.
target_output.system_components: MWAA environment: Airflow scheduler and workers, DAG files in S3, connections and variables from Secrets Manager. EC2 Spot Fleet: 4 x r6i.2xlarge classification workers pulling tasks from SQS; 1 x r6i.2xlarge on-demand fallback. S3 buckets: interchange-etl-staging (raw and enriched Parquet, SSE-KMS, lifecycle delete 24h post-load), interchange-etl-checkpoints (classification worker checkpoints), interchange-etl-dbt-artifacts (dbt manifest and catalog). Snowflake: RAW schema (transaction_records_raw), ENRICHED schema (transaction_records partitioned by transaction_date), MART schema (dbt-managed fee_summary and reporting views), SOC2_AUDIT schema (Snowflake ACCESS_HISTORY export). GitHub Actions: CI/CD pipeline for DAG deployment and dbt model promotion.
target_output.workflows: Nightly ETL Run (00:05 UTC): Airflow DAG nightly_interchange_etl triggers → extract task: Python JDBC connects to Oracle over VPN, selects transactions WHERE transaction_date = CURRENT_DATE - 1, writes 50-file Parquet partition to S3 staging (typical: ~10GB for 5M records) → classify task: Spot workers pick up SQS messages per S3 partition file, apply BIN classifier, write enriched Parquet to S3 enriched prefix → load task: COPY INTO transaction_records_raw from S3 enriched prefix, MERGE into transaction_records using natural key, insert load_manifests record → dbt_run task: dbt build --select fee_summary+ --vars '{run_date: YYYY-MM-DD}' → ge_validate task: Great Expectations checkpoint runs expectations suite, writes results to Snowflake meta schema → notify task: Slack message to #finance-data with row count, fee total, and GE result. Spot Interruption Recovery: worker receives SIGTERM → writes checkpoint file to S3 checkpoints/ with last committed partition offset → Airflow marks task failed after 2-min timeout → Airflow retries task → worker reads checkpoint → resumes from last committed partition. BIN Table Weekly Refresh (Sunday 02:00 UTC): DAG bin_table_refresh → SFTP download of BIN CSV from card network → schema validate → TRUNCATE + INSERT INTO merchant_bins → row count delta alert if >5% change.
target_output.data_model: transaction_records (load_manifest_id UUID, source_transaction_id VARCHAR(36), transaction_date DATE, merchant_id VARCHAR(20), merchant_category_code SMALLINT, card_bin VARCHAR(8), transaction_amount_cents BIGINT, currency_code CHAR(3), interchange_category_id INT FK, fee_amount_cents BIGINT, fee_rate DECIMAL(6,4), classified_at TIMESTAMP_TZ, loaded_at TIMESTAMP_TZ). CLUSTER BY (transaction_date). interchange_categories (id INT PK, network ENUM visa/mc/amex/discover, card_type ENUM credit/debit/commercial, mcc_range_low INT, mcc_range_high INT, rate_pct DECIMAL(6,4), effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE). merchant_bins (bin VARCHAR(8) PK, issuing_bank VARCHAR(100), card_network VARCHAR(20), card_type VARCHAR(20), country_code CHAR(3), updated_at TIMESTAMP_TZ). fee_calculations (id INT PK, settlement_batch_id UUID, total_transactions INT, total_fee_cents BIGINT, avg_fee_rate DECIMAL(6,4), period_date DATE). load_manifests (id UUID PK, batch_date DATE, status ENUM running/success/failed, records_extracted INT, records_loaded INT, ge_result ENUM pass/warn/fail, created_at TIMESTAMP_TZ, completed_at TIMESTAMP_TZ). data_quality_checks (id INT PK, manifest_id UUID FK, check_name VARCHAR, expected_value VARCHAR, actual_value VARCHAR, passed BOOL, checked_at TIMESTAMP_TZ).
target_output.api_design: No external API surface. Internal interfaces: Oracle JDBC: oracledb connection string from Secrets Manager, SELECT transaction_id, transaction_date, merchant_id, mcc, card_bin, amount_cents, currency FROM txn_log WHERE transaction_date = :run_date. Snowflake COPY INTO: COPY INTO raw.transaction_records_raw FROM @stg_s3_enriched/YYYY-MM-DD/ FILE_FORMAT=(TYPE=PARQUET) ON_ERROR=ABORT_STATEMENT. MERGE target: MERGE INTO enriched.transaction_records USING raw.transaction_records_raw ON (enriched.load_manifest_id = raw.load_manifest_id AND enriched.source_transaction_id = raw.source_transaction_id) WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT. dbt ref() model DAG: ref('fee_summary') depends on ref('transaction_records_enriched') depends on source('raw','transaction_records_raw').
target_output.security_and_compliance: Oracle credentials (username/password) in Secrets Manager, rotated every 90 days via Lambda rotation function. Snowflake service account uses keypair authentication; private key in Secrets Manager. S3 staging and enriched buckets: SSE-KMS with CMK, bucket policy denies non-HTTPS access, lifecycle deletes objects 24h after load_manifests.status = 'success' confirmed. Snowflake RBAC: RAW_WRITER service account cannot read ENRICHED schema; DBT_RUNNER cannot write to RAW schema. Snowflake ACCESS_HISTORY and QUERY_HISTORY exported nightly to SOC2_AUDIT.query_audit table for SOC 2 Type II evidence. Load manifests provide change management audit trail for dbt model runs. PAN data explicitly excluded from Oracle SELECT statement and validated by GE expectation: no column named 'pan' or 'card_number' in extracted schema.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions: PR triggers pytest (classification engine, BIN classifier, dbt schema tests) + dbt compile. Merge to main: upload DAG files to s3://mwaa-dags-bucket/dags/ (triggers MWAA DAG refresh). dbt model changes: dbt run --select changed_models in staging Snowflake database, finance team validates fee totals, manual approval via GitHub Environments 'production' gate before merge to main. EC2 Spot Fleet managed via Auto Scaling Group; on-demand fallback instance ensures at least 1 worker always available. Infrastructure as code: Terraform for MWAA environment, EC2 Spot Fleet, S3 buckets, IAM roles, Secrets Manager resources. Environment promotion: dev → staging → production, each with separate Snowflake database and S3 bucket prefix.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboard 'Interchange ETL': Airflow task duration per stage (target: extract <30min, classify <45min, load <20min, dbt <30min), task failure rate, SLA miss count. Prometheus metrics via Airflow StatsD exporter: ti_successes, ti_failures, dag_processing_import_errors. CloudWatch Logs: Airflow task logs in /mwaa/interchange-etl, queried via Logs Insights for Oracle row count and Snowflake COPY INTO result. Custom business metrics in Snowflake: daily record count delta vs. prior 7-day average (alert if >15% deviation), unclassified BIN rate (alert if >0.5%), load_manifests ge_result=fail triggers PagerDuty P2. PagerDuty escalation: DAG SLA miss (batch not complete by 04:30 UTC) → P1; classification worker failure with no healthy workers → P1.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Projected monthly cost: MWAA mw1.small ~$300, EC2 Spot r6i.2xlarge x4 (8h/night) ~$180, on-demand fallback ~$50, S3 storage/requests ~$20, Snowflake compute (2 XS warehouses, ~4h/night) ~$400, data transfer ~$30. Total ~$980/month vs. $15K/month vendor cost. Scaling: if daily volume exceeds 10M transactions, increase Spot Fleet target capacity to 8 and add Snowflake warehouse size to S. S3 Intelligent-Tiering for raw extract archives older than 30 days. Snowflake auto-suspend warehouses after 5 minutes of inactivity.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Core Extract and Load (weeks 1-5): Oracle JDBC extract with explicit column list and schema drift detection. S3 staging bucket with SSE-KMS. BIN classification engine with unit tests (target: 100% coverage of classification rules). Snowflake schema setup (RAW, ENRICHED, MART, SOC2_AUDIT). Idempotent MERGE load with load_manifests table. Airflow nightly_interchange_etl DAG. Phase 2 — Transformation and Quality (weeks 6-9): dbt fee_summary, cost_per_transaction, and interchange_variance models. Great Expectations expectations suite for row counts, null rates, BIN unclassified rate. Weekly BIN table refresh DAG. Parallel run begins (new pipeline + vendor simultaneously). Phase 3 — Validation and Cutover (weeks 10-14): Finance team daily validation of fee totals vs. vendor output. Terraform infrastructure fully codified. SOC 2 audit logging validated with compliance team. Vendor ETL shutdown after 10 consecutive matching days. Grafana dashboard and PagerDuty escalation finalized.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with mypy strict type checking. Ruff for linting. Unit tests for BIN classifier and fee computation functions using pytest; 100% branch coverage required for classification logic. dbt model tests: not_null, unique, accepted_values, relationships for all mart models. Great Expectations expectations versioned in git under expectations/ directory. DAG code must not contain data processing logic — all computation in separate Python modules with unit tests. Airflow connections never hardcoded; always retrieved from Secrets Manager at task runtime.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Oracle schema change breaks JDBC extract. Mitigation: explicit column SELECT list with schema drift detection step that compares extracted column metadata to expected schema on each run; alert and halt on mismatch. Risk: Spot Fleet interruption mid-classification loses progress. Mitigation: 100K-record checkpointing to S3; MERGE idempotency prevents double-load on retry. Risk: BIN lookup table missing new card products causing misclassification. Mitigation: weekly refresh DAG + unclassified rate alert at 0.5%. Tradeoff: Airflow on MWAA vs. self-managed Airflow on EKS — MWAA chosen for managed operations at the cost of version upgrade flexibility. Tradeoff: dbt for all SQL transformation vs. Python Pandas — dbt chosen to keep transformation logic in version-controlled SQL, auditable for SOC 2.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed oracledb thin mode compatible with Oracle 19c (no OCI client needed on MWAA workers). Confirmed Snowflake PrivateLink from MWAA VPC eliminates public internet exposure of financial data. Confirmed PAN fields excluded from Oracle extract by explicit column list. Parallel run duration set to 4 weeks with daily finance team sign-off requirement.

metadata.notes: Round 1 data pipeline; interchange fee ETL replacing vendor tool with Airflow/dbt/Snowflake on Spot instances, SOC 2 compliant.

profile.domain: Card Network Real-Time Payment Events Pipeline

project_goal.value: Ingest authorization, clearing, and settlement events from 200+ issuing banks via ISO 8583 over dedicated TCP connections, normalize to a canonical event schema, and distribute enriched events to downstream analytics, fraud detection, and reconciliation consumers via Kafka — at card network throughput scale.
project_goal.rationale: Core infrastructure requirement replacing a legacy event routing layer that cannot scale to projected network growth.

target_users.value: Downstream platform teams consuming Kafka topics: analytics, fraud, reconciliation; infrastructure engineers operating the pipeline
target_users.rationale: Internal platform consumers identified in system design review.

project_class.rationale: Real-time event streaming pipeline; no user-facing frontend.

capabilities.rationale: Real-time ISO 8583 ingestion, streaming transformation, batch reconciliation, and analytics event distribution.

complexity_level.rationale: 200+ bank TCP connections, exactly-once Kafka delivery, 72-hour auth-clearing join windows, and sub-100ms normalization SLA.

risk_level.rationale: Financial transaction event data with PCI-DSS Level 1 obligations and data residency requirements.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Authorization and settlement events contain card data fields subject to PCI-DSS.

external_exposure.rationale: All bank TCP connections and Kafka consumers are internal network; no public internet exposure.

access_model.value: Kafka ACLs per consumer group: analytics-consumer-group (read on canonical-events topic), fraud-consumer-group (read on canonical-events and high-risk-flags topics), reconciliation-consumer-group (read on reconciliation-exceptions topic). Producers authenticated via mTLS client certificates.
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege Kafka ACL model per consumer team.

feature_scope.value: ISO 8583 TCP receiver for 200+ bank connections, canonical schema normalization, Kafka exactly-once production, Flink auth-clearing join (72h window), reconciliation exception routing, schema version registry, consumer offset management
feature_scope.rationale: Complete replacement feature set for legacy event router.

mvp_scope.value: ISO 8583 receiver, canonical normalization, Kafka production with exactly-once, analytics consumer delivery. Auth-clearing join and reconciliation in phase 2.
mvp_scope.rationale: Core event delivery validated before complex join logic.

security_baseline.value: mTLS for all bank TCP connections and inter-service communication, Kafka SSL with client certificate auth, PAN tokenization before Kafka production (PAN never on Kafka topics), HSM for cryptographic key management
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS Level 1 requirements for cardholder data environment.

frontend_stack.value: None — internal data pipeline; no user-facing frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: Data pipeline project class; no UI required.

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Apache Flink 1.19, Kafka 3.7 (MSK), netty for TCP ISO 8583 receiver
backend_stack.rationale: Java 21 virtual threads for high-concurrency TCP handling; Flink for stateful stream processing with exactly-once semantics.

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for analytics hot storage, Redis 7 for schema version cache and consumer offset state, AWS MSK (Kafka 3.7) for event streaming, S3 for raw event archival
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage for sub-second analytics query performance; MSK for managed Kafka operations.

hosting_target.value: EMR on EKS for Flink jobs, EC2 in private subnets for ISO 8583 receivers, MSK for Kafka, ElastiCache for Redis
hosting_target.rationale: EKS provides container orchestration for Flink job lifecycle; private subnets satisfy bank network isolation requirements.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw ISO 8583 events archived to S3 for 7 years per card network rules; tokenized Kafka events retained 72 hours on topic (covering auth-clearing join window); ClickHouse analytics data retained 90 days hot, 3 years cold on S3 with Iceberg
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Card network data retention requirements and PCI-DSS audit evidence obligations.

future_scope.value: Real-time fraud score enrichment on canonical events, issuer bank onboarding portal, settlement file generation replacing legacy batch process
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 features after core pipeline stabilizes.

constraints.value: Sub-100ms normalization latency P99; 200+ simultaneous bank TCP connections; exactly-once Kafka delivery; data residency: EU bank events must be processed and stored in eu-west-1 only; total infrastructure budget $80K/month
constraints.rationale: SLA commitments to issuing bank partners and regulatory data residency obligations.

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for Java services, Flink metrics to Prometheus, MSK CloudWatch metrics, custom business metrics: events per second per bank, normalization error rate, Kafka consumer lag per consumer group
observability_baseline.rationale: Datadog corporate standard; Flink metrics integration required for job health.

execution_preference.value: Canary deployment for Flink job version upgrades; blue-green for ISO 8583 receiver service updates
execution_preference.rationale: Zero-downtime deployment required for always-on bank connections.

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS Level 1: cardholder data environment (CDE) segmentation, annual QSA audit, HSM for key management, PAN tokenization before any data leaves receiver service. ISO 8583: message format compliance per bank bilateral agreements. Data residency: EU-issued card events processed in eu-west-1 only per GDPR territorial requirements.
compliance_context.rationale: PCI Level 1 and data residency obligations trigger compliance context requirement.

project.goal: Real-time card event streaming pipeline at network scale replacing legacy router
project.target_users: Analytics, fraud, and reconciliation consumer teams

backend.framework: Apache Flink 1.19, Netty 4.x for TCP receiver

security.auth: mTLS for bank connections and inter-service, Kafka SSL + client certificates
security.pan_handling: HSM tokenization at receiver boundary; PAN never on Kafka topics

devops.hosting: EMR on EKS (Flink), EC2 private subnets (receiver)

reasoner_reviews.product: The ISO 8583 receiver is the highest-risk component — it handles the bank TCP connection lifecycle and must be hardened against malformed message payloads. The 72-hour auth-clearing join window is standard for the industry but requires careful Flink state backend sizing (RocksDB with S3 checkpoint). The canonical schema design is critical infrastructure that must be versioned from day one.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Flink exactly-once semantics require two-phase commit coordination between the Kafka source and Kafka sink. The auth-clearing join must use Flink's interval join operator with a 72-hour window on event_time; event time requires watermarks from ISO 8583 message timestamps, which issuer banks may send with clock skew. Recommend 5-minute late-arrival tolerance in watermark strategy. The multi-region data residency constraint requires separate Kafka clusters per region with consumer groups that never cross regional boundaries.
reasoner_reviews.security: PAN tokenization at the receiver boundary is the correct architecture — the HSM call must complete before the event is queued for Kafka production. The tokenization step adds ~5ms latency; this is acceptable within the 100ms P99 budget. All bank TCP connections must be validated with mTLS using bank-issued certificates; certificate revocation must be checked on each new connection establishment.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Sub-100ms P99 normalization is achievable: ISO 8583 parse ~2ms, HSM tokenization ~5ms, canonical schema mapping ~1ms, Kafka produce with acks=all ~15ms, total ~23ms P95. P99 will be dominated by HSM latency variance and Kafka leader election events. Recommend HSM connection pool pre-warmed to 50 connections per receiver instance. The 200+ bank TCP connections require careful connection lifecycle management in Netty — each bank should have a dedicated channel handler with heartbeat monitoring.

backend.service_design: Three services: (1) ISO8583ReceiverService (Netty 4.x, Java 21 virtual threads): manages persistent TCP connections with 200+ banks, decodes ISO 8583 messages using j8583 library, calls HSM tokenization service, produces canonical events to Kafka. (2) NormalizationFlinkJob (Flink 1.19): consumes raw-events Kafka topic, applies bank-specific field mapping transforms from Redis-cached schema version registry, produces to canonical-events topic with exactly-once semantics. (3) AuthClearingJoinFlinkJob (Flink 1.19): interval join on canonical-events topic within 72-hour window keyed by network_transaction_id, produces matched pairs to matched-transactions topic and unmatched to reconciliation-exceptions topic.
backend.api_patterns: Internal gRPC API: TokenizationService.TokenizePan (synchronous, called by receiver before Kafka produce). Admin HTTP API: GET /v1/banks/{bank_id}/connection-status, GET /v1/consumers/{group_id}/lag, POST /v1/flink-jobs/{job_id}/checkpoint.
backend.failure_handling: ISO 8583 parse failure: log raw message to S3 dead-letter prefix, increment parse_error_count metric, send NAK response to bank. HSM tokenization timeout (>50ms): reject message, log to dead-letter, alert on tokenization_timeout_rate >0.01%. Flink job failure: automatic restart strategy (fixed-delay, 3 attempts, 30s delay), resume from last Flink checkpoint. Kafka producer failure (acks=all timeout): receiver buffers up to 10K messages in off-heap queue before circuit breaking bank connection.
backend.scaling_notes: ISO 8583 receivers: 8 instances behind NLB, each handling ~25 bank connections. Auto-scale on TCP connection count metric. Flink NormalizationJob: 16 task managers, 4 slots each = 64 parallelism. Flink AuthClearingJoinJob: 8 task managers; RocksDB state backend sized for 72h of auth events (estimated 500GB, S3 incremental checkpoints every 5 min).

security.auth_design: mTLS for all bank TCP connections: receiver loads bank CA bundle from ACM PCA, validates client certificate on connection handshake, checks CRL endpoint before accepting connection. Inter-service mTLS: Flink task managers and receiver use short-lived certificates from internal PKI.
security.secrets_management: HSM (AWS CloudHSM cluster) for PAN tokenization keys. mTLS certificates managed via ACM PCA with 90-day rotation. Kafka client keystores in AWS Secrets Manager. Admin API JWT signing key in Secrets Manager.
security.abuse_prevention: Bank TCP connections: rate limit 10K messages/second per bank connection; exceeding limit triggers graceful backpressure via NAK responses and alert. CDE network segmentation: ISO 8583 receivers in dedicated PCI subnet; no egress to non-CDE networks except HSM and Kafka.
security.security_risks: Risk: bank certificate expiry causes connection drops. Mitigation: automated certificate expiry monitoring, alert 30 days before expiry, documented bank re-keying procedure. Risk: ISO 8583 message injection via malformed payload. Mitigation: strict j8583 parser with max message size limit (8KB), reject and dead-letter any message exceeding size or failing field type validation.

data.entities: authorization_events (event_id UUID, network_transaction_id VARCHAR(36), event_type ENUM authorization, pan_token VARCHAR(64), amount_cents BIGINT, currency_code CHAR(3), issuer_bank_id, merchant_id, mcc SMALLINT, approval_code, response_code, message_timestamp TIMESTAMP, received_at TIMESTAMP, schema_version SMALLINT). clearing_files (event_id, network_transaction_id FK, clearing_amount_cents BIGINT, interchange_amount_cents BIGINT, settlement_date DATE, clearing_timestamp TIMESTAMP). settlement_records (batch_id UUID, settlement_date DATE, issuer_bank_id, net_amount_cents BIGINT, transaction_count INT, file_reference). issuer_connections (bank_id VARCHAR(20) PK, bank_name, connection_status ENUM connected/reconnecting/degraded, last_heartbeat TIMESTAMP, messages_today BIGINT, cert_expiry DATE). schema_versions (version SMALLINT PK, bank_id, field_mapping_json JSONB, effective_from DATE). consumer_offsets (consumer_group_id, topic, partition, committed_offset BIGINT, lag BIGINT, updated_at TIMESTAMP). processing_checkpoints (job_id VARCHAR, checkpoint_id BIGINT, checkpoint_path S3URI, checkpoint_timestamp TIMESTAMP, state_size_bytes BIGINT).
data.storage_design: MSK Kafka: raw-events topic (64 partitions, replication factor 3, retention 72h), canonical-events (64 partitions, retention 72h), matched-transactions (32 partitions, retention 7 days), reconciliation-exceptions (16 partitions, retention 30 days). ClickHouse: authorization_events and clearing_files tables with MergeTree engine, partitioned by toYYYYMM(message_timestamp), ORDER BY (issuer_bank_id, message_timestamp). Redis 7: schema_versions hash (bank_id → version mapping), consumer_lag hash. S3: raw-events-archive (7-year retention, Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year), flink-checkpoints (incremental, retained 3 generations).
data.consistency_notes: Exactly-once delivery achieved via Flink Kafka source (read committed) + Kafka sink (two-phase commit transaction). Auth-clearing join uses event_time with 5-minute watermark late-arrival tolerance. Unmatched auths after 72h emitted to reconciliation-exceptions topic. ClickHouse inserts are idempotent via ReplacingMergeTree with version column for deduplication.
data.data_risks: Risk: watermark skew from issuer bank clock drift causes late auth events to be dropped from join window. Mitigation: 5-minute late-arrival tolerance in Flink watermark; monitor late_event_drop_rate metric with alert at >0.01%. Risk: RocksDB state backend OOM during high-velocity burst (e.g., Black Friday). Mitigation: pre-provision 750GB RocksDB state; Flink incremental checkpoints to S3 every 5 minutes for recovery.

devops.deployment_topology: us-east-1: MSK cluster (3 brokers, kafka.r6g.2xlarge), ISO 8583 receivers (8 x EC2 c6i.4xlarge in PCI subnet behind NLB), Flink on EMR 7.x on EKS (2 job managers, 16 task managers), ClickHouse cluster (3 x i4i.4xlarge), ElastiCache Redis 7 (r7g.xlarge, cluster mode). eu-west-1: mirror MSK cluster (EU bank events), 4 ISO 8583 receiver instances, Flink normalization job (EU events only).
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins pipelines: unit tests (Java 21 Junit5 + Flink test harness) → integration tests (embedded Kafka + embedded Flink mini-cluster) → docker build → push ECR → deploy to staging EKS. Flink job deployment: Flink REST API submit job with savepoint from previous version; canary: route 5% of raw-events partitions to new job version before full cutover. ISO 8583 receiver: blue-green ECS service update with NLB connection draining (300s timeout for active bank connections).
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM: Java traces for receiver and Flink job operators. Flink metrics: Prometheus JMX exporter → Grafana dashboard (events/sec per bank, normalization latency P50/P95/P99, Flink checkpoint duration, RocksDB state size). MSK CloudWatch: BytesInPerSec, UnderReplicatedPartitions, OffsetLag per consumer group. Custom Datadog metrics: events_per_second_per_bank, tokenization_latency_p99, auth_clearing_match_rate, unmatched_auth_rate.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: Flink savepoint incompatibility between job versions causes cold restart from Kafka earliest offset. Mitigation: maintain Avro schema backward compatibility; test savepoint restore in staging with production-scale state snapshot before each deployment. Risk: MSK broker failure during peak transaction window. Mitigation: 3-broker MSK with replication factor 3; unclean.leader.election.enable=false to prevent data loss on leader election.

ISS-001.title: EU data residency enforcement not specified at Kafka routing layer
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan stated EU bank events must be processed in eu-west-1 but did not specify how the ISO 8583 receiver routes EU-issued card events to the eu-west-1 MSK cluster vs. us-east-1. Without explicit routing logic, EU bank events could be produced to the us-east-1 cluster violating GDPR data residency.

history.note: Identified in round 1 audit: residency constraint acknowledged but routing mechanism unspecified.

ISS-002.title: RocksDB state backend sizing lacks burst capacity analysis
ISS-002.detail: The auth-clearing Flink join holds up to 72 hours of authorization events in RocksDB state. Round 1 plan stated 500GB estimated state size but provided no analysis of peak burst scenarios (Black Friday, major settlement events) that could cause OOM and trigger checkpoint failure.

history.note: Identified in round 1 audit: state size estimate given without burst analysis or OOM mitigation.

focus_issues.title: EU data residency enforcement not specified at Kafka routing layer
focus_issues.detail: No routing logic specified to direct EU bank events to eu-west-1 MSK cluster, risking GDPR residency violation.

focus_issues.title: RocksDB state backend sizing lacks burst capacity analysis
focus_issues.detail: 500GB RocksDB estimate for 72h auth window state has no burst scenario analysis or OOM mitigation plan.

latest_recommendations: Specify EU-issuer detection logic in the ISO 8583 receiver using the bank_id → region mapping table; produce to regional MSK endpoint based on issuer_bank_id prefix.

Model peak state size for Black Friday (3x average volume × 72h window × event size); provision RocksDB to 1.5x peak estimate with incremental checkpoint to S3 every 3 minutes during high-volume periods.
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Card Network Real-Time Payment Events Pipeline — Architecture v1

convergence_state.reason: High-severity data residency issue unresolved; plan requires explicit routing mechanism specification.

previous_audits.summary: Strong Flink and Kafka architecture with correct PCI-DSS tokenization boundary. Two issues unresolved: EU data residency routing mechanism absent, and RocksDB state sizing lacks burst analysis.
strengths: mTLS and HSM tokenization at receiver boundary correctly models PCI CDE
Exactly-once Flink Kafka source/sink coordination is correctly specified
Auth-clearing 72h interval join approach is technically sound
concerns: EU bank event routing to regional MSK not specified — GDPR residency risk
RocksDB state estimate of 500GB has no basis in burst volume analysis
blocking_issues: ISS-001: EU events could flow to us-east-1 without explicit routing guard in receiver
recommendations: Add bank_region_assignments table mapping issuer_bank_id to AWS region; receiver looks up assignment at connection time and selects regional MSK bootstrap servers accordingly.

Perform capacity analysis: peak day ~3x average at 15M auth events × 72h window × 500 bytes/event = 3.24TB peak state; provision 4TB NVMe for RocksDB with 3-minute incremental S3 checkpoints during burst.

issue_updates.title: EU data residency routing gap
issue_updates.detail: Receiver service has no mechanism to differentiate EU vs. non-EU bank events for regional Kafka routing.

issue_updates.title: RocksDB sizing not analytically derived
issue_updates.detail: 500GB estimate is a guess; no formula or burst analysis provided.

previous_plan.title: Card Network Real-Time Payment Events Pipeline — Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Java 21 / Flink / Kafka pipeline ingesting ISO 8583 events from 200+ banks, normalizing to canonical schema, delivering to analytics, fraud, and reconciliation consumers via MSK.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: ISO 8583 Netty receiver → HSM tokenization → Kafka raw-events → Flink normalization job → canonical-events topic → downstream consumers. Auth-clearing join Flink job produces matched-transactions and reconciliation-exceptions.
previous_plan.system_components: ISO8583ReceiverService (Netty), NormalizationFlinkJob, AuthClearingJoinFlinkJob, MSK Kafka, ClickHouse, Redis 7, CloudHSM.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: mTLS bank connections, HSM PAN tokenization at receiver boundary, Kafka ACLs per consumer group, PCI CDE subnet segmentation.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision targeting ISS-001 (EU data residency routing) and ISS-002 (RocksDB burst sizing). ISS-001 requires adding a bank_region_assignments table and regional MSK endpoint selection logic in the ISO 8583 receiver at connection establishment time — bank_id prefix determines AWS region routing before any event is produced. ISS-002 requires an analytically derived state size estimate: peak day 3x average volume = 15M auth events × 72h window × 600 bytes/event = 3.24TB peak; provision 4TB NVMe-backed EBS gp3 for RocksDB task managers with incremental S3 checkpoints every 3 minutes during burst.

fix_report.action_taken: Added bank_region_assignments table (bank_id, aws_region ENUM us-east-1/eu-west-1, msks_bootstrap_servers, effective_from) loaded into Redis at receiver startup. ISO8583ReceiverService now performs region lookup by issuer_bank_id on TCP connection establishment; instantiates a regional KafkaProducer pointed at the correct MSK bootstrap endpoint. EU bank connections are physically isolated to eu-west-1 receiver instances via NLB target group routing by bank_id prefix. Added integration test asserting EU bank_id events are never produced to us-east-1 MSK topic.
fix_report.expected_outcome: EU-issued card authorization events will be produced exclusively to eu-west-1 MSK cluster, satisfying GDPR data residency requirements. QSA audit evidence: bank_region_assignments table audit log and MSK topic-level CloudTrail records.

fix_report.action_taken: Added RocksDB capacity analysis: peak Black Friday volume = 15M auth events/day × 3 (peak multiplier) = 45M events × 72h = 135M event records × 600 bytes/event (canonical event with metadata) = 81GB raw; with RocksDB 4x write amplification = 324GB; with 20% safety margin = 389GB. Provisioned Flink AuthClearingJoinJob task managers with 500GB NVMe-backed EBS gp3 volumes for RocksDB. Added Flink checkpoint interval override to 3 minutes during burst: Flink job parameter checkpoint_interval_ms_burst=180000 activated when events_per_second_per_bank > 2x rolling 24h baseline. Added Datadog alert on RocksDB state size > 400GB (warning) and > 480GB (critical, triggers manual operator review).
fix_report.expected_outcome: RocksDB state backend has analytically derived headroom for peak burst scenarios. Operators receive graduated alerts before capacity is exhausted, enabling proactive task manager scaling.

target_output.title: Card Network Real-Time Payment Events Pipeline — Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: Java 21 / Apache Flink 1.19 / MSK Kafka pipeline ingesting ISO 8583 authorization, clearing, and settlement events from 200+ issuing banks, normalizing to a canonical schema, and distributing to analytics, fraud, and reconciliation consumers — with multi-region data residency enforcement for EU bank events (ISS-001 resolved) and analytically sized RocksDB state backend for 72-hour auth-clearing join windows (ISS-002 resolved).
target_output.architecture_overview: ISO 8583 Netty receiver services in both us-east-1 and eu-west-1 manage persistent mTLS TCP connections to issuing banks. On connection establishment, the receiver looks up the issuer_bank_id in the bank_region_assignments Redis cache to determine the target MSK cluster region; EU bank connections are exclusively routed to eu-west-1 receiver instances via NLB target group rules. Each message is decoded by the j8583 parser, PAN-tokenized via CloudHSM, and produced to the regional raw-events Kafka topic with exactly-once producer transaction. A Flink normalization job consumes raw-events, applies bank-specific field mappings from the schema version registry, and produces to the regional canonical-events topic. A second Flink job performs a 72-hour interval join between authorization and clearing events keyed on network_transaction_id, emitting matched pairs to matched-transactions and unmatched items to reconciliation-exceptions. ClickHouse ingests from canonical-events for analytics hot storage; S3 archives raw events for 7-year retention.
target_output.technology_stack: Event Processing: Java 21 with virtual threads (ISO 8583 receiver), Apache Flink 1.19 (normalization and auth-clearing join jobs), j8583 library for ISO 8583 parsing. Messaging: MSK Kafka 3.7 (64-partition topics, RF=3), per-region clusters. State: RocksDB state backend with 500GB NVMe EBS gp3 per task manager, incremental S3 checkpoints. Analytics: ClickHouse (MergeTree, ReplacingMergeTree for dedup). Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 (cluster mode, bank_region_assignments + schema version cache). Security: CloudHSM for PAN tokenization keys, ACM PCA for mTLS certificates. Hosting: EMR on EKS (Flink), EC2 c6i.4xlarge (receivers), MSK. CI/CD: Jenkins. Monitoring: Datadog APM, Prometheus + Grafana.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ISO 8583 Receiver: persistent TCP connection management for 200+ banks via Netty, j8583 message decode, CloudHSM PAN tokenization, bank_region_assignments lookup for regional MSK routing, exactly-once Kafka produce. Canonical Normalization: Flink job mapping bank-specific ISO 8583 field encodings to canonical event schema using versioned bank field mapping rules from Redis schema registry. Auth-Clearing Join: Flink 72-hour interval join on network_transaction_id, 5-minute late-arrival watermark tolerance, emits matched_transaction_events and reconciliation_exception_events. Bank Region Assignments: Redis-backed lookup table mapping issuer_bank_id to aws_region and MSK bootstrap servers, refreshed from DynamoDB source on receiver startup. Schema Version Registry: Kafka topic schema_versions with bank-specific normalization rule versions, cached in Redis, updated without Flink job restart.
target_output.system_components: ISO8583ReceiverService (Netty 4.x, Java 21, 8 instances us-east-1 + 4 instances eu-west-1, behind regional NLBs). NormalizationFlinkJob (Flink 1.19, 64 parallelism, 16 task managers per region). AuthClearingJoinFlinkJob (Flink 1.19, 8 task managers, 500GB NVMe EBS gp3 RocksDB state per TM). TokenizationService (gRPC, calls CloudHSM, connection pool 50 sessions). MSK clusters: us-east-1 (raw-events-us, canonical-events-us, matched-transactions-us) and eu-west-1 (raw-events-eu, canonical-events-eu). bank_region_assignments table in DynamoDB, cached in Redis 7. ClickHouse cluster (3 x i4i.4xlarge, authorization_events and clearing_files tables). S3: raw-events-archive (7-year), flink-checkpoints (3 generations retained). CloudHSM cluster (2 HSMs for HA). ACM PCA for bank mTLS certificates.
target_output.workflows: ISO 8583 Event Ingestion (per-message, <100ms P99): Bank TCP client sends ISO 8583 MTI-0100 authorization request → Netty channel handler receives bytes → j8583 decoder parses bitmap fields → field validation (reject + dead-letter if malformed) → TokenizationService.TokenizePan gRPC call (CloudHSM, ~5ms) → bank_region_assignments Redis lookup → produce to regional raw-events Kafka topic with transactional producer (transaction ID = bank_id + message_id) → NAK/ACK response to bank within 100ms. Normalization Flow: Flink NormalizationJob consumes raw-events → reads schema_version from Redis for source bank_id → applies field mapping transform (ISO 8583 field 4 → amount_cents, field 41 → merchant_id, etc.) → sets event_time from ISO 8583 field 7 → produces CanonicalEvent to canonical-events topic exactly-once. Auth-Clearing Join: Flink AuthClearingJoinFlinkJob consumes canonical-events filtering event_type IN (authorization, clearing) → interval join on network_transaction_id with 72-hour right-side window → matched pairs produced to matched-transactions topic → unmatched auth events after 72h emitted to reconciliation-exceptions topic → Flink checkpoints to S3 every 5 min (3 min during burst).
