metadata.notes: Round 1 fullstack payment link platform; PCI SAQ A via Stripe hosted fields, HMAC-signed single-use links, KMS-encrypted QuickBooks OAuth tokens, SQS accounting sync for professional services firms.

reasoner_reviews.product: Scope is well-defined: Oracle decommission of reporting workloads is the primary success criterion. The medallion architecture is the right pattern for separating raw ingestion from curated analytics layers. Real-time streaming should be scoped to Phase 2 to reduce migration risk.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Delta Lake ACID guarantees and time-travel capabilities are essential for safe migration with rollback capability. The Scala Spark migration jobs should use date-range partitioned reads from Oracle to avoid OLTP contention. Unity Catalog is the right governance choice but requires careful privilege design with default-deny at the schema level.
reasoner_reviews.security: PCI scope extension to the lakehouse is a significant control burden. Unity Catalog row-level security must be configured before any cardholder-adjacent data lands in the environment. The right-to-erasure requirement must be solved at ingestion: PAN fields must be replaced with tokens before writing to Delta.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 12-month timeline is achievable for migration and core analytics layers but tight for the full feature store roadmap. Oracle parallel operation adds operational complexity; a clear cutover gate per reporting domain is essential to avoid indefinite dual-maintenance.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The plan must define a concrete reconciliation sign-off process with quantified acceptance criteria: row count tolerances, financial total variances, and statistical distribution checks. Without this, the migration has no clear completion condition.

backend.service_design: Spark-based migration jobs with configurable date-range batching (7-day windows), JDBC extraction from Oracle with connection pooling (max 10 connections), idempotent upsert into Delta bronze layer using merge-on-transaction-id
backend.background_jobs: Airflow DAGs: oracle_extraction_daily (incremental), medallion_transformation_silver (hourly post-extraction), dbt_gold_refresh (post-silver), reconciliation_validation (post-each-migration-batch)
backend.failure_handling: Checkpoint files in ADLS track last successfully migrated partition; failed batches retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff; migration error rate threshold 0.01% triggers automatic pause and PagerDuty alert
backend.scaling_notes: Databricks autoscaling clusters: migration clusters scale to 50 nodes for bulk load, steady-state analytics clusters use 4-16 nodes on Photon runtime

security.auth_design: Azure AD SSO to Databricks workspace; service principals for pipeline jobs with rotated secrets in Azure Key Vault
security.authorization_model: Unity Catalog: bronze layer read-only for migration jobs, silver read/write for transformation jobs, gold read-only for analytics teams; column-level masking on token fields for non-privileged analysts
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault for Oracle JDBC credentials, Databricks secrets API for runtime injection; no credentials in notebook or DAG code
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Unity Catalog audit logs for all data access events; Databricks cluster access logs; Azure Monitor for control-plane actions; all logs retained 12 months in Log Analytics

devops.deployment_topology: Three Databricks workspaces (dev/staging/prod) in separate Azure subscriptions; Unity Catalog metastore shared across workspaces via Azure managed identity

devops.rollback_strategy: Delta time-travel: migration jobs record the table version before each batch write; rollback procedure reverts to prior Delta version without data loss

target_output.title: Oracle to Databricks Lakehouse Migration Architecture - Payment Processor Data Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A phased infrastructure migration replacing a monolithic Oracle data warehouse with a Databricks lakehouse on Azure, implementing a bronze/silver/gold Delta Lake medallion architecture with Unity Catalog governance and a dbt transformation layer. The platform processes petabyte-scale payment transaction data with PCI-DSS Level 1 compliance, quantified reconciliation sign-off criteria, and synchronous PAN tokenization at the ADF extraction boundary - enabling 60% storage cost reduction and real-time analytics once Oracle is decommissioned.
target_output.architecture_overview: ADF pipelines extract Oracle transaction records in configurable 7-day date-range batches and apply synchronous PAN-to-token substitution before writing Parquet files to ADLS Gen2 bronze layer - raw PANs never persist on Azure storage. Spark migration jobs on Databricks read bronze Parquet, apply data quality assertions and schema enforcement, and write to silver Delta Lake tables with ACID guarantees. dbt Core computes gold aggregation models consumed by finance and fraud analytics teams via Databricks SQL. Unity Catalog enforces column-level masking on token fields and schema-level RBAC for all three layers across three isolated Databricks workspaces. Airflow on MWAA orchestrates extraction, transformation, and reconciliation DAGs with checkpoint-based resumption on failure.
target_output.technology_stack: Data platform: Delta Lake on Databricks (Azure) with Photon runtime; Unity Catalog for governance. Extraction: Azure Data Factory with synchronous PAN tokenization activity calling internal token vault API. Transformation: Apache Spark (Scala for migration jobs, PySpark for streaming); dbt Core 1.8 for SQL models. Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.9 on MWAA. Storage: ADLS Gen2 with hierarchical namespace. Infrastructure: Azure DevOps for CI/CD; Azure Key Vault for secrets; Datadog for observability.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Historical Migration Engine: date-partitioned Oracle extraction with OLTP-safe connection limits, synchronous PAN tokenization, bronze Parquet landing, silver Delta upsert with deduplication, reconciliation validation against quantified acceptance thresholds. Medallion Transformation Layer: silver cleansing (null checks, amount range validation, duplicate transaction_id elimination), gold dbt models (daily_settlement_summary, interchange_cost_by_category, acquirer_net_payout). Self-Service Analytics: Databricks SQL endpoints with row-level security for finance and fraud teams. Data Governance: Unity Catalog lineage from Oracle source to gold model; column-level access policies; data quality contract enforcement at silver layer.
target_output.system_components: Azure Data Factory (Oracle JDBC extraction + synchronous PAN tokenization activity), ADLS Gen2 (bronze Parquet - tokens only, silver/gold Delta tables), Databricks clusters (migration: 50-node autoscaling D8s_v3; analytics: 4-16 node Photon on Reserved Instances), Unity Catalog metastore (shared across dev/staging/prod), Apache Airflow on MWAA (DAG orchestration), dbt Core (gold layer SQL transformations), PostgreSQL control table (migration checkpoints, batch manifests), Azure Key Vault (Oracle credentials, service principal secrets), Datadog (Spark metrics, pipeline alerts, reconciliation dashboards).
target_output.workflows: Migration Batch Workflow: Airflow oracle_extraction_daily DAG triggers ADF pipeline for prior-day date partition; ADF JDBC activity reads Oracle with max 10 connections (to protect OLTP), synchronously calls internal token vault API to replace all PAN fields, writes Parquet to ADLS Gen2 bronze - circuit breaker halts batch if token vault p99 exceeds 100ms or error rate exceeds 0.1%. Spark silver job validates data quality constraints (non-null transaction_id, amount_cents > 0, valid currency code), deduplicates on transaction_id via Delta merge, writes to silver.transaction_facts. Reconciliation Validation: Post-batch Airflow task executes reconciliation_validation DAG comparing Oracle source vs Delta target with acceptance thresholds: row_delta <=0.001%, financial_variance <=0.0001% of batch gross_amount_cents, KS-test p-value >0.95 on amount distributions - any threshold breach triggers PagerDuty alert and pauses migration queue. Oracle Cutover Gate: Per reporting domain (finance_settlement, fraud_features, acquirer_reporting), sign-off checklist requires 30 consecutive days of reconciliation passing, attestation from domain data owner, and QSA sign-off before Oracle read traffic is migrated.
target_output.data_model: transaction_facts (transaction_id UUID PK, token_pan VARCHAR(19) NOT NULL COMMENT 'payment token only - no raw PAN stored at any layer', merchant_id FK, amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), auth_code VARCHAR(6), settlement_date DATE, interchange_category VARCHAR(20), card_scheme ENUM visa/mc/amex/discover, acquirer_id FK, migrated_at TIMESTAMP, _bronze_batch_id VARCHAR(36)). merchant_dimensions (merchant_id UUID PK, merchant_name VARCHAR, mcc CHAR(4), tier ENUM standard/premium, onboarded_at DATE). card_bin_tables (bin_prefix CHAR(8), scheme, issuer, issuer_country CHAR(2), card_type ENUM debit/credit/prepaid). settlement_aggregates (settlement_date DATE, acquirer_id UUID, gross_amount_cents BIGINT, interchange_fees_cents BIGINT, net_payout_cents BIGINT, transaction_count INT, PK: settlement_date + acquirer_id). data_lineage_records (lineage_id UUID, entity VARCHAR, batch_id UUID, source_row_count INT, target_row_count INT, financial_total_variance_pct DECIMAL(10,6), ks_test_p_value DECIMAL(6,4), validated_at TIMESTAMP). migration_checkpoints (table_name VARCHAR PK, last_migrated_partition DATE, batch_status ENUM running/completed/failed/paused, error_count INT, updated_at TIMESTAMP). Raw PANs are never stored in the lakehouse at any layer.
target_output.api_design: Internal migration orchestration API (FastAPI). POST /migration/batches (trigger extraction batch for date range, returns batch_id). GET /migration/batches/{batch_id} (status, row counts, reconciliation result). GET /migration/reconciliation/{domain} (cumulative reconciliation summary with pass/fail against thresholds). POST /migration/cutover/{domain}/sign-off (record domain sign-off with named attestor). GET /governance/lineage/{entity} (data lineage from Oracle to gold model). PATCH /migration/batches/{batch_id}/pause and /resume. All endpoints require Azure AD service principal JWT; every sign-off action writes an audit log entry.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS Level 1: PAN tokenization executed synchronously in ADF pipeline before any data reaches ADLS - raw PANs never persist in lakehouse at bronze, silver, or gold layers; token vault API call is mandatory extraction step with circuit breaker preventing batch completion if tokenization fails. Unity Catalog: column-level masking on token_pan for non-privileged analyst roles; schema-level read/write separation between pipeline service principals and analyst roles; audit log captures every SELECT on silver.transaction_facts and gold tables. Azure Key Vault: Oracle JDBC credentials and service principal secrets with 90-day automatic rotation; no credentials in DAG code or notebooks. Network: Databricks workspace in Azure VNet with no public endpoints; Oracle extraction over dedicated ExpressRoute. Annual QSA audit covers the lakehouse CDE scope.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Three Databricks workspaces in separate Azure subscriptions (dev/staging/prod) with shared Unity Catalog metastore via Azure managed identity. Azure DevOps CI/CD: dbt test suite (schema tests, referential integrity, financial total balance assertions) runs on PR merge; Spark job smoke tests on staging workspace with synthetic transaction data before prod promotion; migration DAG integration tests verify end-to-end batch with reconciliation validation. Terraform manages Databricks workspace config, Unity Catalog schemas, and ADF pipeline definitions. Migration clusters auto-scale to 50 D8s_v3 nodes during bulk load windows (02:00-06:00 UTC); analytics clusters use 4-16 Photon nodes on Reserved Instances. Rollback: Delta time-travel records table version before each batch write; rollback procedure: RESTORE TABLE silver.transaction_facts TO VERSION AS OF {prior_version}, update migration_checkpoint to rolled_back.
target_output.observability: Datadog agent on migration cluster nodes: Spark task duration histogram, shuffle read/write bytes, OOM event count, JDBC connection pool saturation. Custom Airflow StatsD metrics: DAG success rate, task SLA miss count, migration_queue_depth. Reconciliation dashboard: per-batch row_delta_pct, financial_variance_pct, KS-test p-value trend over migration window. PagerDuty escalation for: reconciliation threshold breach, migration error rate >0.01%, Spark OOM events >5 per batch, Unity Catalog audit log gaps >5 minutes. dbt test failures posted to #data-platform-alerts Slack channel.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Projected 60% storage cost reduction via Delta Lake Parquet compression vs Oracle tablespace; ADLS Gen2 hot/cool tiering for data older than 1 year. Migration clusters auto-terminate after 30-minute idle. Analytics clusters on 1-year Reserved Instances for baseline; burst scaling on Azure Spot VMs for large reconciliation runs. Photon acceleration reduces analytics query compute vs standard Spark by ~40%. Estimated steady-state cost post-migration: ~$28K/month vs ~$70K/month Oracle licensing and storage.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Foundation and Historical Migration (months 1-4): Unity Catalog setup and privilege design, ADF pipeline with synchronous PAN tokenization, bronze layer ingestion of 3-year transaction_facts in 7-day batches, reconciliation validation framework. Sign-off gate: 30 consecutive days passing row_delta <=0.001%, financial_variance <=0.0001%, KS p>0.95, plus QSA attestation. Phase 2 - Silver/Gold Layer and Finance Reporting Migration (months 5-8): Silver deduplication and data quality layer, dbt gold models (daily_settlement_summary, interchange_cost_by_category, acquirer_net_payout), finance team migrated to Databricks SQL. Oracle cutover gate for finance_settlement: 30 days reconciliation passing plus data owner sign-off. Phase 3 - Full Decommission and Real-Time Streaming (months 9-12): Remaining Oracle reporting domains migrated, fraud analytics queries moved to gold layer, Oracle read traffic cutover complete, real-time Kafka streaming ingestion added for sub-minute analytics latency, Oracle decommission sign-off from all domain owners.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Spark jobs in Scala with ScalaTest unit tests; migration scripts in Python with pytest coverage. No credentials in code - Azure Key Vault injection via Databricks secrets API. dbt models require schema.yml with column-level documentation and freshness tests. Migration batch jobs must write checkpoint file before committing Delta transaction. Code review required for Unity Catalog privilege changes. ADRs document: medallion layer boundary decisions, token substitution implementation, reconciliation threshold rationale.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Oracle JDBC extraction creates OLTP contention at peak processing hours. Mitigation: restrict extraction windows to 02:00-06:00 UTC; cap at 10 JDBC connections; monitor Oracle I/O wait. Risk: Token vault API latency spikes during bulk migration cause ADF timeout. Mitigation: circuit breaker with 3-retry exponential backoff; token vault SLA p99 <50ms required before migration start. Risk: Delta merge performance degrades on silver.transaction_facts past 500GB. Mitigation: Z-ORDER on settlement_date and acquirer_id; weekly OPTIMIZE maintenance DAG. Tradeoff: synchronous tokenization adds ~8ms per record but ensures no PCI scope expansion - accepted as mandatory control.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Streaming vs micro-batch: micro-batch at 5-minute intervals for Phase 2 silver refresh; sub-minute Kafka streaming deferred to Phase 3 to reduce migration-phase risk. Oracle extraction method: ADF JDBC over ExpressRoute confirmed over AWS DMS given Azure-only constraint. Reconciliation ownership: Data Platform team owns automated checks; QSA sign-off required for each domain cutover gate.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision resolving PCI tokenization boundary and quantified reconciliation acceptance criteria for Oracle-to-Databricks lakehouse migration.

reasoner_reviews.product: The prototype scope is tightly defined around validating settlement economics: transfer initiation, gas cost logging, and confirmation latency measurement. Chain selection optimization is the core research question. Webhook delivery to partners is essential for realistic integration testing.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: GCP Cloud HSM for private key custody is the right choice for a financial prototype - eliminates the key management complexity of software wallets. web3.py with Polygon RPC endpoints is mature enough for prototype use. The gas price cache in Redis is critical: without it, every transfer requires a fresh gas estimate that adds latency and potential race conditions.
reasoner_reviews.security: Private key custody is the highest-risk element. Keys must never leave the HSM in plaintext - all signing must happen within the HSM via the GCP Cloud KMS signing API. The $50K custody limit must be enforced at the API layer, not just as a policy. mTLS for partner webhooks prevents webhook spoofing from malicious parties.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 3-month timeline is achievable for Polygon MVP with 3 pilot partners. Ethereum mainnet should wait until gas economics data from Polygon validates the use case. FATF Travel Rule compliance for >$3K transfers needs to be built into the transfer initiation flow from the start - retrofitting it is harder.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The gas fee volatility tracking is the most research-critical feature - without it, the prototype cannot answer its primary question. The schema for gas_fee_estimates must capture estimated vs actual fees per chain per transaction to enable cost modeling.

backend.service_design: FastAPI service with modules for wallet_management (custody, provisioning, balance), transfer_execution (initiation, signing via HSM, broadcast), chain_monitor (confirmation tracking, Celery tasks), gas_oracle (price caching, chain selection logic), webhook_dispatcher (partner delivery, retry queue)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0; idempotency keys on POST /transfers to prevent double-spend on retry; partner API keys in Authorization header; pagination on GET /transfers with cursor-based results
backend.failure_handling: Transfer broadcast failures: save transfer to pending_broadcast queue in Redis and retry with exponential backoff up to 6 hours; nonce management with distributed lock to prevent nonce collision on concurrent transfers; dead-letter queue for permanently failed transfers requiring manual review
backend.scaling_notes: GKE Autopilot handles scaling; Celery workers for chain monitoring scale independently; Redis cluster for webhook queue and gas cache with persistence enabled

security.auth_design: Per-partner API keys stored as bcrypt hashes in PostgreSQL; key rotation supported via /auth/rotate-key endpoint; rate limiting per partner: 100 transfer requests per minute
security.authorization_model: Partners can only access their own wallet namespace; custody_limit enforcement: middleware checks current_usdc_balance + pending_amount against $50K cap before initiating any transfer
security.secrets_management: GCP Cloud HSM (FIPS 140-2 Level 3) for wallet private keys; Cloud KMS for application secrets and API key encryption; Secret Manager for RPC endpoint API keys; HSM key operations audited via Cloud Audit Logs
security.incident_response_notes: Key compromise protocol: immediately freeze affected wallet via custody_limit=0, sweep remaining balance to cold storage address, rotate HSM key material, notify affected partner within 1 hour

data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for all transfer and wallet records with ACID guarantees; Redis 7 for gas price cache (TTL 30 seconds), pending_broadcast queue, and webhook delivery queue
data.analytics_events: transfer_initiated, transfer_broadcast, transfer_confirmed, transfer_failed, gas_cost_recorded - all published to internal research analytics for cost modeling

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in GCP us-central1; Cloud HSM in same region for low-latency signing; CloudSQL PostgreSQL with private IP; Memorystore Redis; all traffic within VPC
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: unit tests (web3 interaction mocked), integration tests against Polygon Mumbai testnet, Docker build and push to Artifact Registry, GKE rolling deployment
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus + Grafana: API request rate, transfer success/failure rate, gas_cost_per_transfer histogram, on-chain confirmation latency P50/P99, webhook delivery success rate, custodial balance gauge
devops.cost_controls: GKE Autopilot cost-based autoscaling; Polygon RPC calls via Alchemy with request budget alerts; daily gas cost summary pushed to research Slack channel

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a custodial stablecoin payment API prototype on GCP. Critical decisions: Cloud HSM for private key custody eliminates software wallet risk; Polygon as primary chain reduces gas economics noise for research; idempotency keys on transfer endpoints prevent double-spend on client retries; FATF Travel Rule compliance built in from the start for transfers >$3K. Gas fee tracking is the core research instrument so gas_fee_estimates schema must capture estimated vs actual costs per chain per transaction.
target_output.title: Stablecoin B2B Payment Settlement Research Prototype - USDC on Ethereum/Polygon
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype for a custodial USDC payment API enabling on-chain B2B invoice settlement on Polygon and Ethereum, built with FastAPI on GKE Autopilot with GCP Cloud HSM for private key custody. The system exposes a partner-facing REST API for wallet provisioning, USDC transfer initiation, and on-chain confirmation webhooks, while logging granular gas fee economics data needed to determine whether stablecoin rails can replace SWIFT/ACH for cross-border B2B payments.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI service running on GKE Autopilot manages custodial USDC wallets with private keys stored exclusively in GCP Cloud HSM - all transaction signing occurs within the HSM via Cloud KMS signing API without key material ever leaving hardware. Partners authenticate via per-partner API keys and interact with the transfer initiation and wallet management endpoints. Celery workers on separate GKE node pools monitor on-chain confirmations via Polygon/Ethereum RPC, update transfer status, and dispatch webhooks to partner endpoints over mTLS. Gas prices are cached in Redis with 30-second TTL for chain selection decisions. PostgreSQL 16 stores all wallet, transfer, and gas cost records. A companion research analytics pipeline exports gas_fee_estimates records to BigQuery for cost modeling.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12 with FastAPI, web3.py 6.x for blockchain interaction, Celery 5.x for async chain monitoring. Database: PostgreSQL 16 (CloudSQL with private IP), Redis 7 (Memorystore) for gas cache and webhook queue. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot (GCP us-central1), Cloud HSM for private keys, Cloud KMS for app secrets, Secret Manager for RPC API keys. Blockchain: Polygon PoS (primary), Ethereum mainnet (future), Alchemy RPC endpoints. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Polygon Mumbai testnet integration tests. Observability: Prometheus + Grafana.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Wallet Management: provision custodial USDC wallet per partner with HSM-backed key, query on-chain balance, enforce $50K custody limit. Transfer Execution: initiate USDC transfer with idempotency key, select optimal chain based on gas cost and speed, sign via HSM, broadcast to RPC, return tx_hash. Confirmation Monitoring: Celery workers poll RPC for block confirmations, update transfer status to confirmed at 32 confirmations (Polygon) or 12 (Ethereum), fire webhook. Gas Economics Research: capture estimated vs actual gas_gwei and cost_usdc per transfer, export to BigQuery for settlement cost modeling by invoice size and chain. FATF Compliance: transfers >= $3,000 USDC require counterparty VASP identification before broadcast.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot cluster (FastAPI pods + Celery worker pods), Cloud HSM with wallet signing keys, CloudSQL PostgreSQL 16 (private IP), Memorystore Redis 7 (gas cache + webhook queue), Alchemy RPC gateway (Polygon + Ethereum), Cloud KMS (application secret encryption), Secret Manager (RPC keys, partner webhook certs), BigQuery dataset (gas_fee_estimates for research analytics), Grafana + Prometheus (operational metrics), Cloud Audit Logs (HSM key operation audit trail).
target_output.workflows: Transfer Initiation Workflow: Partner POSTs to /v1/transfers with idempotency_key, source_wallet_id, recipient_address, amount_usdc, reference_invoice_id; API validates partner API key, checks custody_limit ($50K cap minus pending), checks FATF threshold (>=$3K requires counterparty_vasp field), fetches current gas estimates from Redis cache (refreshed every 30s from Alchemy gas oracle), selects chain (Polygon if gas_cost_usdc <$0.50 AND block_time <5s, else Ethereum), constructs unsigned transaction, calls Cloud HSM signing API, broadcasts signed tx to RPC endpoint, persists stablecoin_transfers record with status=broadcast, returns {transfer_id, tx_hash, estimated_confirmation_minutes}. Confirmation Monitoring Workflow: Celery worker subscribes to new-block events on Polygon/Ethereum RPC; for each pending transfer, fetches receipt, counts confirmations; at threshold (32 Polygon / 12 Ethereum) updates transfer status to confirmed, records gas_actual_gwei and gas_cost_usdc from receipt, dispatches webhook to partner mTLS endpoint with {transfer_id, status, confirmed_at, tx_hash}; webhook retry queue handles delivery failures with exponential backoff up to 24 hours.
target_output.data_model: custodial_wallets (wallet_id UUID PK, partner_id UUID FK, chain ENUM polygon/ethereum, address VARCHAR(42) UNIQUE, hsm_key_id VARCHAR, balance_usdc_cached DECIMAL(18,6), custody_limit_usdc DECIMAL(18,6) DEFAULT 50000, created_at TIMESTAMP). stablecoin_transfers (transfer_id UUID PK, idempotency_key VARCHAR(64) UNIQUE, source_wallet_id UUID FK, recipient_address VARCHAR(42), amount_usdc DECIMAL(18,6), chain ENUM, tx_hash VARCHAR(66), gas_estimated_gwei DECIMAL(20,9), gas_actual_gwei DECIMAL(20,9), gas_cost_usdc DECIMAL(10,6), status ENUM pending/broadcast/confirmed/failed, broadcast_at TIMESTAMP, confirmed_at TIMESTAMP). b2b_invoices (invoice_id UUID PK, partner_id UUID FK, amount_usdc DECIMAL(18,6), reference VARCHAR, transfer_id UUID FK, settled_at TIMESTAMP). gas_fee_estimates (estimate_id UUID PK, chain ENUM, block_number BIGINT, base_fee_gwei DECIMAL(20,9), priority_fee_gwei DECIMAL(20,9), estimated_transfer_cost_usdc DECIMAL(10,6), recorded_at TIMESTAMP). on_chain_confirmations (confirmation_id UUID PK, transfer_id UUID FK, block_number BIGINT, confirmations_count INT, finalized_at TIMESTAMP). webhook_subscriptions (subscription_id UUID PK, partner_id UUID FK, endpoint_url VARCHAR, event_types TEXT[], tls_cert_fingerprint VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: RESTful API v1 with OpenAPI 3.0. POST /v1/wallets (provision custodial wallet for partner, returns address). GET /v1/wallets/{wallet_id}/balance (on-chain USDC balance with cache age). POST /v1/transfers (initiate transfer; required: idempotency_key, source_wallet_id, recipient_address, amount_usdc; optional: preferred_chain, counterparty_vasp for FATF). GET /v1/transfers/{transfer_id} (transfer status, tx_hash, gas_cost_usdc, confirmation count). GET /v1/transfers?partner_id=X&status=confirmed&from=ISO8601 (list transfers with cursor pagination). GET /v1/research/gas-economics?chain=polygon&from=ISO8601 (gas cost distribution by invoice size range - research endpoint). POST /v1/webhooks (register partner endpoint with mTLS cert). Auth: Authorization: Bearer {api_key} header; rate limit: 100 req/min per partner.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Private key custody: GCP Cloud HSM (FIPS 140-2 Level 3) stores wallet signing keys; all signing operations invoke Cloud KMS CryptoKey signing API - private key bytes never exposed to application code or logs; Cloud Audit Logs captures every HSM signing operation with caller identity. Custody limit enforcement: FastAPI middleware checks (current_wallet_balance + sum_of_pending_transfers) <= $50K before any transfer proceeds; enforced atomically via PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE on wallet record. FATF Travel Rule: transfers >= $3,000 USDC require counterparty_vasp field in request; API returns 422 without it; VASP record stored with transfer for regulatory audit. mTLS for partner webhook delivery: partner TLS certificate fingerprint stored in webhook_subscriptions; Celery verifier validates fingerprint before delivery. API keys stored as bcrypt hashes with per-partner rate limits.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot in GCP us-central1 with separate node pools for FastAPI pods (CPU-optimized) and Celery workers (general-purpose). All resources in private VPC with no public IPs except Cloud Load Balancer for API. CloudSQL PostgreSQL with private IP, automated backups every 6 hours, 7-day PITR. Memorystore Redis with AUTH token and in-transit encryption. GitHub Actions CI: unit tests with web3.py mocked, integration tests against Polygon Mumbai testnet (dedicated test wallets), Docker build and push to Artifact Registry, GKE rolling deployment with readiness probe on /health. Secrets injected at runtime from Secret Manager via Workload Identity - no secrets in container images or Kubernetes manifests. RPC endpoint failover: secondary Alchemy endpoint configured; circuit breaker triggers on >5% error rate.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics scraped by GKE-managed agent. Key gauges: custodial_balance_usdc_total (research risk limit monitoring), pending_transfers_count by chain. Histograms: transfer_initiation_latency_ms, on_chain_confirmation_latency_seconds (P50/P99 by chain - core research metric), gas_cost_per_transfer_usdc (by chain and invoice size bucket). Grafana dashboards: Settlement Economics (gas cost vs invoice size scatter, confirmation latency by chain), Operational (API error rate, webhook delivery success rate). Alerts: custodial_balance > $45K (approaching limit), confirmation_latency_p99 > 600s (chain congestion), webhook_delivery_failure_rate > 10%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot scales FastAPI pods on CPU (target 60%); Celery worker pods scale on Redis queue depth. CloudSQL db-g1-small adequate for prototype volume. Alchemy growth tier with 300M compute units/month; budget alert at 80% usage. Polygon gas costs for USDC transfers ~$0.01-0.05 per transfer at normal congestion. Total prototype infrastructure cost estimated $800-1,200/month. Research insight: at scale, Polygon gas costs would be 50-100x lower than SWIFT correspondent banking fees for sub-$10K cross-border transfers.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Polygon MVP (weeks 1-6): Cloud HSM setup and wallet provisioning, USDC transfer initiation and confirmation monitoring on Polygon Mumbai testnet, gas fee logging schema and research analytics pipeline, API key auth and per-partner rate limiting, 3 pilot partner integrations. Phase 2 - Ethereum and Research Analytics (weeks 7-10): Ethereum mainnet support with chain selection logic, FATF Travel Rule enforcement for >$3K transfers, gas economics BigQuery export and Grafana cost modeling dashboard, webhook delivery with mTLS, all 5-10 pilot partners onboarded. Phase 3 - Research Conclusions (weeks 11-12): Analysis of gas cost vs settlement time data across chains and invoice sizes, comparison against SWIFT/ACH benchmarks, prototype evaluation report with production readiness recommendation.
target_output.development_guidelines: All HSM signing calls wrapped in a signing_service module with injected key_id - never call Cloud KMS directly from transfer logic. Idempotency keys must be checked before any HSM signing to prevent duplicate transfers. web3.py RPC calls mocked in unit tests; integration tests use Mumbai testnet with funded test wallets. No wallet addresses or transfer amounts in application logs - use transfer_id only. OpenAPI spec generated from FastAPI route decorators; spec changes require PR approval from security lead.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Polygon RPC congestion causes gas estimate staleness leading to under-funded transactions. Mitigation: add 20% buffer to estimated priority fee; retry broadcast with higher gas on EIP-1559 replacement. Risk: Cloud HSM signing API latency (typically 50-200ms) adds to transfer initiation P99. Mitigation: async signing flow - return transfer_id immediately and sign/broadcast asynchronously; partner polls status or receives webhook. Risk: regulatory determination that custody model requires MSB registration forces prototype shutdown. Mitigation: legal review running in parallel; design allows non-custodial mode (partner-held keys) as fallback. Tradeoff: Redis gas price cache (30s TTL) trades accuracy for reduced RPC call volume - acceptable for prototype given 20% gas buffer.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Custodial vs non-custodial wallets: custodial with Cloud HSM chosen for research simplicity; non-custodial MPC model deferred to production. Chain selection algorithm: Polygon if estimated gas < $0.50 AND last block time < 5 seconds; Ethereum otherwise - threshold tunable via config. USDC transfer mechanism: direct ERC-20 transfer, not Circle CCTP bridge for MVP simplicity on single-chain Polygon.

metadata.notes: Research prototype for stablecoin B2B payment settlement with GCP Cloud HSM custody, FATF Travel Rule compliance, and gas economics research instrumentation.

project_goal.value: Migration-phase tokenization API replacing legacy PAN vault with drop-in REST interface backed by modern HSM infrastructure, enabling existing applications to switch without code changes
project_goal.rationale: Legacy vault runs on end-of-life hardware with no vendor support; security posture is unacceptable

target_users.value: Internal payment processing applications and third-party integrators currently consuming the legacy vault API
target_users.rationale: Drop-in compatibility is mandatory - downstream teams cannot change API contracts

project_class.rationale: Pure API service - no UI components; drop-in REST replacement for legacy vault endpoint

capabilities.rationale: Tokenization, detokenization, token lookup, and legacy vault migration batch operations

complexity_level.rationale: HSM integration, format-preserving encryption, and live migration with rollback capability

risk_level.rationale: Stores and processes cardholder PANs; HSM key compromise means permanent PAN exposure; migration must not corrupt existing tokens

data_sensitivity.rationale: Primary cardholder account numbers (PANs) are the most sensitive PCI-DSS data element

external_exposure.rationale: API accessible only from within internal payment processing network; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: Mutual TLS for all callers; per-service client certificates; no shared API keys
access_model.rationale: mTLS provides cryptographic caller identity without secret distribution

feature_scope.value: PAN tokenization (returns format-preserving token), detokenization (returns PAN for authenticated callers), token lookup (check existence), bulk migration from legacy vault, token re-keying under new HSM key hierarchy
feature_scope.rationale: Complete replacement of legacy vault functionality

mvp_scope.value: Tokenization and detokenization endpoints with mTLS auth, new HSM key hierarchy, migration job for legacy vault records with round-trip validation
mvp_scope.rationale: Core read/write operations needed before legacy vault can be decommissioned

security_baseline.value: FIPS 140-2 Level 3 HSM for key operations; AES-256 PAN encryption; mTLS for all API callers; all PAN access logged; no PANs in application logs
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS Level 1 mandatory controls for PAN vault

frontend_stack.value: None - internal API service with no browser interface
frontend_stack.rationale: Pure API service; operational management via internal tooling

backend_stack.value: Rust with Axum; AWS CloudHSM for key operations; custom format-preserving tokenization library
backend_stack.rationale: Rust provides memory safety for sensitive PAN handling; Axum gives async performance for high-throughput tokenization

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for token vault store; Redis 7 for detokenization cache with configurable TTL
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for durable token storage; Redis cache reduces HSM decrypt calls for hot tokens

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate with AWS CloudHSM in same VPC; no public subnets
hosting_target.rationale: CloudHSM collocated in VPC eliminates network latency for signing operations

privacy_retention_policy.value: Token-to-PAN mapping retained as long as token is active; PAN purged from vault on explicit token deletion request or card expiry + 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Tokens must remain valid for recurring billing; purge on expiry per PCI-DSS scope reduction guidance

future_scope.value: Network tokenization (Visa Token Service, Mastercard MDES) as alternative to PAN vault; multi-region active-active replication
future_scope.rationale: Network tokens eliminate PAN storage requirement; active-active for DR requirements

constraints.value: Must maintain 100% API compatibility with legacy vault endpoint signatures; migration must be reversible; zero-downtime cutover; p99 latency < 20ms for tokenization
constraints.rationale: Downstream teams cannot change API contracts; p99 latency is contractual SLA

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM; tokenization throughput and latency metrics; HSM operation rate and error metrics; PAN access audit log to SIEM
observability_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS requires comprehensive audit logging of all cardholder data access

execution_preference.value: Parallel run with legacy vault before cutover; phased migration by calling service
execution_preference.rationale: Parallel run validates new vault produces identical tokens before any cutover

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS Level 1 SAQ D; FIPS 140-2 Level 3 HSM mandatory; all PAN access logged and reported to QSA annually
compliance_context.rationale: PAN vault is the highest-risk PCI-DSS component; annual QSA assessment required

project.goal: Drop-in REST replacement for legacy PAN vault with modern HSM infrastructure
project.target_users: Internal payment applications and third-party integrators

backend.api: REST with mTLS; drop-in URL compatibility with legacy vault

security.encryption: FIPS 140-2 Level 3 HSM; AES-256 PAN encryption

open_questions.token_format: Format-preserving vs random token format for downstream system compatibility

confirmed_decisions.hsm: AWS CloudHSM in same VPC as Fargate
confirmed_decisions.auth: mTLS only - no API keys for PAN vault callers

reasoner_reviews.product: The drop-in compatibility constraint is the dominant design driver. Legacy endpoint signatures must be preserved exactly, including error response formats. The migration job needs careful sequencing: validate round-trip before marking records as migrated.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust with Axum is a strong choice for a PAN vault - memory safety prevents buffer overruns on sensitive PAN data. The detokenization Redis cache is a useful latency optimization but requires careful TTL design: too long increases PAN exposure window; too short eliminates the benefit. HSM operation latency will dominate p99 for tokenization - profile CloudHSM signing latency early.
reasoner_reviews.security: The migration batch job is the highest-risk operation in this plan. Re-encrypting PANs under the new HSM key must validate round-trip correctness for every record before deleting the legacy ciphertext. Error rate threshold triggering automatic rollback is essential. All detokenization calls must be logged with caller identity regardless of cache hit.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: p99 < 20ms for tokenization is achievable on Fargate with CloudHSM in the same VPC but leaves little margin. HSM operations typically run 5-15ms; combined with Postgres write this is tight. Consider async tokenization flow for non-latency-critical callers.

backend.service_design: Axum service with modules: tokenization_handler (PAN intake, HSM encrypt, token generation, vault write), detokenization_handler (token lookup, cache check, HSM decrypt if cache miss), migration_executor (batch legacy vault re-encryption with round-trip validation), admin_handler (token deletion, key rotation status)
backend.api_patterns: REST with mTLS; POST /v1/tokenize (PAN in, token out), POST /v1/detokenize (token in, PAN out - restricted callers), GET /v1/tokens/{token}/exists, POST /v1/migrate (trigger migration batch), DELETE /v1/tokens/{token}. Maintain legacy endpoint aliases for backward compatibility.
backend.failure_handling: HSM unavailability: fail-closed on tokenization (return 503 rather than fall back to software encryption); migration batch: pause on error_rate > 0.001%, alert on-call, require manual resume
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate service autoscaling on request count target 500 req/s per task; CloudHSM cluster scales to 2 HSMs in active-active for throughput and HA

security.auth_design: mTLS enforced at Fargate load balancer; client certificate CN mapped to caller service identity in PostgreSQL; per-caller permissions table defines which endpoints are accessible
security.authorization_model: Tokenization: all authenticated callers. Detokenization: restricted to callers with explicit detokenize permission in caller_permissions table; caller identity logged on every call regardless of Redis cache hit
security.secrets_management: CloudHSM partition credentials in AWS Secrets Manager; TLS server certificate in ACM; client certificate CA in ACM Private CA; no secrets in container images
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every PAN access (tokenize and detokenize) logged to append-only audit_log table with caller_id, token (not PAN), operation, timestamp, and request_id; audit logs shipped to SIEM in real-time via Kinesis Firehose

data.entities: tokens (token VARCHAR(19) PK, ciphertext BYTEA, hsm_key_version VARCHAR, card_expiry DATE, created_at, last_accessed_at), vault_entries (entry_id UUID PK, token FK, encryption_algorithm VARCHAR, key_version VARCHAR, migrated_from_legacy BOOLEAN, migrated_at TIMESTAMP), access_policies (caller_id VARCHAR PK, can_tokenize BOOLEAN, can_detokenize BOOLEAN), audit_logs (log_id UUID PK, caller_id, operation ENUM tokenize/detokenize/delete, token VARCHAR, timestamp, request_id), migration_batches (batch_id UUID PK, legacy_range_start, legacy_range_end, records_processed INT, records_validated INT, error_count INT, status ENUM running/completed/failed/rolled_back, started_at, completed_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ in same VPC; token table encrypted at rest via RDS KMS; Redis 7 detokenization cache with 300s TTL; Redis AUTH token and TLS enabled
data.data_risks: Token collision: format-preserving tokenization must guarantee uniqueness within vault; migration batch failure leaving records in inconsistent state between legacy and new vault

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate tasks in private subnets; CloudHSM cluster in same VPC; ALB with mTLS pass-through; no public subnet access; NACLs restrict all non-mTLS traffic
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Rust tests (unit + integration with CloudHSM simulator), Docker build, ECR push, ECS blue/green deployment with traffic shifting 10% -> 50% -> 100% over 30 minutes with automatic rollback on error rate spike
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with Rust tracing; custom metrics: tokenization_throughput_rps, detokenization_cache_hit_rate, hsm_operation_latency_ms P50/P99, migration_records_per_second, vault_size_gauge
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS blue/green with automatic rollback; migration batches track last validated legacy record for rollback; CloudHSM keeps prior key version active for 90 days post-rotation

target_output.thinking_summary: Building a drop-in PAN vault replacement with two dominant constraints: 100% API signature compatibility and p99 < 20ms. Rust/Axum with AWS CloudHSM in the same VPC gives the latency headroom. The migration batch job is the highest-risk operation - must validate every round-trip before deleting legacy ciphertext and auto-pause on error rate threshold. Redis detokenization cache is a latency optimization but must not bypass audit logging for compliance. Format-preserving tokenization chosen to preserve downstream system compatibility.
target_output.title: Payment Card Tokenization Vault Migration API - HSM-Backed PAN Storage Service
target_output.executive_summary: A high-performance Rust/Axum API service replacing a legacy PAN vault with modern AWS CloudHSM-backed tokenization infrastructure. The service maintains 100% backward-compatible endpoint signatures enabling zero-code-change migration for existing callers. Key capabilities: format-preserving tokenization with p99 < 20ms, mTLS caller authentication, Redis-cached detokenization with forced audit logging on all PAN access, and a batch migration job that re-encrypts legacy vault records under the new HSM key hierarchy with automatic rollback on validation failure.
target_output.architecture_overview: Rust/Axum service runs on ECS Fargate in private subnets with an ALB handling mTLS termination and client certificate validation. All cryptographic operations - PAN encryption during tokenization and PAN decryption during detokenization - are performed via AWS CloudHSM cluster in the same VPC, keeping HSM call latency under 10ms. Token-to-ciphertext mappings are stored in PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ. Detokenization responses are cached in Redis with 300-second TTL, but audit log entries are written for every detokenization call regardless of cache hit. A separate migration ECS task re-encrypts legacy vault records in configurable batches with per-record round-trip validation before committing.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Rust 1.78 with Axum 0.7; custom format-preserving tokenization library using AES-FF1 algorithm; AWS CloudHSM SDK for key operations. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with KMS encryption. Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 with AUTH and TLS. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate (private subnets), ALB with mTLS, CloudHSM cluster, ACM Private CA, Secrets Manager. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with CloudHSM simulator for integration tests. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Tokenization: accept raw PAN over mTLS, encrypt via CloudHSM AES-FF1, store ciphertext in vault, return format-preserving token. Detokenization: accept token, check Redis cache (audit log written regardless), if cache miss decrypt via CloudHSM, return PAN to authorized callers only. Token Lifecycle: check token existence, delete token and ciphertext on request, purge expired tokens via scheduled job. Legacy Migration: batch re-encrypt legacy vault records under new HSM key hierarchy with round-trip validation, pause on error threshold, mark records migrated. Legacy Compatibility: identical endpoint URLs, request/response schemas, and error codes as legacy vault.
target_output.system_components: ALB with mTLS client certificate validation, ECS Fargate cluster (tokenization service tasks + migration task), CloudHSM cluster (active-active 2 HSMs), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (token vault), ElastiCache Redis 7 (detokenization cache, TTL 300s), ACM Private CA (client certificate issuance), Secrets Manager (HSM partition credentials), Kinesis Firehose (audit log shipping to SIEM), Datadog agent sidecar (APM and metrics).
target_output.workflows: Tokenization: caller sends POST /v1/tokenize over mTLS with raw PAN in JSON body; ALB validates client certificate CN against allowed callers list; Axum handler calls CloudHSM AES-FF1 encrypt (expected 5-12ms), generates format-preserving token, writes (token, ciphertext, hsm_key_version) to PostgreSQL vault via prepared statement, writes audit_log entry with caller_id and operation, returns token. Detokenization: caller sends POST /v1/detokenize with token; Axum checks caller detokenize permission in caller_permissions; writes audit_log entry (caller, token, timestamp) before any cache lookup; checks Redis cache (TTL 300s); on cache miss calls CloudHSM AES-FF1 decrypt, populates cache, returns PAN; on cache hit returns cached PAN. Migration Batch: migration task reads legacy vault records in 1,000-record batches; for each record: decrypt PAN using legacy HSM key via legacy_hsm_client, re-encrypt using new CloudHSM key, store new ciphertext, round-trip validate by detokenizing and comparing PANs; if validation succeeds mark record migrated=true; if error_rate for batch > 0.001% pause migration, create PagerDuty alert, await manual resume.
target_output.data_model: tokens (token VARCHAR(19) PK COMMENT 'format-preserving AES-FF1 token matching PAN digit pattern', ciphertext BYTEA NOT NULL, hsm_key_version VARCHAR(64), card_expiry DATE, created_at TIMESTAMP, last_accessed_at TIMESTAMP). vault_entries (entry_id UUID PK, token VARCHAR(19) FK, encryption_algorithm ENUM aes_ff1, key_version VARCHAR, migrated_from_legacy BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, migrated_at TIMESTAMP, legacy_token VARCHAR(19) COMMENT 'original legacy vault token for mapping during migration'). caller_permissions (caller_id VARCHAR PK COMMENT 'TLS certificate CN', can_tokenize BOOLEAN, can_detokenize BOOLEAN, rate_limit_rps INT). audit_logs (log_id UUID PK, caller_id VARCHAR, operation ENUM tokenize/detokenize/delete, token VARCHAR(19) COMMENT 'token only - no PAN in audit logs', cache_hit BOOLEAN, timestamp TIMESTAMP, request_id UUID). migration_batches (batch_id UUID PK, legacy_range_start VARCHAR, legacy_range_end VARCHAR, records_processed INT, records_validated INT, error_count INT, status ENUM running/completed/paused/failed/rolled_back, started_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP). Payment processing entities: tokenization_transactions (id UUID PK, token FK, merchant_id VARCHAR, transaction_type ENUM tokenize/detokenize/validate, pan_last4 CHAR(4), card_brand VARCHAR, requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM success/failure, latency_ms INT), merchant_accounts (id UUID PK, merchant_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, dba_name VARCHAR, settlement_currency CHAR(3), mcc_code CHAR(4), vault_quota_limit INT, active BOOLEAN), settlement_batches (id UUID PK, merchant_id FK, batch_date DATE, transaction_count INT, total_amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), settlement_status ENUM pending/settled/failed).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/tokenize body: {pan: string} response: {token: string}. POST /v1/detokenize body: {token: string} response: {pan: string} - restricted to callers with can_detokenize=true. GET /v1/tokens/{token}/exists response: {exists: boolean}. DELETE /v1/tokens/{token} response: 204. POST /v1/admin/migrate/start body: {batch_size: int, error_threshold: float} response: {batch_id: string}. GET /v1/admin/migrate/{batch_id}/status. All endpoints also served at legacy vault URL paths as aliases. Auth: mTLS only - no bearer token or API key fallback. Error responses match legacy vault schema exactly.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS Level 1 controls: all PAN encryption/decryption via FIPS 140-2 Level 3 CloudHSM - no software encryption fallback; PANs never written to application logs, audit logs, or monitoring metrics - token used as identifier throughout; mTLS enforced at ALB layer with ACM Private CA-issued client certificates; caller_permissions table prevents unauthorized detokenization; audit_log written before cache lookup to ensure no PAN access escapes logging. Key management: CloudHSM partition credentials in Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation; prior HSM key version retained 90 days post-rotation for legacy migration compatibility. Network: all Fargate tasks in private subnets; no internet gateway on vault subnet; VPC endpoints for Secrets Manager, CloudHSM, and RDS. QSA annual assessment covers this service as highest-priority PCI scope.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate tasks in private subnets behind ALB; ALB configured with mTLS listener policy requiring client certificate from ACM Private CA. CloudHSM cluster: 2 HSMs in active-active in us-east-1a and us-east-1b. RDS Multi-AZ with 7-day automated backups and 1-hour PITR. GitHub Actions CI: Rust tests against CloudHSM simulator (PKCS11 mock), integration tests against staging vault with non-production test PANs, Docker build, ECR push, ECS blue/green deployment shifting 10% -> 50% -> 100% traffic over 30 minutes with automatic rollback if p99 latency exceeds 25ms or error rate exceeds 0.01%. Migration task runs as separate ECS task definition with 1 CPU 2GB, invoked via admin API.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on Fargate sidecars: distributed traces for tokenize and detokenize spanning Axum handler, PostgreSQL write, and CloudHSM call. Custom metrics: tokenization_rps, detokenization_cache_hit_rate (target > 85%), hsm_operation_latency_ms (P50/P95/P99 - alert if P99 > 15ms), vault_size_records_total, migration_progress_pct, migration_error_rate. PagerDuty alerts: p99 latency > 20ms (SLA breach risk), HSM error rate > 0.1%, migration error rate > 0.001%, audit log shipping delay > 60s.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: CloudHSM cluster ($1.45/hr per HSM) ~$2,100/month for 2 HSMs. ECS Fargate scales on request count (target 500 rps/task); steady-state 2-4 tasks estimated $120/month. RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large ~$380/month. ElastiCache Redis cache.t4g.medium ~$60/month. Total infrastructure ~$2,700/month. Migration task only active during migration window, minimal ongoing cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - New Vault Stand-Up (weeks 1-4): CloudHSM provisioning and key hierarchy setup, tokenization and detokenization API with mTLS, PostgreSQL vault schema, audit logging to SIEM, staging validation with synthetic PANs. Phase 2 - Legacy Migration (weeks 5-10): Migration batch job with round-trip validation, parallel-run mode where both legacy and new vault serve requests and responses are compared, caller-by-caller cutover starting with lowest-volume internal services. Phase 3 - Full Cutover and Decommission (weeks 11-16): All callers migrated to new vault, legacy vault read-only mode, 4-week validation period, legacy vault decommission and HSM hardware return.
target_output.development_guidelines: PAN values must never appear in: Rust log! macros, Datadog span attributes, PostgreSQL query logs, or CloudWatch logs - use token or request_id as identifier. Format-preserving tokenization library has separate crate with property-based tests verifying: uniqueness, format preservation, and round-trip correctness. Migration job unit tests mock both legacy HSM client and new CloudHSM SDK. All PRs touching tokenization logic require security lead approval.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CloudHSM single-region failure during migration renders vault unavailable. Mitigation: CloudHSM active-active across two AZs; migration can pause and resume without data loss from checkpoints. Risk: Legacy vault re-encryption produces token collisions with new vault token space if formats overlap. Mitigation: new token generation uses different AES-FF1 key and validates uniqueness constraint in PostgreSQL before committing. Risk: p99 latency SLA breach under high load due to CloudHSM throughput limits. Mitigation: 2 HSMs in cluster; Redis cache absorbs detokenization load; load test at 2x expected peak before cutover. Tradeoff: Redis detokenization cache reduces HSM call rate 85% but introduces 300s window where stale PAN could be returned after token deletion - mitigated by explicit cache invalidation on DELETE.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Format-preserving vs random tokens: format-preserving AES-FF1 chosen to preserve downstream system compatibility with PAN-format validation logic. mTLS vs API keys: mTLS only - no API key fallback; legacy callers must present certificates before cutover. Migration parallelism: 1,000 records/batch with sequential validation; parallel batches deferred due to HSM throughput limits.

metadata.notes: Weak-quality first pass for PAN vault migration API - several sections at minimum word count, migration rollback logic thin, HSM latency analysis surface-level.

project_goal.value: API-first open banking data aggregation service connecting to 300+ UK and EU banks via PSD2 APIs, normalizing account and transaction data, and providing a unified REST API for fintech apps to retrieve categorized transaction history, balance feeds, and payment initiation
project_goal.rationale: Fragmented bank API landscape requires a single integration point for fintech partners

target_users.value: Fintech application developers integrating account data and payment initiation capabilities via REST API
target_users.rationale: B2B API consumers building end-user financial apps on top of aggregated bank data

project_class.rationale: Pure API service aggregating and normalizing bank data; no user-facing frontend

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, integrations, public_api, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: API gateway, bank integrations, scheduled consent refresh, and data categorization batch jobs

complexity_level.rationale: 300+ bank integrations with heterogeneous PSD2 API implementations, OAuth 2.0 consent lifecycle management, and ML transaction categorization

risk_level.rationale: Stores bank OAuth refresh tokens enabling ongoing account data access; end-user financial data processed at scale; PSD2 regulatory authorization as AISP/PISP

data_sensitivity.rationale: Bank account numbers, transaction amounts, merchant details, and balance information constitute financial personal data

external_exposure.rationale: API accessible to registered fintech partner companies only; not publicly open to anonymous callers

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials for partner API access; per-partner API keys with scoped permissions; end-user consent managed via Authorization Code Flow redirecting to bank
access_model.rationale: Two-tier auth: partner authenticates to aggregation API; end-user authenticates to their bank via OAuth redirect

feature_scope.value: Bank connection management (OAuth 2.0 consent flow, token storage, refresh), account data retrieval (balances, transaction history), ML transaction categorization, payment initiation (PSD2 PISP), consent management (list, revoke), partner API with OpenAPI spec
feature_scope.rationale: Full AISP and PISP capabilities under PSD2 license

mvp_scope.value: Bank connections and transaction history retrieval for 20 major UK banks; ML categorization; partner API with OpenAPI spec; consent management
mvp_scope.rationale: UK market first; EU bank integrations in Phase 2

security_baseline.value: OAuth refresh tokens encrypted at rest with KMS; TLS 1.3 for all connections; partner JWT validation; bank OAuth tokens never logged; GDPR data minimization
security_baseline.rationale: Bank OAuth tokens are credentials enabling account access; must be protected as secrets

frontend_stack.value: None - API service; partner developer portal (docs + API keys) is a separate project
frontend_stack.rationale: Pure API service; developer portal managed by separate team

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin; bank integration adapters per institution; background workers for token refresh and categorization
backend_stack.rationale: Go concurrency model handles 300+ bank API integrations efficiently; Gin is lightweight for high-throughput API gateway

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for consent records, account metadata, and transaction store; Redis 7 for API response caching and rate limit counters
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for relational consent and transaction data; Redis for low-latency balance caching

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in eu-west-1 for UK/EU data residency; RDS Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis
hosting_target.rationale: EU region for GDPR and PSD2 data residency requirements

privacy_retention_policy.value: Transaction data retained 90 days after consent revocation; account data deleted within 30 days of revocation; OAuth tokens purged immediately on revocation; GDPR right to erasure supported
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: PSD2 and GDPR data minimization: retain only as long as consent is active plus short grace period

future_scope.value: 300 EU bank integrations, real-time webhook notifications on new transactions, variable recurring payments (VRP) API, financial insights and spending analytics API
future_scope.rationale: EU expansion and advanced payment initiation capabilities in roadmap

constraints.value: PSD2 AISP/PISP regulatory license required before production; bank SCA redirect flow must complete in < 5 minutes; EU data residency mandatory
constraints.rationale: Regulatory and data sovereignty constraints non-negotiable

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM; per-bank API error rates and latency; consent health dashboard; token refresh success rate alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Bank API reliability varies widely; monitoring per-bank failure rates is essential for partner SLA

execution_preference.value: API-first development: OpenAPI spec written first, then implementation; weekly partner feedback cycles
execution_preference.rationale: Partner integration speed depends on stable API contract

compliance_context.value: PSD2 AISP license (FCA registration); GDPR data controller obligations; Open Banking UK (OBIE) standards for UK bank integrations; Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) required for payment initiation
compliance_context.rationale: PSD2 license is a regulatory prerequisite; OBIE standards govern UK bank API format

project.goal: Unified open banking aggregation API for 300+ UK/EU banks with transaction categorization and payment initiation
project.target_users: Fintech partner developers
project.timeline: UK MVP in 6 months; EU expansion in 12 months

backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0; OAuth 2.0 consent flows

security.auth: OAuth 2.0 client credentials for partners; bank OAuth tokens encrypted at rest
security.encryption: TLS 1.3; KMS encryption for refresh tokens

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for consent and transaction data
data.cache: Redis 7 for balance cache and rate limits

constraints.sca_timeout: Bank SCA flow must complete in < 5 minutes

open_questions.categorization_model: Rule-based vs ML transaction categorization for MVP
open_questions.bank_adapter_pattern: Generic OAuth2/OpenBanking adapter vs institution-specific adapters for non-standard implementations

confirmed_decisions.token_storage: Encrypted at rest via KMS; never in application logs

reasoner_reviews.product: The adapter pattern for bank integrations is the right architectural choice - UK OBIE banks share a common API format but EU banks vary wildly. The MVP scope of 20 major UK banks is realistic for a 6-month timeline. Transaction categorization accuracy is a key differentiator for partner retention - the ML model must be trainable on UK merchant names.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The consent refresh job is the most operationally complex component: token expiry windows vary by bank (some hourly, some weekly), and a refresh failure silently breaks data feeds for all end-users of a consent. The refresh worker needs idempotent design and a per-consent health state machine. Redis balance caching with bank-specific TTLs is essential to avoid rate limiting from bank APIs.
reasoner_reviews.security: Bank OAuth refresh tokens are the highest-value credential in the system - compromise gives access to all consented accounts. They must be encrypted with a KMS DEK per consent, not a single application-level key. GDPR consent chain must be auditable: which fintech partner obtained consent, for which end-user, at what time, and via which bank.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Data residency in eu-west-1 requires that bank OAuth tokens and transaction data never transit outside AWS eu-west-1. Partner API keys must also be stored in-region. The 5-minute SCA timeout constraint affects how the consent redirect flow is architected - need a short-lived state token tied to the OAuth redirect_uri.

backend.service_design: Go service with domain packages: consent (OAuth flow, token storage, health state machine), account_data (balance and transaction retrieval via bank adapters), categorization (ML inference via internal model service), payment_initiation (PISP flow via bank payment APIs), partner_api (Gin routes, auth middleware, rate limiting), bank_adapters (per-institution adapter implementing BankAdapter interface)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0. Partner auth via OAuth 2.0 client credentials (JWT). Bank connections via Authorization Code Flow with PKCE. Cursor-based pagination on transaction endpoints. Idempotency keys on payment initiation.
backend.business_modules: BankAdapter interface: GetAccounts(), GetTransactions(from, to), GetBalance(), InitiatePayment(). OBIE adapter handles 20 UK major banks via common spec. Per-bank adapters for non-OBIE institutions override methods with institution-specific behavior.
backend.background_jobs: token_refresh_worker: Go goroutine pool processing consent refresh queue; refreshes tokens 30 minutes before expiry using bank-specific refresh TTL; marks consent as degraded on refresh failure. transaction_sync_worker: incremental transaction fetch per active consent on configurable schedule. categorization_batch: applies ML model to uncategorized transactions in batches of 500.

security.auth_design: Partner OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow issues JWT with partner_id claim and scopes. Bank OAuth tokens stored with KMS envelope encryption (DEK per consent). Refresh tokens in PostgreSQL consent_records table, never in Redis.
security.authorization_model: Partner scopes: accounts:read, transactions:read, payments:write. Per-partner rate limits in Redis. End-user data isolated by user_id foreign key on all transaction and account tables.
security.secrets_management: Bank OAuth client credentials (client_id, client_secret per institution) in AWS Secrets Manager with rotation support. KMS CMK for consent token encryption. No bank credentials in code or environment variables.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR deletion job runs daily: identifies consents revoked > 30 days ago, deletes associated transactions, accounts, and OAuth tokens. Audit trail of deletion events retained per GDPR Article 17 documentation requirement.

data.entities: consent_records (consent_id UUID PK, partner_id UUID, user_id VARCHAR, bank_id VARCHAR, access_token_enc BYTEA, refresh_token_enc BYTEA, kms_key_id VARCHAR, scope TEXT[], status ENUM active/degraded/revoked, expires_at TIMESTAMP, last_refreshed_at TIMESTAMP). bank_accounts (account_id UUID PK, consent_id FK, bank_account_ref VARCHAR, account_type ENUM current/savings/credit, currency CHAR(3), balance_cached DECIMAL(18,2), balance_cached_at TIMESTAMP). transaction_records (transaction_id UUID PK, account_id FK, amount DECIMAL(18,2), currency CHAR(3), direction ENUM debit/credit, merchant_name VARCHAR, merchant_category_code CHAR(4), category_label VARCHAR, booked_at TIMESTAMP, bank_transaction_ref VARCHAR). category_labels (label_id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, parent_label_id FK, taxonomy_version VARCHAR). payment_initiations (payment_id UUID PK, consent_id FK, amount DECIMAL(18,2), currency CHAR(3), creditor_iban VARCHAR, reference VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/authorised/executed/failed, bank_payment_ref VARCHAR). refresh_tokens (token_id UUID PK, partner_id FK, hashed_token VARCHAR, scopes TEXT[], expires_at TIMESTAMP, revoked_at TIMESTAMP)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ in eu-west-1. OAuth tokens encrypted via KMS before PostgreSQL write. Redis for: balance cache (bank-specific TTL 30-300s), rate limit counters per partner, consent refresh queue.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate in eu-west-1 private subnets; separate task definitions for API service and background workers; ALB for API; no public subnet access for workers.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Go unit tests with bank adapter mocks, integration tests against Open Banking sandbox environments, Docker build and ECR push, ECS blue/green deployment with 15% canary before full shift.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM: per-bank API latency and error rate, consent refresh success rate, token expiry countdown gauges, transaction sync lag per bank. Custom alert: per-bank error rate > 5% triggers investigation; consent refresh failure rate > 1% triggers on-call.
devops.cost_controls: ECS autoscaling on request count; background workers scale on queue depth; Reserved Instances for baseline RDS and ElastiCache; bank API call budgets per day to avoid rate limit fees.

target_output.thinking_summary: Building a PSD2-regulated open banking aggregation API requires two distinct auth layers: partner OAuth 2.0 client credentials for API access, and bank OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow for end-user consent. The most operationally complex component is the consent lifecycle management - token refresh failures silently break data feeds, so the refresh worker needs a health state machine per consent with degraded status and partner notification. Bank OAuth refresh tokens must use envelope encryption with per-consent KMS DEKs, not a single application key, to minimize blast radius on key compromise.
target_output.title: Open Banking Data Aggregation API - PSD2 AISP/PISP Service for Fintech Partners
target_output.executive_summary: A PSD2-licensed open banking aggregation service built in Go on AWS ECS Fargate that connects to 300+ UK and EU banks via institution-specific adapters, manages end-user OAuth 2.0 consent lifecycles, and exposes a unified partner-facing REST API for transaction history, account balances, ML-categorized spending data, and payment initiation. OAuth refresh tokens are envelope-encrypted per consent with KMS DEKs; GDPR data minimization is enforced via automated post-revocation deletion jobs.
target_output.architecture_overview: Go/Gin API service runs on ECS Fargate behind ALB in eu-west-1, routing partner API requests to domain handlers. Partner authentication uses OAuth 2.0 client credentials (JWT). Bank connections are established via Authorization Code Flow with PKCE - end-users redirect to their bank's SCA flow, returning an auth code that the service exchanges for access and refresh tokens. Access and refresh tokens are envelope-encrypted with per-consent KMS DEKs before PostgreSQL storage. Background worker tasks (separate Fargate task definitions) handle consent token refresh (30 minutes before expiry), incremental transaction sync per active consent, and ML categorization in batches. Redis caches bank balance responses with institution-specific TTLs and stores per-partner rate limit counters. A GDPR deletion job runs daily to purge transaction and account data for consents revoked more than 30 days prior.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22 with Gin 1.9; bank adapters implement BankAdapter interface per institution; Go goroutine pool for background workers. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (eu-west-1). Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 for balance cache, rate limits, and consent refresh queue. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, KMS for token encryption, Secrets Manager for bank OAuth client credentials. Observability: Datadog APM. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Open Banking sandbox integration tests.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Consent Management: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow with PKCE per bank, state token with 5-minute TTL for SCA redirect, refresh token storage with KMS encryption, consent health state machine (active/degraded/revoked), partner notification on degradation. Account Data: GetAccounts() and GetBalance() via BankAdapter, balance cached in Redis per bank TTL. Transaction History: incremental sync from bank API, cursor-based pagination, 90-day history on MVP. ML Categorization: merchant name classification to 50-label taxonomy, applied in 500-record batches by categorization worker. Payment Initiation (PISP): construct payment object, redirect end-user to bank payment SCA flow, poll for execution status, webhook to partner on status change. Partner API: OpenAPI 3.0 spec, client credentials auth, per-partner scoped access.
target_output.system_components: ALB (TLS 1.3 termination), ECS Fargate API tasks (Go/Gin service), ECS Fargate worker tasks (token_refresh_worker + transaction_sync_worker + categorization_batch worker), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (consent records, accounts, transactions), ElastiCache Redis 7 (balance cache + rate limits + refresh queue), KMS CMK (per-consent token encryption DEKs), Secrets Manager (bank OAuth client credentials), 300+ bank OpenAPI adapters (OBIE-compliant and institution-specific), ML categorization model (internal inference service), Datadog agent sidecar.
target_output.workflows: Consent Flow: partner calls POST /v1/connections/initiate with user_id and bank_id; service generates PKCE code_verifier, creates state token (expires 5 minutes), stores in Redis, returns bank authorization_url with redirect_uri pointing to /v1/connections/callback. End-user completes bank SCA, browser redirects to callback with auth code; service validates state token, exchanges auth code for access/refresh tokens via bank token endpoint, encrypts tokens with KMS DEK per consent, stores consent_record, triggers initial transaction sync, returns connection_id to partner. Token Refresh: token_refresh_worker dequeues consents expiring within 30 minutes from Redis sorted set; calls bank token refresh endpoint; on success updates access_token_enc, resets expiry; on failure after 3 retries marks consent status=degraded, publishes degraded_connection webhook to partner; on bank returning invalid_grant revokes consent and notifies partner. Transaction Sync: sync worker fetches new transactions from bank API since last_synced_at bookmark; applies ML categorization service call in batch; upserts to transaction_records with bank_transaction_ref as deduplication key; updates account balance cache in Redis.
target_output.data_model: consent_records (consent_id UUID PK, partner_id UUID FK, user_id VARCHAR, bank_id VARCHAR, access_token_enc BYTEA NOT NULL, refresh_token_enc BYTEA NOT NULL, kms_key_id VARCHAR NOT NULL COMMENT 'per-consent KMS DEK reference', scope TEXT[], status ENUM active/degraded/revoked, expires_at TIMESTAMP, last_refreshed_at TIMESTAMP, created_at TIMESTAMP). bank_accounts (account_id UUID PK, consent_id UUID FK, bank_account_ref VARCHAR, account_type ENUM current/savings/credit/isa, currency CHAR(3), balance_cached DECIMAL(18,2), balance_cached_at TIMESTAMP, sort_code VARCHAR(6), account_number_masked VARCHAR). transaction_records (transaction_id UUID PK, account_id UUID FK, amount DECIMAL(18,2), currency CHAR(3), direction ENUM debit/credit, merchant_name VARCHAR, merchant_category_code CHAR(4), category_label VARCHAR REFERENCES category_labels, category_confidence DECIMAL(4,3), booked_at TIMESTAMP, bank_transaction_ref VARCHAR UNIQUE PER account). payment_initiations (payment_id UUID PK, consent_id UUID FK, amount DECIMAL(18,2), currency CHAR(3), creditor_iban VARCHAR(34), reference VARCHAR(35), status ENUM pending/authorised/executed/failed/cancelled, bank_payment_ref VARCHAR, initiated_at TIMESTAMP, executed_at TIMESTAMP). refresh_tokens (token_id UUID PK, partner_id UUID FK, hashed_token VARCHAR(64), scopes TEXT[], expires_at TIMESTAMP, revoked_at TIMESTAMP). category_labels (label_id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, parent_label_id UUID FK, taxonomy_version VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: Partner REST API with OpenAPI 3.0. POST /v1/connections/initiate body: {user_id, bank_id, scopes[]} returns {authorization_url, state_token}. GET /v1/connections/{connection_id} returns consent status and account list. GET /v1/connections/{connection_id}/accounts returns account list with cached balances. GET /v1/accounts/{account_id}/transactions?from=ISO8601&to=ISO8601&limit=100&cursor=X returns paginated transactions with category_label. GET /v1/accounts/{account_id}/balance returns current balance with cache age. POST /v1/payments/initiate body: {consent_id, creditor_iban, amount, currency, reference} returns {payment_id, authorization_url}. DELETE /v1/connections/{connection_id} revokes consent and triggers GDPR deletion. Auth: POST /v1/oauth/token (client credentials) returns partner JWT. Rate limit: 1,000 req/min per partner.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PSD2 compliance: registered AISP/PISP with FCA; OBIE eIDAS certificate for UK bank API authentication; Strong Customer Authentication handled entirely by bank SCA redirect flow. OAuth token security: bank access and refresh tokens encrypted with per-consent KMS envelope encryption DEK before PostgreSQL write; tokens never appear in application logs, Datadog traces, or Redis; token decryption only in bank adapter layer. Partner auth: OAuth 2.0 client credentials with short-lived JWTs (1-hour expiry); client secrets stored as bcrypt hashes. GDPR: end-user consent records link to partner_id and bank_id for audit; right-to-erasure job deletes transactions, accounts, and tokens within 30 days of revocation; data residency enforced in eu-west-1 with no cross-region replication. Security headers on all API responses: HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate in eu-west-1 private subnets; API tasks behind ALB with TLS 1.3; worker tasks on separate task definition without ALB, triggered via Redis queue. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ with 7-day automated backups and daily snapshots to S3. ElastiCache Redis with AUTH token, in-transit TLS, and daily snapshots. GitHub Actions CI: Go unit tests with bank adapter mocks and Open Banking UK sandbox integration tests, Docker build, ECR push, ECS blue/green with 20% canary. Secrets Manager for bank OAuth client credentials with automated rotation for institutions supporting it. Datadog agent as ECS sidecar for APM and custom metrics.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces spanning partner API request through bank adapter call. Custom metrics: per_bank_api_error_rate (gauge by bank_id - alert if > 5%), consent_refresh_success_rate (alert if < 99%), transaction_sync_lag_minutes (P95 per bank), active_consent_count, degraded_consent_count (gauge - alert if > 10). Dashboards: Bank Health (per-bank error rates and latency heatmap), Consent Health (refresh success rate trend, degraded consent count), Partner Usage (request rate and error rate per partner). PagerDuty: bank API error rate > 5%, consent refresh failure rate > 1%, token expiry within 1 hour with no successful refresh.
