target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS API tasks autoscale on ALB request count (target 200 req/s/task); worker tasks scale on Redis queue depth. RDS db.r6g.large Multi-AZ ~$380/month. ElastiCache cache.t4g.medium ~$60/month. Fargate costs vary with request volume; estimated $300-500/month at 50 partners. Bank API rate limits constrain transaction sync frequency more than compute costs. KMS API calls ~$0.03/1,000 token encrypt/decrypt operations.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - UK MVP (months 1-6): 20 major UK banks via OBIE adapters, consent lifecycle management (connect, refresh, revoke), transaction history and balance retrieval, rule-based transaction categorization for MVP speed, partner API with OpenAPI spec, 5 pilot fintech partners. Phase 2 - ML Categorization and Payment Initiation (months 7-9): ML categorization model replacing rule-based, PISP payment initiation for UK banks, webhook delivery for consent status changes and new transactions, 20+ fintech partners. Phase 3 - EU Expansion (months 10-18): 280+ EU bank adapters (Berlin Group NextGenPSD2, plus institution-specific), multi-language merchant categorization, VRP (variable recurring payments) API, real-time transaction webhooks via bank event subscriptions where available.
target_output.development_guidelines: BankAdapter interface must be implemented for every institution - no direct HTTP calls to bank APIs outside adapters. OAuth tokens must be decrypted only within the adapter layer - never passed as plaintext across package boundaries. All bank API calls must emit per-bank Datadog metrics with bank_id tag for error rate dashboards. GDPR deletion job must be tested with synthetic consents before production deployment. OpenAPI spec is source of truth - generate Go server stubs from spec, not the reverse.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: bank API spec divergence from OBIE standard causes adapter breakage on bank-side updates. Mitigation: adapter integration tests against bank sandboxes run on weekly schedule in CI; bank change notifications monitored via Open Banking UK changelog. Risk: Redis consent refresh queue overflow during bank outage causes mass token expiry. Mitigation: Redis sorted set with expiry timestamps allows bulk refresh on bank recovery; consent status degraded rather than revoked preserving end-user connection. Risk: KMS DEK proliferation (one per consent) creates high KMS API call volume. Mitigation: DEK caching in memory for 60-second window; KMS request budget alerts at 80% of quota. Tradeoff: per-consent KMS DEKs vs single application key - per-consent reduces blast radius on key compromise but increases operational overhead.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Rule-based vs ML categorization for MVP: rule-based merchant name matching chosen for MVP to avoid model training delay; ML model deployed in Phase 2. Generic vs institution-specific adapters: OBIE BankAdapter interface for UK banks; institution-specific adapters extend base with override methods for non-standard behavior. Balance cache TTL: bank-specific TTL from 30 seconds (real-time banking APIs) to 300 seconds (batch-update banks) stored in bank_configuration table.

metadata.notes: PSD2 open banking aggregation API with consent lifecycle management, per-consent KMS token encryption, and 300+ bank adapter pattern. Good overall but phased_implementation and cost sections slightly thin.

project_goal.value: Automation tool analyzing a SaaS company Stripe billing configuration to identify over-provisioned subscription plans, unused metered features, and suboptimal pricing tier assignments, generating prioritized optimization recommendations with projected annual savings
project_goal.rationale: Manual billing review takes 3 days per quarter; automated tool enables continuous monthly optimization

target_users.value: Finance team and revenue operations analysts reviewing monthly billing reports
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of optimization reports; revenue operations owns pricing tier decisions

project_class.rationale: Scheduled automation running against Stripe API; no interactive user interface

capabilities.rationale: Stripe API integration, data analysis, and report generation

complexity_level.rationale: Single external integration (Stripe), rule-based analysis, scheduled execution

risk_level.rationale: Read-only Stripe API access; no write operations; internal tooling only

data_sensitivity.rationale: Subscription amounts, customer billing details, and revenue projections are financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Internal analytics tool; reports shared within finance team only

access_model.value: Internal invocation only; Lambda execution role with Secrets Manager access for Stripe restricted key
access_model.rationale: No user-facing auth needed; Lambda execution role scoped to minimum permissions

feature_scope.value: Stripe subscription data download, usage record analysis, over-provisioning detection, pricing tier optimization identification, savings projection calculation, CSV and HTML report generation, monthly scheduled execution
feature_scope.rationale: Complete monthly billing review automation

mvp_scope.value: Over-provisioned plan detection and CSV report with savings projections for top 50 optimization opportunities
mvp_scope.rationale: Highest-value analysis deliverable for immediate monthly review

security_baseline.value: Stripe restricted API key (read-only billing scope); key stored in Secrets Manager; no customer card data accessed; reports stored in S3 with SSE
security_baseline.rationale: Read-only Stripe key limits blast radius; S3 SSE for report storage

frontend_stack.value: None - generates CSV and HTML reports delivered to S3 and emailed to finance team distribution list
frontend_stack.rationale: No browser interface; reports consumed via email and S3 link

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for internal API; pandas for data analysis; Stripe Python SDK
backend_stack.rationale: Python has best-in-class Stripe SDK and pandas for billing data analysis

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS for historical analysis snapshots; no cache required
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for month-over-month comparison queries; no caching needed for monthly batch

hosting_target.value: AWS Lambda for scheduled execution; S3 for report storage; EventBridge for monthly trigger
hosting_target.rationale: Lambda serverless is cost-optimal for monthly batch with no idle infrastructure

privacy_retention_policy.value: Billing snapshots retained 24 months for trend analysis; reports deleted from S3 after 12 months; no customer PII in reports beyond Stripe customer_id
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 2-year trend data needed for pricing model analysis; 12-month report retention adequate

future_scope.value: Automated Slack approval workflow for recommended tier downgrades, integration with CRM to cross-reference customer health scores before recommending changes
future_scope.rationale: Automate the recommendation-to-action step post-approval

constraints.value: Stripe API read-only key only; must not make any write calls to Stripe; reports must not contain raw card data or full bank account numbers
constraints.rationale: Finance team does not need card data; read-only scope limits accidental modification risk

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for Lambda execution metrics; email alert on Lambda failure
observability_baseline.rationale: Simple scheduled job; CloudWatch covers execution monitoring adequately

execution_preference.value: Monthly EventBridge schedule on first business day; ad-hoc Lambda invocation for quarter-end reviews
execution_preference.rationale: Monthly cadence matches billing review cycle; ad-hoc for QBR preparation

compliance_context.value: Internal Stripe billing data treated as confidential financial data; reports distributed only to finance team; Stripe API key access logged via CloudTrail
compliance_context.rationale: Revenue data is sensitive; access audit trail required for SOX compliance evidence

project.goal: Monthly Stripe billing optimization analysis with prioritized savings recommendations
project.target_users: Finance team and revenue operations analysts

backend.framework: Stripe Python SDK; pandas for analysis
backend.api: No user-facing API; internal Lambda handler

security.auth: Lambda execution role; Stripe restricted read-only key in Secrets Manager
security.encryption: S3 SSE for reports; TLS for Stripe API calls
security.compliance: Internal SOX evidence; no card data accessed

constraints.card_data: No raw card data in reports

open_questions.categorization_rules: Rule thresholds for 'unused feature' detection need calibration with finance team

confirmed_decisions.delivery: CSV and HTML reports to S3 + SES email to finance distribution list
confirmed_decisions.schedule: First business day of each month via EventBridge

reasoner_reviews.product: The analysis logic is the core value: detecting customers on Enterprise plans who have never used SSO, API access, or advanced reporting features represents direct downgrade revenue impact modeling. The savings projection methodology needs to be transparent in the report so finance can validate assumptions before acting.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Lambda with a 15-minute timeout should handle the Stripe API pagination for a typical SaaS company with under 100K subscriptions. For larger subscription bases, the pagination loop should checkpoint progress to PostgreSQL so a timeout doesn't restart from page 1. pandas is the right choice for the cross-tabulation analysis needed to compute feature usage rates by pricing tier.
reasoner_reviews.security: Stripe restricted key must have only the minimum read scopes: subscriptions, customers, invoices, and usage records. No payment methods, bank accounts, or charges scope. The key should be rotated quarterly and the old key invalidated within 24 hours of rotation. CloudTrail will log Secrets Manager GetSecretValue calls providing an audit trail of tool executions.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Stripe API rate limits (100 req/s for most endpoints) mean the full subscription download for 50K+ customers takes 8-10 minutes with proper pagination and backoff. The Lambda timeout should be set to 14 minutes with a CloudWatch alarm if execution approaches 12 minutes.

backend.service_design: Lambda handler with pipeline stages: stripe_extractor (paginated subscription and usage record download), snapshot_writer (persist to PostgreSQL billing_snapshots), analysis_engine (pandas DataFrames for over-provisioning and usage analysis), report_generator (CSV + HTML with savings projections), report_delivery (S3 upload + SES email)
backend.business_modules: OverProvisioningDetector: customers on plan_tier >= premium with zero usage of premium_features in last 90 days. UnusedMeterDetector: metered feature usage_records with zero reported_quantity for consecutive billing periods. TierOptimizer: maps current plan to lowest-cost plan covering actual feature_usage_summary; computes monthly_revenue_delta as savings_projection. SavingsRanker: sort recommendations by projected_annual_saving DESC for prioritized CSV output.
backend.background_jobs: Single Lambda function invoked by EventBridge rule (first business day monthly); checkpoints pagination progress to PostgreSQL; resumes from last page on retry
backend.failure_handling: Stripe API rate limit: exponential backoff with jitter on 429 responses; Lambda DLQ for failed executions triggers SNS alert to finance team alias

security.auth_design: Lambda execution role with IAM policy: secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on Stripe key ARN, s3:PutObject on reports bucket, ses:SendEmail to finance distribution list only
security.secrets_management: Stripe restricted API key (subscriptions:read, customers:read, invoices:read, usage_records:read scopes only) stored in Secrets Manager; quarterly rotation reminder via CloudWatch Events
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail logs all Secrets Manager GetSecretValue calls and S3 PutObject operations; Lambda execution logs in CloudWatch with execution start/end timestamps and record counts for SOX audit evidence

data.entities: billing_snapshots (snapshot_id UUID PK, run_date DATE, customer_id VARCHAR, subscription_id VARCHAR, plan_id VARCHAR, plan_name VARCHAR, monthly_amount DECIMAL(10,2), feature_usage_json JSONB, snapshot_created_at TIMESTAMP), optimization_recommendations (recommendation_id UUID PK, snapshot_id FK, customer_id VARCHAR, recommendation_type ENUM downgrade/remove_meter/tier_change, current_plan_id VARCHAR, suggested_plan_id VARCHAR, monthly_saving DECIMAL(10,2), annual_saving DECIMAL(10,2), confidence ENUM high/medium/low, reason VARCHAR, actioned_at TIMESTAMP), analysis_runs (run_id UUID PK, run_date DATE, subscriptions_analyzed INT, recommendations_generated INT, total_projected_annual_saving DECIMAL(12,2), report_s3_key VARCHAR, status ENUM completed/failed, completed_at TIMESTAMP)
data.storage_design: RDS PostgreSQL db.t4g.micro adequate for monthly batch volumes; S3 bucket with SSE-S3 for report storage; 12-month lifecycle policy on reports prefix
data.analytics_events: analysis_run_completed (run_id, subscriptions_analyzed, total_projected_saving, report_url) published to internal analytics SNS topic

devops.deployment_topology: Lambda function in VPC private subnet for RDS access; S3 and Secrets Manager accessed via VPC endpoints; EventBridge rule triggers on cron schedule
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest unit tests with Stripe API mocked via responses library, Lambda package build, deploy via AWS CDK
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch: Lambda duration, error count, memory utilization; custom metric: subscriptions_analyzed_count, recommendations_generated_count, total_projected_saving_usd; alarm on Lambda error or duration > 12 minutes
devops.cost_controls: Lambda invocation cost <$0.01/month; RDS db.t4g.micro ~$15/month; S3 storage minimal; total tool cost <$25/month

target_output.thinking_summary: Simple monthly batch automation against Stripe read-only API. Key design decisions: Lambda with PostgreSQL for pagination checkpointing handles the 50K+ subscription paginated download within timeout limits; pandas DataFrame analysis for cross-tabulating feature usage by pricing tier is straightforward; savings projections must be clearly documented in the report methodology for finance team trust. The compliance_context field triggered by data_sensitivity=financial requires noting SOX audit trail via CloudTrail for Secrets Manager access.
target_output.title: Stripe Billing Optimization Automation - Monthly SaaS Subscription Analysis Tool
target_output.executive_summary: A scheduled AWS Lambda automation that pulls all active Stripe subscription and usage records monthly, applies rule-based analysis to identify customers on over-provisioned pricing tiers and with unused metered features, and generates a prioritized CSV and HTML report with projected annual savings for finance team review. The tool uses a read-only Stripe restricted API key stored in Secrets Manager and produces no write operations against Stripe.
target_output.architecture_overview: EventBridge cron rule fires on the first business day of each month, invoking a Lambda function in a VPC private subnet. The Lambda downloads all Stripe subscriptions and associated usage records via paginated API calls with checkpoint state stored in PostgreSQL to handle large subscription bases within Lambda timeout limits. A pandas analysis pipeline cross-tabulates feature usage against pricing tiers to identify downgrade and meter removal opportunities. Reports are uploaded to S3 with SSE and emailed to the finance distribution list via SES. All Secrets Manager access is logged via CloudTrail for SOX audit evidence.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12 on AWS Lambda (3GB memory, 14-minute timeout). Libraries: Stripe Python SDK 8.x, pandas 2.x, jinja2 for HTML report templating. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 db.t4g.micro for billing snapshots and pagination checkpoints. Infrastructure: EventBridge for scheduling, S3 for report storage (SSE-S3), SES for email delivery, Secrets Manager for Stripe API key, VPC endpoints for Secrets Manager and S3. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with AWS CDK deployment.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Stripe Data Extraction: paginated download of active subscriptions, subscription items (plan and quantity per line item), and metered usage_records for configurable lookback period (default 90 days). Over-Provisioning Detection: flag customers on paid tiers with zero usage of gated features (SSO logins, API calls, advanced report views) for entire lookback period. Unused Meter Detection: flag metered subscription items with consecutive billing periods reporting zero quantity. Tier Optimization: map each customer to lowest-cost plan that covers their actual feature usage; compute monthly and annual revenue delta as projected saving. Report Generation: prioritized CSV of top 200 recommendations sorted by annual_saving DESC; HTML summary with total projected saving, breakdown by recommendation type, and methodology notes. Trend Comparison: month-over-month change in total projected savings to track whether prior recommendations were actioned.
target_output.system_components: EventBridge rule (monthly cron schedule), Lambda function (Python 3.12 analysis pipeline), RDS PostgreSQL (billing_snapshots, optimization_recommendations, analysis_runs), S3 bucket (reports with SSE and 12-month lifecycle), SES (email delivery to finance distribution list), Secrets Manager (Stripe restricted key), VPC endpoints (Secrets Manager, S3, RDS access without internet routing), CloudWatch (Lambda metrics and alarms), CloudTrail (Secrets Manager access audit log).
target_output.workflows: Monthly Analysis Run: EventBridge invokes Lambda on first business day of month. Lambda retrieves Stripe restricted key from Secrets Manager. Lambda calls Stripe subscriptions.list() with pagination, writing each page of subscription records to billing_snapshots in PostgreSQL and recording last_seen_cursor for checkpoint. After full extraction, pandas loads billing_snapshots into DataFrame, joins with usage_records, applies OverProvisioningDetector and UnusedMeterDetector rules, computes tier optimization savings projections, ranks by annual_saving DESC. Lambda writes optimization_recommendations to PostgreSQL. Jinja2 templates render CSV and HTML report. Lambda uploads reports to S3 at reports/{YYYY-MM}/billing-optimization-{YYYY-MM-DD}.{csv,html}. Lambda sends SES email to finance distribution list with report download links. analysis_runs record updated with status=completed and total_projected_annual_saving. On Lambda error: DLQ receives failed event, SNS notification to finance-alerts email alias. Checkpoint Resume: if Lambda times out mid-extraction, next EventBridge invocation (or manual trigger) reads last_seen_cursor from analysis_runs and resumes Stripe API pagination from that cursor.
target_output.data_model: billing_snapshots (snapshot_id UUID PK, run_date DATE, customer_id VARCHAR COMMENT 'Stripe cus_XXX id', subscription_id VARCHAR, plan_id VARCHAR, plan_name VARCHAR, plan_tier ENUM starter/growth/professional/enterprise, monthly_amount_usd DECIMAL(10,2), feature_usage_json JSONB COMMENT 'map of feature_key to usage_count over lookback period', meter_usage_json JSONB COMMENT 'map of meter_id to total_quantity over lookback period', snapshot_created_at TIMESTAMP). optimization_recommendations (recommendation_id UUID PK, run_date DATE, customer_id VARCHAR, subscription_id VARCHAR, recommendation_type ENUM downgrade_plan/remove_unused_meter/reduce_quantity, current_plan_id VARCHAR, suggested_plan_id VARCHAR, monthly_saving_usd DECIMAL(10,2), annual_saving_usd DECIMAL(10,2), confidence ENUM high/medium/low COMMENT 'high=zero usage 90d, medium=low usage 90d, low=statistical estimate', reason VARCHAR, actioned_at TIMESTAMP). analysis_runs (run_id UUID PK, run_date DATE, last_seen_cursor VARCHAR COMMENT 'Stripe API pagination cursor for checkpoint resume', subscriptions_analyzed INT, recommendations_generated INT, total_projected_annual_saving_usd DECIMAL(12,2), report_s3_csv_key VARCHAR, report_s3_html_key VARCHAR, status ENUM running/completed/failed, started_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP). Billing financial entities: customer_accounts (id UUID PK, stripe_customer_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, company_name VARCHAR, subscription_plan VARCHAR, billing_currency CHAR(3), account_balance_cents BIGINT, credit_balance_cents BIGINT, payment_method_last4 CHAR(4)), invoice_records (id UUID PK, customer_id FK, stripe_invoice_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, invoice_date DATE, subtotal_cents BIGINT, tax_cents BIGINT, total_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), status ENUM draft/open/paid/voided/uncollectible, settlement_date DATE), payment_transactions (id UUID PK, invoice_id FK, stripe_charge_id VARCHAR, amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), payment_method ENUM card/ach/wire, status ENUM succeeded/failed/refunded, processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No partner-facing or user-facing API. Internal invocation only. Lambda handler signature: handler(event, context) where event may include {force_rerun: bool, lookback_days: int} for ad-hoc manual invocations. Results accessible via S3 presigned URL in SES email. Internal admin can invoke Lambda manually via AWS console or CLI for quarter-end ad-hoc runs: aws lambda invoke --function-name stripe-billing-optimizer --payload '{"lookback_days": 180}' response.json.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Stripe API key security: restricted key with scopes limited to subscriptions:read, customers:read, invoices:read, usage_records:read - no payment_methods, charges, or payout scopes; stored in Secrets Manager; quarterly rotation. Lambda execution role IAM policy follows least-privilege: secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on key ARN only, s3:PutObject on reports bucket prefix only, ses:SendEmail to finance distribution list ARN only, rds-db:connect to specific RDS instance. SOX audit evidence: CloudTrail captures every GetSecretValue call with Lambda execution role ARN and timestamp; CloudWatch logs capture subscriptions_analyzed count and recommendations_generated count per run. Reports never contain card numbers, bank account details, or customer email addresses - only Stripe customer_id and plan metadata.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: pytest with Stripe SDK mocked via responses library, CDK synth diff review in PR. AWS CDK stack deploys: Lambda function with VPC config, EventBridge rule, IAM role, Secrets Manager reference, S3 bucket with lifecycle policy, SES configuration set. Lambda deployed with 3GB memory and 14-minute timeout. CloudWatch alarm triggers SNS alert to engineering team if Lambda duration > 12 minutes or error count > 0. Lambda DLQ configured: failed executions publish to SQS DLQ with SNS notification to finance-alerts alias.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Lambda metrics: duration (alarm > 12 minutes), error_count (alarm > 0), concurrent_executions. Custom CloudWatch metrics published by Lambda: subscriptions_analyzed_count, recommendations_generated_count, total_projected_saving_usd (enables month-over-month trend in CloudWatch dashboard). CloudWatch Dashboard: Billing Optimizer - monthly run history, projected savings trend, execution duration trend. CloudTrail: all Secrets Manager access events for SOX audit log export to S3.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Lambda: <$0.01/month (monthly invocation, 14-minute max duration at 3GB = ~$0.05 per run). RDS db.t4g.micro: ~$15/month. S3 storage: minimal (reports are KB-sized). SES: negligible. Total tool cost ~$20/month. At 200K+ subscriptions, Lambda may need upgrading to Step Functions for pagination orchestration to handle multi-hour extraction, but adequate for typical SaaS up to 100K subscriptions.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP (weeks 1-3): Stripe API extraction pipeline with pagination checkpoint, over-provisioning detection rules (zero premium feature usage in 90 days), basic savings projection, CSV report to S3 and SES email. Phase 2 - Enhanced Analysis (week 4): Unused meter detection, tier optimization mapping, HTML report with methodology section, month-over-month trend comparison, PostgreSQL historical snapshots for trend analysis. Phase 3 - Future Approval Workflow (post-MVP): Slack workflow for finance team to flag recommendations as approved, integration with CRM to check customer health score before including downgrade recommendation in report.
target_output.development_guidelines: Stripe API calls must use retry-with-backoff wrapper on 429 and 500 responses. Pagination cursor stored to PostgreSQL after every 100-page batch to enable checkpoint resume. No customer names or email addresses in billing_snapshots or reports - use Stripe customer_id only. Analysis rules documented as Python docstrings with example inputs/outputs. pytest coverage must include: pagination checkpoint resume test, over-provisioning detection edge cases, savings projection calculation validation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Stripe API rate limit changes reduce available throughput mid-extraction causing Lambda timeout before checkpoint. Mitigation: checkpoint every 100 pages; Lambda retry from DLQ resumes from last cursor. Risk: Analysis rules incorrectly flag enterprise customers with intermittent feature usage as over-provisioned. Mitigation: 90-day lookback window and confidence tiers (high=zero usage, medium=low usage) allow finance team to filter by confidence before actioning recommendations. Tradeoff: monthly batch vs daily incremental - monthly batch chosen for simplicity and alignment with billing cycle; daily incremental deferred to future if finance team needs more frequent analysis.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Lookback period for feature usage analysis: 90 days chosen as default, configurable via Lambda event payload for ad-hoc runs with longer windows. Savings projection methodology: current plan MRR minus suggested plan MRR, annualized, with no churn probability adjustment in MVP - methodology documented in HTML report header. Customer identification in reports: Stripe customer_id only - finance team maps to account names via internal CRM; no PII in tool output.

metadata.notes: Simple Stripe billing optimization Lambda tool - weak quality with thinner sections on cost_and_scaling and risks, but domain-specific content throughout.

project_goal.value: Static public compliance disclosure site for a payment institution publishing fee schedules, regulatory notices, T&Cs, and annual reports to replace a costly CMS-hosted site
project_goal.rationale: Regulator link requirements must always resolve to current approved documents; cost reduction by eliminating CMS licensing

target_users.value: Regulators, auditors, and retail customers accessing mandated disclosure documents
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are external parties following regulator-mandated disclosure links

project_class.rationale: No user authentication or server-side logic required; pure content delivery

capabilities.rationale: Static rendering with lightweight analytics to track page access for compliance audit trails

complexity_level.rationale: Static content delivery with no dynamic computation or auth flows

risk_level.rationale: No user data collected, no authentication, no transactional flows

data_sensitivity.rationale: Site serves only publicly mandated disclosure documents with no PII collected

external_exposure.rationale: Regulator-mandated links must be publicly accessible without authentication

access_model.value: Anonymous read-only access; no accounts or login required
access_model.rationale: Disclosure documents are public by regulatory mandate

feature_scope.value: Fee schedule pages, T&C versions, regulatory notices, annual reports archive, document version history with effective dates
feature_scope.rationale: Full set of mandated disclosure categories for FCA and PSD2 obligations

mvp_scope.value: Fee schedules, T&Cs, and regulatory notices migrated from CMS with version history and effective-date listing
mvp_scope.rationale: These three categories satisfy immediate regulator link requirements

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via CloudFront, S3 bucket private with OAI, no user input accepted, HSTS and CSP response headers
security_baseline.rationale: No attack surface beyond static asset delivery

frontend_stack.value: Astro with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Ideal for content-heavy static sites with zero JS by default and typed Content Collections

backend_stack.value: None — fully static, no server-side runtime
backend_stack.rationale: Static hosting eliminates server costs and maintenance overhead

data_platform.value: None — documents stored in Git repository as Markdown and PDF assets via Git LFS
data_platform.rationale: Version control provides document lineage without a database

hosting_target.value: AWS CloudFront + S3 in eu-west-1
hosting_target.rationale: Sub-cent hosting cost for static assets with global CDN edge delivery

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data collected; CloudFront access logs retained 90 days in S3 for security baseline
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No PII means no data subject rights obligations

future_scope.value: Multi-language document variants for EU country-specific disclosures; automated link validation against regulator registers
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 expansion as the institution enters new EU markets

constraints.value: Must maintain stable URLs for all existing regulator links; monthly hosting cost under 50 USD; no interruption to document availability during cutover
constraints.rationale: Existing regulator-on-file links cannot break; cost is primary driver of migration

observability_baseline.value: CloudWatch for CloudFront access metrics; Plausible Analytics for page-level access counts without cookie tracking
observability_baseline.rationale: Privacy-respecting analytics consistent with the no-PII posture

execution_preference.value: Single engineer 4-week sprint; Git-based content workflow for legal team self-service after launch
execution_preference.rationale: Minimal ongoing engineering involvement required after initial build

project.goal: Replace CMS-hosted disclosure site with cost-minimal static site meeting FCA/PSD2 mandatory disclosure link requirements
project.target_users: Regulators, auditors, customers

frontend.approach: Content Collections for document versioning, zero client-side JS on disclosure pages

security.transport: HTTPS via CloudFront with HSTS header
security.bucket_access: S3 OAI — bucket not publicly accessible directly
security.input: No user input fields on the site

data.storage: Git repository for Markdown source and PDF binaries via Git LFS
data.versioning: Git tags per document version with effective date in frontmatter

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for build, link-check, and deploy

constraints.url_stability: Existing regulator-on-file URLs preserved with 301 redirects if paths change

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is tightly defined: migrate mandated disclosure documents to a static site with stable URLs. The document archive with effective-date listing directly satisfies FCA record-keeping requirements for fee schedules and T&Cs. Phased migration starting with the highest-traffic regulator-linked pages reduces rollout risk.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro Content Collections give legal a typed, validated authoring experience in Markdown while the build produces pure HTML pages with no JS payload. CloudFront + S3 with OAI is the canonical pattern; the bucket must never have public-access. URL stability requires an explicit redirect map in the CloudFront function validated in CI before every deploy.
reasoner_reviews.security: No auth, no user input, and no PII collection means the attack surface is limited to CDN configuration. Security headers — HSTS, X-Frame-Options DENY, CSP disallowing inline scripts — must be injected via CloudFront response headers policy. The S3 bucket must be private with block-all-public-access enabled.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 50 USD/month is achievable at typical disclosure-site traffic volumes; CloudFront static asset costs are sub-5 USD for most disclosure traffic patterns. The URL stability constraint is the highest-risk item and must be enforced in CI via a redirect validator script that checks all known regulator links.

backend.service_design: No server-side service — all content served as pre-built static HTML from S3 via CloudFront OAI
backend.api_patterns: No API surface; document delivery is pure CDN asset fetch with CloudFront function handling legacy URL redirects
backend.failure_handling: CloudFront custom error pages for 404 responses pointing to a friendly static error page with link to document index

security.auth_design: No authentication — anonymous public access for all disclosure content
security.secrets_management: GitHub Actions OIDC role for S3 deployment; no long-lived AWS credentials stored in GitHub secrets
security.abuse_prevention: CloudFront WAF rate-limiting rule blocking 1000+ req/min from single IP to prevent scraping abuse
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudFront access logs to S3 for 90 days; standard HTTP access fields only, no PII beyond IP address

data.entities: disclosure_documents (slug, title, category ENUM fee_schedule/terms/regulatory_notice/annual_report, effective_date, pdf_asset_path, version_tag, published_at), fee_schedule_line_items (schedule_slug, service_name, fee_amount, currency, tier)
data.storage_design: Markdown content in Git with PDF binaries tracked via Git LFS; Astro Content Collections enforce frontmatter schema via Zod at build time
data.retention_and_deletion: All versions retained indefinitely in Git history; CloudFront access logs purged at 90 days via S3 lifecycle rule

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions CI builds Astro site, runs html-proofer for broken links and redirect validation, syncs to S3 with --delete flag, invalidates CloudFront distribution cache
devops.environments: Staging: s3://compliance-docs-staging/ behind a password-protected CloudFront distribution. Production: s3://compliance-docs-prod/ with custom domain and ACM certificate
devops.ci_cd_design: On push to main: build -> link-check -> deploy to staging. On tag (vYYYY-MM-DD): deploy to production with manual approval gate in GitHub Environments
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous S3 object versions retained 30 days via S3 versioning; rollback by syncing prior build artifact and invalidating CloudFront

frontend.app_structure: Astro static site with TypeScript; fee schedule and T&C pages generated from structured JSON/YAML data at build time; versioned page URLs
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home, Fee Schedule (by product), Terms & Conditions (versioned), Regulatory Notices, Annual Reports Archive, Document Download Index
frontend.accessibility_notes: All data tables must have proper thead/th scope attributes; PDF download links must identify file type and size in link text
frontend.performance_notes: All content is static HTML; no JS required for core pages; document downloads served from CDN with correct Content-Disposition headers

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a cost-minimization migration of a regulatory disclosure site with zero dynamic functionality. Key decisions: Astro Content Collections for typed document versioning with effective-date-aware history, S3+CloudFront with OAI for private bucket delivery, strict URL stability enforcement in CI via redirect validator, and Plausible Analytics for cookie-free access tracking. No server runtime, no database, no user input surface.
target_output.title: Payment Institution Compliance Disclosure Static Site
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static Astro-built disclosure site for a regulated payment institution, delivering fee schedules, T&Cs, regulatory notices, and annual reports from CloudFront-fronted S3 with stable URLs, Git-based document versioning, and sub-50 USD monthly hosting. Replaces a licensed CMS with a zero-server architecture that meets FCA and PSD2 mandatory disclosure link requirements without collecting any personal data.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro static site generator compiles Markdown and MDX content files — organized into typed Content Collections validated by Zod schema (category ENUM, effective_date, version_tag) — into plain HTML pages at build time. GitHub Actions CI builds the site on each commit, runs html-proofer for link integrity, executes a redirect-validator.ts script checking every regulator-on-file URL against the CloudFront function redirect map, then syncs the output to a private S3 bucket. CloudFront serves S3 content via Origin Access Identity; block-public-access is enabled on the bucket. A CloudFront response headers policy injects HSTS, CSP (script-src 'self' plus Plausible CDN), and X-Frame-Options DENY on every response. Plausible Analytics provides privacy-respecting page view counts without cookie storage or GDPR consent requirement.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS for layout, @astrojs/sitemap for XML sitemap generation, Astro Content Collections with Zod schema validation on frontmatter. Build tooling: html-proofer for link validation, custom redirect-validator.ts Node script checking regulator link register. Hosting: AWS CloudFront (eu-west-1) with ACM certificate, S3 with OAI and versioning enabled, CloudFront response headers policy and WAF rule. Analytics: Plausible Analytics (cloud). CI: GitHub Actions with OIDC role assumption scoped to S3 bucket ARN. Git LFS for PDF binary assets.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Fee Schedule pages listing all applicable charges per service with effective dates and a comparison link to the superseded version. T&C document pages with a version history table showing effective-from dates and PDF download links for each historical version. Regulatory Notices section listing all FCA and PSD2 notices in reverse chronological order. Annual Reports archive with year-indexed PDF downloads. Document Archive page generated at build time listing all versions with effective dates per category to satisfy record-keeping requirements. XML sitemap for regulator and search engine discovery.
target_output.system_components: GitHub repository storing Markdown source files and PDF binary assets via Git LFS. GitHub Actions runners executing CI pipeline with OIDC AWS credentials. Astro build process with Content Collections and Zod schema validation producing static HTML. S3 bucket (private, eu-west-1) with versioning enabled and lifecycle rule purging access logs at 90 days. CloudFront distribution with OAI, custom domain, ACM TLS certificate, WAF rate-limit rule, and response headers policy. Route 53 for DNS. CloudWatch for CloudFront 4xx/5xx error rate alarms via SNS.
target_output.workflows: Document Publication Workflow: Legal team member edits or adds a Markdown file (e.g. fee-schedules/2026-Q1.md with effective_date and version_tag in frontmatter), opens a PR, CI builds and validates the site including redirect checks and Zod schema validation, the PR is reviewed by a compliance approver and merged — triggering the production deploy pipeline that syncs to S3 and invalidates the CloudFront cache within 5 minutes. Document Versioning Workflow: When a T&C document is superseded, the old file is retained in the repository at its original slug; the new version is added under a new slug; the index page lists both with their effective date range and a 301 redirect from the old canonical URL to the current version; existing regulator-on-file URLs continue to resolve to the appropriate historical document.
target_output.data_model: disclosure_documents (slug STRING PK derived from file path, title STRING, category ENUM fee_schedule/terms/regulatory_notice/annual_report, effective_date DATE, superseded_date DATE nullable, pdf_asset_url STRING, version_tag STRING, published_at TIMESTAMP) — represented as Astro Content Collection entries validated by Zod schema at build time. fee_schedule_line_items (schedule_slug FK, service_name STRING, fee_amount DECIMAL, currency STRING, tier STRING nullable) — structured YAML frontmatter parsed into fee schedule pages. All persistence is Git history; no relational database. CloudFront access logs provide the only runtime record: timestamp, client_ip, uri, status, bytes_sent.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Static asset endpoints served by CloudFront: GET /fee-schedules/ (index of all fee schedule versions), GET /fee-schedules/[slug]/ (specific version page), GET /terms/ (T&C version index), GET /terms/[slug]/ (specific T&C version page), GET /regulatory-notices/ (chronological notice index), GET /annual-reports/ (year-indexed archive), GET /sitemap.xml (auto-generated). 301 redirects from legacy CMS URL patterns (e.g. /legal/fees.html -> /fee-schedules/current/) configured in a CloudFront Function and validated in CI.
target_output.security_and_compliance: S3 bucket: block-public-access enabled, OAI as the sole allowed GetObject principal, no public bucket policy. CloudFront response headers policy: Strict-Transport-Security max-age=31536000 includeSubDomains preload, Content-Security-Policy default-src 'self' script-src 'self' https://plausible.io, X-Frame-Options DENY, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff. GitHub Actions deployment via OIDC role with s3:PutObject and cloudfront:CreateInvalidation scoped to production resource ARNs — no long-lived AWS credentials. CloudFront WAF rule blocks IPs sending over 1000 requests per minute. TLS 1.2 minimum enforced on CloudFront viewer protocol policy with redirect-to-HTTPS for HTTP requests.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline on push to main: (1) bun install, (2) astro build, (3) html-proofer validates all internal links and external links on a 30-second timeout, (4) redirect-validator.ts checks CloudFront function redirect map against the stored regulator-links.json register and fails build on any unresolved mapping, (5) AWS S3 sync with --delete and Cache-Control headers (immutable for content-hashed assets, no-cache for HTML index pages), (6) CloudFront cache invalidation for /* paths. Production deploys trigger on tag push matching vYYYY-MM-DD with a required GitHub Environments manual approval. Staging uses a separate S3 prefix behind a CloudFront distribution with a Cognito-protected behavior for stakeholder review. Rollback by re-syncing prior S3 object versions and re-invalidating CloudFront cache.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch alarm on CloudFront 4xxErrorRate exceeding 2% and 5xxErrorRate exceeding 0.5% over 5-minute windows, notifying ops team via SNS email. CloudFront access logs stored in a separate S3 bucket with 90-day lifecycle expiry providing HTTP access records. Plausible Analytics dashboard tracks unique page views per disclosure category (fee_schedules, terms, regulatory_notices, annual_reports) — surfacing which documents receive the most regulator and customer access. GitHub Actions build duration and failure notifications via Slack webhook.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Projected monthly cost under 15 USD at typical disclosure-site traffic (estimated 10,000 monthly page views): CloudFront data transfer for ~500 MB of HTML and PDFs ~1 USD, S3 storage ~0.01 USD, Route 53 hosted zone ~0.50 USD, CloudWatch logs ~2 USD, WAF baseline ~5 USD, ACM certificate free. Scales to millions of page views per month without infrastructure changes. No compute costs, no database costs. Estimated reduction from 200-400 USD/month CMS licensing to under 20 USD/month operational cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Foundation and Migration (weeks 1-2): Configure S3 bucket with OAI, CloudFront distribution with ACM certificate and WAF rule, and response headers policy. Scaffold Astro project with Content Collections and Zod schemas for all five document categories. Migrate fee schedules, T&Cs, and regulatory notices from CMS export into typed Markdown files. Build CI pipeline including html-proofer and redirect-validator.ts. Deploy to staging for legal sign-off. Phase 2 — Production Cutover (week 3): Pre-stage DNS TTL reduction to 5 minutes. Cut DNS to CloudFront. Verify all known regulator-on-file URLs resolve correctly via automated smoke test. Decommission CMS after 2-week parallel-serve window. Phase 3 — Enhancements (week 4): Annual reports archive, XML sitemap submission to FCA and search engines, Plausible Analytics embed, CloudWatch alarms and SNS notification configuration.
target_output.development_guidelines: All document Markdown files must pass Astro Content Collection Zod schema validation; CI blocks merge on schema errors. The effective_date frontmatter field is mandatory for every document. The redirects-register.json file must be updated atomically with any slug changes; redirect-validator.ts enforces this in CI. PDF assets committed via Git LFS with a 10 MB per-file soft limit. No client-side JavaScript on disclosure pages except the Plausible script — enforced by CSP policy. Conventional commits enforced by commitlint for clear publication audit trail in Git history.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Regulator-on-file URL breaks during migration if the redirect map is incomplete. Mitigation: redirect-validator.ts CI step compares every entry in regulator-links.json against the built site and fails the build on any miss before the change can reach production. Risk: Git LFS bandwidth cost if PDF asset volume grows significantly. Mitigation: PDFs over 10 MB compressed before commit; Git LFS stored in S3 backend to decouple from GitHub bandwidth limits. Tradeoff: Git-based content workflow requires legal team to use GitHub for document updates — a future Decap CMS or TinaCMS layer can be added if self-service editing proves too high-friction without changing the underlying architecture.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Astro over Hugo for typed Content Collections with Zod validation matching the strongly-typed content workflow requirement. Confirmed Plausible Analytics over Google Analytics to avoid cookie consent banner given the no-PII collection posture. Confirmed S3 OAI over public bucket policy for security baseline. Annual reports confirmed as Git LFS PDFs rather than links to an external document management system.

metadata.notes: Static compliance disclosure site migration with Git-based document versioning, URL stability enforcement in CI, and sub-20 USD/month hosting replacing a licensed CMS.

project_goal.value: GDPR-compliant cookie consent and privacy policy hub for a pan-European payment gateway serving jurisdiction-specific consent banners, privacy notices, and data subject request forms based on visitor country
project_goal.rationale: Existing single-page approach failed DPA audits; jurisdiction-specific delivery required for GDPR Article 7 and ePrivacy Directive compliance

target_users.value: Payment gateway end-users across EU member states requiring jurisdiction-specific consent experiences and data subject rights forms
target_users.rationale: DPA guidelines differ by member state; a single consent banner does not satisfy German ULD and French CNIL requirements simultaneously

project_class.rationale: Content is jurisdiction-keyed static pages; dynamic routing handled at CDN edge via CloudFront Function

capabilities.rationale: Static rendering with consent-respecting analytics; no server-side processing of personal data on this site

complexity_level.rationale: Static content with CDN-level routing logic; no database or compute required

risk_level.rationale: Consent records and data subject request submissions involve personal data and carry regulatory risk if mishandled

data_sensitivity.rationale: Data subject request forms collect name and email; consent decisions are personal data under GDPR

external_exposure.rationale: Must be accessible without authentication to all visitors to the payment gateway

access_model.value: Anonymous public access; no login required for consent or privacy notice pages
access_model.rationale: GDPR requires consent to be freely given and accessible before any account creation

feature_scope.value: Jurisdiction-specific cookie consent banners, per-country privacy notices, data subject request forms (erasure, access, portability), cookie category management, consent audit log forwarding
feature_scope.rationale: Covers GDPR Article 7 consent, Article 13/14 transparency, and Articles 15-20 data subject rights

mvp_scope.value: Jurisdiction detection, per-country consent banner with accept/reject/customize, privacy notice pages for DE, FR, NL, PL, and data subject request form routing to privacy team queue
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers the five largest EU corridors for the payment gateway

security_baseline.value: HTTPS via CloudFront, HSTS, CSP, no third-party scripts except Plausible; form submissions encrypted in transit via TLS 1.3
security_baseline.rationale: Minimal attack surface; no user-facing server-side compute

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Static export with per-jurisdiction page generation using getStaticPaths; team already uses Next.js

backend_stack.value: None — fully static export; jurisdiction routing via CloudFront Function
backend_stack.rationale: No server needed; CDN edge handles country detection and routing to pre-built locale pages

data_platform.value: None — jurisdiction configs in Git as JSON; consent audit logs forwarded to the payment gateway's central data warehouse via presigned S3 write endpoint
data_platform.rationale: Audit log persistence delegated to existing data platform; no new database required

hosting_target.value: AWS CloudFront + S3 with CloudFront Function for jurisdiction routing
hosting_target.rationale: CloudFront Functions execute at edge with sub-millisecond overhead for country header inspection

privacy_retention_policy.value: Consent records retained 3 years per GDPR Article 7(1) accountability requirement; data subject request submissions retained 90 days after completion
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Must be able to demonstrate valid consent was obtained if challenged by DPA

future_scope.value: Expand to 15 additional EU jurisdictions; integrate consent preference centre with the payment gateway's backend user profile service
future_scope.rationale: Phased rollout to all EU markets the gateway operates in

constraints.value: Must respond in under 200ms for jurisdiction detection and banner render; no Google Analytics or Meta Pixel on consent pages; GitLab CI pipeline required
constraints.rationale: Performance required for good user experience at payment checkout; analytics tools that set cookies cannot appear on a consent page before consent is obtained

observability_baseline.value: Plausible Analytics for page views; CloudWatch for CloudFront error rates; consent submission success/failure logged to S3 audit bucket
observability_baseline.rationale: Plausible requires no consent banner itself; CloudWatch provides infrastructure health visibility

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; content updates managed by legal team via PR workflow; no-code jurisdiction config changes via JSON files
execution_preference.rationale: Legal team must be able to update consent text without engineering involvement

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 7 consent, ePrivacy Directive, national DPA guidelines (BfDI Germany, CNIL France, AP Netherlands, UODO Poland); consent records must be demonstrably compliant with IAB TCF v2.2 for advertising partners
compliance_context.rationale: data_sensitivity=personal triggers compliance_context requirement; pan-EU operation requires multi-DPA alignment

project.goal: GDPR-compliant static site serving jurisdiction-specific consent and privacy content to payment gateway visitors
project.target_users: EU payment gateway users
project.timeline: Round 3 revision addressing ISS-001 and ISS-002

frontend.approach: getStaticPaths generates one HTML page per jurisdiction per document type; CloudFront Function routes visitor to correct locale page

backend.approach: Fully static; CloudFront Function handles country detection from CloudFront-Viewer-Country header

security.transport: HTTPS via CloudFront with TLS 1.3, HSTS preload
security.csp: script-src 'self' https://plausible.io; no third-party ad scripts on consent pages
security.form_submission: Data subject request forms POST to a presigned S3 URL with server-side encryption

data.jurisdiction_configs: JSON files in Git keyed by ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code
data.consent_audit: Consent decisions written to S3 audit bucket via presigned POST with AES-256 SSE

devops.ci: GitLab CI for build and deploy

constraints.latency: Under 200ms for jurisdiction detection and first meaningful paint
constraints.no_tracking: No Google Analytics or Meta Pixel on consent pages

reasoner_reviews.product: The jurisdiction-specific consent banner architecture correctly addresses the DPA audit failures. The five-jurisdiction MVP covering DE, FR, NL, PL, and a default EU banner satisfies the immediate compliance gap. The data subject request form routing to a dedicated privacy team queue is essential — previous design lacked a clear submission path and was flagged by the DPA.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using CloudFront Function for country routing is the right pattern — it executes at edge before the origin fetch, adding under 1ms overhead, well within the 200ms constraint. The Next.js static export approach with getStaticPaths per jurisdiction means all consent pages are pre-built HTML with no SSR latency. The presigned S3 POST for data subject request submissions avoids the need for a server-side endpoint while maintaining encryption and audit logging.
reasoner_reviews.security: The CSP constraint that no third-party scripts appear on consent pages before consent is obtained is architecturally correct and must be enforced in the build via a CSP header check in CI. The presigned S3 POST for form submissions must have a short expiry (15 minutes) and be scoped to a write-only prefix. Consent audit log objects in S3 must be immutable (Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode) to satisfy the 3-year accountability requirement under GDPR Article 7.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 200ms latency target is achievable: CloudFront Function execution adds ~1ms, S3 origin fetch for pre-built HTML adds 5-15ms at edge, total well under budget. The GitLab CI constraint is satisfied; the pipeline mirrors the GitHub Actions approach. The no-Google-Analytics constraint requires Plausible to be self-hosted or cloud-hosted at a non-Google domain — confirmed.

backend.service_design: No server-side service; CloudFront Function (JS runtime) performs country detection from CloudFront-Viewer-Country request header and rewrites the URI to the pre-built locale-specific HTML page
backend.api_patterns: Presigned S3 POST URL generated by a Lambda function invoked at deployment time for each form type; URL embedded in the static page at build time with a 24-hour expiry and refresh via scheduled GitLab CI job
backend.failure_handling: CloudFront returns a default EU consent banner page if the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header is absent or unrecognized; 404 routes to a static error page listing all available privacy notice URLs

security.auth_design: No user authentication; data subject request forms use honeypot field and reCAPTCHA v3 (score-gated, not blocking) to filter bot submissions
security.secrets_management: GitLab CI OIDC role for S3 deployment; presigned POST URL lambda uses least-privilege IAM role scoped to the consent-submissions bucket write prefix
security.privacy_controls: Consent audit log objects written to S3 with Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode retention of 3 years; no consent record stored client-side beyond a session cookie recording the decision timestamp
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every consent decision (accept/reject/customize) triggers a client-side POST to the presigned S3 audit endpoint with jurisdiction, cookie categories accepted, timestamp, and a random session token — no IP address stored

data.entities: consent_banners (jurisdiction_code, banner_text, cookie_categories JSON, last_updated, version_tag), privacy_notices (jurisdiction_code, language_code, content_md, effective_date, dpa_reference), jurisdiction_configs (country_code, applicable_dpa, default_consent_model ENUM opt_in/opt_out, required_categories JSON), data_subject_request_forms (form_type ENUM erasure/access/portability, jurisdiction_code, fields JSON, routing_email)
data.storage_design: Jurisdiction config JSON files in Git; built into static pages at compile time. Consent audit records written to S3 consent-audit/ prefix with AES-256 SSE and Object Lock. Data subject request submissions written to S3 dsr-submissions/ prefix with 90-day lifecycle and Object Lock GOVERNANCE mode.
data.retention_and_deletion: Consent audit S3 objects locked for 3 years; DSR submission objects expire after 90 days post-completion; no PII stored in CloudFront access logs (IP masking enabled)

devops.deployment_topology: GitLab CI: next build with static export, html-proofer for link validation, CSP header check verifying no third-party analytics scripts on consent pages, S3 sync, CloudFront invalidation, presigned URL refresh Lambda invocation
devops.environments: Staging: separate S3 bucket with a CloudFront distribution behind Cognito-protected behavior for legal review. Production: live CloudFront with custom domain and WAF.
devops.ci_cd_design: On merge to main: build -> validate -> deploy to staging. On tag: deploy to production with manual approval. Nightly scheduled job refreshes presigned S3 POST URLs.
devops.rollback_strategy: S3 object versioning on production bucket; rollback by re-syncing prior build artifact and invalidating CloudFront cache

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 static export with TypeScript; jurisdiction-specific pages under route segments (/privacy/de, /privacy/fr); cookie consent banner as client island
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home, Per-Country Privacy Notice pages, Cookie Consent Management page, Data Subject Rights Request form, Consent History view
frontend.frontend_security_notes: Consent state stored in a first-party cookie (not localStorage) to survive subdomain hops; no consent = no analytics scripts load
frontend.frontend_risks: Jurisdiction detection by IP may be inaccurate; always allow user to override jurisdiction selection manually

ISS-001.title: Consent audit log stores IP address in violation of GDPR data minimization
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan logged full IP address in consent audit records; CNIL guidance requires IP minimization or pseudonymization in consent logs

ISS-002.title: Data subject request form missing acknowledgment email within GDPR required response window
ISS-002.detail: GDPR Article 12 requires acknowledgment of DSR within one month; round 2 plan had no automated acknowledgment mechanism

ISS-003.title: Presigned S3 POST URL expiry not refreshed — forms break after 7 days
ISS-003.detail: Initial presigned URL had a 7-day expiry with no refresh mechanism; forms would silently fail after expiry

focus_issues.title: Consent audit log stores IP address in violation of GDPR data minimization
focus_issues.detail: Removed IP address from consent audit record schema; random session token used as the only quasi-identifier

focus_issues.title: Data subject request form missing acknowledgment email
focus_issues.detail: Added SES trigger on S3 dsr-submissions/ prefix PUT event; acknowledgment email sent within 60 seconds of submission

latest_recommendations: Remove IP address from consent audit record and replace with session token
Add automated DSR acknowledgment email via SES Lambda trigger on S3 PUT event
Enforce no-IP logging in CloudFront access logs via IP masking configuration
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Pan-European Payment Gateway GDPR Consent Hub v2

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity GDPR compliance issues remain unresolved after round 2

previous_audits.summary: Core jurisdiction routing architecture is sound but GDPR data minimization failures in consent audit logging and missing DSR acknowledgment workflow are blocking issues
strengths: CloudFront Function jurisdiction routing is correctly designed
Static export approach meets 200ms latency constraint
Object Lock on consent audit S3 objects correctly addresses 3-year accountability requirement
concerns: Consent audit log schema includes full IP address violating GDPR data minimization
No automated acknowledgment for data subject requests within GDPR Article 12 one-month window
blocking_issues: IP address in consent audit log violates GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimization for German and French DPA requirements
recommendations: Replace IP address in audit log with a pseudonymized session token
Add S3 event notification triggering SES acknowledgment email on DSR submission PUT

issue_updates.detail: No acknowledgment mechanism described in workflows section; GDPR Article 12 requires written acknowledgment within one month

strengths: Nightly presigned URL refresh Lambda correctly addresses ISS-003
Jurisdiction config JSON structure is well-typed and extensible to additional EU countries

Implement S3 event notification on dsr-submissions/ prefix PUT triggering Lambda SES send
