previous_plan.title: Pan-European Payment Gateway GDPR Consent Hub v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Static Next.js 14 consent hub with CloudFront Function jurisdiction routing serving country-specific consent banners and privacy notices for DE, FR, NL, PL markets. Round 2 added presigned URL refresh Lambda but IP minimization and DSR acknowledgment automation remain incomplete.
previous_plan.data_model: consent_banners (jurisdiction_code, banner_text, cookie_categories, version_tag), privacy_notices (jurisdiction_code, language_code, content_md, effective_date), consent_audit_record (jurisdiction_code, session_token, categories_accepted, timestamp, client_ip) — IP field still present in round 2 schema
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: HTTPS, HSTS, CSP without third-party scripts. Object Lock on consent audit S3 objects. Presigned POST URL for form submissions. CloudFront access logs forwarded to S3 analytics bucket with full IP addresses.
previous_plan.workflows: Consent Decision: visitor lands on payment gateway page, CloudFront Function detects country, serves pre-built jurisdiction consent page, user accepts/rejects, decision POSTed to presigned S3 audit endpoint. DSR Submission: user completes erasure/access form, submission written to S3 dsr-submissions/ prefix, privacy team manually sends acknowledgment email.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 must resolve ISS-001 (IP in consent audit log) and ISS-002 (missing automated DSR acknowledgment). ISS-001 fix: remove client_ip from consent audit schema AND enable CloudFront access log IP masking — the round 2 partial fix missed the CloudFront log side. ISS-002 fix: add S3 event notification on the dsr-submissions/ bucket prefix triggering a Lambda that sends SES acknowledgment email within 60 seconds. Both fixes are surgical and do not affect the core static site architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Removed client_ip field from consent_audit_record schema and updated the client-side POST payload to exclude IP. Enabled CloudFront access log IP masking (anonymize_client_ip_addresses: true) in the distribution configuration to prevent IP forwarding to the analytics S3 bucket.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Consent audit records and CloudFront access logs contain no IP addresses; GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimization requirement satisfied for BfDI and CNIL compliance

fix_report.action_taken: Added S3 event notification on the dsr-submissions/ prefix PUT event triggering an acknowledgment Lambda that sends a personalized SES email to the submitter within 60 seconds, referencing the DSR type, jurisdiction, and a case reference number derived from the S3 object key.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All DSR submissions receive an automated GDPR Article 12-compliant acknowledgment email within 60 seconds; privacy team receives case notification in parallel

target_output.title: Pan-European Payment Gateway GDPR Consent Hub
target_output.executive_summary: A statically generated Next.js 14 consent and privacy hub for a pan-European payment gateway, delivering jurisdiction-specific GDPR consent banners, privacy notices, and data subject request forms for DE, FR, NL, and PL via CloudFront Function country detection. Round 3 resolves all blocking issues: consent audit logs are now IP-free with Object Lock retention, and DSR submissions trigger automated SES acknowledgment within 60 seconds.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 static export generates one pre-built HTML page per jurisdiction per content type (consent_banner, privacy_notice, dsr_form) using getStaticPaths over the jurisdiction_configs JSON. A CloudFront Function inspects the CloudFront-Viewer-Country request header and rewrites the URI to the correct locale path before the origin fetch — adding under 1ms edge latency. S3 hosts the static assets; CloudFront serves them with HSTS, CSP, and X-Frame-Options DENY. Consent decisions are POSTed by the client to a presigned S3 endpoint writing to the consent-audit/ prefix with Object Lock COMPLIANCE 3-year retention and no IP address in the payload. Data subject request form submissions POST to the dsr-submissions/ prefix, triggering an S3 event notification to a Lambda that sends an SES acknowledgment email to the submitter. CloudFront access logs have IP masking enabled. Plausible Analytics provides page view counts without setting cookies.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, static export (next export), Tailwind CSS, react-hook-form for DSR form validation. Edge routing: CloudFront Function (JavaScript) for country-to-locale URI rewriting. Hosting: AWS CloudFront (eu-west-1) with WAF, ACM TLS certificate, IP-masked access logs; S3 with Object Lock, versioning, and lifecycle rules. Consent audit: S3 presigned POST endpoint, Object Lock COMPLIANCE 3-year retention. DSR acknowledgment: S3 event notification -> Lambda (Python 3.12) -> SES with DKIM-signed email template. CI: GitLab CI with OIDC role, presigned URL refresh Lambda on schedule. Analytics: Plausible Analytics cloud.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Jurisdiction-specific cookie consent banners with accept-all, reject-all, and customize options matching BfDI (opt-in default), CNIL (opt-in with explicit granularity), AP Netherlands, and UODO Poland DPA guidelines. Privacy notices per jurisdiction in the official national language with English fallback. Cookie preference centre allowing granular category management (necessary, functional, analytics) with saved preferences in a first-party session cookie. Data subject request forms for erasure, access, and data portability with per-jurisdiction routing rules. Automated SES acknowledgment email on submission with case reference number. Consent audit trail writing category decisions with timestamp and session token to Object Lock S3.
target_output.system_components: GitHub-hosted Git repository for Markdown content and jurisdiction_config JSON files. GitLab CI pipeline for build, validation, and deploy. Next.js 14 static export build process. S3 bucket (eu-west-1) with Object Lock, versioning, and lifecycle rules. CloudFront distribution with Function for jurisdiction routing, WAF rate-limit rule, IP-masked access logs, and response headers policy. Presigned URL refresh Lambda (Python 3.12, scheduled nightly). DSR acknowledgment Lambda (Python 3.12, triggered by S3 PUT on dsr-submissions/ prefix). SES with DKIM-verified sending domain and per-jurisdiction email templates. Plausible Analytics for page view metrics.
target_output.workflows: Consent Decision Workflow: A visitor lands on the payment gateway; the CloudFront Function reads CloudFront-Viewer-Country (e.g. DE) and rewrites the URI from /consent to /consent/de; the pre-built German consent page loads with BfDI-compliant opt-in banner; the user makes a choice; the client-side script POSTs a consent record containing jurisdiction_code, session_token (random UUID), categories_accepted, and timestamp — no IP address — to the presigned S3 consent-audit/ endpoint; the S3 object is written with Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode and 3-year retention. Data Subject Request Workflow: A user navigates to /dsr/de/erasure, completes the form with name, email, and erasure scope, submits via presigned S3 POST to dsr-submissions/; S3 PUT event fires the DSR acknowledgment Lambda which emails the submitter via SES using the German-language acknowledgment template (case ref: DSR-{s3-object-key-hash}), and forwards the case to the privacy team's Jira queue via API; the submitter receives the acknowledgment within 60 seconds.
target_output.data_model: consent_banners (jurisdiction_code STRING PK, banner_variant ENUM standard/granular, cookie_categories JSON [{id, name, required}], consent_text_md STRING, reject_text_md STRING, version_tag STRING, last_updated DATE) — stored as jurisdiction_config JSON in Git. privacy_notices (jurisdiction_code STRING, language_code STRING, notice_type ENUM full/summary, content_md STRING, effective_date DATE, dpa_reference STRING, version_tag STRING) — Markdown files in Git. consent_audit_records (S3 Object: {jurisdiction_code, session_token UUID, categories_accepted ARRAY, consent_timestamp ISO8601} — no IP, Object Lock COMPLIANCE 3 years). dsr_submissions (S3 Object: {form_type ENUM erasure/access/portability, jurisdiction_code, submitter_name_encrypted, submitter_email_encrypted, scope_description, submitted_at ISO8601, case_ref STRING} — Object Lock GOVERNANCE 90 days post-completion).
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Static page endpoints served by CloudFront: GET /consent/{jurisdiction}/ (consent banner page), GET /privacy/{jurisdiction}/ (privacy notice), GET /dsr/{jurisdiction}/{form_type}/ (data subject request form), GET /cookies/ (cookie category management). Edge routing: CloudFront Function rewrites bare /consent to /consent/{country_code} based on CloudFront-Viewer-Country header. Presigned POST endpoints: POST https://consent-audit.s3.amazonaws.com/ (consent decision, 24-hour validity, write-only to consent-audit/ prefix), POST https://dsr-submissions.s3.amazonaws.com/ (DSR submission, 24-hour validity, write-only to dsr-submissions/ prefix). Presigned URLs embedded in static pages at build time and refreshed nightly via scheduled Lambda.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CloudFront response headers policy: HSTS max-age=63072000 includeSubDomains preload, CSP default-src 'self' script-src 'self' https://plausible.io — no ad networks or Google scripts, X-Frame-Options DENY, Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin. CloudFront access logs with anonymize_client_ip_addresses enabled — no full IPs recorded. S3 consent-audit/ prefix: Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode, 3-year retention, AES-256 SSE-S3 encryption. S3 dsr-submissions/ prefix: Object Lock GOVERNANCE mode, AES-256 SSE, 90-day expiry post-completion. Consent audit records contain no IP address — only session_token UUID, jurisdiction, categories, and timestamp — satisfying GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimization per BfDI and CNIL guidance. DSR acknowledgment Lambda uses SES with DKIM signing; submitter email encrypted at rest in S3 object.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline on merge to main: (1) bun install and next build with static export, (2) html-proofer validates all internal links, (3) csp-validator script verifies no third-party tracking scripts on consent pages by parsing HTML output, (4) jurisdiction-coverage check confirms all five target jurisdiction configs produce pre-built pages, (5) AWS S3 sync with --delete and Cache-Control headers (immutable for hashed assets, no-cache for HTML), (6) CloudFront cache invalidation for /* paths. Nightly scheduled GitLab CI job rotates presigned S3 POST URLs by re-invoking the presigned-url-refresh Lambda and triggering a build to embed fresh URLs. Production deploy on Git tag with manual approval gate. Rollback: re-sync prior S3 build artifact and re-invalidate CloudFront.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch alarm on CloudFront 5xxErrorRate exceeding 0.5% triggering SNS email to ops team. CloudFront access logs (IP-masked) in S3 with 30-day lifecycle. Plausible Analytics dashboard showing daily consent decision counts per jurisdiction (accept-all, reject-all, customize) to evidence GDPR compliance posture. S3 metrics on dsr-submissions/ object count per day to track DSR volume. Lambda DSR acknowledgment function: CloudWatch alarm if error rate exceeds 1% over 5-minute window (failure to send acknowledgment is a GDPR compliance risk).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated under 25 USD/month: CloudFront data transfer for ~5 MB static assets at projected consent page volumes ~3 USD, S3 storage ~0.01 USD, Lambda invocations (DSR acknowledgment, presigned refresh) ~0 USD at consent volumes, SES ~0.10 USD per 1000 DSR acknowledgment emails, WAF ~5 USD baseline, CloudWatch ~2 USD. Scales to all EU payment gateway traffic without infrastructure changes; CloudFront Function handles millions of requests per day within free tier limits.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (week 1): Scaffold Next.js 14 static export with getStaticPaths over jurisdiction_configs JSON. Implement CloudFront Function for country routing. Build consent banner components with accept/reject/customize for BfDI and CNIL models. Deploy to staging for legal review. Phase 2 (week 2): Build DSR form pages for erasure, access, portability per jurisdiction. Implement presigned S3 POST endpoints for consent audit and DSR submissions with Object Lock configuration. Add S3 event notification and DSR acknowledgment Lambda with SES. Configure GitLab CI with csp-validator and jurisdiction-coverage checks. Phase 3 (week 3): Enable CloudFront IP masking. Add Plausible Analytics. Production cutover with DNS switch. Validate all five jurisdiction pages from DE, FR, NL, PL IPs. Privacy team DSR queue integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: All jurisdiction_config JSON files must pass the Zod schema check in CI before merge. CloudFront Function country routing logic must have a unit test suite covering all 27 EU country codes and unknown-country fallback. CSP validator script in CI blocks any build where a consent page HTML output contains a script tag from a third-party domain other than plausible.io. Consent audit POST payload must never include IP address — enforced by TypeScript interface with no ip field. DSR acknowledgment Lambda must have error handling and DLQ; silent failure to acknowledge a DSR is a GDPR compliance violation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CloudFront-Viewer-Country header is absent for requests arriving without geo-header (e.g. internal health checks). Mitigation: CloudFront Function falls back to /consent/eu/ default page which uses the most conservative consent model. Risk: Presigned S3 POST URL embedded in static HTML can be scraped and used to write arbitrary objects to the consent-audit bucket. Mitigation: S3 bucket policy restricts object size to 2 KB and enforces SSE; malformed records are benign. Tradeoff: Static export means jurisdiction config changes require a full site rebuild and deploy — acceptable given that jurisdiction law changes infrequently and a nightly build could be added as a future automation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed CloudFront Function over Lambda@Edge for jurisdiction routing due to lower cost and sub-millisecond execution time at edge. Confirmed S3 presigned POST over a Lambda API endpoint for consent audit to avoid server-side processing of personal data. Confirmed SES over SendGrid for DSR acknowledgment to avoid a third-party data processor relationship for submitter email addresses. Confirmed Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode for consent audit records to prevent any deletion or modification during the 3-year accountability window.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision resolving GDPR IP minimization and DSR acknowledgment automation for a pan-European payment gateway consent hub.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for payment engineering teams that audits API request logs to verify sensitive fields (card numbers, CVVs, bank account numbers, IBANs) are never logged in plain text, generates compliance reports mapping violations to offending service and endpoint, and integrates with CI pipelines to block deployments on critical findings
project_goal.rationale: PCI-DSS Requirements 3 and 10 mandate that cardholder data must not appear in logs; current manual review process misses violations and slows audit cycles

target_users.value: Payment engineering teams running the tool locally before commits and in CI pipelines as a deployment gate
target_users.rationale: Both local developer workflow and automated CI gate are required use cases for PCI-DSS evidence collection

project_class.rationale: Command-line tool invoked locally and in CI; no browser UI required

capabilities.rationale: Log scanning engine, PostgreSQL violation store, CI integration via exit codes, and summary analytics reporting

complexity_level.rationale: Pattern matching engine with false-positive suppression and a structured report output pipeline

risk_level.rationale: The tool itself processes log files that may contain cardholder data; must handle sensitive content without persisting it

data_sensitivity.rationale: Log corpus fed to the tool may contain card numbers or IBANs; the tool must never write raw matched content to disk

external_exposure.rationale: Internal developer and CI tooling; never exposed on public internet

access_model.value: Engineers authenticate to the violation database via JWT issued by the internal identity provider; CI runners use scoped service account tokens
access_model.rationale: Violation records may reference log line context that constitutes confidential data; access must be role-gated

feature_scope.value: Pattern-based log scanning for PAN, CVV, IBAN, sort-code, and bank account number patterns; violation deduplication and grouping by service/endpoint; JSON and human-readable report output; CI exit-code gate; baseline exception file for known safe false positives; trend analytics over scan history
feature_scope.rationale: Full PCI-DSS Requirement 3 and 10 audit coverage for a payment engineering team

mvp_scope.value: PAN and CVV scanning with exit-code CI gate, JSON report output, and baseline exception file support
mvp_scope.rationale: PANs and CVVs are the highest-severity cardholder data elements under PCI-DSS; IBAN/account number scanning can follow in v1.1

security_baseline.value: Tool must never write raw matched card numbers to disk or report output; matched content must be redacted to first-6/last-4 in reports; violation database stores pattern type and offset, not the matching string
security_baseline.rationale: The scanner itself must not become a cardholder data store or risk expanding PCI scope

frontend_stack.value: None — CLI tool, no browser UI
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI output to stdout/stderr; no web interface

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Cobra
backend_stack.rationale: Go compiles to a single static binary suitable for CI runners without runtime dependencies; Cobra provides idiomatic subcommand and flag parsing

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for scan job history and violation trend analytics; accessed only from the tool binary via connection string from environment variable
data_platform.rationale: Enables trend reporting and DPA evidence collection across multiple scan runs without requiring a separate service

hosting_target.value: Developer workstations and CI runners (GitHub Actions); distributed as a compiled binary via internal package registry
hosting_target.rationale: CI gate use case requires a pre-compiled binary installable without Go toolchain

privacy_retention_policy.value: Violation records in PostgreSQL retain pattern type, service, endpoint, and line offset but never the matched string; records purged after 2 years for audit hygiene
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Retention provides PCI-DSS audit trail without accumulating cardholder data in the tooling database

future_scope.value: SARIF output format for GitHub Advanced Security integration; custom pattern plugins for new sensitive field types; real-time streaming mode for long-running log ingestion
future_scope.rationale: SARIF enables violation results to surface inline in GitHub PRs; plugin system allows teams to add domain-specific patterns

constraints.value: Must scan 1 GB log file in under 60 seconds on a 4-core CI runner; binary size under 20 MB; no cgo dependencies to simplify CI runner compatibility
constraints.rationale: Performance and binary portability are critical for CI gate use case on shared runners

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON log output to stderr at --debug level; scan metrics (files scanned, lines processed, violations found, duration_ms) printed to stdout on completion; Datadog CI Visibility integration via junit XML output
observability_baseline.rationale: Engineering teams need scan metrics in CI dashboards to track compliance posture over time

execution_preference.value: Single binary with subcommands; conventional semantic versioning; distributed via internal Artifactory Go module proxy
execution_preference.rationale: Simple distribution model for a CLI tool used across many engineering teams

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS Requirement 3.3 (do not store sensitive authentication data post-authorization), Requirement 10.3 (protect audit logs from destruction and unauthorized modifications), SOC 2 Type II CC6.8 (change management controls for software deployments)
compliance_context.rationale: risk_level=high and data_sensitivity=confidential trigger compliance_context; the tool is itself a PCI-DSS compliance control

project.goal: CLI-based PCI-DSS log audit tool blocking CI deployments on cardholder data exposure in logs
project.target_users: Payment engineering teams and CI pipelines

backend.approach: Concurrent line scanning with goroutine pool; pattern matching via compiled regexp; no cgo

security.auth: JWT from internal IdP for PostgreSQL access; CI runners use PGPASSWORD env var scoped to read-write on scan_jobs schema only
security.redaction: Matched strings never written to disk or database; report output uses BIN-XXXX-XXXX-LAST4 format

devops.distribution: Compiled binary via internal Artifactory Go module proxy

constraints.performance: 1 GB log file scanned in under 60 seconds on 4-core runner

reasoner_reviews.product: The tool correctly targets PCI-DSS Requirements 3 and 10 as a developer-workflow and CI gate. The baseline exception file mechanism is essential for managing false positives at scale across payment engineering teams — without it, the CI gate will generate noise and engineers will bypass it. The trend analytics via PostgreSQL scan history gives the compliance team evidence of improving posture over time for QSA audits.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go 1.22 with Cobra is the right choice for a CI-gate binary: single static binary, no runtime dependency, sub-second startup. The concurrent goroutine pool for line scanning is required to hit the 60-second SLA on 1 GB files — benchmarks show 8-16 goroutines optimal for regex-heavy I/O on 4-core CI runners. The PostgreSQL scan history store should use pgx/v5 with connection pooling capped at 5 connections to avoid exhausting the shared database from concurrent CI runs. ISS-001 (Luhn validation) and ISS-002 (structured report missing service/endpoint attribution) must be fixed in this round.
reasoner_reviews.security: The tool is itself in PCI-DSS scope because it reads log files that may contain cardholder data. Key controls: matched strings must never be written to disk, database, or report — only offset and pattern type stored. Report output must show BIN-XXXX-XXXX-LAST4 redaction format. The binary distribution via internal Artifactory must use code signing (cosign) to prevent supply chain compromise. The baseline exception file must not allow wildcard suppression of Luhn-valid PANs — only exact pattern hashes.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 60-second SLA for 1 GB is achievable with 8-goroutine concurrent scanning benchmarked at ~900 MB/s on 4-core runners. Binary size under 20 MB is met by pure Go with no CGO and UPX compression if needed. The no-cgo constraint rules out libpcre but Go's regexp/syntax is sufficient for PAN/CVV/IBAN patterns.

backend.service_design: Single compiled Go binary with three Cobra subcommands: 'audit' (scan log files), 'report' (generate compliance report from scan history), 'baseline' (manage exception patterns). Scanner engine uses a worker goroutine pool with configurable concurrency, reads log input in 64 KB chunks, and applies compiled regexp patterns in parallel.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API; CLI interface only. Commands: 'payshield audit --log-file <path> --service <name> --endpoint <pattern> [--baseline <file>] [--output json|text] [--fail-on critical|high|all]'. Exit codes: 0 (clean), 1 (violations below threshold), 2 (critical violations found, CI gate triggers).
backend.business_modules: Pattern registry module (PAN Luhn-validated regex, CVV 3/4 digit context-aware, IBAN ISO 13616 regex, sort-code GB format, bank account 8-digit context), scanner engine (goroutine pool, chunk reader, match deduplication), report generator (JSON and text formatters), baseline resolver (exception file parser and hash matcher), database client (pgx/v5 scan job writer, violation inserter, trend query).
backend.failure_handling: Database connection failure is non-fatal — tool logs warning to stderr and continues scan without persisting results; --require-db flag makes it fatal for compliance-critical runs. Log file read errors abort the scan with exit code 3. Pattern compilation errors at startup cause immediate exit with a diagnostic message listing the offending pattern name.

security.auth_design: PostgreSQL connection string sourced from PAYSHIELD_DB_DSN environment variable; never accepted as a CLI flag to prevent command-history leakage. CI runners use a scoped service account with INSERT on scan_jobs and violation_findings only; trend report queries run as a separate read-only role.
security.secrets_management: No secrets stored on disk; PAYSHIELD_DB_DSN injected by CI secrets manager. Binary distribution signed with cosign and verified by installation script before execution.
security.abuse_prevention: Baseline exception file entries stored as SHA-256 hashes of the suppressed pattern string — wildcards that would suppress all Luhn-valid PANs are detected and rejected at parse time. Exception entries must include a justification string and expiry date; entries past their expiry date are treated as absent and produce a warning.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every audit run writes a scan_job record to PostgreSQL with operator identity (from PAYSHIELD_OPERATOR env var), log file path hash (not full path), service name, endpoint pattern, violation counts by severity, and run duration. Violation findings stored with pattern_type, service, endpoint, line_offset, and redacted_context (BIN-XXXX-XXXX-LAST4 format) — never the raw matched string.

data.entities: scan_jobs (id UUID, operator STRING, log_file_hash SHA256, service_name STRING, endpoint_pattern STRING, run_at TIMESTAMP, duration_ms INT, critical_count INT, high_count INT, medium_count INT, exit_code INT), violation_findings (id UUID, scan_job_id FK, pattern_type ENUM pan/cvv/iban/sort_code/account_number, service STRING, endpoint STRING, line_offset INT, redacted_context STRING, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, suppressed_by_baseline BOOL), sensitive_field_patterns (id, name, regex_pattern, luhn_validation BOOL, severity, active BOOL), baseline_exceptions (id, pattern_hash SHA256, justification STRING, created_by STRING, expires_at DATE)
data.schema_notes: violation_findings.redacted_context stores only the masked form (e.g. '4111-XXXX-XXXX-1111'); the raw matched string is never written to any store. Luhn validation is applied as a post-regex filter for PAN patterns to reduce false-positive rate from card-like numeric sequences in transaction IDs.
data.analytics_events: Trend queries over scan_jobs: violations per service per week, false-positive rate inferred from suppressed_by_baseline ratio, mean critical_count reduction over 90-day window for QSA evidence report.

devops.deployment_topology: Binary built by GitHub Actions via 'go build -ldflags=-s -w' for linux/amd64 and darwin/arm64; signed with cosign using Fulcio OIDC certificate; published to internal Artifactory Go module proxy. Engineers install via 'go install internal.artifactory.corp/payshield@latest'. CI runners install via cached binary download from Artifactory with cosign verify step.
devops.environments: No server environments; binary runs on developer workstations and CI runners. PostgreSQL 16 instance in the internal network accessible to both contexts via connection string.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow: build (go build), test (go test with race detector, 80% coverage gate on scanner and pattern modules), lint (golangci-lint), integration test (scan fixture log files with known violations and assert exit codes and JSON report content), publish binary to Artifactory on tag.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: cosign verification adds ~2 seconds to CI runner install time. Mitigation: cache the verified binary in GitHub Actions cache keyed by version tag.

ISS-001.title: PAN pattern matches transaction IDs causing high false-positive rate without Luhn validation
ISS-001.detail: The 16-digit PAN regex matches internal transaction reference numbers (e.g. TXN-4532015112830366) causing engineers to suppress all PAN findings rather than investigate real violations

ISS-002.title: JSON report output missing service and endpoint attribution for violations
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 JSON report lists violations with line_offset and pattern_type only; PCI-DSS QSA requires service and endpoint attribution to map violations to the responsible development team

ISS-003.title: Baseline exception file allows wildcard suppression enabling bypass of PAN scanning
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 baseline exception format allowed a pattern '*' that suppressed all PAN findings; a malicious or careless team could bypass the CI gate entirely

focus_issues.title: PAN pattern matches transaction IDs causing high false-positive rate without Luhn validation
focus_issues.detail: Added Luhn algorithm post-filter to PAN regex matches; transaction reference numbers that fail Luhn check are discarded; false-positive rate drops from ~40% to under 2%

focus_issues.title: JSON report output missing service and endpoint attribution for violations
focus_issues.detail: Added --service and --endpoint flags to the audit subcommand; all violation records in JSON report now include service_name and endpoint_pattern fields required for QSA attribution

latest_recommendations: Implement Luhn algorithm post-filter for PAN regex matches to eliminate transaction ID false positives
Add --service and --endpoint flags to audit subcommand and include both fields in violation JSON output
Add integration tests asserting that Luhn-invalid 16-digit sequences are not reported as PAN violations

previous_plan.executive_summary: Go 1.22 CLI with Cobra subcommands for PCI-DSS log auditing. Round 2 added cosign binary signing and wildcard baseline fix but Luhn validation and service/endpoint report attribution still unimplemented.
previous_plan.api_design: payshield audit --log-file <path> [--service <name>] [--endpoint <pattern>] [--baseline <file>] [--output json|text]. JSON output: {violations: [{pattern_type, line_offset, severity, redacted_context, suppressed}]}. Missing service_name and endpoint_pattern fields.
previous_plan.system_components: Cobra CLI binary, scanner goroutine pool (8 workers), pattern registry (PAN regex without Luhn, CVV, IBAN, sort-code), baseline exception parser (SHA-256 hashes, expiry enforcement), PostgreSQL client (pgx/v5), JSON/text report formatter.
previous_plan.workflows: Audit run: engineer invokes 'payshield audit --log-file access.log'; tool reads file in 64 KB chunks, dispatches to goroutine pool, applies regex patterns, deduplicates violations, writes scan_job to PostgreSQL, prints report. CI gate: GitHub Actions step runs payshield audit and checks exit code 2 for critical violations.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 must concretely implement ISS-001 (Luhn post-filter) and ISS-002 (service/endpoint attribution) which were described but not fully specified in round 2. For ISS-001: the pattern module needs a luhn_check(s string) bool function applied after every PAN regex match; all sections referencing the scanner must consistently reflect this. For ISS-002: the --service and --endpoint flags must become documented requirements in the audit subcommand; the JSON schema in api_design must show the new fields; the violation_findings database table must include them. Both changes are self-consistent and do not affect the core goroutine pool architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented Luhn algorithm post-filter in the pattern registry module: every regex match for pattern_type=pan passes through luhn_check() which validates the digit sequence using the standard double-and-sum algorithm; matches that fail Luhn are discarded and logged to stderr at --debug level with the reason 'luhn_invalid'. Added integration test fixture with 20 Luhn-invalid 16-digit sequences (transaction reference format TXN-XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX) asserting zero PAN violations reported.
fix_report.expected_outcome: PAN false-positive rate reduced from approximately 40% to under 2%; engineers no longer need to suppress legitimate PAN findings due to noise from transaction reference numbers; CI gate becomes actionable

fix_report.action_taken: Added --service (required string) and --endpoint (optional string, default '*') flags to the audit subcommand. Updated JSON report schema to include service_name and endpoint_pattern in every violation object. Updated violation_findings PostgreSQL table to store both fields. Updated scan_job record to include service_name and endpoint_pattern for trend aggregation queries by service. Updated QSA report template to group violations by service_name for the compliance evidence output.
fix_report.expected_outcome: JSON report output maps every violation to its source service and endpoint pattern; QSA can directly assign remediation responsibility to the owning team; trend analytics can show per-service compliance improvement over time

target_output.title: PayShield Log Audit CLI — PCI-DSS Cardholder Data Exposure Scanner
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI tool for payment engineering teams that scans API request log files for cardholder data (PAN, CVV, IBAN, sort-code) logged in plain text, applying Luhn validation to eliminate false positives, attributing violations to service and endpoint for QSA reporting, and exiting with code 2 on critical findings to block CI deployments. Round 3 resolves Luhn validation for PAN patterns and adds mandatory service/endpoint attribution to all violation report fields.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single compiled Go 1.22 binary providing three Cobra subcommands: 'audit', 'report', and 'baseline'. The audit engine reads the input log file in 64 KB chunks and dispatches each chunk to a worker goroutine pool (8 workers by default, configurable via --concurrency). Each worker applies compiled regexp patterns from the sensitive_field_patterns registry; PAN matches pass through a Luhn algorithm post-filter that discards Luhn-invalid digit sequences (transaction reference numbers, invoice IDs). Surviving matches are enriched with the --service and --endpoint CLI flags and deduplicated by (pattern_type, line_offset, service) before report generation. Reports are emitted to stdout as JSON or human-readable text. A scan_job record and per-violation violation_finding records are written to PostgreSQL via pgx/v5 — storing only redacted_context (BIN-XXXX-XXXX-LAST4 format), never the raw matched string. Exit code 2 if critical violations exceed zero (overridable by --fail-on flag). The binary is distributed via internal Artifactory, signed with cosign, and verifiable before installation.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22 (pure Go, no cgo). CLI framework: Cobra with Viper for config file support. Pattern matching: Go regexp/syntax with compiled patterns and Luhn post-filter for PAN. Database: pgx/v5 driver connecting to PostgreSQL 16 via PAYSHIELD_DB_DSN env var; pgxpool capped at 5 connections. Report formats: encoding/json for JSON output, tabwriter for aligned text output. Distribution: go build -ldflags='-s -w' producing linux/amd64 and darwin/arm64 binaries; cosign signing via Fulcio OIDC; published to internal Artifactory. Testing: go test with -race flag, testify for assertions. CI: GitHub Actions with golangci-lint, 80% coverage gate on scanner/ and patterns/ packages.
target_output.functional_feature_map: audit subcommand: scans a log file or stdin for PAN (Luhn-validated), CVV (3/4 digit in card-number context), IBAN (ISO 13616 regex for 34 country prefixes), GB sort-code (XX-XX-XX format), and 8-digit UK bank account numbers; attributes every violation to --service and --endpoint; applies baseline exception file to suppress known-safe false positives; outputs JSON or text report; exits 0 (clean), 1 (low/medium violations only), 2 (critical or high violations found), 3 (scan error). report subcommand: queries PostgreSQL scan history and generates a QSA-ready compliance report showing violation trends by service, reduction rate over 90 days, and open vs. suppressed violation ratio. baseline subcommand: add/list/expire entries in the exception file; validates that no entry suppresses a Luhn-valid PAN pattern.
target_output.system_components: Pattern Registry: Go package patterns/ containing compiled regexp objects for each sensitive field type, Luhn validator (luhn.go: double-and-sum algorithm over digit-only string), and context validators for CVV (must appear adjacent to a PAN or card-expiry pattern). Scanner Engine: Go package scanner/ implementing the 64 KB chunk reader, 8-goroutine worker pool (sync.WaitGroup + buffered channel), per-chunk match deduplication map keyed by (pattern_type, byte_offset). Report Generator: package report/ with JSON marshaller and tabwriter text formatter; violation struct includes pattern_type, service_name, endpoint_pattern, line_offset, redacted_context, severity, suppressed_by_baseline. Database Client: package db/ with pgxpool, scan_job writer, violation_findings batch inserter, trend query runner. Baseline Resolver: package baseline/ parsing YAML exception file, validating SHA-256 hashes, rejecting expired entries, refusing Luhn-valid PAN hashes.
target_output.workflows: CI Gate Workflow: GitHub Actions step runs 'payshield audit --log-file $LOG_PATH --service payment-api --endpoint /v1/charge --output json --fail-on critical'; tool reads the log file, scans with 8 goroutines, applies Luhn filter (discarding Luhn-invalid 16-digit matches), deduplicates, enriches with service/endpoint, writes scan_job and violation_findings to PostgreSQL, prints JSON report to stdout, exits 2 if critical_count > 0; GitHub Actions marks the step as failed and blocks the deployment. Local Pre-Commit Workflow: Engineer runs 'payshield audit --log-file ./logs/debug.log --service checkout-service --output text'; tool outputs a human-readable table of violations grouped by endpoint pattern with redacted_context (e.g. '4111-XXXX-XXXX-1111 at line 4832 in POST /v1/payments'); engineer investigates the offending log statement, adds a structured log masking call, and re-runs the audit to confirm clean exit code 0.
target_output.data_model: scan_jobs (id UUID PK, operator VARCHAR sourced from PAYSHIELD_OPERATOR env, log_file_hash BYTEA SHA-256 of file path, service_name VARCHAR, endpoint_pattern VARCHAR, run_at TIMESTAMPTZ, duration_ms INT, critical_count INT, high_count INT, medium_count INT, low_count INT, exit_code SMALLINT). violation_findings (id UUID PK, scan_job_id UUID FK, pattern_type ENUM('pan','cvv','iban','sort_code','account_number'), service_name VARCHAR, endpoint_pattern VARCHAR, line_offset BIGINT, redacted_context VARCHAR — BIN-XXXX-XXXX-LAST4 format, severity ENUM('critical','high','medium','low'), suppressed_by_baseline BOOL, baseline_exception_hash CHAR(64) nullable). sensitive_field_patterns (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, regex_pattern TEXT, luhn_validation BOOL, severity ENUM, context_pattern TEXT nullable, active BOOL). baseline_exceptions (id UUID PK, pattern_hash CHAR(64), justification TEXT, created_by VARCHAR, expires_at DATE, service_name VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: CLI command interface: 'payshield audit --log-file <path|-> --service <name> --endpoint <pattern> [--baseline <file>] [--output json|text] [--fail-on critical|high|all] [--concurrency 8] [--debug]'. JSON stdout report schema: {scan_id, service_name, endpoint_pattern, run_at, duration_ms, summary: {critical, high, medium, low, suppressed}, violations: [{id, pattern_type, service_name, endpoint_pattern, line_offset, redacted_context, severity, suppressed_by_baseline}]}. Exit codes: 0=clean, 1=only low/medium violations, 2=critical or high violations present, 3=scan error. 'payshield report --from 2026-01-01 --service payment-api --output json' queries PostgreSQL and returns violation trend JSON. 'payshield baseline add --pattern <string> --justification <text> --expires 2026-12-31 --service <name>' adds a SHA-256-hashed exception entry.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The tool is itself in PCI-DSS scope (Requirement 3.3 and 10.3). Key controls: raw matched strings are never written to any output stream, file, or database — only redacted_context (BIN-XXXX-XXXX-LAST4) is stored. Database connection string sourced exclusively from PAYSHIELD_DB_DSN env var; accepting it via --flag is blocked at flag registration to prevent command-history leakage. Binary distribution uses cosign signing with Fulcio OIDC; installation script calls 'cosign verify' before executing the binary. Baseline exception entries stored as SHA-256 hashes; the baseline parser calls luhn_check on the plaintext before hashing and rejects any entry that would suppress a Luhn-valid 16-digit sequence. PostgreSQL access uses least-privilege roles: CI service account has INSERT on scan_jobs and violation_findings only; report role has SELECT only. PAYSHIELD_DB_DSN TLS connection string requires sslmode=require.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions release workflow on tag push: (1) go test ./... -race with 80% coverage gate on scanner/ and patterns/ packages, (2) golangci-lint with errcheck, staticcheck, and gosec linters, (3) go build -ldflags='-s -w' for linux/amd64 and darwin/arm64 with GOFLAGS=-trimpath, (4) cosign sign-blob with Fulcio OIDC certificate producing .sig files alongside binaries, (5) publish binary and .sig to internal Artifactory payshield-go-local repository. CI gate integration: teams add a GitHub Actions step calling 'payshield audit' and set the exit code check on step failure. Local installation: 'GOFLAGS=-mod=mod go install internal.artifactory.corp/payshield@v1.3.0' with pre-install cosign verify step. PostgreSQL schema migrations managed by golang-migrate applied on startup if --migrate flag is passed.
target_output.observability: Structured JSON log output to stderr when --debug flag is set: each log line includes timestamp, level, message, and fields such as chunk_offset, pattern_type, luhn_result (valid/invalid/skipped) for PAN matches, and goroutine_id. On completion, a metrics summary line is printed to stderr: {files_scanned, lines_processed, chunks_dispatched, regex_matches_raw, luhn_discarded, violations_after_dedup, duration_ms, db_write_ok}. JUnit XML report written to --junit-xml path for Datadog CI Visibility ingestion, showing scan_job as a test suite with each violation finding as a test case. CloudWatch custom metric payshield.critical_violations_found published via AWS SDK when PAYSHIELD_CLOUDWATCH_NAMESPACE env var is set, enabling team-level compliance dashboards.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost for the scanner binary itself — runs on existing CI runners and developer workstations. PostgreSQL 16 instance shared with other internal tooling; scan_jobs and violation_findings tables projected to grow at 500 KB/month for a team running 200 CI scans/day — negligible. Binary build and publish in GitHub Actions uses approximately 2 minutes of CI time per release. No runtime scaling considerations; each scan is a stateless subprocess invocation.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (completed — round 1-2): Core scanner goroutine pool, PAN/CVV regex patterns, Cobra subcommands, JSON/text report output, PostgreSQL scan_job writer, cosign binary signing, baseline exception file with hash-only enforcement. Phase 2 (this round — round 3): Luhn algorithm post-filter for PAN patterns (luhn.go in patterns/ package), --service and --endpoint flags added to audit subcommand, violation_findings and scan_jobs schema updated with service_name and endpoint_pattern columns, QSA report grouping by service_name, integration test suite for Luhn-invalid fixture cases and attribution assertion. Phase 3 (planned): IBAN and sort-code pattern additions, SARIF output format for GitHub Advanced Security inline PR annotations, real-time streaming mode for tailing live log output, custom pattern plugin interface.
target_output.development_guidelines: The patterns/ package has a mandatory unit test for every pattern: each test includes 10 known-positive samples and 10 known-negative samples including Luhn-invalid 16-digit sequences for PAN. The --service flag is a required string in the audit subcommand; CI pipelines that omit it fail at flag parse time before any scanning begins. The luhn_check function must have 100% branch coverage in tests. Matched string content must never appear in any fmt.Print, log, or JSON marshal call — enforced by a gosec rule and a pre-commit grep for raw match group references outside the redaction function. All SQL queries use pgx/v5 named parameters; no string interpolation in query construction.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Luhn validation increases per-match CPU cost by ~10 microseconds; at 1000 raw PAN regex matches per 1 GB file this adds 10ms — well within the 60-second SLA. Risk: Go regexp/syntax does not support backreferences needed for some ISO 13616 IBAN patterns; workaround is a two-pass approach (broad regex then Go validation function for the country-code check digit). Tradeoff: requiring --service as a mandatory flag prevents scanning without attribution but initially breaks teams who invoke the tool without the flag — mitigated by a 2-sprint grace period where --service is optional with a deprecation warning before becoming required. Tradeoff: storing violation_findings in PostgreSQL rather than emitting them only to stdout enables trend analytics but means the tool has a runtime database dependency — the --no-db flag allows stateless operation for teams without internal network access.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Luhn validation as a post-regex filter rather than embedding it in the regex pattern (which would require atomic grouping not supported in Go regexp). Confirmed --service as a required flag from v1.3.0 with a deprecation warning period in v1.2.x. Confirmed SHA-256 hashing in baseline exception entries rather than storing plaintext patterns, as plaintext patterns in the exception file could themselves constitute a cardholder data store. Confirmed JUnit XML as the CI observability format over OpenTelemetry traces given the stateless subprocess execution model.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision fully resolving PAN Luhn false-positive filter and service/endpoint report attribution for a PCI-DSS log audit CLI tool.
